topics.title: Application-Layer Paradigms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application-Layer Paradigms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application-Layer Paradigms** are the architectural blueprints that govern how distributed applications organize, communicate, and coordinate themselves over a network. They define the *interaction topology* between communicating end-systems (hosts) at the highest layer of the TCP/IP (or OSI) reference model — the layer closest to the end-user.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCST501)** syllabus, the three canonical paradigms recognized are:

1. **Client–Server Paradigm (C/S)** — A centrally orchestrated model where a dedicated, always-on host (the *server*) provides a defined service to multiple requesting hosts (the *clients*).
2. **Peer-to-Peer Paradigm (P2P)** — A decentralized, symmetric model where every host (peer) can simultaneously act as both client and server, sharing resources directly without a central authority.
3. **Hybrid Paradigm** — A blended architecture that combines the strengths of both models (e.g., centralized indexing with decentralized data transfer).

The **Application Layer (Layer 7)** supports these paradigms using fundamental abstractions:

- **Process** — A running program on a host (identified by a 16-bit **port number**).
- **Socket** — The software endpoint (API) through which a process sends/receives messages.
- **Addressing** — Uniquely identifying a process via the tuple `(IP address, Port number)`.
- **Transport Service Interface** — The contract specifying reliability, throughput, timing, and security.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an Application-Layer Paradigm as the "organizational culture" of a network.**

### 🍽️ Analogy 1: The Restaurant
- **Client–Server Paradigm** → A formal restaurant.
  - **Waiter (Client)** takes your order to the **kitchen (Server)**.
  - The kitchen is always open (always-on server), has one fixed menu (defined service), and serves many tables (scalability).
  - Clients never serve food to each other — there is a strict role separation.

- **Peer-to-Peer Paradigm** → A potluck dinner party.
  - Every guest (**peer**) brings a dish *and* eats from others' dishes.
  - There is no single kitchen; everyone is both a provider and consumer.
  - More guests arrive → more food is available (natural scalability, but coordination is messy).

- **Hybrid Paradigm** → A potluck with a group chat coordinator.
  - A small "organizer" tracks who is bringing what (central index), but food is still exchanged directly peer-to-peer.

### 🏢 Analogy 2: The Office Network
A **client process** is like an employee requesting a report; a **server process** is the HR department fulfilling it. A **socket** is the intercom button on the employee's desk — pressing it dials HR's specific extension (port).

---

## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 Scheme)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concepts for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **Always-on server** characteristic of C/S (hosts with public IP).
> - **Self-scalability** of P2P (each peer adds demand *and* capacity).
> - **Socket = (IP address, Port number)** — the addressing tuple.
> - **Port number = 16 bits** → range **0 to 65535**.
> - Application-layer protocols (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS) are tied to the C/S paradigm; BitTorrent, Gnutella are tied to P2P.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Client–Server Paradigm — Operational Logic

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Server Process Initialization**
   - The server process boots *before* any client request.
   - It binds to a **well-known port number** (e.g., HTTP → **80**, HTTPS → **443**, FTP → **21**).
   - It enters an infinite `listen()` loop, blocking on the socket.

2. **Client Process Invocation**
   - The client process starts (or is invoked) when the user requests a service.
   - It dynamically acquires an **ephemeral port** (range **49152 to 65535**) from the OS.
   - It issues a connection request: `connect(server_IP, server_port)`.

3. **Handshake & Data Exchange**
   - The OS kernel completes the transport-layer handshake (TCP 3-way handshake or UDP datagram send).
   - The client sends a **request message** conforming to the application protocol (e.g., HTTP `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`).
   - The server parses the request, processes it, and returns a **response message**.

4. **Connection Termination**
   - After exchange, the client closes the socket; the server returns to `listen()`.

**Core Characteristics of Client–Server:**
- **Always-on server** with a fixed, well-known address (public IP + well-known port).
- **Clients communicate directly with the server**, not with each other.
- **Server has a fixed role**; clients have a fixed role — *asymmetric*.
- **Resource concentration** at the server → bottleneck, single point of failure.
- **Requires infrastructure** (managed data centers, stable IP, power backup).

---

## 2.2 The Peer-to-Peer Paradigm — Operational Logic

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Peer Discovery**
   - A peer joins the network (e.g., via a *bootstrap node* or distributed hash table).
   - It learns the addresses of other peers (via a *tracker*, DHT, or gossip protocol).

2. **Direct Peering**
   - Peers communicate **directly** without a permanent intermediary server.
   - Any peer can act as a client (downloader) or server (uploader) at any moment.

3. **Resource Contribution**
   - Each peer contributes bandwidth, storage, and CPU cycles.
   - **Self-scaling property**: more peers → more demand *and* more supply.

**Core Characteristics of P2P:**
- **Symmetric / Symmetrical roles** — every node is equal.
- **No always-on server**; decentralization = high fault tolerance.
- **Self-scalability** (each new peer adds service capacity).
- **Complex management** of peers joining/leaving (**churn**).
- **Security challenges** — peers are untrusted; require cryptographic verification.

---

## 2.3 The Hybrid Paradigm — Operational Logic

The **Hybrid Paradigm** combines a lightweight centralized component (e.g., an index server) with peer-to-peer data transfer. Examples:

- **Napster** (original): centralized index of files, but P2P file transfer.
- **Skype** (older versions): centralized login + relay server, but P2P voice data.
- **Modern BitTorrent trackers**: trackers coordinate `.torrent` swarms; peers exchange chunks directly.

**Why Hybrid?** Pure C/S suffers from server bottlenecks; pure P2P suffers from discovery inefficiency. Hybrid captures the best of both — *centralized metadata, decentralized payload*.

---

## 2.4 Processes, Sockets, and Addressing

### Processes
- A **process** is a program in execution within a host.
- Within the **same host**, processes are distinguished by the OS using **process IDs**.
- Across **different hosts**, processes are distinguished using the **(IP address, Port number)** tuple.

### Sockets
> [!NOTE]
> A **socket** is the interface (API) between the application layer and the transport layer within a host. It is the "doorway" through which a process sends and receives messages.

For the **TCP/IP suite**, sockets follow the **Berkeley Sockets API** standard:

$$\text{Socket} = (\text{IP Address}, \text{Port Number})$$

### Port Number Allocation (16-bit space)

| Port Range (Decimal) | Port Range (Hex) | Category | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0 – 1023** | `0x0000 – 0x03FF` | **Well-Known Ports** | HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), FTP (21), SSH (22), DNS (53), SMTP (25) |
| **1024 – 49151** | `0x0400 – 0xBFFF` | **Registered Ports** | Assigned by IANA to user applications (e.g., MySQL → 3306) |
| **49152 – 65535** | `0xC000 – 0xFFFF` | **Dynamic / Private / Ephemeral Ports** | Assigned automatically by OS to client sockets |

### Transport Service Requirements of Common Applications

| Application | Data Loss | Bandwidth | Time-Sensitive? |
|---|---|---|---|
| File Transfer (FTP) | No loss | Elastic (high preferred) | No |
| E-mail (SMTP) | No loss | Elastic | No |
| Web (HTTP) | No loss | Elastic (variable) | No (mostly) |
| Real-time Audio/Video (VoIP) | Loss-tolerant | Minimum threshold (e.g., 32 kbps for audio) | **Yes** (delay < 400 ms) |
| Stored Video (YouTube) | Loss-tolerant | Elastic | **Yes** (buffered) |
| Interactive Games | Loss-tolerant | Moderate | **Yes** (delay < 50 ms ideal) |
| Financial Trading | No loss | High | **Yes** (latency-critical) |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table for direct numerical problems in the KTU exam.**

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Port number bits | $n = 16$ | bits |
| 2 | Total possible ports | $N_{ports} = 2^{16} = 65\,536$ | unique values 0–65535 |
| 3 | Well-known port count | $2^{10} = 1024$ | range 0–1023 |
| 4 | Registered port count | $49\,152 - 1024 + 1 = 48\,128$ | range 1024–49151 |
| 5 | Ephemeral port count | $65\,535 - 49\,152 + 1 = 16\,384$ | range 49152–65535 |
| 6 | IPv4 address size | $32$ | bits → $\sim 4.3 \times 10^9$ addresses |
| 7 | IPv6 address size | $128$ | bits → $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ addresses |
| 8 | Socket tuple | $\text{Socket} = \langle \text{IP}, \text{Port} \rangle$ | uniquely identifies a process |
| 9 | Throughput requirement | $R$ bits/sec | application-dependent |
| 10 | End-to-end delay bound | $d \le 400$ ms (interactive) | ITU-T G.114 recommendation |
| 11 | P2P distribution time (worst-case C/S) | $D_{C/S} \ge \max\left\{\dfrac{NF}{u_s},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{min}}\right\}$ | N peers, file size F, server upload $u_s$ |
| 12 | P2P distribution time (BitTorrent ideal) | $D_{P2P} \ge \max\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\; \dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{min}}\right\}$ | includes peer aggregate bandwidth |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web architects** choose C/S for **stateless RESTful APIs** with **load balancers** (e.g., Netflix microservices).
- **CDNs (Content Delivery Networks)** deploy a **hybrid paradigm**: centralized origin servers with geographically distributed edge caches serving clients directly.
- **Blockchain / Distributed Ledger** technology extends the P2P paradigm with cryptographic consensus (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum).
- **Industrial IoT** uses C/S for **SCADA master stations** querying remote terminal units (RTUs).
- **Video conferencing** (Zoom, Teams) uses a **hybrid SFU/MCU** model — centralized relay (C/S) combined with selective forwarding units.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Number of Port Numbers

The transport layer identifies a process using a **16-bit unsigned integer** (port number). We derive the total addressable space:

$$
n = 16 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
N_{ports} = 2^{n} = 2^{16}
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{16} &= 2^{10} \times 2^{6} \\
&= 1024 \times 64 \\
&= 65\,536
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Since the numbering starts at **0**, the valid port range is $\lbrace 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 65535 \rbrace$, giving exactly **65 536 distinct values**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Boundary Between Port Categories

The **IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority)** partitions the 16-bit port space into three disjoint regions. We derive the cardinalities:

### Category A — Well-Known Ports ($0$ to $1023$ inclusive)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{well-known} &= 1023 - 0 + 1 \\
&= 1024 \\
&= 2^{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A 10-bit field can address $2^{10} = 1024$ values. These are reserved for **privileged services** requiring root/administrator access to bind (HTTP, FTP, SSH, etc.).

### Category B — Registered Ports ($1024$ to $49151$ inclusive)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{registered} &= 49\,151 - 1024 + 1 \\
&= 48\,128
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** IANA assigns these to user-level applications upon request (e.g., MySQL → 3306, PostgreSQL → 5432).

### Category C — Dynamic / Ephemeral Ports ($49152$ to $65535$ inclusive)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{ephemeral} &= 65\,535 - 49\,152 + 1 \\
&= 16\,384 \\
&= 2^{14}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The OS auto-allocates these to client sockets from the upper end of the port space. They are short-lived, hence *ephemeral*.

### Verification (Conservation Law)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= N_{well-known} + N_{registered} + N_{ephemeral} \\
&= 1024 + 48\,128 + 16\,384 \\
&= 65\,536 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the partition is **complete and non-overlapping**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Socket Uniqueness Theorem

**Claim:** The tuple $\langle \text{IP}_{\text{host}}, \text{Port}_{\text{process}} \rangle$ uniquely identifies a process within the global Internet.

**Justification:**

1. Every host connected to the Internet is assigned a globally unique IP address (IPv4 or IPv6).
2. Every active process on a host binds to a port number that is unique *within that host* (managed by the OS kernel's transport-layer table).
3. Therefore, the Cartesian product $\text{IP}_{\text{host}} \times \text{Port}_{\text{process}}$ yields a globally unique identifier.

$$
\text{Global PID}_{net} = \langle \text{IPv4}_{32-bit},\; \text{Port}_{16-bit} \rangle
$$

**Total identifying bits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{total} &= 32 + 16 \quad (\text{IPv4}) \\
&= 48 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For IPv6:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{total} &= 128 + 16 \quad (\text{IPv6}) \\
&= 144 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

The IPv6 socket tuple provides $2^{144} \approx 2.23 \times 10^{43}$ unique identifiers — astronomically large and effectively inexhaustible.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Paradigms

| Dimension | Client–Server | Peer-to-Peer | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Centralized (star) | Fully distributed (mesh) | Partially centralized |
| **Roles** | Asymmetric (fixed server/clients) | Symmetric (peers equal) | Mixed |
| **Always-on component** | Yes (server) | No | Yes (small index) |
| **Scalability** | Limited by server capacity | Self-scaling | High |
| **Single point of failure** | Yes | No | Partial |
| **Discovery complexity** | Trivial (well-known address) | Complex (DHT, tracker) | Moderate |
| **Security model** | Centralized trust (PKI, ACL) | Distributed trust (PGP, blockchain) | Hybrid PKI |
| **Management** | Easy | Hard (churn handling) | Moderate |
| **Bandwidth efficiency** | Server bottleneck at high load | Best (load spread across peers) | Good |
| **Examples** | Web (HTTP), Email (SMTP), DNS | BitTorrent, Gnutella | Skype (early), Napster, Modern CDNs |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Breakdown: P2P File Distribution Time

A classic **KTU problem** is to derive the minimum distribution time of an $F$-bit file to $N$ peers.

### Case 1 — Client–Server Architecture

The server must upload the file (or chunks) sequentially. With server upload rate $u_s$ and each peer's minimum download rate $d_{min}$:

$$
D_{C/S} \ge \max\left\{\frac{NF}{u_s},\; \frac{F}{d_{min}}\right\}
$$

**Derivation of Server Upload Term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bits to upload} &= N \times F \\
\text{Server upload rate} &= u_s \\
\therefore \text{Time to upload to all} &= \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of Slowest Peer Term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slowest peer's download rate} &= d_{min} \\
\text{Bits to download (one copy)} &= F \\
\therefore \text{Worst-case reception time} &= \frac{F}{d_{min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The system finishes only when **both** conditions are satisfied → take the **maximum**.

### Case 2 — P2P Architecture (BitTorrent Ideal)

After the initial seed, peers redistribute chunks among themselves. Let $u_i$ be peer $i$'s upload rate and $u_s$ the original seeder's upload rate:

$$
D_{P2P} \ge \max\left\{\frac{F}{u_s},\; \frac{NF}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i},\; \frac{F}{d_{min}}\right\}
$$

**Derivation of Aggregate Upload Term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bits to distribute} &= N \cdot F \\
\text{Aggregate upload bandwidth} &= u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i \\
\therefore \text{Aggregate upload time} &= \frac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Comparative Insight

For large $N$, the C/S term $\frac{NF}{u_s}$ grows **linearly** (bottleneck), while the P2P term $\frac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i}$ grows **sub-linearly** (since $\sum u_i \propto N$). This mathematically proves the **self-scaling** advantage of P2P.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture Diagram — Client–Server Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLIENT["CLIENT TIER"]
        C1["CLIENT A Browser or App"]
        C2["CLIENT B Browser or App"]
        C3["CLIENT C Browser or App"]
    end

    subgraph NETWORK["TRANSPORT NETWORK TCP or UDP"]
        NW["PACKET FORWARDING ROUTERS AND SWITCHES"]
    end

    subgraph SERVER["SERVER TIER"]
        SOCK["LISTEN SOCKET BOUND PORT 80 OR 443"]
        SVC["WEB SERVICE APPLICATION LOGIC"]
        DB["DATABASE BACKEND STORAGE"]
    end

    C1 -->|HTTP Request over Socket| NW
    C2 -->|HTTP Request over Socket| NW
    C3 -->|HTTP Request over Socket| NW
    NW --> SOCK
    SOCK --> SVC
    SVC <--> DB
    SVC -->|HTTP Response over Socket| NW
    NW --> C1
    NW --> C2
    NW --> C3
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Clients A, B, C issue **HTTP requests** through **ephemeral sockets** (random high ports).
- All converge on the server's **well-known socket** (port 80/443).
- The server processes the request via application logic + database, then returns a response on the same connection.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture Diagram — Peer-to-Peer Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PEER["PEER GROUP"]
        P1["PEER 1 Has Chunk A"]
        P2["PEER 2 Has Chunk B"]
        P3["PEER 3 Has Chunk C"]
        P4["PEER 4 Has Chunk D"]
        P5["PEER 5 Needs All Chunks"]
        P6["PEER 6 Needs All Chunks"]
    end

    P1 <-->|Chunk Swap| P2
    P1 <-->|Chunk Swap| P5
    P2 <-->|Chunk Swap| P3
    P3 <-->|Chunk Swap| P4
    P4 <-->|Chunk Swap| P6
    P5 <-->|Chunk Swap| P6
    P2 <-->|Chunk Swap| P4
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- There is **no central server**; every node (peer) acts as both client and server.
- Peers exchange **chunks** of a file directly using random ephemeral ports.
- A peer's role is **dynamic** — it may upload chunk A while downloading chunk B.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture Diagram — Hybrid Paradigm (Napster Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CENTRAL["CENTRAL COMPONENT"]
        IDX["CENTRAL INDEX SERVER Stores File Metadata Only"]
    end

    subgraph P2PSWARM["PEER-TO-PEER SWARM"]
        X1["PEER 1 Has Music File X"]
        X2["PEER 2 Has Music File Y"]
        X3["PEER 3 Seeks Music File X"]
    end

    X3 -->|1 Query Index| IDX
    IDX -->|2 Return Peer Address| X3
    X3 -->|3 Direct Download| X1
    X3 -.->|Indirect Download| X2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Step 1–2**: Discovery is centralized (index server tells Peer 3 where to find file X).
- **Step 3**: The actual data transfer is **peer-to-peer**, bypassing the central server and saving bandwidth.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture Diagram — Socket Addressing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOST_A["HOST A IP 192.168.1.10"]
        PROC_A1["Web Client Process Port 49152"]
        PROC_A2["SSH Client Process Port 49153"]
    end

    subgraph HOST_B["HOST B IP 203.0.113.5"]
        PROC_B1["Web Server Process Port 80"]
        PROC_B2["SSH Server Process Port 22"]
    end

    PROC_A1 -->|Socket Tuple 192.168.1.10 49152| NW(("ROUTER"))
    NW -->|Destined For| PROC_B1
    PROC_A2 -->|Socket Tuple 192.168.1.10 49153| NW
    NW -->|Destined For| PROC_B2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- A **socket** on Host A is identified by the tuple $\langle 192.168.1.10,\; 49152 \rangle$.
- The destination is identified by $\langle 203.0.113.5,\; 80 \rangle$.
- The network routes packets using the **destination IP**, and the **destination OS** delivers to the correct process using the **port number**.

---

## 4.5 Process Communication Flow Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as APPLICATION PROCESS
    participant Sock as SOCKET API
    participant Trans as TRANSPORT LAYER TCP or UDP
    participant Net as NETWORK LAYER IP
    participant Remote as REMOTE SOCKET

    App->>Sock: Create and Bind Socket
    Sock->>Trans: Request Connection Setup
    Trans->>Net: Encapsulate into IP Packet
    Net->>Remote: Transmit over Network
    Remote-->>Net: Receive IP Packet
    Net-->>Trans: Deliver to Remote Transport Layer
    Trans-->>Sock: Receive Data on Remote Socket
    Sock-->>App: Deliver Payload to Application
    App->>App: Process and Respond
```

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the Client–Server and Peer-to-Peer paradigms. List any two real-world applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Client–Server | Peer-to-Peer |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | Centralized (star) | Distributed (mesh) |
| Roles | Asymmetric — dedicated server | Symmetric — peers equal |
| Always-on host | Yes (server) | No |
| Scalability | Limited by server | Self-scaling |
| Example apps | **Web (HTTP)**, **Email (SMTP)**, DNS | **BitTorrent**, **Gnutella**, Blockchain |

*(2 marks for any 4 valid points, 1 mark for correct examples.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define a socket. Explain how a process is uniquely identified across the global Internet.**

**Model Answer:**

A **socket** is the software endpoint (API) between the application layer and the transport layer, used by a process to send and receive messages over the network.

A process is uniquely identified across the Internet by the **tuple**:

$$
\langle \text{IP Address},\; \text{Port Number} \rangle
$$

- The **IP address** identifies the *host* (32 bits for IPv4, 128 bits for IPv6).
- The **port number** identifies the *process* within that host (16 bits, range **0–65535**).

Together they form a globally unique identifier for an end-to-end communication endpoint. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks addressing tuple explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Client–Server Paradigm** with a neat block diagram. Describe its characteristics and list **four** application-layer protocols that use this paradigm. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Peer-to-Peer Paradigm** with a suitable diagram. Compare its advantages and disadvantages with the Client–Server model. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a)

The **Client–Server Paradigm** is a centralized architecture where a dedicated, always-on **server** host provides services to multiple **client** hosts that initiate requests.

**Characteristics:**
1. **Always-on server** with a fixed, well-known IP address and a **well-known port number** (e.g., HTTP → 80, FTP → 21).
2. **Clients communicate only with the server**, never directly with each other.
3. **Asymmetric roles** — server and clients have fixed, distinct responsibilities.
4. **Server is a single point of failure** and a **bottleneck** under heavy load.
5. Requires managed infrastructure — stable power, public IP, redundancy.

**Four Application-Layer Protocols:**
- **HTTP (Port 80)** — Web
- **FTP (Port 21)** — File Transfer
- **SMTP (Port 25)** — Email
- **DNS (Port 53)** — Domain Name Resolution

*(Diagram from Section 4.1 may be reproduced — 2 marks; 4 marks for characteristics; 1 mark for the four protocols.)*

#### Model Solution (b)

The **Peer-to-Peer Paradigm** is a decentralized architecture where every host (peer) can simultaneously act as both a client and a server, sharing resources directly with other peers.

**Diagram:** (Refer Section 4.2 — Peer-to-Peer mesh.)

**Advantages of P2P over Client–Server:**
1. **Self-scaling** — each new peer adds both demand *and* service capacity.
2. **No single point of failure** — fault tolerance through redundancy.
3. **Cost-effective** — no dedicated infrastructure; uses peers' spare bandwidth.
4. **High aggregate bandwidth** — load is distributed across many peers.
5. **Resilience to censorship** — no central authority to shut down.

**Disadvantages of P2P:**
1. **Complex peer discovery** — requires DHTs, trackers, or gossip protocols.
2. **Security challenges** — peers are untrusted; risk of malware and poisoned chunks.
3. **Variable performance** — depends on peers' voluntary contribution; churn handling.
4. **Difficult to manage and audit** — no central admin.

*(2 marks diagram + 3 marks advantages + 2 marks disadvantages = 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **concept of processes, sockets, and addressing** in the application layer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a diagram, explain the **Hybrid Paradigm**. How does it combine features of both Client–Server and P2P models? Give **two** examples. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a)

**Processes:**
- A **process** is a running program on a host.
- Within a host, the OS distinguishes processes by **process IDs**.
- Across the network, processes are distinguished by the tuple $\langle \text{IP},\; \text{Port} \rangle$.

**Sockets:**
- A **socket** is the API endpoint through which a process sends and receives messages.
- It is the interface between the application layer and the transport layer (TCP/UDP).
- Two processes communicating over the Internet use a pair of sockets — one on each host.

**Addressing:**
- The **IP address** uniquely identifies the host.
- The **port number** (16 bits, 0–65535) identifies the specific process on the host.
- Combined: $\langle \text{IP},\; \text{Port} \rangle$ — global uniqueness for end-to-end delivery.

**Diagram:** (Refer Section 4.4 — Socket Addressing diagram.)

**Port Number Categories:**

| Range | Category | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| 0–1023 | Well-Known | HTTP 80, FTP 21, DNS 53 |
| 1024–49151 | Registered | MySQL 3306 |
| 49152–65535 | Ephemeral | Assigned by OS to clients |

*(2 marks processes + 2 marks sockets + 2 marks addressing + 1 mark categories = 7 marks.)*

#### Model Solution (b)

The **Hybrid Paradigm** combines a lightweight centralized component with peer-to-peer data exchange.

**Diagram:** (Refer Section 4.3 — Napster Model.)

**How it Combines C/S and P2P:**
- A **central index server** (C/S feature) handles **discovery** — peers query it to find who has the desired resource.
- **Data transfer is peer-to-peer** (P2P feature) — once peers discover each other, they exchange data directly.
- This eliminates the central bottleneck for data delivery while retaining fast, centralized search.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Napster** — Central server indexes music files; peers download directly from each other.
2. **Skype (early versions)** — Central login server authenticates users; voice data flows peer-to-peer.
3. **Modern BitTorrent with trackers** — Trackers coordinate `.torrent` swarms; chunks are exchanged directly between peers.

*(2 marks diagram + 3 marks C/S-P2P integration explanation + 2 marks examples = 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Socket with Port:** A *port* is just a number; a *socket* is the endpoint (IP + Port). Many students write "socket = port number" — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting "well-known port" range:** Stating port ranges as 0–65535 without breaking down into well-known / registered / ephemeral loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Writing "P2P = no server" strictly:** Modern P2P uses bootstrap nodes / trackers — explain the nuance to gain **full 2 marks** for hybrid understanding.
> 4. **Missing the role asymmetry:** In C/S, "client and server are different roles" is a key 1-mark point.
> 5. **Diagram mistakes:** Drawing P2P with a central server or omitting the socket tuple in the addressing diagram → **deduct 2 marks**.
> 6. **Application protocol examples:** Always cite **standard port numbers** (HTTP = 80, not just "HTTP"). Examiners reward this precision.
> 7. **No sketch for Part A:** Part A questions that ask "with diagram" *without* a diagram lose **1 mark** — even if the answer text is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Application-Layer Paradigms**

- **Three Paradigms:** Client–Server, Peer-to-Peer, Hybrid.
- **Client–Server:** Centralized; **always-on server**; well-known port; asymmetric roles; bottleneck.
- **P2P:** Decentralized; **symmetric roles**; **self-scaling**; complex discovery; resilient but untrusted.
- **Hybrid:** Centralized metadata + decentralized payload (e.g., **Napster**, **Skype**, **BitTorrent trackers**).
- **Process** = running program; identified globally by $\langle \text{IP},\; \text{Port} \rangle$.
- **Socket** = API endpoint = $\langle \text{IP address},\; \text{port number} \rangle$.
- **Port = 16 bits** → range **0 to 65535** → **65 536** total values.
- **Well-known ports:** 0–1023 (1024 ports; e.g., HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, FTP=21, SSH=22, DNS=53, SMTP=25).
- **Registered ports:** 1024–49151 (48 128 ports).
- **Ephemeral ports:** 49152–65535 (16 384 ports).
- **C/S distribution time:** $D_{C/S} \ge \max\!\left\{\dfrac{NF}{u_s},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{min}}\right\}$.
- **P2P distribution time:** $D_{P2P} \ge \max\!\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\; \dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{min}}\right\}$.
- **Application requirements** vary: FTP = lossless + elastic; VoIP = loss-tolerant + time-sensitive (< 400 ms).
- **Socket uniqueness:** IPv4 socket = 48 bits; IPv6 socket = 144 bits — practically unique.
- **Standard protocols & ports to memorize:** HTTP(80), HTTPS(443), FTP(21), SSH(22), Telnet(23), SMTP(25), DNS(53), DHCP(67/68), POP3(110), IMAP(143).
- **Draw diagrams** for every 7-mark question; partial marks heavily depend on it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Client-Server Applications: World Wide Web and HTTP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Client-Server Applications: World Wide Web and HTTP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Client-Server Paradigm

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> The **Client-Server Model** is a distributed application architecture that partitions tasks between **service providers (servers)** which host, manage, and deliver resources, and **service requesters (clients)** which initiate communication sessions and consume those resources over a network. In the context of the World Wide Web, the **Web Client (Browser)** requests hypertext documents from a **Web Server** using the **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine walking into a large restaurant:
- **You (the Client)** are seated at a table with a menu.
- **The Waiter (HTTP Protocol)** is the agreed-upon language and procedure for taking your order and delivering food.
- **The Kitchen (Web Server)** holds all the food (HTML pages, images, videos) but never comes out to the table.
- **The Menu (URL/URI)** is the unique address of every dish (resource).

You don't walk into the kitchen; you speak a standardized language to the waiter. The waiter enforces the procedure (order, prepare, serve). This is exactly how HTTP works — the **browser never directly accesses the server's filesystem**; it makes **structured requests** and receives **structured responses** through a well-defined protocol.

---

## 1.2 The World Wide Web (WWW)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition:**
> The **World Wide Web (WWW / Web)** is a global, distributed hypermedia system built on top of the Internet, where linked documents and resources (web pages, images, videos, applications) are identified by **Uniform Resource Locators (URLs)**, transferred primarily via **HTTP/HTTPS**, and rendered by **web browsers** using markup languages like **HTML** and styling standards like **CSS**.

### Three Pillars of the Web

| Pillar | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** | Application-layer message-exchange protocol | GET, POST requests |
| **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** | Content structure and hyperlinking language | `<a>`, `<h1>`, `<div>` |
| **URL (Uniform Resource Locator)** | Unique addressing scheme for every resource | `http://www.ktu.edu.in/index.html` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> The **Internet** is the global network infrastructure (cables, routers, TCP/IP).
> The **World Wide Web (WWW)** is *one* of the services running on top of the Internet, alongside Email, FTP, DNS, etc.
> The Web ≠ The Internet.

---

## 1.3 The HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (RFC 7230 / RFC 9110):**
> **HTTP** is a **stateless, request-response, text-based (in HTTP/1.1) or binary-framed (in HTTP/2 and HTTP/3) application-layer protocol** used by the World Wide Web. It defines how clients and servers communicate, format messages, transfer representations of resources, and indicate the success or failure of operations.

### Key Properties of HTTP

1. **Application Layer Protocol:** Operates over **TCP** (port **80** for HTTP, port **443** for HTTPS) on top of the OSI model's application layer.
2. **Client-Initiated:** The client always opens the connection; the server never initiates a session.
3. **Stateless:** Each request is independent — the server retains no memory of prior requests from the same client. (State is maintained externally using **Cookies** or **Sessions**.)
4. **Connectionless (HTTP/1.0 default):** A new TCP connection is opened for every object on a page.
5. **Reliable Transport:** Relies entirely on **TCP** for in-order, error-free delivery.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Borrower's Card"

When you borrow a book from a library, the librarian does not remember your previous borrowings after you leave. Every time you come back, you must show your borrower's card (Cookie/Session ID) and present your request (HTTP Request) clearly. The librarian verifies your card, finds the book (Resource), and hands it over (HTTP Response). This is the **stateless** nature of HTTP.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The HTTP Transaction Lifecycle

A complete HTTP interaction consists of four logical phases:

1. **Connection Establishment:** The client opens a TCP connection to the server on port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS). This is a **3-way handshake** consuming **1 RTT**.
2. **Request Transmission:** The client sends an HTTP request message describing the desired resource.
3. **Response Transmission:** The server processes the request and returns an HTTP response message containing the resource (or an error).
4. **Connection Termination:** The TCP connection is closed (or kept alive for reuse).

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of an HTTP Request Message

```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.ktu.edu.in
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html
Connection: keep-alive
[blank line - CRITICAL separator]
[Optional Body]
```

### Structural Breakdown

| Component | Mandatory? | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Request Line** | YES | Method + URL + HTTP Version |
| **Host Header** | YES (from HTTP/1.1) | Domain name of the server |
| **User-Agent** | NO | Identifies the client software |
| **Accept** | NO | MIME types the client can handle |
| **Connection** | NO | Controls connection persistence |
| **Blank Line (CRLF)** | YES | Separates headers from the body |
| **Entity Body** | Only for POST/PUT | Payload data |

---

## 2.3 Anatomy of an HTTP Response Message

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Mon, 15 Jan 2025 10:30:00 GMT
Server: Apache/2.4
Content-Type: text/html
Content-Length: 1234
Connection: keep-alive
[blank line]
<html><body>Hello KTU!</body></html>
```

### HTTP Status Code Categories (KTU High-Yield)

| Code Range | Category | Meaning | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx** | Informational | Request received, continuing | 100 Continue |
| **2xx** | Success | Request successfully processed | 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| **3xx** | Redirection | Further action needed | 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified |
| **4xx** | Client Error | Malformed request | 400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found |
| **5xx** | Server Error | Server failed to fulfill valid request | 500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The **404 Not Found** error means the server understood the request but cannot find the resource. The **400 Bad Request** means the request itself is syntactically malformed. **Do not confuse them in answers.**

---

## 2.4 HTTP Methods — The Verb Set

| Method | Type | Safe? | Idempotent? | Body Allowed? | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GET** | Read | YES | YES | NO | Retrieve a resource |
| **HEAD** | Read | YES | YES | NO | Retrieve headers only |
| **POST** | Write | NO | NO | YES | Submit data / create a resource |
| **PUT** | Write | NO | YES | YES | Replace / create a resource at a URI |
| **DELETE** | Write | NO | YES | NO | Delete a resource |
| **OPTIONS** | Meta | YES | YES | NO | Discover supported methods |
| **PATCH** | Write | NO | NO | YES | Apply partial modification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Safe** = Method does not modify server state.
> **Idempotent** = Executing the method multiple times produces the same final state (e.g., DELETE-ing an already-deleted resource is still 204 OK, hence idempotent).

---

## 2.5 URL Structure (Uniform Resource Locator)

```
http://www.ktu.edu.in:80/cs/network/index.html?topic=http#section2
\___/   \_______________/ \__/ \____________________/ \________/ \______/
Scheme     Authority      Port        Path             Query     Fragment
```

- **Scheme:** `http`, `https`, `ftp`, `file` — defines the protocol.
- **Authority:** `www.ktu.edu.in` — domain name (resolved via DNS to an IP).
- **Port:** Optional. Default is 80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS.
- **Path:** `/cs/network/index.html` — location of the resource on the server.
- **Query:** `?topic=http` — parameters passed to the resource.
- **Fragment:** `#section2` — client-side sub-resource identifier (never sent to the server).

---

## 2.6 Connection Modes: Persistent vs Non-Persistent

### Non-Persistent HTTP (Default in HTTP/1.0)
- A **separate TCP connection** is opened and closed for **each object** on a web page.
- **Cost:** 2 RTTs per object (1 RTT for TCP handshake + 1 RTT for request/response).
- High overhead due to repeated TCP slow-start and connection setup.

### Persistent HTTP (Default from HTTP/1.1)
- The **same TCP connection** is reused for multiple HTTP requests.
- **Without pipelining:** Client waits for each response before sending the next request → 1 RTT per object after the first.
- **With pipelining:** Client sends all requests back-to-back → all responses arrive in 1 RTT total.

> [!NOTE]
> HTTP/1.1 made persistent connections the **default** via the `Connection: keep-alive` header. Modern browsers may open **6 parallel persistent connections per host** to mitigate head-of-line blocking.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU Board Exam Numerical Problems**

### Table 1: Response Time Models

| Parameter | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $RTT$ | Round-Trip Time between client and server | seconds (s) |
| $F_i$ | Transmission time (file size $/$ bandwidth) of object $i$ | seconds (s) |
| $n$ | Number of referenced objects (images, scripts) on a page | dimensionless |
| $O$ | Object size in bits | bits |
| $B$ | Bottleneck link bandwidth | bits/second |

### Table 2: Total Response Time Formulas

| Mode | Total Response Time Formula |
|---|---|
| **Non-Persistent HTTP** (no parallelism) | $T_{NP} = 2RTT + \sum_{i=0}^{n} (2RTT + F_i)$ |
| **Non-Persistent HTTP** (simplified, ignoring $F$) | $T_{NP} \approx 2(n+1)RTT$ |
| **Persistent HTTP (No Pipelining)** | $T_{PN} = 2RTT + \sum_{i=0}^{n} (RTT + F_i)$ |
| **Persistent HTTP (Pipelining)** | $T_{PP} = 2RTT + \sum_{i=0}^{n} F_i$ |
| **File Transmission Time** | $F_i = O_i / B$ |
| **Number of RTTs (Non-Persistent, parallel $k$ connections)** | $RTT_{NP,k} \approx 2RTT \cdot \lceil n/k \rceil + 2RTT$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> In the table above, the ceiling operator $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ represents the smallest integer greater than or equal to the argument. When $n=0$ (only the base HTML file), the non-persistent formula reduces to $2RTT$ for the base file's TCP setup + request/response, hence the $(n+1)$ factor.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The HTTP model underpins virtually every modern internet service:
- **REST APIs** (used in microservices, mobile apps) are direct extensions of HTTP semantics.
- **CDNs (Content Delivery Networks)** use persistent connections to stream multimedia efficiently.
- **WebSockets** upgrade an HTTP connection to a full-duplex channel for real-time apps (chat, trading).
- **HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 (QUIC)** introduce multiplexing and binary framing to overcome the head-of-line blocking and 6-connection limit of HTTP/1.1.
- **Authentication flows** (OAuth 2.0, JWT) all ride on top of HTTP headers and bodies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Setup — A Canonical KTU Numerical

> **Standard KTU Problem Statement (Frequently Asked):**
> "Consider an HTTP client that wants to retrieve a web page consisting of a base HTML file and **n = 5 referenced objects** (e.g., 5 small images). The **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** between the client and the server is **100 ms**. The transmission time of each object is negligible compared to RTT. Compute the total response time under:
> (a) Non-Persistent HTTP with no parallel connections
> (b) Persistent HTTP without pipelining
> (c) Persistent HTTP with pipelining
> (d) Non-Persistent HTTP with 2 parallel connections."

### Given Data

$$
RTT = 100 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ s}, \quad n = 5, \quad F_i \approx 0
$$

We treat the base HTML file as object index $i = 0$, so the total number of file transfers is $n + 1 = 6$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation for Part (a) — Non-Persistent HTTP

### Step 1: Identify the per-object cost
For each object in non-persistent mode, the client must:
1. Open a fresh TCP connection → consumes $1 \cdot RTT$ (3-way handshake).
2. Send HTTP request and wait for response → consumes another $1 \cdot RTT$.

Therefore, the per-object time is:

$$
T_{\text{per object}} = 2 \cdot RTT
$$

### Step 2: Multiply by the total number of objects
There are $n + 1 = 6$ objects (1 base HTML + 5 images).

$$
T_{NP} = (n + 1) \cdot 2 \cdot RTT
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the numerical values

$$
T_{NP} = 6 \cdot 2 \cdot 100 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{NP} = 12 \cdot 100 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{NP} = 1200 \text{ ms} = 1.2 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation for Part (b) — Persistent HTTP Without Pipelining

### Step 1: First object pays the TCP-setup cost
The first HTTP request (the base HTML) requires a TCP handshake.

$$
T_{\text{first}} = 1 \cdot RTT \text{ (TCP setup)} + 1 \cdot RTT \text{ (request/response)} = 2 \cdot RTT
$$

### Step 2: Subsequent objects reuse the connection
For each of the remaining $n$ objects, the client sends a request and receives a response on the same open TCP socket. This costs $1 \cdot RTT$ per object (no separate TCP setup needed).

$$
T_{\text{rest}} = n \cdot 1 \cdot RTT
$$

### Step 3: Total time

$$
T_{PN} = 2 \cdot RTT + n \cdot RTT = (n + 2) \cdot RTT
$$

### Step 4: Substitute numerical values

$$
T_{PN} = (5 + 2) \cdot 100 \text{ ms} = 7 \cdot 100 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{PN} = 700 \text{ ms} = 0.7 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation for Part (c) — Persistent HTTP With Pipelining

### Step 1: Send all requests back-to-back
With pipelining, the client does not wait for each response. It fires all $n + 1$ requests immediately after the first response, and the server streams them back in order.

### Step 2: Total time
Only the first RTT for TCP setup and the second RTT for the first request/response matter. All remaining objects arrive in a single RTT burst.

$$
T_{PP} = 2 \cdot RTT + 1 \cdot RTT = 3 \cdot RTT
$$

### Step 3: Substitute numerical values

$$
T_{PP} = 3 \cdot 100 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{PP} = 300 \text{ ms} = 0.3 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation for Part (d) — Non-Persistent HTTP With 2 Parallel Connections

### Step 1: Objects are grouped into batches
With 2 parallel persistent-style connections (a hybrid used by browsers like Chrome and Firefox), the $n + 1 = 6$ objects are distributed across $\lceil (n+1)/2 \rceil$ rounds.

$$
\text{Rounds} = \left\lceil \frac{n + 1}{2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{6}{2} \right\rceil = 3
$$

### Step 2: Each round costs $2 \cdot RTT$ (TCP setup + request/response for 2 objects in parallel)

$$
T_{NP,k} = \left\lceil \frac{n+1}{k} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \cdot RTT
$$

### Step 3: Substitute $k = 2$

$$
T_{NP,2} = 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 100 \text{ ms} = 6 \cdot 100 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{NP,2} = 600 \text{ ms} = 0.6 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix consolidates all four scenarios for a 6-object page with $RTT = 100$ ms. This is the **single most important table** for any KTU numerical on HTTP.

| Connection Strategy | Rounds Required | RTT Multiplier | Total Time (ms) | Speedup vs Worst |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Non-Persistent, No Parallelism | 6 sequential | $2(n+1)$ | 1200 | 1.0x (baseline) |
| Non-Persistent, 2 Parallel | 3 rounds | $2 \lceil (n+1)/2 \rceil$ | 600 | 2.0x |
| Non-Persistent, 3 Parallel | 2 rounds | $2 \lceil (n+1)/3 \rceil$ | 400 | 3.0x |
| Persistent, No Pipelining | $n+1$ sequential | $(n+2)$ | 700 | 1.71x |
| Persistent, With Pipelining | 1 burst | 3 | 300 | 4.0x |

---

## 3.7 Worked Example With Non-Zero File Transmission Time

> **Extended KTU Problem:**
> "Suppose the base HTML file is **15,000 bits** and each of the 5 images is **50,000 bits**. The bottleneck bandwidth is **1 Mbps** (i.e., $B = 10^6$ bits/s). Compute the response time for persistent HTTP without pipelining."

### Step 1: Compute file transmission times
File transmission time is file size divided by bandwidth.

$$
F_{\text{HTML}} = \frac{15{,}000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
F_{\text{image}} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 50 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the persistent-without-pipelining formula

$$
T_{PN} = 2 \cdot RTT + RTT \cdot n + F_{\text{HTML}} + n \cdot F_{\text{image}}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute all values

$$
T_{PN} = 2(100) + 5(100) + 15 + 5(50)
$$

$$
T_{PN} = 200 + 500 + 15 + 250
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{PN} = 965 \text{ ms} \approx 0.965 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — HTTP Request-Response Cycle

The following Mermaid `sequenceDiagram` illustrates the complete HTTP/1.1 transaction between a web browser and a web server, including TCP setup, request, response, and connection closure.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant BROWSER as WEB CLIENT (BROWSER)
    participant SOCKET as TCP SOCKET (PORT 80)
    participant SERVER as WEB SERVER (APACHE NGINX)

    BROWSER ->> SOCKET: SYN (1st HANDSHAKE)
    SOCKET ->> BROWSER: SYN ACK (2nd HANDSHAKE)
    BROWSER ->> SERVER: ACK (3rd HANDSHAKE COMPLETE)
    Note over BROWSER, SERVER: TCP CONNECTION ESTABLISHED 1 RTT ELAPSED

    BROWSER ->> SERVER: HTTP GET REQUEST INDEX HTML
    SERVER ->> BROWSER: HTTP 200 OK WITH HTML BODY
    Note over BROWSER, SERVER: FIRST OBJECT RECEIVED 2 RTT ELAPSED

    BROWSER ->> SERVER: HTTP GET REQUEST IMAGE1 PNG
    SERVER ->> BROWSER: HTTP 200 OK WITH IMAGE DATA
    BROWSER ->> SERVER: HTTP GET REQUEST IMAGE2 PNG
    SERVER ->> BROWSER: HTTP 200 OK WITH IMAGE DATA
    BROWSER ->> SERVER: HTTP GET REQUEST IMAGE3 PNG
    SERVER ->> BROWSER: HTTP 200 OK WITH IMAGE DATA
    Note over BROWSER, SERVER: PERSISTENT CONNECTION REUSED NO NEW TCP HANDSHAKE

    BROWSER ->> SERVER: FIN (CONNECTION CLOSE)
    SERVER ->> BROWSER: FIN ACK
    Note over BROWSER, SERVER: TCP CONNECTION TERMINATED
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Client-Server Application Architecture

The following Mermaid `flowchart` depicts the functional architecture of a typical client-server web application, mapping the logical layers and the direction of data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CLIENTTIER["CLIENT TIER (USER DEVICE)"]
        UI1[WEB BROWSER UI]
        ENG1[HTML CSS JS RENDERING ENGINE]
        NET1[HTTP CLIENT LIBRARY]
        TCP1[TCP SOCKET INTERFACE]
    end

    subgraph NETWORKTIER["NETWORK TIER (INTERNET)"]
        ROUTER1[ROUTER 1]
        ROUTER2[ROUTER 2]
        ROUTER3[ROUTER 3]
    end

    subgraph SERVERTIER["SERVER TIER (DATA CENTER)"]
        NET2[HTTP SERVER DAEMON APACHE NGINX]
        APP1[APPLICATION LOGIC PHP PYTHON JAVA]
        DB1[DATABASE MYSQL POSTGRES]
        FS1[FILE SYSTEM STATIC ASSETS]
    end

    UI1 --> ENG1
    ENG1 --> NET1
    NET1 --> TCP1
    TCP1 --> ROUTER1
    ROUTER1 --> ROUTER2
    ROUTER2 --> ROUTER3
    ROUTER3 --> NET2
    NET2 --> APP1
    APP1 --> DB1
    APP1 --> FS1

    DB1 -.->|RESPONSE| APP1
    FS1 -.->|RESPONSE| APP1
    APP1 -.->|HTTP RESPONSE| NET2
    NET2 -.->|PACKETS| ROUTER3
    ROUTER3 -.->|PACKETS| ROUTER2
    ROUTER2 -.->|PACKETS| ROUTER1
    ROUTER1 -.->|BYTES| TCP1
    TCP1 -.->|BYTES| NET1
    NET1 -.->|BYTES| ENG1
    ENG1 -.->|PIXELS| UI1
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — HTTP Request/Response Message Format

The following Mermaid `flowchart` decomposes the structural layout of an HTTP request and an HTTP response into their constituent components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REQSTRUCT["HTTP REQUEST MESSAGE STRUCTURE"]
        R1[REQUEST LINE: METHOD URL VERSION]
        R2[HOST HEADER: WWW KTU EDU IN]
        R3[USER AGENT HEADER: MOZILLA]
        R4[ACCEPT HEADER: TEXT HTML]
        R5[CONNECTION HEADER: KEEP ALIVE]
        R6[BLANK LINE CRLF SEPARATOR]
        R7[OPTIONAL ENTITY BODY]
    end

    subgraph RESPSTRUCT["HTTP RESPONSE MESSAGE STRUCTURE"]
        S1[STATUS LINE: VERSION CODE PHRASE]
        S2[DATE HEADER]
        S3[SERVER HEADER: APACHE 2 4]
        S4[CONTENT TYPE HEADER]
        S5[CONTENT LENGTH HEADER]
        S6[BLANK LINE CRLF SEPARATOR]
        S7[ENTITY BODY: HTML CONTENT]
    end

    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 --> R7
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Persistent and Non-Persistent HTTP connections. Mention which version of HTTP uses persistent connections as default.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Non-Persistent HTTP | Persistent HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| **TCP Connection Lifetime** | One connection per object | One connection for multiple objects |
| **RTT Cost per Object** | $2 \cdot RTT$ (handshake + req/resp) | $1 \cdot RTT$ (req/resp only, after first) |
| **Server Overhead** | High (repeated setup) | Low (setup amortized) |
| **Default in Version** | HTTP/1.0 | HTTP/1.1 (via `Connection: keep-alive`) |

> **1 mark per key differentiator + 0 marks** (0.5 for version) — Use 3 valid points.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the significance of HTTP status codes. List any four 4xx codes with their meanings.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

HTTP status codes are 3-digit integers in the response message that inform the client about the **outcome** of its request. They are categorized into five classes: **1xx (Informational), 2xx (Success), 3xx (Redirection), 4xx (Client Error), 5xx (Server Error)**.

| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **400** | Bad Request — syntax error in the request |
| **401** | Unauthorized — authentication required |
| **403** | Forbidden — server refuses to authorize |
| **404** | Not Found — resource does not exist on the server |
| **405** | Method Not Allowed — HTTP verb not supported for this resource |

> Award **1 mark** for general explanation, **2 marks** for listing 4 codes with correct meanings (0.5 per code).

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the working of the HTTP protocol. Compare HTTP/1.0, HTTP/1.1, and HTTP/2 in terms of connection management, message format, and performance.**

#### Part (a) — HTTP Working (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define HTTP and its role (1 mark)**
HTTP is an **application-layer, stateless, request-response protocol** used by the World Wide Web. It runs over **TCP port 80** (or 443 for HTTPS) and defines the rules for formatting, transmitting, and interpreting messages between web clients and web servers.

**Step 2: Describe the working sequence (4 marks)**

1. **DNS Resolution:** The client resolves the server's domain name to an IP address (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in` → `103.0.0.1`).
2. **TCP Connection:** The client initiates a 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK), costing **1 RTT**.
3. **HTTP Request:** The client sends a request message comprising a **request line** (`GET / HTTP/1.1`), **headers** (`Host`, `User-Agent`, `Accept`), a **blank line**, and an optional body.
4. **Server Processing:** The web server daemon (Apache/Nginx) receives the request, authenticates/authorizes it, and invokes the application logic or fetches the resource.
5. **HTTP Response:** The server sends back a **status line** (`HTTP/1.1 200 OK`), **headers** (`Content-Type`, `Content-Length`), a **blank line**, and the **entity body** (HTML content).
6. **Rendering:** The browser parses the HTML, issues additional GET requests for embedded objects (images, CSS, JS), and renders the final page.
7. **Connection Management:** Depending on the HTTP version, the connection is closed (HTTP/1.0 non-persistent) or kept alive (HTTP/1.1 persistent).

**Step 3: Diagram (2 marks)** — Use a sequence diagram similar to Section 4.1 of these notes. Label all 7 steps with arrows for SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK, REQUEST, RESPONSE, FIN.

#### Part (b) — HTTP Version Comparison (7 Marks)

| Feature | **HTTP/1.0** | **HTTP/1.1** | **HTTP/2** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year Released** | 1996 | 1999 | 2015 |
| **Connection Model** | Non-persistent (default) | Persistent (default) | Persistent + Multiplexed |
| **Message Format** | Text-based | Text-based | **Binary-framed** (HPACK header compression) |
| **Pipelining** | Not supported | Supported but rarely used by browsers | Native multiplexing (no HOL blocking) |
| **Parallel Streams per TCP** | None (sequential) | Up to 6 (browser limit) | **Unlimited** (100s of streams) |
| **Header Compression** | None | None | **HPACK** (compresses redundant headers) |
| **Server Push** | No | No | **Yes** (server proactively sends resources) |
| **Performance Bottleneck** | TCP setup overhead | Head-of-line blocking | Mitigated; new bottleneck is TCP itself |
| **Transport** | TCP | TCP | TCP (with TLS by default) |
| **Use Case Today** | Legacy | **Most common** | **Modern, high-performance** |

> **Concluding remark (0.5 marks):** HTTP/2 dramatically reduces page load times by addressing HOL blocking and enabling multiplexed, concurrent transfers over a single TCP connection. HTTP/3 (over QUIC/UDP) further reduces handshake latency to 0-RTT.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**Q: An HTTP client needs to retrieve a web page with 1 base HTML file and 9 embedded images from a server. The RTT between client and server is 150 ms. The HTML file is 50,000 bits, each image is 200,000 bits, and the bottleneck link bandwidth is 2 Mbps. Calculate the total response time for:
(a) Non-persistent HTTP without parallel connections.
(b) Persistent HTTP without pipelining.**

#### Given Data Extraction (1 mark)

$$
n = 9, \quad RTT = 150 \text{ ms} = 0.15 \text{ s}, \quad O_{\text{HTML}} = 50{,}000 \text{ bits}, \quad O_{\text{img}} = 200{,}000 \text{ bits}, \quad B = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}
$$

$$
F_{\text{HTML}} = \frac{50{,}000}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.025 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
F_{\text{img}} = \frac{200{,}000}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.100 \text{ s} = 100 \text{ ms}
$$

#### Part (a) — Non-Persistent HTTP (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Per-object cost** — Each object requires 1 RTT for TCP handshake + 1 RTT for request/response + file transmission time.

$$
T_{\text{per obj}} = 2 \cdot RTT + F_{\text{obj}}
$$

**Step 2: Total objects = $n + 1 = 10$** (1 base HTML + 9 images)

$$
T_{NP} = (n + 1) \cdot 2 \cdot RTT + F_{\text{HTML}} + n \cdot F_{\text{img}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$
T_{NP} = 10 \cdot 2 \cdot 150 + 25 + 9 \cdot 100
$$

$$
T_{NP} = 3000 + 25 + 900
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{NP} = 3925 \text{ ms} \approx 3.925 \text{ seconds}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Persistent HTTP Without Pipelining (7 Marks)

**Step 1: First object (base HTML) costs 2 RTT + $F_{\text{HTML}}$**

$$
T_{\text{first}} = 2 \cdot RTT + F_{\text{HTML}} = 300 + 25 = 325 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Each subsequent object (image) costs 1 RTT + $F_{\text{img}}$**

$$
T_{\text{rest}} = n \cdot (RTT + F_{\text{img}}) = 9 \cdot (150 + 100) = 9 \cdot 250 = 2250 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Total**

$$
T_{PN} = T_{\text{first}} + T_{\text{rest}} = 325 + 2250
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{PN} = 2575 \text{ ms} \approx 2.575 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**Inference (1 mark):** Persistent HTTP reduces the response time by approximately **34.4%** compared to non-persistent HTTP, by eliminating 9 redundant TCP handshakes.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Board Exams**
> 1. **Confusing `2 RTT` with `1 RTT` per object** in non-persistent mode. Many students forget the TCP handshake cost and write $T = (n+1) \cdot RTT$ instead of $2(n+1) \cdot RTT$. This costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Ignoring file transmission time $F$** in numerical problems. When file sizes and bandwidth are given, $F = O/B$ must be added for every object.
> 3. **Miscounting total objects** as $n$ instead of $n + 1$ (forgetting the base HTML file).
> 4. **Mixing up `404 Not Found` and `400 Bad Request`** in status code questions. Remember: 4xx = client's fault.
> 5. **Writing "HTTP is a protocol of the Internet"** instead of "HTTP is an **application-layer** protocol **running on top of** the Internet." Lose 1 mark for imprecision.
> 6. **Drawing the request-response diagram without labeling the 3-way TCP handshake** (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) — costs 1.5 marks in diagram questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- The **WWW** is a service **on top of** the Internet (Internet ≠ Web).
- The **Web** has three pillars: **HTTP, HTML, URL**.
- **HTTP** is **stateless, application-layer, request-response**, and runs over **TCP port 80** (or 443 for HTTPS).
- A **URL** = Scheme `://` Authority `:` Port `/` Path `?` Query `#` Fragment.
- The **fragment** (`#...`) is **never sent to the server** — it is client-side only.
- HTTP request message = **Request Line + Headers + Blank Line + Body (optional)**.
- HTTP response message = **Status Line + Headers + Blank Line + Body**.
- The **blank line (CRLF)** is a **mandatory separator** between headers and body.
- HTTP status codes: **1xx Info, 2xx Success, 3xx Redirection, 4xx Client Error, 5xx Server Error**.
- `404` = resource not found; `400` = malformed request; `500` = server crashed.
- HTTP methods: **GET (safe, idempotent), POST (not safe, not idempotent), PUT (not safe, idempotent), DELETE (idempotent)**.
- **HTTP/1.0** = non-persistent (default); **HTTP/1.1** = persistent (default); **HTTP/2** = multiplexed, binary.
- **Non-persistent HTTP** = $2(n+1) \cdot RTT + \sum F_i$ (no parallelism).
- **Persistent HTTP without pipelining** = $(n+2) \cdot RTT + \sum F_i$.
- **Persistent HTTP with pipelining** = $3 \cdot RTT + \sum F_i$ (for an $n$-object page).
- **File transmission time** $F = O / B$ (file size / bandwidth).
- Modern browsers open up to **6 parallel persistent connections per host** to mitigate HOL blocking.
- **Cookies** are used to **simulate state** over the inherently stateless HTTP.
- **HTTPS = HTTP + TLS/SSL**, providing encryption, authentication, and integrity.
- **HTTP/3 (over QUIC)** runs over **UDP** and achieves **0-RTT** connection establishment.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FTP and Electronic Mail
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FTP and Electronic Mail

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 File Transfer Protocol (FTP)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
**File Transfer Protocol (FTP)** is a standard **TCP-based application-layer protocol** defined in **RFC 959** that provides reliable, bidirectional file transfer services between a client and a server over the Internet. FTP operates on the **client–server model**, uses **two parallel TCP connections** (a *control connection* on **port 21** and a *data connection* on **port 20**), and supports authentication, directory listing, file upload, file download, rename, and delete operations.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Takeaway Counter 🍔**
> Think of FTP like ordering food at a takeaway restaurant:
> - The **waiter (Control Connection on Port 21)** stands at your table, takes your order, tells you the bill, and confirms everything. This conversation happens on one persistent line.
> - The **kitchen runner (Data Connection on Port 20)** actually brings the food to your table from a different door. This is a separate, temporary trip just to deliver the goods.
> - You can keep talking to the waiter (issuing more commands) while the runner brings food (data transfer) in the background. That is exactly how FTP works — two parallel channels.

### Why FTP Uses Two Connections
- **Separation of concerns**: Control commands must not be interrupted by bulky data flows.
- **Out-of-band signaling**: Inspired by the **FTAM (File Transfer, Access, and Management)** model — control and data semantics are isolated.
- **Flexibility**: Data connection can be initiated by either client or server depending on **active vs. passive mode**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** FTP is one of the oldest application protocols (1971, RFC 114). The two-channel architecture is a guaranteed exam topic — know it cold.

---

## 1.2 Electronic Mail (E-mail)

### Formal Definition
**Electronic Mail (E-mail)** is an **asynchronous store-and-forward** application-layer messaging service that allows users to compose, send, receive, and store text and multimedia messages across computer networks. The KTU 2024 syllabus lists the core e-mail protocol stack as:
- **SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol)** – RFC 5321 — for *pushing* mail between Mail Transfer Agents (MTAs).
- **POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3)** – RFC 1939 — for *pulling* mail from a server to a local client.
- **IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol)** – RFC 3501 — for *remote management* of mail on a server.
- **MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)** – RFC 2045–2049 — for encoding non-ASCII attachments.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Indian Postal System 📬**
> - **SMTP** is the long-haul **postal truck** that carries a sealed letter from the sender's city post office to the receiver's city post office.
> - **POP3** is like **bringing the entire bag of letters home** — once you collect, the bag is empty at the post office.
> - **IMAP** is like **leaving letters in the post office locker** and just peeking inside from your phone — the post office keeps the original, and you can read, flag, or delete from any device.
> - **MIME** is the **packaging rules** that let you send a birthday cake (image) or a video (audio) inside the envelope without spoiling the letter.

### Standard E-mail Addresses and Metrics
- A typical address: `user@domain.org` — the **@** symbol was chosen by **Ray Tomlinson** in **1971**.
- **Maximum SMTP command line length:** **512 characters** (including CRLF).
- **SMTP default port:** **25** (plain), **465/587** (with TLS/STARTTLS).
- **POP3 default port:** **110** (plain), **995** (TLS).
- **IMAP default port:** **143** (plain), **993** (TLS).
- **Minimum SMTP reply code:** **3 digits** (e.g., `250 OK`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** "Push" vs. "Pull" is the single most tested concept. SMTP *pushes* mail; POP3/IMAP *pull/manage* mail. E-mail uses **persistent connections** in modern SMTP (RFC 5321) instead of one-MAIL-per-connection of the old RFC 821.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 FTP — Architecture and Communication

### 2.1.1 FTP Components
- **Client (User-PI — Protocol Interpreter):** issues commands.
- **Server (Server-PI):** receives commands and sends replies.
- **Data Transfer Process (DTP):** establishes the data connection and handles the file payload.

### 2.1.2 Two Connections
| Connection | Port | Purpose | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Control Connection** | **21** (TCP) | Sends FTP commands and replies | Persists for the entire session |
| **Data Connection** | **20** (active mode) | Transfers files / directory listings | Opened and closed per transfer |

### 2.1.3 Active vs. Passive Mode
- **Active Mode (PORT command):**
  1. Client opens control connection from random port *X* → server's port 21.
  2. Client sends `PORT h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2` declaring its IP and port.
  3. Server initiates data connection **FROM its port 20 TO client's port p1·256+p2**.
  4. **Problem:** blocked by client-side firewalls / NAT.
- **Passive Mode (PASV command):**
  1. Client sends `PASV`.
  2. Server replies `227 Entering Passive Mode (h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2)`.
  3. Client initiates **both** control and data connections **outbound** to the server.
  4. Solves the firewall problem — universally used today.

### 2.1.4 FTP Command Set (Selected High-Yield)
| Command | Function |
|---|---|
| `USER <username>` | Send user-id for authentication |
| `PASS <password>` | Send password |
| `LIST` | List files (sent over data connection) |
| `RETR <filename>` | Retrieve (download) a file |
| `STOR <filename>` | Store (upload) a file |
| `DELE <filename>` | Delete a file |
| `MKD <dirname>` | Make directory |
| `PWD` | Print working directory |
| `QUIT` | Logout / close session |

### 2.1.5 FTP Reply Codes (3-digit, like HTTP)
| First Digit | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1xx | Positive Preliminary | `125 Data connection already open` |
| 2xx | Positive Completion | `200 Command okay`, `226 Transfer complete` |
| 3xx | Positive Intermediate | `331 User name okay, need password` |
| 4xx | Transient Negative | `425 Can't open data connection` |
| 5xx | Permanent Negative | `530 Not logged in`, `550 File unavailable` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** FTP codes mirror the SMTP reply code structure. KTU often asks to *interpret a transcript* (e.g., "What does `550` mean?").

---

## 2.2 Electronic Mail — System Architecture

### 2.2.1 Major Components
1. **MUA (Mail User Agent):** the user-facing client (Outlook, Thunderbird, Gmail web).
2. **MTA (Mail Transfer Agent):** server that routes mail (Sendmail, Postfix, Exim, Microsoft Exchange).
3. **MDA (Mail Delivery Agent):** delivers mail from MTA → mailbox store (Procmail, Dovecot LDA).
4. **MRA (Mail Retrieval Agent):** allows MUA to retrieve mail using POP3/IMAP (Dovecot, Cyrus).
5. **Mailbox / Message Store:** the disk-resident mailbox where delivered mail is kept.

### 2.2.2 Flow of a Message
```
Sender's MUA  →  Sender's MTA  ⇢  Internet (SMTP hops)  ⇢  Receiver's MTA
                                                          ↓
                                                       MDA
                                                          ↓
                                                    Receiver's Mailbox
                                                          ↓
                                                   MRA (POP3 / IMAP)
                                                          ↓
                                                  Receiver's MUA
```

### 2.2.3 SMTP — Three Phases of Transfer
| Phase | Description | Example Commands |
|---|---|---|
| **Connection Setup** | Client opens TCP to port 25; server replies `220` | `HELO / EHLO` |
| **Mail Transfer** | Identify sender, recipients, then stream the message | `MAIL FROM:`, `RCPT TO:`, `DATA` |
| **Connection Termination** | Client sends `QUIT`; server replies `221` | `QUIT` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EHLO vs. HELO:** `EHLO` (Extended HELO) is used with **ESMTP (RFC 5321)** to negotiate service extensions like `SIZE`, `8BITMIME`, `STARTTLS`, `AUTH`.

### 2.2.4 POP3 vs. IMAP — Comparison
| Feature | POP3 | IMAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Pull (download) | Remote manage |
| **Default Port** | 110 | 143 |
| **Mode** | Offline (typically) | Online / offline / disconnected |
| **Server Storage After Read** | Usually deleted (configurable) | Mail remains on server |
| **Folder Support** | Single INBOX only | Multiple folders on server |
| **Partial Fetch** | No (full message) | Yes (RFC 3501 `FETCH <part>`) |
| **State** | Stateless per session | Stateful — tracks message flags (`\Seen`, `\Answered`) |
| **Bandwidth for Multi-Device** | Poor | Excellent |

### 2.2.5 MIME — Encoding Non-ASCII Content
**MIME** adds five header fields to RFC 822 messages:
1. **MIME-Version** — e.g., `1.0`.
2. **Content-Type** — e.g., `text/plain`, `text/html`, `image/jpeg`, `application/pdf`, `multipart/mixed`.
3. **Content-Transfer-Encoding** — e.g., `7bit`, `8bit`, `binary`, `quoted-printable`, `base64`.
4. **Content-ID** — unique reference for embedded parts.
5. **Content-Description** — optional human description.

**Base64 encoding** is the most-tested encoding in KTU.
- Encodes every **3 bytes (24 bits)** into **4 ASCII characters** (6 bits each).
- Output alphabet: `A–Z, a–z, 0–9, +, /`, with `=` for padding.

### 2.2.6 High-Yield Formula Sheet
| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Passive mode data port | $P_d = p_1 \times 256 + p_2$ | Decimal port from PASV reply tuple |
| Active mode client port | $P_c = p_1 \times 256 + p_2$ | Decimal port from PORT command |
| Base64 expansion ratio | $\text{Output length} = \lceil 4 \times \frac{N}{3} \rceil$ | Bytes |
| Base64 padded length | $L_{b64} = 4 \times \lceil \frac{N_{bytes}}{3} \rceil$ | Bytes |
| SMTP max command length | $512$ | Characters (incl. CRLF) |
| Maximum RFC 822 line length | $998$ | Characters |
| Recommended max line length | $78$ | Characters |
| FTP control port | $21$ | TCP |
| FTP active data port | $20$ | TCP (server → client) |
| SMTP port | $25$ | TCP (submission: 587) |
| POP3 port | $110$ | TCP (TLS: 995) |
| IMAP port | $143$ | TCP (TLS: 993) |

### 2.2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **FTP** powers enterprise file-sync, web-hosting uploads, scientific dataset mirroring (NCBI, NASA), and IoT firmware updates.
- **SMTP** is the *de facto* backplane of every modern messaging app (Slack, WhatsApp Business API, transactional e-mail like SendGrid).
- **MIME** is reused far beyond e-mail — it is the foundation of **HTTP multipart/form-data** (file uploads in browsers).
- **IMAP** is the backbone of Gmail, Outlook.com, and Apple iCloud Mail.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Base64 Encoding of a Plaintext Byte String

### Problem
Encode the ASCII string **`"Net"`** using Base64 (Content-Transfer-Encoding).

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Convert characters to their 8-bit ASCII codes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{`N'} &= 0x4E = 0100\;1110 \\
\text{`e'} &= 0x65 = 0110\;0101 \\
\text{`t'} &= 0x74 = 0111\;0100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Concatenate the 3 bytes into a single 24-bit bitstream.**

$$
01001110\;01100101\;01110100
$$

**Step 3 — Split the 24-bit stream into four 6-bit groups.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_1 &= 010011 \;=\; 19 \\
g_2 &= 100110 \;=\; 38 \\
g_3 &= 010101 \;=\; 21 \\
g_4 &= 110100 \;=\; 52
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Look up each 6-bit value in the Base64 alphabet.**

$$
\begin{array}{ccl}
g_1 = 19 &\to& \text{`T'} \\
g_2 = 38 &\to& \text{`m'} \\
g_3 = 21 &\to& \text{`V'} \\
g_4 = 52 &\to& \text{`0'}
\end{array}
$$

**Step 5 — Output.**

$$
\boxed{\text{``Net''} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{``TmV0''}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse check:** Decoding `TmV0` by reversing the alphabet yields `01001110 01100101 01110100` → `0x4E 0x65 0x74` → `Net`. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Padding Rule in Base64

### Problem
Encode the 2-character string **`"Hi"`**.

### Working

**Step 1 — ASCII codes.**

$$
\text{`H'} = 0x48,\quad \text{`i'} = 0x69
$$

**Step 2 — Concatenate 16 bits and zero-pad to 18 bits (multiple of 6).**

$$
01001000\;01101001\;00
$$

**Step 3 — Form three 6-bit groups (the last is a padding group).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_1 &= 010010 = 18 \;\to\; \text{`S'} \\
g_2 &= 000110 = 6  \;\to\; \text{`G'} \\
g_3 &= 1001\underline{00} = 36 \;\to\; \text{`k'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Append `=` padding equal to (3 − (N mod 3)) = 1 pad byte.**

$$
\boxed{\text{``Hi''} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{``SGk=''}}
$$

### General Padding Rule

$$
\text{Number of `='} \;=\; 3 - (N_{\text{bytes}} \bmod 3)
$$

| Input bytes | Mod 3 | Output groups | `=` pads |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | 0 | 4 | 0 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Converting an FTP PASV Tuple to a Decimal Port

### Problem
An FTP server replies:
`227 Entering Passive Mode (192,168,10,5,7,220)`.
Find the IP and port the client should connect to for the data channel.

### Working

**Step 1 — Extract the four IP octets and the two port bytes.**

$$
\text{IP octets} = (192, 168, 10, 5), \quad (p_1, p_2) = (7, 220)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the port formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= p_1 \times 256 + p_2 \\
    &= 7 \times 256 + 220 \\
    &= 1792 + 220 \\
    &= 2012
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final connection target.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Data connection: } 192.168.10.5 : 2012 \;\; (\text{TCP})}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — FTP Mode Decision Table

| Scenario | Mode Used | Who Opens Data Connection? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Client behind a NAT gateway | **Passive (PASV)** | Client (outbound) | Active mode is blocked by NAT |
| Server behind a firewall | **Passive (PASV)** | Client (outbound) | Server cannot accept inbound data |
| Both on the public Internet | Either | — | NAT not in the path |
| Browser-based upload (e.g., cURL) | **Passive** by default | Client | Library default |
| Legacy mainframe FTP client | **Active (PORT)** | Server | Some old clients never supported PASV |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — SMTP Reply Code Family Tree

| First Digit | Class | Typical Scenarios | Example Replies |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2xx** | Success | Command acknowledged, action completed | `250 OK`, `221 Bye` |
| **3xx** | Intermediate | Need more input to finish action | `354 Start mail input` |
| **4xx** | Transient Failure | Server temporarily cannot comply (try later) | `421 Service not available` |
| **5xx** | Permanent Failure | Server cannot comply (do not retry) | `550 Mailbox unavailable` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FTP Two-Connection Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLIENT["FTP Client (User-PI + User-DTP)"]
        A1["User-PI: issues commands"]
        A2["User-DTP: opens data socket"]
    end

    subgraph SERVER["FTP Server (Server-PI + Server-DTP)"]
        B1["Server-PI: parses commands and replies"]
        B2["Server-DTP: opens data socket on port 20"]
    end

    A1 -- "FTP Commands (USER, PASS, RETR, STOR, LIST, QUIT) over TCP 21" --> B1
    B1 -- "Reply codes (220, 331, 226, 550, ...) over TCP 21" --> A1
    A2 -- "PORT tuple or PASV response" --> B1
    B2 -- "File payload / directory listing over TCP 20" --> A2
    A2 -- "Ack / FIN" --> B2
```

## 4.2 Electronic Mail End-to-End Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENDER["Sender Side"]
        UA1["Sender MUA (Outlook, Gmail web)"]
        MTA1["Sender MTA (Postfix / Sendmail)"]
    end

    subgraph NET["Internet / WAN"]
        HOP["Intermediate SMTP Relays and MX Lookups via DNS"]
    end

    subgraph RECEIVER["Receiver Side"]
        MTA2["Receiver MTA"]
        MDA["MDA (Dovecot LDA)"]
        STORE[("Receiver Mailbox Store")]
        MRA["MRA (Dovecot IMAP / POP3 server)"]
        UA2["Receiver MUA (Thunderbird, iPhone Mail)"]
    end

    UA1 -- "Submit via SMTP submission 587" --> MTA1
    MTA1 -- "SMTP over TCP 25 (EHLO, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO, DATA, QUIT)" --> HOP
    HOP -- "SMTP relay" --> MTA2
    MTA2 -- "Local delivery" --> MDA
    MDA -- "Append to INBOX" --> STORE
    STORE -- "Fetch on demand" --> MRA
    MRA -- "IMAP 143 or POP3 110" --> UA2
```

## 4.3 SMTP Session — Message Exchange Sequence (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as SMTP Client (Sender MTA)
    participant S as SMTP Server (Receiver MTA)

    C->>S: TCP SYN to port 25
    S-->>C: 220 mail.receiver.org ESMTP ready
    C->>S: EHLO sender.org
    S-->>C: 250-mail.receiver.org Hello, supports SIZE 50M, 8BITMIME, STARTTLS, AUTH PLAIN
    C->>S: AUTH PLAIN
    S-->>C: 334 (challenge)
    C->>S: <base64 credentials>
    S-->>C: 235 Authentication successful
    C->>S: MAIL FROM:<alice@sender.org>
    S-->>C: 250 Sender OK
    C->>S: RCPT TO:<bob@receiver.org>
    S-->>C: 250 Recipient OK
    C->>S: DATA
    S-->>C: 354 End data with CRLF.CRLF
    C->>S: (Headers + body, terminated by CRLF.CRLF)
    S-->>C: 250 Message accepted for delivery
    C->>S: QUIT
    S-->>C: 221 Bye
    C->>S: TCP FIN
```

## 4.4 POP3 vs. IMAP — Functional Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POP3["POP3 Model: Download-and-Delete"]
        P1["Client connects to server port 110"]
        P2["USER and PASS commands"]
        P3["STAT, LIST to enumerate messages"]
        P4["RETR n downloads message n"]
        P5["DELE n marks for deletion on quit"]
        P6["Mail typically removed from server"]
    end

    subgraph IMAP["IMAP Model: Remote Synchronization"]
        I1["Client connects to server port 143"]
        I2["LOGIN with credentials"]
        I3["LIST to see folders and SELECT to open INBOX"]
        I4["UID FETCH n (BODY.PEEK[HEADER.FIELDS FROM])"]
        I5["UID STORE \\Seen flag updates server state"]
        I6["Mail and flags remain on server"]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6
    I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 --> I5 --> I6
```
