## 4.5 MIME Multipart Message Structure (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["multipart/mixed message"]
    ROOT --> H["Headers: From, To, Subject, MIME-Version 1.0, Content-Type multipart/mixed boundary=BOUND"]
    ROOT --> P1["Part 1: text/plain body"]
    ROOT --> P2["Part 2: image/png attachment (base64 encoded)"]
    ROOT --> P3["Part 3: application/pdf report"]
    ROOT --> END["Final delimiter: --BOUND--"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between FTP and HTTP. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. | Parameter | FTP | HTTP |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Purpose | File transfer (upload, download, delete, rename) | Hypertext document retrieval |
| 2 | Connections | **Two parallel TCP connections** (control 21, data 20) | **Single TCP connection** (port 80/443) |
| 3 | State | Stateful, requires login (`USER`, `PASS`) | Stateless, no login by default |
| 4 | Data Format | Binary or text; preserves file contents | Hypertext, no side effects |
| 5 | Command Set | Rich (`RETR`, `STOR`, `LIST`, `MKD`, `DELE`) | Limited (`GET`, `POST`, `HEAD`) |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is MIME? Why is it required in e-mail systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)** is a set of extensions to the original RFC 822 e-mail format defined in **RFC 2045–2049**.
- It is required because the original SMTP body was restricted to **7-bit US-ASCII** text and could not carry binary attachments (images, audio, video, PDFs, executables).
- MIME provides:
  1. **Content-Type** header to declare media type/subtype (e.g., `image/jpeg`).
  2. **Content-Transfer-Encoding** header specifying encodings such as `base64` or `quoted-printable`.
  3. **Multipart** structure to combine text, images, and attachments in one message.
  4. **Non-ASCII character sets** for international (UTF-8) text.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 + 7 marks. Provide solutions for both alternatives.

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) Describe the File Transfer Protocol (FTP) in detail. Explain the two connections used in FTP and the role of the PORT and PASV commands.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

1. **Definition of FTP (1 mark)**
   FTP is a TCP-based, client–server application-layer protocol standardized in **RFC 959** for transferring files between hosts on a TCP/IP network such as the Internet.

2. **Two-Connection Architecture (3 marks)**
   FTP maintains two parallel TCP connections:
   - **Control Connection (port 21):** Persistent throughout the session. Used to exchange FTP commands (`USER`, `PASS`, `RETR`, `STOR`, `LIST`, `QUIT`) and 3-digit reply codes (`220`, `331`, `226`, `550`).
   - **Data Connection (port 20 in active mode, ephemeral in passive mode):** Used to actually transfer file contents or directory listings. It is opened on demand and closed after the transfer completes.

3. **PORT Command — Active Mode (1.5 marks)**
   The client sends `PORT h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2` to declare its IP address and the port number $P_c = p_1 \times 256 + p_2$ on which the **server** should connect back. The server then opens a TCP connection from its port 20 to that client port. This fails when the client is behind a NAT or firewall.

4. **PASV Command — Passive Mode (1.5 marks)**
   The client sends `PASV`; the server replies with `227 Entering Passive Mode (h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2)`. The client extracts $P_d = p_1 \times 256 + p_2$ and opens the data connection **outbound** to the server. This is the modern default because it works across NATs and firewalls.

---

**(b) Explain the working of SMTP with a neat session transcript. Mention at least four reply codes.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

1. **Role of SMTP (1 mark)**
   SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol, RFC 5321) is the application-layer protocol used by **MTAs** to *push* e-mail from sender to receiver across the Internet. It is connection-oriented, uses **persistent connections**, and operates over **TCP port 25**.

2. **Sample SMTP Session (4 marks)**
   ```
   S: 220 mail.receiver.org ESMTP Postfix
   C: EHLO sender.org
   S: 250-mail.receiver.org Hello sender.org [203.0.113.10]
   S: 250-SIZE 52428800
   S: 250-STARTTLS
   S: 250 OK
   C: MAIL FROM:<alice@sender.org>
   S: 250 2.1.0 Sender OK
   C: RCPT TO:<bob@receiver.org>
   S: 250 2.1.5 Recipient OK
   C: DATA
   S: 354 End data with <CR><LF>.<CR><LF>
   C: From: Alice <alice@sender.org>
   C: To: Bob <bob@receiver.org>
   C: Subject: KTU Exam
   C:
   C: Hello Bob, please find the schedule.
   C: .
   S: 250 2.0.0 Message accepted
   C: QUIT
   S: 221 2.0.0 Bye
   ```

3. **Reply Codes Used (2 marks)**
   - **220** — Service ready.
   - **250** — Requested mail action okay, completed.
   - **354** — Start mail input; end with `<CRLF>.<CRLF>`.
   - **221** — Service closing transmission channel.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a) Compare POP3 and IMAP. Describe a typical IMAP session with a transcript.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

1. **Comparison Table (3 marks)**
   | Feature | POP3 | IMAP |
   |---|---|---|
   | RFC | 1939 | 3501 |
   | Default Port | 110 | 143 |
   | TLS Port | 995 | 993 |
   | Storage | Client side (after download) | Server side |
   | Multi-device sync | Poor | Excellent |
   | Folder support | Single INBOX | Multiple folders |
   | State | Mostly stateless | Stateful with flags (`\Seen`, `\Answered`) |

2. **Typical IMAP Session Transcript (4 marks)**
   ```
   S: * OK [CAPABILITY IMAP4rev1] Dovecot ready.
   C: A001 LOGIN bob secret
   S: A001 OK [CAPABILITY IMAP4rev1] Logged in
   C: A002 SELECT INBOX
   S: * 12 EXISTS
   S: * 0 RECENT
   S: * FLAGS (\Answered \Flagged \Deleted \Seen \Draft)
   S: A002 OK [READ-WRITE] Select completed
   C: A003 UID FETCH 5 (BODY.PEEK[HEADER.FIELDS (FROM SUBJECT)])
   S: * 5 FETCH (UID 5 RFC822.SIZE 1024 BODY[HEADER.FIELDS (FROM SUBJECT)] {60}
   S: From: Alice <alice@sender.org>
   S: Subject: KTU Exam
   S: )
   S: A003 OK Fetch completed
   C: A004 LOGOUT
   S: * BYE Logging out
   S: A004 OK Logout completed
   ```

---

**(b) Explain MIME in detail. With a suitable example, show how a multipart message with a text part and an image attachment is structured.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

1. **MIME Overview (1 mark)**
   MIME (RFC 2045–2049) extends the RFC 822 e-mail format to support non-text bodies, multi-part messages, and non-ASCII character sets by adding five new header fields.

2. **MIME Headers (2 marks)**
   - `MIME-Version: 1.0`
   - `Content-Type: <type>/<subtype>`
   - `Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit | 8bit | binary | quoted-printable | base64`
   - `Content-ID: <id>`
   - `Content-Description: <text>`

3. **Multipart Example (4 marks)**
   ```
   From: Alice <alice@sender.org>
   To: Bob <bob@receiver.org>
   Subject: Photos
   MIME-Version: 1.0
   Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary="Boundary42"

   --Boundary42
   Content-Type: text/plain; charset="UTF-8"

   Hi Bob, see the attached photograph.

   --Boundary42
   Content-Type: image/jpeg
   Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64
   Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="ktu.jpg"

   /9j/4AAQSkZJRgABAQEASABIAAD/2wBDAAEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEB
   AQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEBAQEB/9k=

   --Boundary42--
   ```

   - The **top-level** Content-Type is `multipart/mixed`, declaring a unique **boundary delimiter** (`Boundary42`).
   - **Part 1** is `text/plain` carrying the readable body.
   - **Part 2** is `image/jpeg`, base64-encoded, presented as a downloadable **attachment**.
   - The final line `--Boundary42--` marks the end of the multipart message.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in FTP and E-Mail Questions**
> 1. **Confusing FTP data port in passive vs active mode:** In active mode, the **server** opens the data connection **from its port 20** to the client. In passive mode, the **client** opens the data connection to an **ephemeral** server port. Marks are lost if you say "server always uses port 20" — that is true only in active mode.
> 2. **SMTP uses TCP, not UDP:** SMTP requires reliable, ordered delivery, so it is **TCP-based** on port 25. Writing "UDP" is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the EHLO vs HELO distinction:** Modern SMTP uses `EHLO` to negotiate **ESMTP** extensions. Using only `HELO` is acceptable but limits features. Examiners check the `250-` capability list reply.
> 4. **POP3 vs IMAP conflation:** POP3 is mainly **download-and-delete**; IMAP is **server-resident** and supports folders/flags. Saying "both work the same" is a fatal error.
> 5. **Base64 output length mistake:** For $N$ input bytes the Base64 output has length $4 \times \lceil N/3 \rceil$ characters. Forgetting the `=` padding loses 1 mark in numerical problems.
> 6. **MIME is NOT a transport protocol:** Students often write "MIME is used to send mail" — that is wrong. MIME is a **representation** standard; **SMTP** is the transport.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **FTP** = TCP-based, client–server, two-connection design (control 21, data 20 active / ephemeral passive).
- **Control connection** persists; **data connection** is opened/closed per transfer.
- **Active mode** uses `PORT`; **Passive mode** uses `PASV`. Passive is the modern default due to NAT/firewall.
- **FTP reply codes** are 3 digits; classes: 1xx preliminary, 2xx success, 3xx intermediate, 4xx transient, 5xx permanent.
- **E-mail architecture**: MUA → MTA ⇢ MTA → MDA → Mailbox → MRA (POP3/IMAP) → MUA.
- **SMTP** pushes mail (port 25), uses commands `HELO/EHLO`, `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`, `DATA`, `QUIT`.
- **ESMTP** reply `250-` advertises extensions: `SIZE`, `8BITMIME`, `STARTTLS`, `AUTH`.
- **POP3** (port 110) is offline/download-and-delete; stateless per session.
- **IMAP** (port 143) is stateful, server-side, multi-folder, supports partial fetch and flags.
- **MIME** adds 5 headers; supports `multipart/mixed`, `text/plain`, `image/jpeg`, etc.
- **Base64** packs **3 bytes → 4 ASCII chars**; pad with `=` when input is not a multiple of 3.
- **Standard ports to memorize**: 20/21 (FTP), 25 (SMTP), 110 (POP3), 143 (IMAP), 587 (submission), 465/993/995 (TLS variants).
- **SMTP uses persistent connections** in RFC 5321 (unlike the legacy one-MAIL-per-connection RFC 821).
- **Know the difference between *push* (SMTP) and *pull* (POP3/IMAP).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DNS and Peer-to-Peer Paradigm: P2P Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DNS and Peer-to-Peer Paradigm: P2P Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Domain Name System (DNS)

### Formal Definition
The **Domain Name System (DNS)** is a hierarchical, distributed, and globally scalable naming system defined in **RFC 1034** and **RFC 1035** that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., `103.25.61.18`). It functions as the *phonebook of the Internet*, decoupling an endpoint's identity (name) from its routable location (address).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DNS is studied as a core *Application Layer* service. KTU expects you to know (a) the namespace hierarchy, (b) iterative vs. recursive queries, and (c) DNS message structure.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Post Office & Telephone Directory"
Imagine a massive, country-wide telephone directory. Instead of one giant book, the directory is split across thousands of branch offices, each handling a different region. When you want Mr. Raj's phone number in Kochi:
1. You first call the *Kerala branch* (TLD + authoritative server).
2. They look up "Raj" in *Kochi section* and give you the number.
3. Your phone *caches* that number so you don't have to ask again for a while.

DNS works exactly like this — your browser asks a local resolver, which delegates to root, TLD, and authoritative servers, and then *caches* the result in RAM.

### Key DNS Services
| Service | Function |
|---|---|
| **Hostname to IP Translation** | Core A/AAAA record lookup |
| **Host Aliasing** | CNAME records (`mail.ktu.edu.in` → `server1.ktu.edu.in`) |
| **Mail Server Aliasing** | MX records |
| **Load Distribution** | Round-robin A records across replicated servers |

### 1.2 Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Paradigm

### Formal Definition
A **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) network** is a distributed application architecture in which peers (nodes) act as both *suppliers* (servers) and *consumers* (clients) of resources. There is no dedicated always-on central server. Each participating host, called a **peer**, can dynamically join or leave the overlay and contribute/upload content while simultaneously downloading.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** In KTU's Computer Networks syllabus (PCCST501 Module 1), P2P is contrasted directly with the Client-Server model using the *file distribution time* formula. Memorize the three terms: `F`, `N`, `u_s`, `u_i`, `d_min`, `d_i`.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Neighborhood Book Library"
Instead of going to one central library, imagine a neighborhood where everyone owns some books. If you want a book:
- *Client-Server model*: You drive to the *single central library* and check out (or wait for) the book.
- *P2P model*: You ask your neighbors. After you get the book, you also lend it to *other* neighbors. The more neighbors that arrive, the *faster* the book spreads.

This is the *self-scaling* property of P2P — **peers serve as amplifiers**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DNS Architecture — The Four-Tier Hierarchy

DNS namespace follows a **distributed database** model with a strict inverted-tree hierarchy:

**Tier 1 — Root DNS Servers**
- **13 logical root servers** (A through M) globally, replicated via *anycast* to hundreds of physical instances.
- Operated by organizations like ICANN, VeriSign, ISC, NASA.
- Do not store individual hostnames; only refer to TLD servers.

**Tier 2 — Top-Level Domain (TLD) Servers**
- Responsible for `.com`, `.org`, `.net`, `.in`, `.edu`, `.gov`, country codes, etc.
- Maintain records of authoritative name servers for each domain.

**Tier 3 — Authoritative DNS Servers**
- Organization's own DNS server (e.g., `ns1.ktu.edu.in`).
- Provides the final, definitive IP address mapping for a hostname.

**Tier 4 — Local DNS Resolver**
- Operated by the ISP or the OS stub resolver.
- The first point of contact for a host's DNS query.

### 2.2 Two Query Modes

**A) Iterative Query ("Refer and Done")**
- The contacted server returns the *best answer it has* — either the IP, or a referral to another server.
- Burden of resolution lies with the *querier* (the local resolver).
- Used between *server-to-server* interactions.

**B) Recursive Query ("Ask and Wait")**
- The contacted server takes full responsibility — it queries on your behalf and returns the final answer.
- Burden of resolution lies with the *contacted server*.
- Typically used by *host → local resolver*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** In practice, the host sends a *recursive* query to the local resolver, which then issues a series of *iterative* queries to root, TLD, and authoritative servers. Examiners love asking: *"What type of query does a host send to its local DNS server?"* — Answer: **Recursive**.

### 2.3 Common DNS Resource Records (RRs)

| Record Type | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Maps hostname → IPv4 address | `host.ktu.edu.in A 192.168.1.10` |
| **AAAA** | Maps hostname → IPv6 address | `host.ktu.edu.in AAAA 2001:db8::1` |
| **CNAME** | Canonical name alias | `www → host.ktu.edu.in` |
| **MX** | Mail exchange server | `ktu.edu.in MX 10 mail.ktu.edu.in` |
| **NS** | Authoritative name server | `ktu.edu.in NS ns1.ktu.edu.in` |
| **PTR** | Reverse lookup (IP → name) | `1.1.168.192.in-addr.arpa PTR host.ktu.edu.in` |
| **SOA** | Start of Authority (zone info) | Contains admin email, TTL, serial |
| **TXT** | Arbitrary text (SPF, DKIM) | `v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all` |

### 2.4 DNS Caching
- All DNS responses carry a **Time-To-Live (TTL)** field (seconds).
- Resolvers cache the answer for that duration, dramatically reducing load on root/TLD servers.
- *Cache poisoning* (an attack) exploits weak transaction IDs and predictability — mitigated by **DNSSEC** (cryptographic signing of records).

### 2.5 P2P Architecture Classification

**1. Pure P2P (Fully Decentralized)**
- No central server at any time.
- Examples: **Gnutella, BitTorrent, Chord, Kademlia (DHT-based)**.
- Peers discover each other via flooding or DHT lookups.

**2. Hybrid P2P (Centralized Index + Distributed Data)**
- A *central server* holds only the *index* (which peer has which file).
- Actual file transfer happens peer-to-peer.
- Example: **Napster** (file sharing), **Skype** (older versions).

**3. Structured P2P — Distributed Hash Table (DHT)**
- Peers are organized using a consistent hashing scheme (e.g., **Chord ring**).
- Lookup of a key takes O(log N) hops.
- Examples: **Chord, Pastry, CAN, Kademlia**.

### 2.6 BitTorrent — The Reference P2P File Distribution Protocol
- A file is split into **pieces** (typically 256 KB each).
- A **.torrent** metadata file contains SHA-1 hashes for each piece and the *tracker* URL.
- A **tracker** maintains the list of peers in a *swarm*.
- **Tit-for-tat** policy: peers preferentially upload to those who reciprocate (the top 4 "unchoked" peers).
- **Optimistic unchoking**: periodically (every 30 s) unchoke a random peer to discover better reciprocators.
- Modern trackers are replaced by **DHT (Mainline DHT)** and **Peer Exchange (PEX)** for trackerless operation.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — File Distribution Models

| Parameter | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| File size | $F$ | Total bytes of the distributed file (bits) |
| Number of peers | $N$ | Total clients downloading the file |
| Server upload rate | $u_s$ | Server's link upload bandwidth (bits/sec) |
| Peer i upload rate | $u_i$ | Peer *i*'s upload capacity (bits/sec) |
| Minimum client download rate | $d_{\min}$ | Slowest peer's download bandwidth (bits/sec) |
| Peer i download rate | $d_i$ | Peer *i*'s download bandwidth (bits/sec) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Pipes in Tables:** I have used \vert (LaTeX) for the absolute-value-like semantic in the formulas below (no `|` character in raw text). The vertical pipe `|` in markdown would have broken the table syntax.

### Client-Server Model Distribution Time
$$D_{cs} \;=\; \max\left\{\,\dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_{s}}\,,\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}\,\right\}$$

- First term: time the server takes to sequentially upload $N$ copies (one after another, since $u_s$ is the bottleneck for uploads).
- Second term: time for the slowest peer to download its own copy.

### P2P Model Distribution Time
$$D_{p2p} \;=\; \max\left\{\,\dfrac{F}{u_{s}}\,,\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}\,,\ \dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_{s} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_{i}}\,\right\}$$

- First term: server uploads *one* full copy (after that, peer-to-peer cascades).
- Second term: slowest peer download.
- Third term: aggregate upload capacity (server + all peers) must serve $N$ copies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** This formula is the foundation for CDN planning, BitTorrent swarm sizing, and content distribution cost optimization. Cloud providers use the same analytical model to determine how many edge servers to spin up for a viral video launch.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the P2P File Distribution Formula

The KTU 2024 examiners regularly ask students to **derive** the $D_{p2p}$ formula. Let us do it step by step with no skips.

### Step 1 — Define the System
Consider a single file of size $F$ bits to be distributed from a server to $N$ peer clients. Each peer $i$ has:
- Upload bandwidth $u_i$
- Download bandwidth $d_i$

The server has upload bandwidth $u_s$ (we ignore the server's own download bandwidth — it already has the file).

### Step 2 — Identify the Three Bottlenecks

**Bottleneck A — Server Upload Capacity**
The server must upload the file at least once to seed the swarm. After the first peer has the file, that peer itself becomes a distributor. So the server's load is just **one copy** of $F$.

$$\text{Time}_{\text{server}} \;=\; \dfrac{F}{u_{s}}$$

> *Reasoning:* Unlike client-server where the server ships $N$ copies, in P2P the server ships only 1 copy (to the first peer). The rest propagate peer-to-peer.

**Bottleneck B — Slowest Peer Download**
A peer cannot receive faster than its own download link. The slowest peer sets the global lower bound.

$$\text{Time}_{\text{min download}} \;=\; \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}} \;=\; \dfrac{F}{\min_{i} d_{i}}$$

**Bottleneck C — Aggregate Upload Capacity**
Total bits that must be delivered to the swarm: $N \cdot F$ bits (each of the $N$ peers must receive a full copy).
Total upload capacity available in the swarm: $u_s$ (server) $+ \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i$ (all peers).

Therefore the time for the aggregate upload pipeline to push $N \cdot F$ bits:

$$\text{Time}_{\text{aggregate}} \;=\; \dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_{s} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_{i}}$$

### Step 3 — Combine Using the max() Operator
The actual distribution time is governed by the **slowest** of the three bottlenecks. We use the *maximum* time because all three must complete.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{p2p} &= \max\left\{\,\text{Time}_{\text{server}},\ \text{Time}_{\text{min download}},\ \text{Time}_{\text{aggregate}}\,\right\} \\[6pt]
&= \max\left\{\,\dfrac{F}{u_{s}}\,,\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}\,,\ \dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_{s} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_{i}}\,\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic Check:* If the server is slow (Bottleneck A), no matter how fast the peers are, the first seed takes time. If the slowest peer is bandwidth-starved (Bottleneck B), the rest are held up. If aggregate upload is low (Bottleneck C), the swarm cannot supply $N$ copies fast enough. The actual finish time is the worst (largest) of these three.

### Step 4 — Compare P2P vs. Client-Server in the Large-N Limit

For the **client-server** model, replace Bottleneck A with $\frac{N F}{u_s}$ (server must serve $N$ copies).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} D_{cs} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \max\left\{\dfrac{NF}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}\right\} \;=\; \infty \\[4pt]
\lim_{N \to \infty} D_{p2p} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \max\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}},\ \dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

In the symmetric case where $u_i = u$ for all peers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} D_{p2p} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \max\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}},\ \dfrac{NF}{u_s + N \cdot u}\right\} \\[4pt]
&= \max\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}},\ \dfrac{F}{u}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **killer property of P2P**: as $N \to \infty$, the distribution time stays **bounded** by a constant (independent of $N$), while the client-server time grows **linearly without bound**. This is why BitTorrent scales to millions of peers while a single webserver collapses.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 1 Gbit file must be distributed to $N = 10$ peers. Server upload $u_s = 100$ Mbps. Each peer has $u_i = 5$ Mbps upload and $d_{\min} = 10$ Mbps download. Find the distribution time for (a) client-server, (b) P2P.

**Given values** (in consistent units of bits and bits/sec):
$F = 10^9$ bits, $u_s = 10^8$ bps, $u_i = 5 \times 10^6$ bps, $d_{\min} = 10^7$ bps, $N = 10$.

### Part (a) — Client-Server

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{NF}{u_s} &= \dfrac{10 \cdot 10^9}{10^8} \;=\; 100\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{F}{d_{\min}} &= \dfrac{10^9}{10^7} \;=\; 100\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
D_{cs} &= \max\{100,\ 100\} \;=\; \mathbf{100\ \text{seconds}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — P2P

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{F}{u_s} &= \dfrac{10^9}{10^8} \;=\; 10\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{F}{d_{\min}} &= \dfrac{10^9}{10^7} \;=\; 100\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i} &= \dfrac{10 \cdot 10^9}{10^8 + 10 \cdot 5\times10^6} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{10}}{1.5 \times 10^8} \;\approx\; 66.67\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
D_{p2p} &= \max\{10,\ 100,\ 66.67\} \;=\; \mathbf{100\ \text{seconds}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** In this case, the slowest peer's download link dominates. P2P does *not* always beat client-server — it shines when $N$ is large and peer uploads are abundant.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When Does P2P Win?

| Condition | Winning Model | Why |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ is very large, $u_s$ low | **P2P** | Peer upload capacity scales with $N$ |
| All peers have slow download $d_{\min}$ | **Client-Server** (or CDN) | Bottleneck B dominates both equally |
| Server has huge $u_s$, few peers | **Client-Server** | Aggregate upload (P2P C) is wasted |
| File is streamed live | **P2P** with care | Low latency + scaling — e.g., live video P2P |


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DNS Resolution Flow — Iterative Query Path

The following block-diagram illustrates the standard **iterative DNS resolution** flow when a host queries `www.ktu.edu.in`. This is the most frequently requested KTU diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HostA[Host: Client Browser] -->|1. Recursive Query www.ktu.edu.in| LocalDNS[Local DNS Resolver ISP]
    LocalDNS -->|2. Iterative Query: Where is ktu.edu.in?| RootSrv[Root DNS Server Tier 1]
    RootSrv -->|Referral: See .in TLD| LocalDNS
    LocalDNS -->|3. Iterative Query: Where is ktu.edu.in?| TLDSrv[TLD Server for .in Tier 2]
    TLDSrv -->|Referral: ns1.ktu.edu.in at IP X.X.X.X| LocalDNS
    LocalDNS -->|4. Iterative Query: IP of www| AuthSrv[Authoritative DNS ns1.ktu.edu.in Tier 3]
    AuthSrv -->|Answer: 103.25.61.18| LocalDNS
    LocalDNS -->|5. Final Answer 103.25.61.18| HostA
    AuthSrv -.Caches for TTL.-> Cache[(Resolver Cache TTL 86400s)]
    LocalDNS -.Caches A Record.-> Cache
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Steps 1 and 5 are *recursive* (host ↔ local resolver). Steps 2, 3, 4 are *iterative* — the queried server returns only a referral, not a final answer.

## 4.2 P2P Architecture Comparison — Block Schematics

The three primary P2P architectures in the KTU syllabus are contrasted below.

### A) Centralized (Napster-style Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph C1[Centralized P2P]
        CS[Central Index Server] --- P1[Peer 1]
        CS --- P2[Peer 2]
        CS --- P3[Peer 3]
        CS --- P4[Peer 4]
        P1 <-->|File Transfer| P2
        P2 <-->|File Transfer| P3
        P3 <-->|File Transfer| P4
        P1 <-->|File Transfer| P4
    end
```

> **Properties:** Central server holds *index only*. All file transfers peer-to-peer. Single point of failure (legal vulnerability — Napster was shut down).

### B) Decentralized (Gnutella-style Pure P2P)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph D1[Pure P2P Flooding]
        Q1[Peer A] --- Q2[Peer B]
        Q2 --- Q3[Peer C]
        Q3 --- Q4[Peer D]
        Q4 --- Q5[Peer E]
        Q5 --- Q6[Peer F]
        Q1 --- Q7[Peer G]
        Q7 --- Q3
        Q2 --- Q8[Peer H]
    end
```

> **Properties:** No central server. Queries are flooded with bounded TTL. No guarantee of lookup completeness.

### C) Structured (Chord DHT)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Chord Ring DHT]
        N10[Node 10] --- N20[Node 20]
        N20 --- N32[Node 32]
        N32 --- N40[Node 40]
        N40 --- N55[Node 55]
        N55 --- N10
        N10 -.Finger Table.-> N20
        N20 -.Finger Table.-> N32
        N32 -.Finger Table.-> N40
    end
```

> **Properties:** Keys are hashed onto the ring. Each node stores keys for which it is the successor. Lookup in O(log N) hops via finger tables. Used by Mainline DHT (BitTorrent).

## 4.3 BitTorrent Swarm — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Tracker Server] -->|Peer List| A[Peer Alice]
    T -->|Peer List| B[Peer Bob]
    T -->|Peer List| C[Peer Carol]
    T -->|Peer List| D[Peer Dave]
    A <-->|Piece Exchange| B
    A <-->|Piece Exchange| C
    B <-->|Piece Exchange| D
    C <-->|Piece Exchange| D
    A -.Optimistic Unchoke every 30s.-> C
    B -.Tit-for-tat policy top 4 peers.-> D
    Tor[Torrent File: piece hashes, tracker URL] --> A
    Tor --> B
    Tor --> C
    Tor --> D
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The tracker coordinates membership but never touches file data. The *torrent file* is static metadata containing SHA-1 piece hashes. The actual data flow is fully peer-to-peer.


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of DNS in computer networks. Differentiate between iterative and recursive DNS queries with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of DNS (1 mark):** The Domain Name System (DNS) is a hierarchical and distributed naming service that maps human-readable hostnames (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) to machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., `103.25.61.18`). It is the Internet's naming directory, supporting aliasing, mail server lookup, and load distribution.

**Iterative vs Recursive (2 marks):**
- **Recursive Query:** The contacted server takes full responsibility and returns the final answer. Example: A host sending a query to its local DNS resolver.
- **Iterative Query:** The contacted server returns only the *best partial answer* it has — either the IP, or a referral to another server. Example: Local resolver querying root → TLD → authoritative servers in sequence.

> Each query type must be illustrated with a one-line example. A diagram of the resolution flow is a 1-mark bonus.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is a P2P network? Compare pure P2P and hybrid P2P architectures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) network** is a distributed application architecture in which every participating node (peer) acts as both a client (consumer) and a server (supplier) of resources. There is no always-on dedicated central server.

**Pure P2P (1.5 marks):** No central server at any time. Peers self-organize via flooding or DHT. Example: Gnutella, Chord. *Drawback:* discovery overhead; *benefit:* no single point of failure.

**Hybrid P2P (1.5 marks):** A central server maintains only the *index* of which peer has which file; actual file transfer is peer-to-peer. Example: Napster. *Drawback:* single point of failure for the index; *benefit:* efficient lookup.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the architecture of the Domain Name System. Explain the different types of DNS servers and resource records with examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 4 Gbit file is to be distributed to $N = 100$ peers. The server upload rate is $u_s = 1$ Gbps, each peer's upload is $u_i = 200$ Mbps, and the minimum download rate is $d_{\min} = 500$ Mbps. Calculate and compare the file distribution time for the **client-server** and **P2P** models.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — DNS Architecture (7 marks)

**1. Hierarchical Distributed Database (2 marks)**
DNS is a distributed database implemented across thousands of name servers worldwide. The namespace is an inverted tree whose root is the anonymous root `"."`. Each node has a label (max 63 chars) and a fully qualified domain name is read leaf-to-root (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in.`).

**2. Types of DNS Servers (3 marks)**

| Server Type | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Root DNS Server** | Returns TLD server info; 13 logical root servers globally (A–M) | `a.root-servers.net` |
| **TLD Server** | Maintains entries for `.com`, `.in`, `.org`, country codes | `in. TLD server` |
| **Authoritative Server** | Holds the final, definitive records for an organization | `ns1.ktu.edu.in` |
| **Local DNS Resolver** | First point of contact; caches replies per TTL | ISP resolver `8.8.8.8` |

**3. Resource Records (2 marks)**
A Resource Record is a 4-tuple `(Name, Value, Type, TTL)`.
- **A Record:** `(www.ktu.edu.in, 103.25.61.18, A, 86400)` — maps hostname to IPv4.
- **NS Record:** `(ktu.edu.in, ns1.ktu.edu.in, NS, 86400)` — identifies authoritative server.
- **MX Record:** `(ktu.edu.in, mail.ktu.edu.in, MX, 86400)` — mail server.
- **CNAME Record:** `(www.ktu.edu.in, host.ktu.edu.in, CNAME, 86400)` — alias.

#### Part (b) — File Distribution Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
$F = 4 \times 10^9$ bits, $N = 100$, $u_s = 10^9$ bps, $u_i = 2 \times 10^8$ bps, $d_{\min} = 5 \times 10^8$ bps.

**Client-Server Model (3.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{NF}{u_s} &= \dfrac{100 \cdot 4 \times 10^9}{10^9} \;=\; 400\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{F}{d_{\min}} &= \dfrac{4 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^8} \;=\; 8\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
D_{cs} &= \max\{400,\ 8\} \;=\; \mathbf{400\ \text{seconds}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**P2P Model (3.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{F}{u_s} &= \dfrac{4 \times 10^9}{10^9} \;=\; 4\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{F}{d_{\min}} &= \dfrac{4 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^8} \;=\; 8\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
\sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i &= 100 \cdot 2 \times 10^8 \;=\; 2 \times 10^{10}\ \text{bps} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i} &= \dfrac{100 \cdot 4 \times 10^9}{10^9 + 2 \times 10^{10}} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times 10^{11}}{2.1 \times 10^{10}} \;\approx\; 19.05\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
D_{p2p} &= \max\{4,\ 8,\ 19.05\} \;\approx\; \mathbf{19.05\ \text{seconds}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** P2P is **~21× faster** than client-server in this scenario because the server is the bottleneck in CS, while P2P pools 100 peer uploads.

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the iterative and recursive DNS query resolution process for a host accessing `www.example.com`.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **BitTorrent protocol** in detail. Explain the tit-for-tat mechanism, optimistic unchoking, and the role of the tracker.

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — DNS Query Resolution (7 marks)

A host H wants the IP of `www.example.com`. (5 marks for stepwise explanation + 2 marks for diagram.)

**Step 1:** Host H sends a **recursive** query to its local DNS resolver `LDNS`.
**Step 2:** LDNS checks its cache. On miss, it sends an **iterative** query to a root DNS server.
**Step 3:** Root server returns a referral to the `.com` TLD server.
**Step 4:** LDNS queries `.com` TLD server iteratively → gets referral to `example.com`'s authoritative server.
**Step 5:** LDNS queries the authoritative server → gets the A record IP for `www.example.com`.
**Step 6:** LDNS returns the IP to host H and caches it for the TTL duration.

**Diagram (textual form for fallback):**
```
Host → [Recursive] → Local DNS
                          ↓ [Iterative]
                        Root → .com TLD → Authoritative (example.com)
                          ↑ [Referrals]
                       Local DNS → [Recursive answer] → Host
```

#### Part (b) — BitTorrent Protocol (7 marks)

**1. File Splitting and Torrent Metadata (2 marks):**
The original file $F$ is split into *pieces* of fixed size (typically **256 KB**). A `.torrent` file is generated, containing:
- SHA-1 hash of each piece
- Tracker URL
- File names, lengths, piece length

**2. Tracker Role (2 marks):**
A **tracker** is a server that maintains a list of peers in a *swarm* (a group of peers sharing the same torrent). New peers register with the tracker and receive a random subset of peer IP addresses. Modern trackers may be replaced by **Mainline DHT** for trackerless swarms.

**3. Tit-for-Tat and Optimistic Unchoking (3 marks):**
- Peers periodically measure the rate at which each of their connected peers is uploading to them.
- Each peer *unchokes* (uploads to) the **top 4 peers** by download rate. This is the **tit-for-tat** mechanism — it ensures fair reciprocity and prevents free-riding.
- Every **30 seconds**, a peer *optimistically unchokes* a randomly chosen peer regardless of current reciprocity, to discover potentially better partners and to allow newcomers to bootstrap.
- This policy maximizes swarm-wide throughput while discouraging leechers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Marks Deduction Warning):**
> 1. **Mixing up query types** — A common mistake is saying "the host sends an iterative query." The host sends a *recursive* query to the local resolver. The local resolver sends *iterative* queries upstream. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the third term in the P2P formula** — Many students write only $\frac{F}{u_s}$ and $\frac{F}{d_{\min}}$, omitting the aggregate upload term $\frac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i}$. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Confusing *piece* size with *block* size** — BitTorrent operates on **256 KB pieces**, with sub-pieces (typically 16 KB) used for sliding-window flow control. Stating "1 MB" is wrong. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Saying Napster is pure P2P** — Napster is **hybrid P2P** (centralized index, distributed data). (-1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> **Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision):**

- **DNS** is an application-layer protocol (default **port 53** — UDP for queries, TCP for large responses / zone transfers).
- DNS namespace is an **inverted tree** rooted at `"."`. Reading order is leaf → root.
- There are **13 logical root servers** (A through M) but hundreds of physical instances via *anycast*.
- **Host → Local Resolver: Recursive.** **Resolver → Root/TLD/Authoritative: Iterative.**
- DNS uses **caching with TTL** to reduce load.
- **DNSSEC** = cryptographic signing of DNS records to prevent cache poisoning.
- A **P2P network** has no dedicated server; peers serve as both client and server.
- Three P2P architectures: **Centralized (Napster), Decentralized (Gnutella), Structured (Chord DHT)**.
- **BitTorrent** = piece-based P2P, SHA-1 hashing, tracker coordination, tit-for-tat, optimistic unchoking every 30 s.
- **DHT (Distributed Hash Table)** enables O(log N) lookup in a fully decentralized network (Chord, Kademlia).
- **File distribution formulas (memorize both):**
  - $D_{cs} = \max\left\{\dfrac{NF}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}\right\}$
  - $D_{p2p} = \max\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}},\ \dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}\right\}$
- **Key insight:** As $N \to \infty$, $D_{cs} \to \infty$ but $D_{p2p}$ remains bounded — **P2P scales linearly with peer upload capacity**.
- In a numerical problem, **always state units clearly** (bits vs. bytes; Mbps vs. Gbps) and pick a **consistent system of units** before plugging into formulas.
- Draw the **3-tier DNS resolution diagram** at least once before the exam — it appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 question paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: BitTorrent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Case Study: BitTorrent ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **BitTorrent** is a **peer-to-peer (P2P) protocol** for distributing large files over the Internet. It was designed by **Bram Cohen in 2001** and operates by breaking a target file into fixed-size *pieces* and enabling efficient parallel downloading from multiple peers simultaneously. BitTorrent is a canonical case study for **application-layer P2P architecture** and is widely used in KTU's Computer Networks syllabus (PCCST501, Module 1) to illustrate the shift from the traditional client-server model to distributed content delivery. Unlike the traditional HTTP/FTP **client-server model** where a single server handles all download requests, BitTorrent leverages the **upload bandwidth of every downloading client**, thereby reducing the load on the original distributor and enabling scalability for millions of concurrent users. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** BitTorrent is a *protocol*, not an application. Many clients (µTorrent, qBittorrent, Transmission, Deluge) implement the same BitTorrent protocol specification defined in **BEP-3 (BitTorrent Enhancement Proposal 3)**. --- ### 1.2 Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary) | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **Torrent** | A set of peers exchanging pieces of a specific file(s) | | **Swarm** | The complete set of peers (seeders + leechers) participating in a torrent | | **Seeder** | A peer that has the **complete file** and is only uploading | | **Leecher** | A peer that is still downloading pieces (also uploads) | | **Tracker** | Central server that coordinates peers in a swarm | | **.torrent file** | A small metadata file containing info about the file(s), piece length, SHA-1 hashes, and tracker URL | | **Piece** | A fixed-size chunk of the file (typically **256 KB** or **512 KB**) | | **Block (Sub-piece)** | A smaller transmission unit within a piece (typically **16 KB**) | | **Choking** | Temporarily refusing to upload to a particular peer | | **Unchoking** | Granting permission to a peer to download from you | --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pizza Party" Model Imagine **100 students** want a pizza delivered to their classroom from a single shop. If only the shop delivers, it becomes a bottleneck (classic **client-server**). In BitTorrent's model: - The shop cuts the pizza into 100 slices and gives one slice to each student. - Now, every student can share **their slice** with others who don't have it yet. - After a few rounds, **every student has the entire pizza** — without overloading the shop. - The "shop" is the **initial seeder**, the slices are **pieces**, the students are **peers**, and the classroom whiteboard listing everyone's name is the **tracker**. > [!TIP] > **Intuition Tip:** The "magic" of BitTorrent is that **every downloader is also an uploader**. The aggregate upload capacity of the swarm grows as more people join — the opposite of a traditional server that gets overwhelmed as load increases. --- ### 1.4 Protocol Position in the TCP/IP Stack BitTorrent operates at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** of the TCP/IP model. It uses **TCP** for reliable piece transfer and **HTTP/HTTPS** for tracker communication (BEP-3, BEP-23). Modern extensions also use **UDP** for uTP (uTorrent Transport Protocol) and DHT. > **Standard Metrics used in BitTorrent:** > - **Default piece size:** **256 KB** (powers of 2: 16 KB, 32 KB, 64 KB, …, 16 MB) > - **Default block size:** **16 KB** > - **SHA-1 hash per piece:** 20 bytes > - **Connection port:** **6881–6889** (clients now use random ephemeral ports) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 BitTorrent File Distribution Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic The protocol follows a precise sequence of operations: 1. **Torrent Creation:** A *seeder* (initial distributor) chops the file into fixed-size pieces and computes the **SHA-1 hash** of each piece. The SHA-1 hashes, file name, piece length, total length, and tracker URL are bundled into a small **`.torrent` file** (typically a few hundred KB). 2. **Torrent Publication:** The `.torrent` file is published on a website or magnet link is shared. 3. **Peer Joins Swarm:** A new peer (a *leecher*) downloads the `.torrent` file, connects to the **tracker**, and registers itself. 4. **Tracker Response:** The tracker returns a random list of ~50 peers currently active in the swarm. 5. **Handshake:** The leecher performs a BitTorrent protocol handshake with each peer (exchanging peer IDs, info_hash, reserved bytes). 6. **Piece Exchange:** Peers exchange **bitfield messages** (which pieces each has), then use the **piece-selection algorithm** to decide which piece to request from whom. 7. **Choking Algorithm:** Periodically (every **10 seconds**), each peer decides *which* peers to upload to using the **tit-for-tat** strategy. 8. **Completion:** Once a leecher has all pieces, it becomes a **seeder** and continues uploading to help others. --- ### 2.2 Piece Selection Strategies (Critical for KTU) BitTorrent uses **four** piece selection algorithms, each with a specific role: | Strategy | Purpose | Algorithm Logic | |----------|---------|-----------------| | **Strictly Rarest First** | Promote piece diversity in the swarm | Count availability of each missing piece across all peers; request the piece with the **lowest count** | | **Random First Piece** | Bootstrap downloading quickly | For the **first piece**, pick a random piece so the peer can immediately start uploading | | **Endgame Mode** | Avoid the "last piece" problem | When only a few pieces remain missing, request them from **all peers** to reduce download time (causes redundant uploads which are discarded via a `CANCEL`) | | **Downloading from Multiple Peers (Pipelining)** | Keep network pipes full | Issue **5 simultaneous requests** to each peer (avoid waiting for ACKs before sending next request) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strictly Rarest First is the primary algorithm** and is a guaranteed KTU question. The "rarest first" strategy ensures **piece replication** — a piece held by only 1 peer gets prioritized, ensuring no single peer becomes a critical bottleneck. --- ### 2.3 Choking Algorithm — The Heart of BitTorrent The choking algorithm decides **which peers a node will upload to** at any moment. It runs every **10 seconds** and has three components: #### A. Tit-for-Tat (Reciprocity) - A peer uploads to the **4 peers** that have provided the **highest download rates** to it in the last 20 seconds. - This incentivizes contribution: "You scratch my back, I'll scratch yours." #### B. Optimistic Unchoking - Every **30 seconds**, a peer selects **one additional peer at random** and unchokes it — even if that peer hasn't reciprocated. - This discovers new peers and helps bootstrapping newcomers. #### C. Anti-Snubbing - If a peer has been choked by **all** connected peers for 60 seconds, it is "snubbed." - The snubbed peer then gives optimistic unchokes more frequently to recover. > **Standard Choke Interval:** **10 seconds** | **Optimistic Unchoke Interval:** **30 seconds** | **Snub Timeout:** **60 seconds** --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes the key quantitative relations and constants used in BitTorrent problem-solving. | Symbol / Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | $N$ | Total swarm size | peers | Number of active participants | | $S$ | Seeders | peers | Peers with 100% of the file | | $L$ | Leechers | peers | Peers still downloading | | $F$ | File size | bytes | Total size of the distributed file | | $P$ | Piece size | bytes | Typically 256 KB | | $n$ | Number of pieces | dimensionless | $n = \lceil F / P \rceil$ | | $T_{chk}$ | Choke interval | 10 s | Period of choking decision | | $T_{opt}$ | Optimistic unchoke | 30 s | Period of random unchoke | | $T_{snub}$ | Snub timeout | 60 s | Time after which anti-snubbing triggers | | $u_i$ | Upload rate to peer $i$ | B/s | B/s = Bytes per second | | $R_{agg}$ | Aggregate swarm rate | B/s | $R_{agg} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} u_j^{\text{out}}$ | | SHA-1 | Hash function | 20 B output | Per-piece integrity check | | Optimal peers in unchoke list $k$ | $k = 4$ | peers | Standard tit-for-tat quota | #### Key Identity The total number of pieces in a torrent is given by: $$ n = \left\lceil \frac{F}{P} \right\rceil $$ #### Piece Availability For a peer $i$, the **availability count** of a piece $p$ is the number of peers (other than $i$) holding piece $p$: $$ A_p^{(i)} = \sum_{j \neq i} \mathbb{1}[\text{peer } j \text{ has piece } p] $$ A piece is selected if $A_p^{(i)}$ is **minimized** (rarest first). --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility BitTorrent's design has influenced several modern systems: - **Content Delivery Networks (CDNs):** Akamai and Cloudflare use BitTorrent-like swarming. - **Software Updates:** Many open-source distros (Ubuntu, Linux Mint) and games distribute updates via BitTorrent. - **Blockchain & IPFS:** Distributed file systems borrow the chunking, hashing, and DHT concepts directly from BitTorrent. - **Video Streaming (WebTorrent):** Modern WebRTC-based variants stream video in a P2P swarm. > [!TIP] > **For KTU Viva:** Always mention that BitTorrent is one of the **most successful application-layer protocols**, accounting for an estimated **3–6% of all Internet traffic** historically (Sandvine reports). It is a textbook example of how application-layer design can overcome transport-layer limitations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Performance Modeling We now derive the fundamental performance relations in BitTorrent. These derivations are exam-relevant for the 14-mark analytical questions. --- #### 3.1.1 Derivation of File Distribution Time (Simplified Model) Consider a swarm of size $N$ peers sharing a file of size $F$ bytes. Assume: - Each peer has upload capacity $u$ (bytes/s) - Each peer has download capacity $d$ (bytes/s) - The file is initially held by **one** seeder **Step 1 — Total data the swarm must upload to distribute $F$ to all $N$ peers:** Each peer must receive $F$ bytes. Total distribution work = $N \cdot F$ bytes. $$ D_{total} = N \cdot F \quad \text{(bytes)} $$ **Step 2 — Total upload capacity of the swarm:** As the original seeder leaves and new seeders join, the swarm has roughly $N$ uploaders (each at rate $u$). $$ U_{total} = N \cdot u \quad \text{(bytes/s)} $$ **Step 3 — Lower bound on distribution time** (assuming optimal parallelism): $$ T_{min} = \frac{D_{total}}{U_{total}} = \frac{N \cdot F}{N \cdot u} = \frac{F}{u} $$ So the **minimum time to distribute** $F$ **to** $N$ **peers** with $N$ uploaders is $\dfrac{F}{u}$. **Step 4 — Comparison with Client-Server Model:** In a traditional client-server model, the server has upload capacity $u_s$, and the bottleneck is the server itself. Distribution time is: $$ T_{cs} = \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s} $$ **Step 5 — Performance Ratio:** $$ \frac{T_{cs}}{T_{min}} = \frac{N \cdot F / u_s}{F / u} = \frac{N \cdot u}{u_s} $$ If $u_s = u$ (the server is just as fast as a client), then BitTorrent is $N$ times faster than client-server. $$ \boxed{\,T_{BitTorrent} \approx \frac{F}{u} \quad \text{vs} \quad T_{Client\text{-}Server} = \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s}\,} $$ --- #### 3.1.2 Derivation of Number of Pieces Given a file of size $F$ bytes and piece size $P$ bytes: $$ n = \left\lceil \frac{F}{P} \right\rceil $$ **Example:** $F = 700$ MB, $P = 256$ KB. $$ n = \left\lceil \frac{700 \cdot 1024 \text{ KB}}{256 \text{ KB}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2800 \right\rceil = 2800 \text{ pieces} $$ --- #### 3.1.3 Optimal Unchoke Bandwidth Allocation Each peer unchokes $k = 4$ peers under tit-for-tat and 1 peer under optimistic unchoke. If a peer's total upload capacity is $U$, the **per-peer upload rate** is: $$ u_{i \to j} = \frac{U}{k + 1} = \frac{U}{5} $$ For a swarm where every peer reciprocates equally, the **steady-state download rate** of peer $i$ is: $$ d_i = \sum_{j \in \text{unchoked}(i)} \frac{U}{5} = k \cdot \frac{U}{5} = \frac{4U}{5} $$ --- #### 3.1.4 Rarest-First Availability Tracking Let the swarm have $N$ peers and a piece set $\mathcal{P} = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$. Define the **availability matrix**: $$ \mathbf{A} \in \{0, 1\}^{N \times n}, \quad A_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if peer } i \text{ has piece } j \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ The availability of piece $j$ **from peer $i$'s perspective** is: $$ A_j^{(i)} = \sum_{\ell \neq i} A_{\ell j} = \left(\sum_{\ell = 1}^{N} A_{\ell j}\right) - A_{ij} $$ Peer $i$ selects piece $j^*$ such that: $$ j^* = \underset{j \,\in\, \text{missing}(i)}{\arg\min}\; A_j^{(i)} $$ This greedy strategy **maximizes the minimum availability** in the swarm over time, preventing the disappearance of rare pieces. --- #### 3.1.5 Tracker Communication — Set Reconciliation The tracker does not store the *content* of pieces. It only stores peer IP/port pairs. On query from a peer, the tracker returns at most **$m = 50$** random peers (BEP-3 recommendation): $$ \text{Tracker response: } \mathcal{L} = \{ (\text{ip}_k, \text{port}_k) \}_{k=1}^{m}, \quad m \le 50 $$ This caps the **tracker overhead** per query to a constant ~200 bytes, regardless of swarm size. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem:** A BitTorrent swarm has $N = 100$ peers, $S = 10$ seeders, and $L = 90$ leechers. The file is $F = 1$ GB. Each peer has upload rate $u = 500$ KB/s. The original seeder has upload capacity $u_s = 2$ MB/s. Find: > (a) The number of pieces for a piece size $P = 256$ KB. > (b) The lower bound on distribution time in BitTorrent. > (c) The distribution time in a client-server model. > (d) The BitTorrent speedup factor. **Solution:** **Part (a):** $$ n = \left\lceil \frac{F}{P} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1 \cdot 1024 \cdot 1024 \text{ KB}}{256 \text{ KB}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 4096 \right\rceil = 4096 \text{ pieces} $$ **Part (b):** Using the BitTorrent formula: $$ T_{BT} = \frac{F}{u} = \frac{1024 \cdot 1024 \text{ KB}}{500 \text{ KB/s}} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{500} = 2097.15 \text{ s} \approx 34.95 \text{ min} $$ **Part (c):** Client-server: $$ T_{CS} = \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s} = \frac{100 \cdot 1 \text{ GB}}{2 \text{ MB/s}} = \frac{102{,}400 \text{ MB}}{2 \text{ MB/s}} = 51{,}200 \text{ s} \approx 14.22 \text{ hours} $$ **Part (d):** Speedup: $$ S = \frac{T_{CS}}{T_{BT}} = \frac{51{,}200}{2097.15} \approx 24.4 $$ > **Result:** BitTorrent is approximately **24.4× faster** than client-server for this configuration. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 BitTorrent System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef ext fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef peer fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef proc fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef store fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef dec fill:#c62828,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff Origin[Original Seeder
Initial File Holder]:::ext Torrent[.torrent Metadata File
SHA-1 Hashes + Tracker URL]:::store Tracker[Tracker Server
Peer Registry]:::ext Website[Index Website
Publishes .torrent]:::ext P1[Peer 1
Leecher or Seeder]:::peer P2[Peer 2
Leecher or Seeder]:::peer P3[Peer 3
Leecher or Seeder]:::peer P4[Peer N
Leecher or Seeder]:::peer HANDSHAKE[Protocol Handshake
BitTorrent Peer Wire]:::proc BFEX[Bitfield Exchange
Piece Availability Map]:::proc PIECESEL[Piece Selection
Rarest First Algorithm]:::proc CHOKE[Choking Decision
Tit for Tat + Optimistic]:::proc PIECETRANS[Piece Transfer
TCP Upload Download]:::proc Origin -- "publishes .torrent" --> Website Origin -- "seeds file pieces" --> PIECETRANS Website -- "downloads .torrent" --> P1 Website -- "downloads .torrent" --> P2 Website -- "downloads .torrent" --> P3 Website -- "downloads .torrent" --> P4 P1 -- "registers IP port" --> Tracker P2 -- "registers IP port" --> Tracker P3 -- "registers IP port" --> Tracker P4 -- "registers IP port" --> Tracker Tracker -- "returns peer list ~50" --> P1 Tracker -- "returns peer list ~50" --> P2 Tracker -- "returns peer list ~50" --> P3 Tracker -- "returns peer list ~50" --> P4 P1 --> HANDSHAKE P2 --> HANDSHAKE P3 --> HANDSHAKE P4 --> HANDSHAKE HANDSHAKE --> BFEX BFEX --> PIECESEL PIECESEL --> CHOKE CHOKE --> PIECETRANS PIECETRANS -- "exchanges pieces" --> P1 PIECETRANS -- "exchanges pieces" --> P2 PIECETRANS -- "exchanges pieces" --> P3 PIECETRANS -- "exchanges pieces" --> P4 ``` --- ### 4.2 State Diagram — Peer Lifecycle in BitTorrent ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Downloading : peer joins swarm via .torrent Downloading --> Downloading : receives piece
updates bitfield Downloading --> Seeding : all pieces acquired
becomes 100 percent source Seeding --> Seeding : continues uploading
choking algorithm active Downloading --> [*] : user stops client
before completion Seeding --> [*] : user stops client
or seed ratio limit hit note right of Downloading Piece selection active Rarest First algorithm Tit for Tat unchoke Optimistic unchoke every 30 s end note note right of Seeding No more downloads Only uploads Helps swarm health end note ``` --- ### 4.3 Choking Decision Flow (Per 10-Second Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef start fill:#1565c0,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff classDef decision fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#e65100,color:#ffffff classDef action fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff classDef endnode fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#4a148c,color:#ffffff START[Every 10 Seconds]:::start CHK1{Has Peer Snubbed Me
for 60 Seconds}:::decision CHK2{Peer Provided
High Download Rate
in Last 20 Seconds}:::decision CHK3{30 Second Timer
for Optimistic Unchoke}:::decision RANK[Rank Peers by
Download Rate Reciprocity]:::action UNCHOKE4[Unchoke Top 4 Peers
Tit for Tat Quota]:::action OPT[Unchoke 1 Random Peer
Optimistic Unchoke]:::action CHOKEOTHERS[Choke All Remaining Peers]:::action WAIT[Wait 10 Seconds
for Next Cycle]:::endnode START --> CHK1 CHK1 -- Yes --> CHK3 CHK1 -- No --> CHK2 CHK2 --> RANK RANK --> UNCHOKE4 UNCHOKE4 --> CHK3 CHK3 -- Timer Expired --> OPT CHK3 -- Not Yet --> CHOKEOTHERS OPT --> CHOKEOTHERS CHOKEOTHERS --> WAIT WAIT --> START ``` --- ### 4.4 Piece Exchange Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant L as Leecher Peer A participant S as Seeder Peer B L->>S: TCP Handshake (info_hash, peer_id) S-->>L: Handshake ACK L->>S: Bitfield (pieces I have) S->>L: Bitfield (pieces I have) L->>S: Interested (I want pieces you have) S->>L: Unchoke (I will upload to you) L->>S: Request (piece index, block offset, length) S-->>L: Piece (data block) L->>S: Request (next block) S-->>L: Piece (data block) Note over L,S: Continue until piece complete or 5 pipelined requests L->>S: Have (I now have this piece) S->>L: Request (I want a piece you have) L->>S: Unchoke (I will upload to you) L-->>S: Piece (data block) Note over L,S: Tit for tat reciprocity established ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the BitTorrent protocol. List and briefly explain the role of any four entities involved in a BitTorrent swarm.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** BitTorrent is a peer-to-peer (P2P) protocol designed for efficient distribution of large files over the Internet. It splits a file into small fixed-size pieces and enables peers to download and upload pieces simultaneously, distributing the load across the swarm. **Four Key Entities (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Seeder:** A peer possessing the complete copy of the file; only uploads pieces. 2. **Leecher:** A peer that is still downloading pieces; both uploads and downloads. 3. **Tracker:** A central server that maintains a registry of all active peers in a swarm and provides a random list of peers to a newly joined peer. 4. **.torrent File:** A small metadata file that contains the SHA-1 hashes of all pieces, file name, total length, piece length, and the tracker's URL. It is published for peers to join the swarm. > *Examiner Note: Award full marks only if all four entities are correctly identified with their roles.* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the "tit-for-tat" choking strategy in BitTorrent. Why is it essential?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Explanation (2 Marks):** Tit-for-tat is a choking strategy in which every peer continuously measures the download rates it has received from its connected peers over the last 20 seconds and unchokes (allows uploads to) the **top 4 peers** providing the highest rates. This reciprocal arrangement means "I will upload to you only if you upload to me." **Importance (1 Mark):** It is essential because it creates a **strong incentive for cooperation** — free-riders (peers that only download) are penalized with low download rates, while contributing peers receive high download rates. This **self-policing mechanism** is what makes BitTorrent scalable and resilient without a central authority. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *BitTorrent Architecture & Piece Selection* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]** *(a)* Describe the **complete architecture** of the BitTorrent protocol with a neat diagram. Explain the roles of the **tracker, seeder, leecher, .torrent file, and swarm** in detail. *(7 Marks)* *(b)* What is a **piece selection strategy**? Explain the four strategies used in BitTorrent: **strictly rarest first, random first piece, endgame mode, and pipelining**. Justify why "rarest first" is the most important. *(7 Marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Architecture (7 Marks):** The BitTorrent architecture is a **distributed P2P system** with the following components: **1. .torrent File (1 Mark):** The original distributor creates a `.torrent` file containing: - `announce`: URL of the tracker - `info`: dictionary with `name`, `piece length`, `pieces` (concatenation of SHA-1 hashes of all pieces), and `length` (or `files` for multi-file torrents) **2. Tracker Server (1 Mark):** A lightweight HTTP/HTTPS server that maintains an in-memory list of (peer_id, IP, port) tuples for all active peers in a swarm. When a peer joins, the tracker returns a random subset (~50 peers) of the swarm. **3. Swarm (1 Mark):** The complete set of peers (seeders + leechers) currently participating in the distribution of a specific torrent. As the swarm grows, the aggregate bandwidth grows, making the system self-scaling. **4. Seeders and Leechers (2 Marks):** - **Seeders** possess the complete file and serve pieces. - **Leechers** are downloading peers that also upload pieces they already have. **5. Workflow (2 Marks):** - Seeder creates `.torrent` → publishes on index site. - New leecher downloads `.torrent` → contacts tracker → gets peer list. - Leecher performs handshakes with peers → exchanges bitfields → requests pieces using the rarest-first strategy → uploads pieces reciprocally via tit-for-tat. **Diagram (sketch):** ``` [.torrent file] --published on--> [Index Website] | v [Leecher A] <--peer list-- [Tracker] [Leecher B] <--peer list-- [Tracker] [Seeder] <--peer list-- [Tracker] \ | / \ | / (piece exchange) v v v (SWARM) ``` --- **Part (b) — Piece Selection Strategies (7 Marks):** Piece selection is the algorithm that decides **which piece a leecher requests next** from a peer. The four strategies are: **1. Strictly Rarest First (3 Marks — most important):** For each missing piece, the peer counts how many other peers in the swarm have that piece. The peer then requests the piece with the **lowest count**. *Justification:* It maximizes piece replication, ensuring that no piece becomes a bottleneck or vanishes when the only peer holding it leaves. This is the **primary** strategy used during normal operation. **2. Random First Piece (1.5 Marks):** When a peer first joins and has zero pieces, requesting the rarest piece is counterproductive (it may not be available from any peer). Instead, the peer randomly selects the first piece to **bootstrap itself into the swarm** as quickly as possible. After acquiring this first piece, it can immediately start reciprocating and switch to rarest-first. **3. Endgame Mode (1.5 Marks):** When only a few pieces remain missing, the peer sends requests for the same missing pieces to **all** peers that have them. This parallelism avoids being slowed down by one slow peer. When the piece arrives, a `CANCEL` is sent to the other peers to discard their redundant uploads. The waste is small because this mode is activated only near the end of the download. **4. Pipelining (1 Mark):** The peer issues **up to 5 outstanding requests** to each connected peer simultaneously, without waiting for an ACK. This keeps the TCP connection's pipe full and avoids wasting bandwidth due to the round-trip-time delay. > *Concluding Statement:* "Rarest first is the most important because it is the *only* strategy that actively shapes the swarm's health over time, ensuring **no single piece becomes a chokepoint**." --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Choking Algorithm & Performance Analysis* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]** *(a)* What is **choking** in BitTorrent? Explain the **tit-for-tat, optimistic unchoking, and anti-snubbing** mechanisms with their respective time intervals. *(7 Marks)* *(b)* A BitTorrent swarm of **N = 200 peers** is distributing a file of size **F = 4 GB**. Each peer has an upload rate of **u = 1 MB/s**. The original seeder has an upload rate of **u_s = 5 MB/s**. Compute the **number of pieces** (for piece size **P = 256 KB**), the **minimum distribution time in BitTorrent**, the **distribution time in a client-server model**, and the **speedup factor**. *(7 Marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Choking Algorithm (7 Marks):** **Choking (1 Mark):** Choking is the act of temporarily **refusing to upload** pieces to a particular peer. A peer can only upload to peers it has unchoked. Every 10 seconds, each peer re-evaluates which peers to unchoke. **1. Tit-for-Tat (3 Marks):** A peer continuously tracks the **download rate** from each connected peer over the last 20 seconds. The peer **unchokes the top 4 peers** that have provided the highest download rates. This creates reciprocal behavior: peers that upload more to you will get higher download rates from you. Free-riders who only download are choked and receive very little. **2. Optimistic Unchoking (2 Marks):** Every **30 seconds**, regardless of reciprocation, the peer unchokes **one additional peer chosen at random**. The purpose is twofold: - **Discovery:** New peers that just joined the swarm get a chance to receive their first piece, enabling them to reciprocate later. - **Recovery:** Helps a peer that has been "snubbed" by all others to bootstrap back. **3. Anti-Snubbing (1 Mark):** If a peer has been choked by all its connected peers for a continuous period of **60 seconds**, it is "snubbed" — it has no source of new pieces. To recover, the snubbed peer gives **more frequent optimistic unchokes** so it can rejoin the reciprocal exchange. **Summary Table (Bonus point):** | Mechanism | Interval | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Choke decision | 10 s | Re-evaluate unchoke list | | Rate measurement window | 20 s | Compute top 4 for tit-for-tat | | Optimistic unchoke | 30 s | Discover new peers, bootstrap newcomers | | Anti-snubbing | 60 s | Recover a snubbed peer | --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Number of pieces (1.5 Marks):** $$ n = \left\lceil \frac{F}{P} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{4 \cdot 1024 \cdot 1024 \text{ KB}}{256 \text{ KB}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 16{,}384 \right\rceil = 16{,}384 \text{ pieces} $$ **Step 2 — BitTorrent minimum distribution time (2 Marks):** $$ T_{BT} = \frac{F}{u} = \frac{4 \cdot 1024 \text{ MB}}{1 \text{ MB/s}} = 4096 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3 — Client-server distribution time (2 Marks):** $$ T_{CS} = \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s} = \frac{200 \cdot 4 \cdot 1024 \text{ MB}}{5 \text{ MB/s}} = \frac{819{,}200 \text{ MB}}{5 \text{ MB/s}} = 163{,}840 \text{ s} $$ **Step 4 — Speedup factor (1.5 Marks):** $$ S = \frac{T_{CS}}{T_{BT}} = \frac{163{,}840}{4096} = 40 $$ > **Result:** BitTorrent is **40× faster** than client-server. The file is split into **16,384 pieces** and the BT distribution time is **4096 s (~68.3 min)** versus **163,840 s (~45.5 hours)** for client-server. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "piece" and "block":** Piece = 256 KB, Block = 16 KB. Examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for this confusion. > 2. **Forgetting the ceiling function:** Use $\lceil F/P \rceil$, not $F/P$ — the last piece may be smaller. > 3. **Wrong tit-for-tat count:** It is **4 peers**, not 3 or 5. The optimistic unchoke adds 1 more, total 5 active uploads. > 4. **Tracker ≠ Web Server:** Tracker does not store file content — it only stores peer IP/port pairs. Many students incorrectly describe the tracker as a "file server." > 5. **Choking interval confusion:** Choking is re-evaluated every **10 s**, optimistic unchoke every **30 s**, anti-snubbing at **60 s**. Mixing these is a **favourite deduction point**. > 6. **Unit mismatch:** Use KB, MB, GB consistently. $1 \text{ GB} = 1024 \text{ MB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ KB}$. Always show the conversion. > 7. **Saying "tracker is mandatory":** Modern BitTorrent supports **trackerless operation via DHT (Mainline DHT, BEP-5)**, magnet links (BEP-9), and PEX (Peer Exchange, BEP-11). Examiners may award bonus marks for mentioning this. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for BitTorrent (Module 1):** - **Definition:** BitTorrent is a P2P file distribution protocol by Bram Cohen (2001). - **Piece size:** 256 KB (default). **Block size:** 16 KB. - **Hashing:** SHA-1 per piece (20 bytes per hash). - **Tracker port:** 6881–6889 (legacy). Modern clients use random ports. - **Tracker response size:** ~50 peers per query (BEP-3). - **Choke interval:** 10 s. **Optimistic unchoke:** every 30 s. **Anti-snub timeout:** 60 s. - **Unchoke quota:** Top **4** peers by reciprocity + 1 random = 5 total uploads. - **Rarest First:** Selects piece with lowest swarm availability — maximizes replication. - **Random First Piece:** First piece picked randomly to bootstrap. - **Endgame Mode:** When only a few pieces remain, request them from all peers. - **Pipelining:** Up to 5 outstanding requests per peer to fill TCP pipe. - **Speedup formula:** BitTorrent scales as $F/u$ vs client-server $N \cdot F / u_s$. - **Pieces formula:** $n = \lceil F / P \rceil$. - **Trackerless BitTorrent:** Uses Mainline DHT (Kademlia-based) and magnet links. - **Advantage over HTTP/FTP:** Bandwidth scales with swarm size; resilient to server failure; efficient for large files. - **Disadvantages:** Free-riding problem (mitigated by tit-for-tat); potential copyright misuse; requires simultaneous upload (asymmetric broadband penalty). - **BitTorrent Enhancement Proposals (BEPs):** BEP-3 (core protocol), BEP-5 (DHT), BEP-9 (magnet links), BEP-10 (extension protocol), BEP-11 (PEX), BEP-23 (tracker over HTTPS). - **Real-world uses:** Linux ISO distribution (Ubuntu, Fedora), game patches, open-source software, large dataset distribution. - **Exam mantra:** *"Every downloader is also an uploader — that's the soul of BitTorrent."*

topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Services

# 🌐 Network Services — The Foundation of Inter-Layer Communication

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **OSI Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP Protocol Suite**, a **Service** is formally defined as a *set of capabilities* that a particular layer (the **Service Provider** or **N-layer**) offers to the layer immediately above it (the **Service User** or **(N+1)-layer**). 

The complete specification of services is described by **Service Primitives** — standardized, atomic operations used to invoke the service, and **Service Access Points (SAPs)** — the logical interfaces through which the upper layer accesses the lower layer's services.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition (Direct from Module 2 Syllabus):**
> A *Service* specifies *what* a layer does (functional behaviour), while a *Protocol* specifies *how* a layer performs that service (operational rules). The service is the **external view** seen by the upper layer, while the protocol is the **internal view** used between peer entities on different machines.

### Service Access Point (SAP) — The Logical Gateway
A **Service Access Point (SAP)** is a conceptual logical interface at the boundary between two adjacent layers. Each SAP is uniquely identified by an address (for example, in the **Data Link Layer** it is the **MAC Address**; in the **Network Layer** it is the **IP Address**; in the **Transport Layer** it is the **Port Number**).

### Service Primitives — The Atomic Operations
The four primitive types defined in the **ISO/OSI Reference Model** (and listed in the KTU module outcomes) are:

| # | Primitive | Direction | Meaning |
|---|-----------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **REQUEST** | User → Provider | The upper layer asks the lower layer to perform a service |
| 2 | **INDICATION** | Provider → User | The lower layer informs the upper layer of an event (e.g., incoming call) |
| 3 | **RESPONSE** | User → Provider | The upper layer acknowledges an indication |
| 4 | **CONFIRM** | Provider → User | The lower layer confirms the completion of a requested service |

> [!NOTE]
> 📌 **KTU Board Favourite (3-Mark Question):** *"List the four OSI service primitives and explain the role of INDICATION."* — Memorise this table; it appears in nearly every KTU Dec/Jan cycle.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Service"

Imagine you are sending a parcel from your house in Kerala to a friend in Delhi.

- **You (Sender)** = the **Application Layer** (the *Service User*).
- **The Post Office Counter** = the **Transport Layer**.
- **The Truck / Plane** = the **Network Layer** (the actual mover).
- **The Roads / Runways** = the **Data Link + Physical Layer**.

When you walk up to the counter and say *"I want to send this parcel to Delhi"* — that is a **REQUEST primitive**. 

When the counter clerk in Delhi walks up to your friend and says *"Sir, you have a parcel"* — that is the **INDICATION primitive**.

Your friend then *signs the receipt* — that is a **RESPONSE primitive**. 

The clerk in Kerala then hands *you* a tracking slip saying *"Delivered"* — that is a **CONFIRM primitive**.

> 🎯 **The magic here:** You, the user, do **not** care whether the parcel went by road, air, or train (the *protocol*). You only care that it **arrived safely and intact** (the *service*). This is the exact philosophy of the **OSI Layered Architecture** taught in KTU Module 2.

---

## 1.3 Service vs Protocol — A Critical Distinction (High-Frequency KTU Q)

> [!WARNING]
> 🚨 **Confusion Zone:** Students frequently write "Service and Protocol are the same". They are **NOT**. The KTU board examiner marks this answer as wrong every year.

- **Service:** A *vertical* relationship — between **adjacent layers** on the **same machine**.
- **Protocol:** A *horizontal* relationship — between **peer layers** on **different machines**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Service} = \text{Inter-Layer (Vertical)} \quad \vert \quad \text{Protocol} = \text{Intra-Layer (Horizontal)}}
$$

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> 🔢 **Standard Network Metrics You Must Memorise:**
> - **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) of Ethernet:** **1500 bytes**
> - **Typical Service Primitive Address Bits (MAC):** **48 bits** (IEEE 802)
> - **Standard OSI Layers:** **7** (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical)
> - **TCP/IP Layers:** **4** (Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access)

---

## 1.5 Categories of Services (KTU Module 2 Pillar)

Network services are broadly classified into the following categories. The KTU 2024 module outcomes directly map to these.

### (A) Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless Services
- **Connection-Oriented Service:** A *logical connection* (virtual path) is established **before** data transfer, akin to a phone call. Phases → *Establish → Data Transfer → Release*. Example: **TCP**, **ATM**.
- **Connectionless Service:** Each **Service Data Unit (SDU)** is treated as an *independent entity* and routed independently. No prior setup. Example: **UDP**, **IP**, **Ethernet**.

### (B) Reliable vs Unreliable Services
- **Reliable Service:** Acknowledgements, retransmissions, and sequencing are guaranteed.
- **Unreliable Service:** *Best-effort* delivery. No guarantee (e.g., UDP, IP).

### (C) Service Primitives — Verified vs Unverified
- **Verified Primitives:** All four primitives (REQUEST, INDICATION, RESPONSE, CONFIRM) are used (e.g., a confirmed CONNECT).
- **Unverified Primitives:** Only REQUEST and INDICATION are used (e.g., a DATA-REQUEST, DATA-INDICATION — no confirmation).

### (D) The Three Phases of Connection-Oriented Service
1. **Connection Establishment Phase**
2. **Data Transfer Phase**
3. **Connection Release Phase**

### (E) Quality of Service (QoS) Parameters
The KTU syllabus lists four mandatory QoS parameters for service specification:
1. **Reliability** — probability of failure-free operation.
2. **Delay** — transit time of an SDU.
3. **Jitter** — variation in delay.
4. **Bandwidth / Throughput** — bits per second successfully delivered.
