# 📚 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Service Model — Layer N, N+1, and Peer Entities

In the KTU 2024 scheme's Module 2, the **Service Model** is taught as a *strictly hierarchical contract*:

- **Layer (N+1) — Service User:** The layer that *consumes* the service.
- **Layer N — Service Provider:** The layer that *supplies* the service.
- **Peer entities of Layer N:** Two Layer-N instances on different hosts that *cooperate* to deliver the service using a *protocol*.

The service is delivered through the **SAP** using **Service Primitives**. The data passed across is called:
- **SDU (Service Data Unit):** Data from the upper layer to the lower layer.
- **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** Data unit exchanged between peer entities.
- **ICI (Interface Control Information):** Control info passed across the SAP.
- **IDU (Interface Data Unit):** SDU + ICI.

> 🎓 **Why does this matter?** In KTU board exams, the question *"Differentiate between SDU and PDU"* is a guaranteed **3-marker**. The relationship is:
> 
> $$ \text{PDU}_N = \text{Header}_N + \text{SDU}_N $$
> 
> where the **SDU of Layer N** is the **PDU of Layer (N+1)** (with possible segmentation/reassembly).

---

## 2.2 Connection-Oriented Service — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

A connection-oriented service is a **stateful** service. KTU examiners expect the student to know the **exact sequence of primitives**.

### Step 1 — Connection Establishment (3-Way Handshake Analogy)
1. Source (User A) issues **CONNECT.request** → SAP A → Network Layer.
2. Network Layer on Host A sends a *call request* PDU to Host B.
3. Host B's Network Layer issues **CONNECT.indication** → SAP B → User B.
4. User B accepts → **CONNECT.response** is sent back.
5. User A receives **CONNECT.confirm** → connection is **ESTABLISHED**.

### Step 2 — Data Transfer
- Either side can now issue **DATA.request** (unverified) or **DATA-ACK primitives** (verified).
- The PDU carries the user's SDU as its payload.

### Step 3 — Connection Release
- **DISCONNECT.request** → PDU sent → **DISCONNECT.indication** at the other end.
- The connection state is freed in both endpoints.

---

## 2.3 Connectionless Service — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

Connectionless services are **stateless** and **simpler**. Each SDU is independent.

1. Sender issues **DATA.request** with the *full destination address* in the SDU.
2. Network Layer encapsulates the SDU into a PDU (datagram) and forwards it.
3. Receiver gets **DATA.indication** when the PDU arrives.
4. There is **no** INDICATION/CONFIRM handshake for *release* because no connection existed.

---

## 2.4 Six Types of Services (Tanenbaum Classification — Direct KTU Module 2 Topic)

The KTU 2024 module includes the **six service types** from Tanenbaum's *Computer Networks* textbook, the standard reference. These are routinely asked as **"Explain the six types of services" — 14 marks**.

| # | Service Type | Connection | Reliability | Sequence | Example |
|---|--------------|------------|-------------|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Reliable Connection-Oriented** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ Guaranteed | TCP, X.25 |
| 2 | **Reliable Connectionless** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ Per-message | TFTP (acknowledged datagrams) |
| 3 | **Unreliable Connection-Oriented** | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ In-order | ATM CBR (Constant Bit Rate) |
| 4 | **Unreliable Connectionless** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ No order | UDP, IP, Ethernet |
| 5 | **Datagram Acknowledged Service** | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ No order | ISO 8208 / CLNP |
| 6 | **Request-Reply Service** | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | RPC, DNS queries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🧠 **Examiner's Trick Question (Frequent in KTU):**
> *"Is UDP reliable or unreliable? Is it connection-oriented or connectionless?"*
> **Answer:** UDP is **unreliable** and **connectionless**. It uses the four primitive types but only unverified DATA primitives. *Do not confuse UDP with TCP.*

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations for "Services"

> [!NOTE]
> 📐 The "Services" topic is mostly *qualitative*, but the following mathematical relationships **must** be memorised for KTU numerical/derivation sub-parts.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{PDU}_N = \text{Header}_N + \text{SDU}_N$ | Encapsulation relationship |
| 2 | $\text{IDU} = \text{SDU} + \text{ICI}$ | Interface Data Unit |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{L}{L+H}$ | Encapsulation efficiency (L = payload, H = header) |
| 4 | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}}$ | Total service delay |
| 5 | $\text{Bandwidth-Delay Product} = B \times \tau$ | Bits in flight (relevant for service throughput) |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{W}{T_{\text{end}}}$ | Effective service rate |
| 7 | $N_{\text{services}} = 2^{2^n} = 16$ (for n=2) | Number of distinct service classifications for *n* binary traits |
| 8 | $S_{\text{eff}} = \left(1 - \dfrac{H}{L+H}\right) \times 100\%$ | Effective service payload ratio |

> ⚠️ **KTU Pitfall:** When using $\eta$ in tables, **never write absolute-value pipes** like `|L/H|`. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$. The markdown parser in the KTU portal will break your answer script otherwise.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Service Class | Real-World Deployment |
|---------------|------------------------|
| **Reliable Connection-Oriented (TCP)** | Web (HTTP/HTTPS), Email (SMTP), File Transfer (FTP) |
| **Unreliable Connectionless (UDP)** | Live video (Zoom, YouTube live), VoIP, Online gaming |
| **Unreliable Connection-Oriented (ATM)** | Legacy ISDN voice circuits, real-time industrial control |
| **Request-Reply** | DNS lookups, RPC, REST API calls |

The KTU board expects you to *map* every service to at least one **real-world protocol** in your exam answers.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Encapsulation Efficiency of a Service

When a service is delivered, each layer adds a **header (H)** to the user payload (**L**). The KTU question *"Calculate the fraction of channel capacity used for overhead"* maps directly to this derivation.

**Given:**
- Payload size (SDU) = $L$ bytes
- Header added by Layer N = $H_N$ bytes
- Header added by Layer N+1 = $H_{N+1}$ bytes

**Step 1: Define the efficiency.**
Efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of *useful payload bits* to the *total transmitted bits*.

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Payload}}{\text{Total Transmitted}}
$$

**Step 2: Express the total transmitted bits.**
The total size on the wire equals the payload plus all headers added by every layer in the path.

$$
\text{Total} = L + H_N + H_{N+1}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the efficiency definition.**

$$
\eta = \frac{L}{L + H_N + H_{N+1}}
$$

**Step 4: Generalize for k layers of headers.**

$$
\eta = \frac{L}{L + \sum_{i=1}^{k} H_i}
$$

**Step 5: Limiting case analysis (for analytical depth).**

$$
\lim_{L \to \infty} \eta = \lim_{L \to \infty} \frac{L}{L + \sum H_i} = 1
$$

$$
\lim_{H \to \infty} \eta = 0 \quad \text{(all bandwidth wasted on headers)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Larger payloads amortize the per-layer header overhead. This is why KTU examiners *love* asking: *"Why does a 1500-byte Ethernet frame have better efficiency than 50-byte frames?"* — because of the $\eta$ relationship above.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Delay for a Connection-Oriented Service

The total delay experienced by an SDU is the sum of three KTU-mandated delay components.

**Step 1: Define each delay component.**

- $T_{\text{trans}}$ — *Transmission delay* (time to push bits onto the medium):

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R}
$$

where $L$ is the SDU size in bits and $R$ is the link rate in bits/second.

- $T_{\text{prop}}$ — *Propagation delay* (time for a bit to travel the physical medium):

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v}
$$

where $d$ is the link length and $v$ is the propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fibre).

- $T_{\text{queue}}$ — *Queuing delay* (waiting time at intermediate routers).

**Step 2: Sum the three delays.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{v} + T_{\text{queue}}
$$

**Step 3: For a connection-oriented service with $N$ hops, sum the per-hop delays.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot \left( \frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{v} + T_{\text{queue}_i} \right) + T_{\text{ACK}}
$$

where $T_{\text{ACK}}$ is the round-trip acknowledgement delay for verified primitives.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product (Critical for Service Design)

The **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** tells you how many *bits* can be "in flight" on the medium at any instant — a critical design parameter when choosing between a *reliable* and *unreliable* service.

**Step 1: Define the variables.**

- $B$ — Bandwidth in bits/second.
- $\tau$ — One-way propagation delay in seconds.

**Step 2: Derive the BDP.**

If a sender pushes bits onto the medium continuously for $\tau$ seconds before the first bit arrives at the receiver, the number of bits "in the pipe" is:

$$
\text{BDP} = B \times \tau \quad \text{[bits]}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example (KTU Style).**

Suppose a service runs over a $R = 1 \text{ Gbps}$ link, and the one-way propagation is $\tau = 20 \text{ ms}$.

$$
\text{BDP} = (10^9) \times (20 \times 10^{-3}) = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ bits} = 25 \text{ MB}
$$

**Interpretation:** 25 MB of data are *unacknowledged* at any time. The sender's *window size* (for reliable service) **must** be at least 25 MB to fully utilize the pipe — a fact KTU examiners test in connection-oriented service questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Service Selection

The KTU board frequently asks: *"Which service would you use for X application?"*. The decision matrix below is your reference.

| Application | Required Latency | Required Reliability | Recommended Service Type | Why |
|-------------|------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|-----|
| **Live video streaming** | Low | Low–Medium | Unreliable Connectionless (UDP) | Retransmission of old frames is useless |
| **Email (SMTP)** | High | High | Reliable Connection-Oriented (TCP) | Every byte must arrive correctly |
| **DNS query** | Low | Medium | Request-Reply (UDP) | Tiny payload, retransmit on timeout |
| **Online gaming** | Very Low | Medium | Unreliable Connectionless (UDP) | Lag > packet loss for player experience |
| **Banking transaction** | Low | Very High | Reliable Connection-Oriented (TCP) | Zero tolerance for loss |
| **VoIP call** | Very Low | Low | Unreliable Connection-Oriented (ATM) | Real-time ordering matters; loss acceptable |
| **File download (HTTP)** | Medium | High | Reliable Connection-Oriented (TCP) | Full file integrity required |

> [!NOTE]
> 🎯 **KTU Board Pattern:** When a question says *"Justify your choice"*, you **must** reference at least **two** metrics from the table (latency + reliability, or jitter + bandwidth) in your answer. The examiner deducts marks for generic answers.

---

## 3.5 KTU-Mandated Numerical Example (Practice)

**Question:** A reliable connection-oriented service is to be set up between two hosts. The payload is **1200 bytes**, the Transport Layer header is **20 bytes**, the IP header is **20 bytes**, and the Ethernet frame header+trailer is **38 bytes**. Calculate (i) the *encapsulation efficiency*, and (ii) the *number of bits of overhead*.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate total bits on the wire.**

$$
\text{Total} = L + H_{\text{TCP}} + H_{\text{IP}} + H_{\text{Eth}} = 1200 + 20 + 20 + 38 = 1278 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to bits.**

$$
1278 \times 8 = 10224 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate efficiency $\eta$.**

$$
\eta = \frac{L}{\text{Total}} = \frac{1200}{1278} = 0.9390 = 93.9\%
$$

**Step 4: Calculate overhead bits.**

$$
H_{\text{total}} = 20 + 20 + 38 = 78 \text{ bytes} = 78 \times 8 = 624 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5: Verify.**

$$
\frac{624}{10224} = 0.0610 = 6.1\% \quad \text{(which equals } 100\% - 93.9\%\text{)} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 4.1 OSI Service Architecture — Vertical Layering with SAPs

This diagram represents the KTU-mandated service architecture: layers, SAPs, and the flow of primitives.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[LAYER 7 APPLICATION\nHTTP FTP DNS SMTP]:::user
    P[LAYER 6 PRESENTATION\nTLS JPEG MPEG]:::user

    N[LAYER 3 NETWORK\nIP - IP SAP]:::provider
    D[LAYER 2 DATA LINK\nEthernet - MAC SAP]:::provider
    PH[LAYER 1 PHYSICAL\nBits on wire]:::provider

    A -- SAP56 PRIMITIVES --> P
    P -- SAP57 PRIMITIVES --> S
    S -- SAP67 PRIMITIVES --> T
    T -- SAP78 PRIMITIVES --> N
    N -- SAP89 PRIMITIVES --> D
    D -- SAP90 PRIMITIVES --> PH

    A:::user
    P:::user
    S:::user
    T:::provider
    N:::provider

    classDef user fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

---

## 4.2 Four Service Primitives — Sequence Diagram for Connection Establishment

This is the **most important diagram for KTU exams** — the lifecycle of the four primitives during a `CONNECT` service.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant UserA as USER A (Layer N+1)
    participant SAP_A as SAP on HOST A
    participant LayerN_A as LAYER N (Host A)
    participant LayerN_B as LAYER N (Host B)
    participant SAP_B as SAP on HOST B
    participant UserB as USER B (Layer N+1)

    UserA->>SAP_A: CONNECT REQUEST
    SAP_A->>LayerN_A: Primitive Issued
    LayerN_A->>LayerN_B: Protocol PDU Call Request
    LayerN_A->>UserA: CONNECT CONFIRM (eventually)

    Note over LayerN_B,SAP_B: Connection arrives at remote

    LayerN_B->>SAP_B: Deliver Primitive
    SAP_B->>UserB: CONNECT INDICATION
    UserB->>SAP_B: CONNECT RESPONSE
    SAP_B->>LayerN_B: Primitive Issued
    LayerN_B->>LayerN_A: Protocol PDU Call Accept
    LayerN_A->>UserA: CONNECT CONFIRM

    Note over UserA,UserB: DATA TRANSFER PHASE BEGINS
    UserA->>SAP_A: DATA REQUEST
    SAP_A->>UserB: (via PDU) DATA INDICATION
    UserB->>SAP_B: DATA RESPONSE (if verified)
    SAP_B->>UserA: DATA CONFIRM
```

---

## 4.3 Connection-Oriented Service — State Machine (Full Lifecycle)

The KTU board expects a **state diagram** for connection-oriented services. Below is the exact state machine.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> CONNECT_REQUEST_SENT: CONNECT.request
    CONNECT_REQUEST_SENT --> CONNECT_INDICATION_RECEIVED: PDU arrives at remote
    CONNECT_INDICATION_RECEIVED --> CONNECT_RESPONSE_SENT: CONNECT.response
    CONNECT_RESPONSE_SENT --> ESTABLISHED: CONFIRM returned
    CONNECT_REQUEST_SENT --> ESTABLISHED: (also via CONFIRM)
    ESTABLISHED --> DATA_TRANSFER: DATA.request primitives
    DATA_TRANSFER --> ESTABLISHED: DATA.indication handled
    ESTABLISHED --> DISCONNECT_REQUEST_SENT: DISCONNECT.request
    DISCONNECT_REQUEST_SENT --> IDLE: PDU arrives, INDICATION given
    ESTABLISHED --> IDLE: DISCONNECT.indication received
    IDLE --> [*]

    note right of ESTABLISHED: Connection is now open
    note right of IDLE: No resources held
```

---

## 4.4 Connectionless Service — Stateless Datagram Flow

Connectionless services have **no states** — each PDU is independent.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SENDER - Layer N+1]:::sender
    B[LAYER N - Sender]:::sender
    C[NETWORK - Routers]:::network
    D[LAYER N - Receiver]:::receiver
    E[RECEIVER - Layer N+1]:::receiver

    A -- DATA REQUEST with full dest addr --> B
    B -- Encapsulated datagram PDU --> C
    C -- Hop by hop forwarding --> D
    D -- DATA INDICATION to user --> E

    A2[SENDER 2nd SDU]:::sender
    A2 -- Independent DATA REQUEST --> B
    B -. Independent datagram .-> C
    C -. Independent route .-> D
    D -. Independent DATA INDICATION .-> E

    classDef sender fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px
    classDef network fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#FBC02D,stroke-width:2px
    classDef receiver fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.5 Service vs Protocol — The Two Orthogonal Relationships

This diagram is critical to answering the KTU 14-marker *"Differentiate between service and protocol"*.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOST1[HOST 1]
        direction TB
        L4U1[Layer 4 - User]:::user
        L3P1[Layer 3 - Provider]:::prov
        L2P1[Layer 2 - Provider]:::prov
        L4U1 -- VERTICAL SERVICE via SAP --> L3P1
        L3P1 -- VERTICAL SERVICE via SAP --> L2P1
    end

    subgraph HOST2[HOST 2]
        direction TB
        L4U2[Layer 4 - User]:::user
        L3P2[Layer 3 - Provider]:::prov
        L2P2[Layer 2 - Provider]:::prov
        L4U2 -- VERTICAL SERVICE via SAP --> L3P2
        L3P2 -- VERTICAL SERVICE via SAP --> L2P2
    end

    L3P1 -. HORIZONTAL PROTOCOL .-> L3P2
    L2P1 -. HORIZONTAL PROTOCOL .-> L2P2

    classDef user fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
    classDef prov fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
```

**How to read this in a KTU answer:**
- The **blue arrows (vertical)** represent *services* between adjacent layers on the *same host*.
- The **dashed arrows (horizontal)** represent *protocols* between *peer layers* on *different hosts*.
- The Service Access Point (SAP) sits at the boundary where the blue arrows terminate.
