Mastering elementary TCP sockets is the **foundation** for advanced topics: select/poll/epoll, non-blocking I/O, SSL/TLS sockets, and zero-copy techniques (e.g., `sendfile()`, `splice()`).

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Walkthrough

### 3.1 The TCP Three-Way Handshake (From the Socket's View)

A TCP connection is established through a three-step exchange between client and server. The kernel performs this **transparently** when the user calls `connect()` and `accept()`. Each step involves a specific TCP flag in the segment header:

$$
\text{Step 1: Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN, seq}=x} \text{Server}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN+ACK, seq}=y,\ \text{ack}=x+1} \text{Client}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK, seq}=x+1,\ \text{ack}=y+1} \text{Server}
$$

**Explanatory narration of each row:**

- **Step 1 — The client's `connect()`** triggers the kernel to send a TCP segment with the **SYN** flag set and an **initial sequence number (ISN) = $x$**. The client enters the `SYN_SENT` state. No application data is included.
- **Step 2 — The server's kernel** receives the SYN at the listening socket (placed in the accept queue by `listen()`), replies with a segment that has both **SYN and ACK** flags set, choosing its own **ISN = $y$** and acknowledging the client's SYN by setting the acknowledgement field to $x + 1$. The server enters `SYN_RCVD`.
- **Step 3 — The client's kernel** sends a final **ACK** (acknowledging the server's SYN with $y + 1$). Upon its receipt, the server moves the connection to the `ESTABLISHED` state and `accept()` returns the new connected socket to the application.

$$
\text{Connection Established} \iff \text{Both sides are in } \texttt{ESTABLISHED} \text{ state}
$$

After this exchange, both processes may freely call `write()` / `read()` and the kernel handles segment transmission, retransmission, flow control, and congestion control automatically.

### 3.2 Deriving the Connection State Diagram (Conceptual)

The TCP state transitions for an elementary connection can be summarized as:

$$
\texttt{CLOSED} \xrightarrow{\text{connect()}} \texttt{SYN\_SENT} \xrightarrow{\text{+SYN+ACK}} \texttt{ESTABLISHED}
$$

$$
\texttt{LISTEN} \xrightarrow{\text{accept() blocks}} \xrightarrow{+\text{SYN}} \texttt{SYN\_RCVD} \xrightarrow{+\text{ACK}} \texttt{ESTABLISHED}
$$

Closing is symmetric (or asymmetric) using `close()` which sends a **FIN**:

$$
\texttt{ESTABLISHED} \xrightarrow{\text{close()}} \texttt{FIN\_WAIT\_1} \xrightarrow{+\text{ACK}} \texttt{FIN\_WAIT\_2} \xrightarrow{+\text{FIN}} \texttt{TIME\_WAIT} \xrightarrow{2 \times MSL} \texttt{CLOSED}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime)** is typically **60–120 seconds**. After close, the side that initiated termination remains in `TIME_WAIT` for $2 \times MSL$ to ensure all stray segments are discarded.

### 3.3 Throughput Derivation — How Long Does a Bulk Transfer Take?

For a file of size $R$ bytes sent over a TCP connection with bandwidth $C$ (bytes/sec) and round-trip time $RTT$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{propagation}} + T_{\text{ack wait}} \\
&= \frac{R}{C} + \frac{d}{v} + n \times RTT
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanatory narration:**

- $R / C$ is the **transmission delay** — time to push all bits onto the wire.
- $d / v$ is the **propagation delay** — time for the first bit to traverse the physical medium.
- $n \times RTT$ counts the number of round-trips needed; for a large file, the steady-state window-limited throughput is dominated by:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\max} = \frac{W}{RTT} \quad \text{where } W = \min(C_{\text{wnd}},\ C_{\text{cwnd}})
$$

This shows the deep coupling between socket buffer sizes (`SO_RCVBUF`, `SO_SNDBUF`) and end-to-end performance — a practical reason KTU questions test both API and underlying protocol.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Bulk Transfer Time

**Problem:** A 1 MB file ($R = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes) is sent over TCP. Bandwidth $C = 1$ Mbps $= 125{,}000$ bytes/s. RTT = 50 ms. Window is unlimited.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{transmit}} &= \frac{R}{C} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{125{,}000} \approx 8.39 \text{ s} \\
T_{\text{RTT}} &= 0.05 \text{ s} \\
T_{\text{total}} &\approx 8.39 + 0.05 = 8.44 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the window limits throughput to $W / RTT$ with $W = 64$ KB:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\max} = \frac{65{,}536}{0.05} = 1{,}310{,}720 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 10.5 \text{ Mbps}
$$

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TCP Server Lifecycle — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Server Process] --> B[socket AF_INET SOCK_STREAM 0]
    B --> C{Return value valid?}
    C -- No --> Z1[Print error and exit]
    C -- Yes --> D[bzero and servaddr]
    D --> E[servaddr.sin_family = AF_INET]
    E --> F[servaddr.sin_addr.s_addr = htonl INADDR_ANY]
    F --> G[servaddr.sin_port = htons SERV_PORT]
    G --> H[bind sockfd servaddr sizeof]
    H --> I{Bind success?}
    I -- No --> Z2[Print bind error and exit]
    I -- Yes --> J[listen sockfd LISTENQ]
    J --> K{Listen success?}
    K -- No --> Z3[Print listen error and exit]
    K -- Yes --> L[Infinite for loop begin]
    L --> M[clilen = sizeof cliaddr]
    M --> N[connfd = accept sockfd cliaddr clilen]
    N --> O{Accept success?}
    O -- No --> Z4[Print accept error and continue]
    O -- Yes --> P[fork child process]
    P --> Q{Parent or Child?}
    Q -- Parent --> R[close connfd and continue loop]
    Q -- Child --> S[close sockfd]
    S --> T[str_echo child reads and writes to connfd]
    T --> U[exit 0 after serving client]
```

### 4.2 TCP Client Lifecycle — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Client Process] --> B[socket AF_INET SOCK_STREAM 0]
    B --> C{Return value valid?}
    C -- No --> Z1[Print error and exit]
    C -- Yes --> D[bzero and servaddr]
    D --> E[servaddr.sin_family = AF_INET]
    E --> F[servaddr.sin_port = htons SERV_PORT]
    F --> G[inet_pton AF_INET argv 1 servaddr.sin_addr]
    G --> H{Conversion success?}
    H -- No --> Z2[Print inet_pton error and exit]
    H -- Yes --> I[connect sockfd servaddr sizeof]
    I --> J{Connect success?}
    J -- No --> Z3[Print connect error and exit]
    J -- Yes --> K[str_cli reads stdin writes sockfd and reads sockfd writes stdout]
    K --> L[exit 0]
```

### 4.3 TCP Three-Way Handshake — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client Process
    participant Kc as Client Kernel TCP
    participant Ks as Server Kernel TCP
    participant S as Server Process
    C->>Kc: connect sockfd addr addrlen
    Kc->>Ks: SYN seq equals x
    Ks-->>Kc: SYN plus ACK seq equals y ack equals x plus 1
    Kc-->>Ks: ACK seq equals x plus 1 ack equals y plus 1
    Note over Kc,Ks: Connection now ESTABLISHED
    Kc-->>C: connect returns 0
    Ks-->>S: accept returns new connfd
    C->>Kc: write connfd buf n
    S->>Ks: read connfd buf n
    S->>Ks: write connfd buf n
    Ks-->>Kc: data segment
    Kc-->>C: read returns n
    C->>Kc: close sockfd
    C->>Ks: FIN
    Ks-->>C: ACK
    Ks->>S: read returns 0 EOF
    S->>Ks: close connfd
    Ks-->>C: FIN
    C-->>Ks: ACK and enter TIME_WAIT for 2 MSL
```

### 4.4 Elementary TCP Echo Server — Annotated Code Skeleton

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/socket.h>
#include <netinet/in.h>
#include <arpa/inet.h>

#define SERV_PORT   9877
#define LISTENQ     1024
#define MAXLINE     4096

void str_echo(int sockfd) {
    ssize_t n;
    char    buf[MAXLINE];
    for (;;) {
        if ((n = read(sockfd, buf, MAXLINE)) == 0)
            return;            // connection closed by peer
        writen(sockfd, buf, n); // echo back
    }
}

int main(int argc, char **argv) {
    int                 listenfd, connfd;
    pid_t               childpid;
    socklen_t           clilen;
    struct sockaddr_in  cliaddr, servaddr;

    listenfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);

    bzero(&servaddr, sizeof(servaddr));
    servaddr.sin_family      = AF_INET;
    servaddr.sin_addr.s_addr = htonl(INADDR_ANY);
    servaddr.sin_port        = htons(SERV_PORT);

    bind(listenfd, (struct sockaddr *) &servaddr, sizeof(servaddr));
    listen(listenfd, LISTENQ);

    for ( ; ; ) {
        clilen = sizeof(cliaddr);
        connfd = accept(listenfd, (struct sockaddr *) &cliaddr, &clilen);
        if ((childpid = fork()) == 0) {   /* child process */
            close(listenfd);
            str_echo(connfd);
            exit(0);
        }
        close(connfd);                     /* parent closes connected socket */
    }
}
```

### 4.5 Elementary TCP Echo Client — Annotated Code Skeleton

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/socket.h>
#include <netinet/in.h>
#include <arpa/inet.h>

#define SERV_PORT   9877
#define MAXLINE     4096

void str_cli(FILE *fp, int sockfd) {
    char    buf[MAXLINE];
    while (fgets(buf, MAXLINE, fp) != NULL) {
        writen(sockfd, buf, strlen(buf));
        readline(sockfd, buf, MAXLINE);
        fputs(buf, stdout);
    }
}

int main(int argc, char **argv) {
    int                 sockfd;
    struct sockaddr_in  servaddr;

    if (argc != 2) { fprintf(stderr, "usage: tcpcli <IPaddress>\n"); exit(1); }

    sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);

    bzero(&servaddr, sizeof(servaddr));
    servaddr.sin_family = AF_INET;
    servaddr.sin_port   = htons(SERV_PORT);
    inet_pton(AF_INET, argv[1], &servaddr.sin_addr);

    connect(sockfd, (struct sockaddr *) &servaddr, sizeof(servaddr));
    str_cli(stdin, sockfd);
    exit(0);
}
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between `listen()` and `connect()` in TCP socket programming. Who calls each function and what is the role of the `backlog` argument?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `listen()` | `connect()` |
|---|---|---|
| Calling process | **Server** | **Client** |
| Purpose | Marks a socket as **passive** — ready to accept incoming connection requests | Actively **initiates** a three-way handshake to a remote server |
| Side effect | Allocates kernel resources (two queues: incomplete & completed) | Triggers `SYN` segment transmission |
| Return | `0` on success | `0` on success |
| Role of `backlog` | Defines the **maximum length** of the queue of *completed* connections waiting to be `accept()`-ed | Not applicable |

The `backlog` argument is a **hint** to the kernel of how many pending connections may be queued. Modern kernels treat it as the maximum number of *incomplete* (SYN_RCVD) **plus** *completed* connections. The recommended value is `5` for simple servers and `128`–`1024` for high-traffic servers.

*(1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for roles, 1 mark for backlog explanation)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of `INADDR_ANY` in `bind()`? Why is it useful for a server and what happens if it is replaced with a specific IP?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`INADDR_ANY` is a symbolic constant (value `0.0.0.0`) that tells the kernel to **bind the socket to *all* available network interfaces** of the host.

- **Significance for server:** A multi-homed server (with multiple NICs/IPs) will accept client connections arriving on **any** of its interfaces. This is the standard, recommended choice for general-purpose servers.
- **Replacement with specific IP:** If replaced with a specific IP (e.g., `192.168.1.10`), the server will **only** accept connections destined to that specific interface. Connections arriving on the other interfaces are silently dropped by the kernel.
- **Caveat:** Always wrap with `htonl(INADDR_ANY)` because the field expects **network byte order**.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for server utility, 1 mark for specific-IP implication)*

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TCP client–server communication model** using elementary socket functions. List the sequence of system calls executed on both the server and client sides. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **TCP three-way handshake** mechanism. Show the segment exchange, sequence-number progression, and state transitions on both sides. Why is a three-step exchange necessary, instead of just two? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q A (a)**

**1. The TCP Client–Server Model**

In a TCP client–server model, the **server** listens for connections on a well-known port; the **client** initiates a connection to that port. The elementary flow is:

**Server-side sequence of system calls:**

1. `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)` — Create an endpoint.
2. `bind(sockfd, (struct sockaddr*)&servaddr, sizeof)` — Bind to IP + port.
3. `listen(sockfd, backlog)` — Mark as passive listener.
4. `accept(sockfd, ...)` — **Block** until a client connects; return a new `connfd`.
5. `read()/write()` on `connfd` — Exchange data with client.
6. `close(connfd)` — Terminate the connection.

**Client-side sequence of system calls:**

1. `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)` — Create an endpoint.
2. `connect(sockfd, (struct sockaddr*)&servaddr, sizeof)` — Initiate 3-way handshake.
3. `read()/write()` on `sockfd` — Exchange data with server.
4. `close(sockfd)` — Terminate the connection.

**Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1 and 4.2 above):** The server creates a **listening socket** bound to a port. For each arriving client, `accept()` returns a **new connected socket** — the original listening socket is kept open to accept *more* clients. This is why concurrent servers usually call `fork()` (or `pthread_create()`) to handle each client in a child process/thread while the parent loops back to `accept()`.

**Key points:**
- The server's *listening socket* and the *connected socket* are **different file descriptors**.
- The client uses a single socket for both connecting and communicating.
- After `accept()`, communication is **symmetric** — both sides use `read`/`write`.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q A (b)**

**2. The Three-Way Handshake**

The three-way handshake is the procedure used to establish a TCP connection. It synchronizes sequence numbers and establishes both sides' parameters.

**Segment exchange:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. Client} \rightarrow \text{Server:} & \quad \text{SYN, SeqNo} = x \\
\text{2. Server} \rightarrow \text{Client:} & \quad \text{SYN+ACK, SeqNo} = y,\ \text{AckNo} = x+1 \\
\text{3. Client} \rightarrow \text{Server:} & \quad \text{ACK, SeqNo} = x+1,\ \text{AckNo} = y+1
\end{aligned}
$$

**State transitions:**

- **Client:** `CLOSED → SYN_SENT → ESTABLISHED`
- **Server:** `LISTEN → SYN_RCVD → ESTABLISHED`

**Sequence-number progression:**
- The client picks an Initial Sequence Number (ISN) $x$.
- The server picks its own ISN $y$.
- The client's third ACK consumes one sequence number (since SYN counts as 1 byte), so it uses `SeqNo = x + 1` and `AckNo = y + 1`.

**Why three steps and not two?**

1. **Two-way exchange is insufficient to establish a *full-duplex* connection.** A single SYN/ACK pair would synchronize only one direction's sequence number. The third ACK is needed to *confirm* that the client has received the server's SYN, completing synchronization in the reverse direction.
2. **Prevents old duplicate connection requests** from being accepted — the third ACK ties the client's final confirmation to *this* handshake instance, not a stale one.
3. **Ensures both sides are ready** to send and receive data, i.e., both have allocated buffers and agreed on initial sequence numbers.

**Additional annotation (KTU favourite):**
The third segment **may carry data** (a "piggybacked" request), which is why the client's first data byte has sequence number $x + 1$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of `struct sockaddr_in` and `struct in_addr`. Why is `htons()` applied to the port field? With code snippets, show how an IPv4 server address is set up for `bind()`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **TCP** and **UDP** sockets with respect to the following: connection establishment, reliability, byte order, function call sequence, typical use cases, and header overhead. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q B (a)**

**1. Socket Address Structures**

```c
struct sockaddr_in {
    sa_family_t     sin_family;   /* AF_INET                       */
    in_port_t       sin_port;     /* Port in NETWORK byte order    */
    struct in_addr  sin_addr;     /* 32-bit IPv4 in NETWORK order  */
    char            sin_zero[8];  /* Padding                       */
};

struct in_addr {
    in_addr_t  s_addr;            /* Unsigned 32-bit integer       */
};
```

**Why `htons()` on the port field?**
TCP/IP uses **Network Byte Order** (big-endian) for all multi-byte integer fields in protocol headers. Most general-purpose computers (x86/x86_64) use **little-endian**. The `htons()` function converts a 16-bit value from host byte order to network byte order. If we skip this conversion on a little-endian machine, the kernel will interpret port 9877 as a different number (e.g., `0x9226` in big-endian vs `0x2692` in little-endian), and clients will fail to connect.

**Code snippet — setting up a server address:**

```c
bzero(&servaddr, sizeof(servaddr));
servaddr.sin_family      = AF_INET;
servaddr.sin_addr.s_addr = htonl(INADDR_ANY);   /* any local interface */
servaddr.sin_port        = htons(9877);          /* well-known port     */

bind(listenfd, (struct sockaddr *) &servaddr, sizeof(servaddr));
```

**Key points:**
- `bzero()` / `memset()` zeros the structure to remove garbage in `sin_zero`.
- `INADDR_ANY` is `0.0.0.0` — wildcard binding.
- The cast `(struct sockaddr *)` is required because `bind()` accepts a generic `sockaddr*`; the kernel uses `sa_family` to interpret the rest.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q B (b)**

**2. TCP vs UDP Socket Comparison**

| Feature | **TCP Sockets** | **UDP Sockets** |
|---|---|---|
| Connection establishment | Required — three-way handshake (`connect()`) | Not required — `sendto()` works without it |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery, in-order, no duplicates | Best-effort — packets may be lost, duplicated, or reordered |
| Byte order | All integers in **Network Byte Order** (`htons`, `htonl`) | Same requirement |
| Function call sequence (server) | `socket → bind → listen → accept → read/write` | `socket → bind → recvfrom/sendto` |
| Function call sequence (client) | `socket → connect → read/write` | `socket → sendto/recvfrom` |
| Header overhead | **20 bytes** (TCP) + 20 bytes (IP) = 40 bytes | **8 bytes** (UDP) + 20 bytes (IP) = 28 bytes |
| Typical use cases | Web (HTTP), email (SMTP), SSH, file transfer (FTP) | DNS, video streaming, VoIP, online gaming, TFTP |
| Flow & congestion control | Yes (Sliding window, slow start, AIMD) | No |
| Socket type argument | `SOCK_STREAM` | `SOCK_DGRAM` |
| State at server | Listening + per-client connected sockets | Single socket, all clients share it |
| `listen()` / `accept()` | Required | **Not used** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Don't write "TCP is fast, UDP is slow."** That is *not* a valid distinction. UDP is *faster* in some sense, but TCP is reliable. Phrase it precisely: *TCP provides reliable, ordered, connection-oriented service; UDP provides best-effort, connectionless service.*
> - **Don't forget `htons()` in code.** Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for missing byte-order conversion.
> - **Don't say "UDP has no header."** UDP has an 8-byte header (length, checksum, port pairs).
> - **Don't claim `bind()` is unnecessary for UDP servers** if they want to receive on a known port — it is required.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Bulletins — Read Before Every KTU Exam:**

- ✅ A **socket** is identified by the **5-tuple** (protocol, local IP, local port, remote IP, remote port).
- ✅ `socket()` returns a **file descriptor** — the handle for all subsequent operations.
- ✅ Server order: `socket → bind → listen → accept → read/write → close`. Client order: `socket → connect → read/write → close`.
- ✅ `listen()` is called **only on the server**; it converts an active socket into a passive one.
- ✅ `accept()` is a **blocking call**; it returns a **brand-new socket descriptor** for the connection.
- ✅ `htons()` — 16-bit (port); `htonl()` — 32-bit (IP). **Always** apply before `bind()` or `connect()`.
- ✅ `INADDR_ANY` (i.e., `0.0.0.0`) means *accept on all local interfaces*.
- ✅ `struct sockaddr_in` must be **zero-initialized**; the cast to `struct sockaddr *` is mandatory for the API.
- ✅ TCP three-way handshake: **SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK**. Both sides transition to `ESTABLISHED`.
- ✅ After `close()`, the side that sent FIN first lingers in `TIME_WAIT` for **2 × MSL**.
- ✅ TCP socket type: `SOCK_STREAM`; UDP socket type: `SOCK_DGRAM`. Both use `AF_INET` for IPv4.
- ✅ Concurrency: the server typically calls `fork()` after `accept()`; parent closes `connfd`, child closes `listenfd`.
- ✅ `read()` returning **0** means **EOF** (peer closed). Returning **−1** means **error**.
- ✅ The maximum TCP payload per segment is $MSS = MTU - 40$ (typical $MTU = 1500 \Rightarrow MSS = 1460$).
- ✅ Functions like `send()`/`recv()` are equivalent to `write()`/`read()` but with an extra `flags` argument.
- ✅ Always include `<sys/socket.h>`, `<netinet/in.h>`, `<arpa/inet.h>`, `<unistd.h>`.
- ✅ Compile with: `gcc server.c -o server` (no special libraries on Linux/glibc).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mnemonic (KTU 2024 Examiner's Favourite):**
> **"S-B-L-A-C"** = **S**ocket, **B**ind, **L**isten, **A**ccept, **C**onnect/Communicate (read-write loop), then **C**lose.
> Memorize this — it has appeared in 4 of the last 6 KTU End-Semester papers in some form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TCP Client/Server Example
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** TCP Client/Server Example ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **TCP Client/Server Example** is a canonical programmatic demonstration of the **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** session management using the **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)**, typically implemented through the **Berkeley Sockets API** (or its Java equivalent: `java.net.Socket` and `java.net.ServerSocket`). It illustrates the complete lifecycle of a connection-oriented communication session: socket creation, the **three-way handshake**, data exchange via byte streams, and the **graceful four-way termination**. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCCST501 – Module 2: Application Layer)**, this example is based on the classical **Kurose & Ross "Uppercase Conversion Application"** and is essential for understanding how application-layer processes map to the **TCP/IP protocol suite**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Definitions (KTU Board Standard):** > - **Socket**: The endpoint of a two-way communication link between two programs running on a network. It is defined by an IP address concatenated with a port number, written as `(IP:Port)`. > - **ServerSocket**: A special type of socket used only by servers to listen for incoming TCP connection requests on a specified port. > - **Port Number**: A 16-bit integer (range **0 to 65535**) used to identify a specific process-to-process communication. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **telephone call system** in a large office: - The **Server** is like a receptionist who sits at a dedicated desk (port `6789`) waiting for calls. - A **Client** picks up the phone, dials the receptionist's desk number, and a dedicated line gets established (the **3-way handshake**). - The client speaks (sends bytes), the receptionist responds (echoes uppercased data), and when done, both hang up politely (the **4-way FIN termination**). - The *receptionist's desk* is the `ServerSocket` (always open), and *each active call* is a separate `Socket` object. ### 1.3 The Application Context (Uppercase Server) In the canonical Kurose-Ross example, the application protocol is trivial: 1. **Client** reads a line of text from its keyboard and sends it to the server. 2. **Server** reads the line from its socket. 3. **Server** converts the line to **UPPERCASE**. 4. **Server** sends the modified line back to the client. 5. The client reads the response and prints it on its monitor. > [!NOTE] > This application uses TCP because the services require: > - **Reliable data transfer** (no lost characters) > - **In-order delivery** (echo must be correct) > - **Connection-oriented service** (handshake before data) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a TCP Socket (Logical Structure) A TCP socket is not a physical object but a **software abstraction** maintained by the OS kernel. It is uniquely identified by a **4-tuple** for the connection: $$ \text{Connection ID} = (\text{Source IP}, \text{Source Port}, \text{Destination IP}, \text{Destination Port}) $$ The client-side socket and server-side socket together form a single **TCP Connection** (also called an **Association**). ### 2.2 Operational Steps — Client Side The client performs the following sequence of operations: 1. **Create a `Socket` object** — This implicitly triggers the OS to assign an **ephemeral port number** (typically in the range `49152–65535`) and to initiate the **TCP three-way handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK). 2. **Obtain Input/Output Streams** — `getInputStream()` and `getOutputStream()` retrieve the byte-stream interfaces to the socket buffer. 3. **Wrap Streams** — To read text, a `BufferedReader` is wrapped around an `InputStreamReader` around the `InputStream`. Similarly, a `PrintWriter` (or `DataOutputStream`) is wrapped around the `OutputStream`. 4. **Send / Receive Data** — Bytes flow through the socket's **send buffer (SO_SNDBUF)** and **receive buffer (SO_RCVBUF)**. The default size is typically **8 KB to 64 KB**. 5. **Close the Socket** — This triggers the **4-way termination** (FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK). ### 2.3 Operational Steps — Server Side The server performs a slightly different sequence: 1. **Create a `ServerSocket` object** bound to a **well-known port** (e.g., `6789`). This opens a **listening socket** that does not yet have an established connection. 2. **Call `accept()`** — This is a **blocking system call**. The server process suspends until a client initiates a connection. When a client connects, `accept()` returns a **new `Socket` object** representing the established connection. 3. **Process the Client** — Read from the socket, perform computation (uppercase), write back the result. 4. **Loop back to Step 2** — The original listening `ServerSocket` continues to wait for more clients, while the new socket handles the current client. This allows **concurrent client handling** (in multi-threaded servers). ### 2.4 Stream Wrapping Pattern (Java) The Java I/O system uses the **Decorator Design Pattern**. Each wrapper adds functionality: | Wrapper Class | Purpose | Adds | |---------------|---------|------| | `Socket.getOutputStream()` | Raw byte channel | Basic I/O | | `DataOutputStream` | Primitive type support | `writeUTF()`, `writeInt()` | | `PrintWriter` | Text formatting | `println()` auto-flush | | `BufferedReader` | Buffered character input | `readLine()` for text | | `InputStreamReader` | Byte-to-char bridge | UTF-8 decoding | > [!NOTE] > **Why wrap streams?** Raw byte streams cannot read text lines directly. The wrapping converts bytes → characters → buffered text lines, which is essential for the application protocol. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---------|----------------|---------------| | Port Number Range | $0 \le P \le 65535$ | **2^16** values | | Well-Known Ports | $0 \le P \le 1023$ | Reserved for **IANA** services | | Registered Ports | $1024 \le P \le 49151$ | For user applications | | Ephemeral Ports | $49152 \le P \le 65535$ | Auto-assigned by OS | | TCP Header (min) | 20 bytes | + options (up to 60 bytes) | | 4-Tuple Identifier | $(\text{SrcIP}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstIP}, \text{DstPort})$ | Uniquely identifies a TCP connection | | Default Buffer Size | $\vert \text{SO\_RCVBUF} \vert = 8\text{ KB}$ to $64\text{ KB}$ | OS-dependent | | Throughput Cap | $B \approx \frac{\text{Window Size}}{\text{RTT}}$ | **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** | | MSS | $MSS = MTU - 40$ bytes | IPv4: $1460$; IPv6: $1440$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The well-known port for HTTP is **80**, HTTPS is **443**, FTP-data is **20**, FTP-control is **21**, Telnet is **23**, and SMTP is **25**. These are frequently asked! ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility TCP client-server programming is the foundation of: - **Web servers** (Apache, Nginx) — HTTP over TCP - **Database systems** (MySQL on port 3306, PostgreSQL on 5432) - **REST APIs** (Spring Boot, Node.js, Django) - **Chat applications** (WhatsApp backend, Slack) - **IoT device management** (MQTT over TCP) - **Microservices** communication (gRPC) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Java Code: TCP Client (Exhaustive Annotated Listing) ```java import java.io.*; import java.net.*; public class TCPClient { public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException { // STEP 1: Get arguments (server hostname and port) // args[0] = "localhost" or an IP like "192.168.1.5" // args[1] = port number, e.g., 6789 String serverHost = args[0]; int serverPort = Integer.parseInt(args[1]); // STEP 2: Create client socket and connect to server // This single line performs: // (a) DNS resolution of serverHost → IP address // (b) Local OS assigns an ephemeral source port // (c) TCP three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) is executed Socket clientSocket = new Socket(serverHost, serverPort); System.out.println("Connected to server!"); // STEP 3: Create output stream to SEND data to server // DataOutputStream allows writing Java primitives + UTF strings DataOutputStream outToServer = new DataOutputStream(clientSocket.getOutputStream()); // STEP 4: Create input stream to RECEIVE data from server // BufferedReader wraps an InputStreamReader to enable readLine() BufferedReader inFromServer = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(clientSocket.getInputStream())); // STEP 5: Create input stream to READ from user's keyboard // System.in is the standard keyboard byte stream BufferedReader inFromUser = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in)); // STEP 6: Main communication loop // User types line → sent to server → server replies → displayed String sentence; String modifiedSentence; while ((sentence = inFromUser.readLine()) != null) { // 6a. Send the user's line to the server (append newline) outToServer.writeBytes(sentence + '\n'); // 6b. Read the server's response (until newline) modifiedSentence = inFromServer.readLine(); // 6c. Print the uppercase result System.out.println("FROM SERVER: " + modifiedSentence); } // STEP 7: Close the socket (triggers TCP 4-way termination) clientSocket.close(); } } ``` ### 3.2 Java Code: TCP Server (Exhaustive Annotated Listing) ```java import java.io.*; import java.net.*; public class TCPServer { public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException { // STEP 1: Get the well-known port from arguments int welcomePort = Integer.parseInt(args[0]); // e.g., 6789 // STEP 2: Create the WELCOME socket (listening socket) // ServerSocket is bound to the well-known port and starts listening ServerSocket welcomeSocket = new ServerSocket(welcomePort); System.out.println("Server listening on port " + welcomePort + "..."); // STEP 3: BLOCKING wait for an incoming client connection // When a client connects, accept() returns a NEW Socket object // This new socket is dedicated to this specific client Socket connectionSocket = welcomeSocket.accept(); System.out.println("New client connected: " + connectionSocket.getInetAddress()); // STEP 4: Create input stream to READ from client BufferedReader inFromClient = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(connectionSocket.getInputStream())); // STEP 5: Create output stream to SEND to client DataOutputStream outToClient = new DataOutputStream(connectionSocket.getOutputStream()); // STEP 6: Main processing loop String clientSentence; String capitalizedSentence; while ((clientSentence = inFromClient.readLine()) != null) { // 6a. Apply the application logic: UPPERCASE conversion capitalizedSentence = clientSentence.toUpperCase() + '\n'; // 6b. Send the modified sentence back to the client outToClient.writeBytes(capitalizedSentence); } // STEP 7: Close the per-client socket // The welcomeSocket remains open for future clients connectionSocket.close(); // STEP 8: (In production) Close welcomeSocket on shutdown // welcomeSocket.close(); } } ``` ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Connection Lifecycle | Step | Client Action | Server Action | TCP State Transition | Network Packet | |------|---------------|---------------|----------------------|----------------| | 1 | `new Socket(host, port)` | — | CLOSED → SYN_SENT | **SYN** (seq = x) | | 2 | — | `ServerSocket(port)` + `accept()` | LISTEN | (none yet) | | 3 | Receive SYN-ACK | Send SYN-ACK | SYN_RCVD → ESTABLISHED | **SYN-ACK** (seq = y, ack = x+1) | | 4 | Send ACK | Receive ACK | ESTABLISHED | **ACK** (ack = y+1) | | 5 | `writeBytes(sentence)` | — | ESTABLISHED | **PSH-ACK** (data) | | 6 | — | `readLine()` receives | ESTABLISHED | **ACK** | | 7 | `readLine()` receives | `writeBytes(upper)` | ESTABLISHED | **PSH-ACK** (data) | | 8 | `close()` | `close()` | FIN_WAIT_1 → FIN_WAIT_2 → TIME_WAIT | **FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK** | ### 3.4 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product for Stream Buffer Sizing To avoid the **silly window syndrome** and ensure pipelining, the socket buffer must be at least equal to the **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)**: $$ \text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT} $$ $$ \text{Minimum Buffer Size} = \text{BDP} = B \times t_{\text{RTT}} $$ **Example (KTU-style numerical):** If a client-server link has bandwidth $B = 100 \text{ Mbps}$ and round-trip time $t_{\text{RTT}} = 50 \text{ ms}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{BDP} &= 100 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s} \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\[4pt] &= 5 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} \\[4pt] &= \frac{5 \times 10^6}{8} \text{ bytes} \\[4pt] &= 625{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt] &\approx 610.35 \text{ KB} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the `SO_RCVBUF` and `SO_SNDBUF` should be set to at least **625 KB** to fully utilize the link. The default **8 KB** buffer would severely under-utilize this link (only ~1.3% utilization). ### 3.5 Concurrency Analysis: Single vs. Multi-Threaded Server A naive single-threaded server can only handle **one client at a time**. If we let $N$ be the number of clients, $\mu$ the service rate per client, and $\lambda$ the arrival rate, the system behaves as an **M/M/1 queue**: $$ \rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} $$ For a multi-threaded server with $c$ threads, it becomes **M/M/c**, and the utilization per thread is: $$ \rho_c = \frac{\lambda}{c \cdot \mu} $$ The **mean response time** for M/M/c (Erlang C formula, simplified) is: $$ T \approx \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda/c} \quad \text{(per thread utilization model)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse `Socket` (used by both client and server, after `accept()`) with `ServerSocket` (used only by server, for listening). Examiners test this distinction. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System Architecture: Client-Server Interaction Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLIENT["CLIENT PROCESS (java.net.Socket)"] C1["User types: 'hello'"] --> C2["BufferedReader
readLine()"] C2 --> C3["DataOutputStream
writeBytes()"] C3 --> C4["Socket
(local IP : ephemeral port)"] C4 --> C5["OS TCP Stack
Send Buffer"] end subgraph NETWORK["TRANSPORT NETWORK (TCP/IP)"] N1["Three-Way Handshake
SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK"] --> N2["DATA Transfer
PSH-ACK segments"] N2 --> N3["Four-Way Termination
FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK"] end subgraph SERVER["SERVER PROCESS (java.net.ServerSocket)"] S1["ServerSocket
(server IP : port 6789)"] S1 --> S2["accept()
(blocking call)"] S2 --> S3["New Socket
(per client)"] S3 --> S4["BufferedReader
readLine()"] S4 --> S5["toUpperCase()
application logic"] S5 --> S6["DataOutputStream
writeBytes()"] S6 --> S7["OS TCP Stack
Send Buffer"] end C5 -- "Packet to 6789" --> N1 N3 --> S7 S1 -. "Listening
on port 6789" .-> N1 ``` ### 4.2 Stream Decoration Hierarchy (Java I/O Decorator Pattern) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Application: readLine() / writeBytes()"] A --> B["BufferedReader
(buffering + readLine)"] A --> BB["PrintWriter / DataOutputStream
(primitive support)"] B --> C["InputStreamReader
(byte → char bridge)"] BB --> D["Socket.getOutputStream()
(raw byte channel)"] C --> D2["Socket.getInputStream()
(raw byte channel)"] D --> E["OS Socket Buffer
(kernel space)"] D2 --> E E --> F["Network Interface Card
(physical transmission)"] ``` ### 4.3 TCP State Machine (Client + Server Combined View) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> CLOSED CLOSED --> LISTEN : Server: new ServerSocket(port) CLOSED --> SYN_SENT : Client: new Socket(host, port) LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD : Receive SYN SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED : Receive SYN-ACK, send ACK SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED : Receive ACK ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1 : Client: close() sends FIN ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT : Server: receives FIN, sends ACK FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2 : Receive ACK from server CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK : Server: close() sends FIN FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT : Receive FIN from server, send ACK LAST_ACK --> CLOSED : Receive final ACK TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED : Wait 2*MSL timeout (typically 60s) ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Why Two Sockets on the Server? ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SERVERPROCESS["SERVER MACHINE (Single Java Process)"] WSS["ServerSocket Object
WELCOME SOCKET
Bound to Port 6789
State: LISTENING"] WSS -->|accept() returns
when client connects| CS1["Socket Object #1
Per-Client Connection A
State: ESTABLISHED"] WSS -->|accept() returns
for next client| CS2["Socket Object #2
Per-Client Connection B
State: ESTABLISHED"] WSS -->|accept() returns
for next client| CS3["Socket Object #N
Per-Client Connection N
State: ESTABLISHED"] CS1 --> A1["Thread T1
handles Client A"] CS2 --> A2["Thread T2
handles Client B"] CS3 --> A3["Thread TN
handles Client N"] end EXT1["Client A"] -.->|TCP connection| CS1 EXT2["Client B"] -.->|TCP connection| CS2 EXT3["Client N"] -.->|TCP connection| CS3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why two sockets?** The `ServerSocket` is a *passive* listening endpoint that never participates in data transfer. Each successful `accept()` creates a *new*, *active* `Socket` for one specific client. This separation allows the server to multiplex many clients on a single well-known port. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Differentiate between `Socket` and `ServerSocket` classes in Java. Which one is used by the client and which by the server?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | `Socket` | `ServerSocket` | |---------|----------|----------------| | Purpose | Represents an **established connection** endpoint | Represents a **listening endpoint** waiting for connections | | Used By | **Both** client and server (after `accept()`) | **Only** the server | | Key Method | `getInputStream()`, `getOutputStream()` | `accept()` (blocking) | | Construction | `new Socket(host, port)` — initiates connection | `new ServerSocket(port)` — opens listening port | | State | ESTABLISHED | LISTEN | - The **client** uses `Socket` to actively connect to a server. - The **server** uses `ServerSocket` to listen, then `accept()` returns a new `Socket` for the established connection. **(3 marks)** ✓ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Explain why TCP is called a "connection-oriented" protocol. What role does the three-way handshake play in TCP client-server communication?** **Model Answer:** TCP is called **connection-oriented** because, before any application data is exchanged, the two communicating processes must first **establish a virtual circuit** through a handshake procedure. This is in contrast to UDP, which sends data without prior coordination. The **three-way handshake** involves: 1. **SYN**: Client sends a SYN (synchronize) segment with an initial sequence number $x$. 2. **SYN-ACK**: Server responds with SYN-ACK, acknowledging $x+1$ and providing its own sequence $y$. 3. **ACK**: Client acknowledges $y+1$, completing connection establishment. The role of the handshake is to: - **Synchronize** initial sequence numbers (ISN) on both sides. - **Negotiate** TCP options (MSS, window scaling). - **Reserve** resources (buffers, state variables) in both OS kernels. - **Confirm** bidirectional reachability. **(3 marks)** ✓ --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TCP client-server socket programming model** using Java. Describe the role of `Socket`, `ServerSocket`, `BufferedReader`, and `DataOutputStream` in the model. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A TCP client sends the string `"computer networks"` to an uppercase-conversion server over a link with $B = 1 \text{ Gbps}$ and RTT = $20 \text{ ms}$. Calculate the **bandwidth-delay product** and determine the **minimum socket buffer size** required for full link utilization. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) TCP Client-Server Socket Programming Model** (7 marks) The TCP client-server model uses two Java classes from `java.net` package: 1. **`ServerSocket` (Server side)**: Created with `new ServerSocket(port)`. It binds to a well-known port and **listens** for incoming TCP connection requests. When a client connects, the blocking `accept()` method returns a new `Socket` object representing the established connection. 2. **`Socket` (Client side)**: Created with `new Socket(host, port)`. The constructor **performs DNS resolution** and **initiates the 3-way handshake**. Once connected, it provides streams for I/O. 3. **`BufferedReader`**: Wraps the raw `InputStream` to provide **buffered character-based reading**, enabling the convenient `readLine()` method for text-line protocols. 4. **`DataOutputStream`**: Wraps the raw `OutputStream` to provide methods like `writeBytes()` and `writeUTF()` for writing Java primitives and strings. **Diagram** (refer to SECTION 4.1 above for the full flowchart). **Flow of execution**: - Server creates `ServerSocket`, calls `accept()` (blocks). - Client creates `Socket(host, port)` (triggers handshake). - Both sides obtain I/O streams and exchange data. - Both call `close()` (triggers 4-way termination). **(b) BDP Calculation** (7 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} B &= 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 \text{ bits/s} \\ t_{\text{RTT}} &= 20 \text{ ms} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\ \text{BDP} &= B \times t_{\text{RTT}} \\ &= 10^9 \text{ bits/s} \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\ &= 20 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} \\ &= \frac{20 \times 10^6}{8} \text{ bytes} \\ &= 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 2.5 \text{ MB} \end{aligned} $$ **Minimum socket buffer size = 2.5 MB = 2,500,000 bytes (approx 2,441 KB)** This means both `SO_RCVBUF` and `SO_SNDBUF` should be at least **2.5 MB** to keep the pipe full. The default 8 KB buffer would only allow ~0.3% link utilization, creating a severe bottleneck. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice – KTU 2024 Pattern] **(a)** Describe in detail the **complete lifecycle of a TCP connection** between a client and a server, including the **3-way handshake** and **4-way termination**. Use state diagrams to support your answer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With reference to the Java TCP client-server example, explain the **stream wrapping pattern** (Decorator pattern). Why can't we directly use `socket.getInputStream().readLine()` to read a line of text? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) TCP Connection Lifecycle** (7 marks) The lifecycle consists of three phases: **Phase 1: Connection Establishment (3-Way Handshake)** - **Step 1**: Client sends **SYN** with initial sequence number $x$. Client enters `SYN_SENT` state. - **Step 2**: Server responds with **SYN-ACK** (seq=$y$, ack=$x+1$). Server enters `SYN_RCVD` state. - **Step 3**: Client sends **ACK** (ack=$y+1$). Both sides enter `ESTABLISHED` state. **Phase 2: Data Transfer** - Application data is broken into **TCP segments** (max size = MSS, typically 1460 bytes for Ethernet). - Each segment carries a **sequence number** for ordering and an **acknowledgment number** to confirm receipt. - The **sliding window protocol** allows pipelining. **Phase 3: Connection Termination (4-Way Handshake)** - **Step 1**: Client sends **FIN**. Client enters `FIN_WAIT_1` state. - **Step 2**: Server sends **ACK**. Server enters `CLOSE_WAIT` state; client enters `FIN_WAIT_2`. - **Step 3**: Server sends **FIN**. Server enters `LAST_ACK` state. - **Step 4**: Client sends **ACK**. Client enters `TIME_WAIT` for `2 × MSL` (typically 60s) to handle delayed segments, then `CLOSED`. **State Diagram** (refer to SECTION 4.3 above). **(b) Stream Wrapping Pattern** (7 marks) The Java I/O system uses the **Decorator Design Pattern**, where each wrapper adds functionality around a base stream. **Why can't we use `socket.getInputStream().readLine()`?** The method `getInputStream()` returns a raw `java.io.InputStream`, which provides only **byte-level** reading: - `read()` — reads one byte at a time - `read(byte[] buf)` — reads into a buffer - `read(byte[] buf, int off, int len)` — reads a portion There is **no `readLine()` method** in the `InputStream` class. To read a line of text, we need: 1. **Byte → Character conversion** (via `InputStreamReader`, which uses a charset like UTF-8). 2. **Buffering for efficiency** (via `BufferedReader`, which reads a large chunk and scans for newlines in memory). 3. **`readLine()` method** (provided by `BufferedReader`). **Decorator chain example**: ``` Socket.getInputStream() → raw bytes ↓ wrapped by InputStreamReader → converts bytes to chars (UTF-8) ↓ wrapped by BufferedReader → adds readLine() and buffering ``` **Why the chain matters**: - Without `InputStreamReader`, we cannot interpret multi-byte characters correctly. - Without `BufferedReader`, every `readLine()` call would be a separate system call — extremely inefficient. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing `accept()` with `connect()`**: `accept()` is a **server-side blocking call** that returns a *new* socket. `connect()` is a **client-side** call inside the `Socket` constructor. Writing `s.accept()` in the client is a guaranteed **0-mark** answer. > 2. **Forgetting `flush()`**: When using `PrintWriter`, data may sit in the buffer. Always use `autoFlush = true` in the constructor or call `flush()` manually. The Kurose-Ross example uses `DataOutputStream.writeBytes()` which auto-flushes. > 3. **Not closing the `ServerSocket`**: Forgetting to close the welcome socket will leave the port in `TIME_WAIT`, causing "Address already in use" errors on restart. Use `welcomeSocket.setReuseAddress(true)` in production. > 4. **Confusing port types**: Students often mix up *well-known* ports (server) with *ephemeral* ports (client). Examiners specifically test this! > 5. **In BDP problems**: Forgetting to convert **bits → bytes** (divide by 8). This is the most common calculation error. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — TCP Client/Server Example** - ✅ **Two distinct socket types**: `Socket` (active/connected) and `ServerSocket` (passive/listening). - ✅ **Client always uses `Socket`** to actively initiate a connection. - ✅ **Server uses `ServerSocket`** to listen, then `accept()` returns a per-client `Socket`. - ✅ **`accept()` is BLOCKING** — the server thread suspends until a client connects. - ✅ **Stream wrapping order** (read side): `InputStream` → `InputStreamReader` → `BufferedReader` (for text). - ✅ **Stream wrapping order** (write side): `OutputStream` → `DataOutputStream` / `PrintWriter`. - ✅ **Port range**: $0$ to $65535$. Well-known: $0$–$1023$. Registered: $1024$–$49151$. Ephemeral: $49152$–$65535$. - ✅ **TCP connection ID** is a 4-tuple: (SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort). - ✅ **3-way handshake**: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK (establishes connection). - ✅ **4-way termination**: FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK (graceful close). - ✅ **`TIME_WAIT` state** lasts $2 \times MSL$ (typically **60 seconds**). - ✅ **BDP = Bandwidth × RTT** determines minimum buffer size. - ✅ **Default socket buffer**: **8 KB** (often too small for high-BDP links). - ✅ **Kurose-Ross Uppercase Server** uses TCP because the application needs reliable, in-order delivery. - ✅ **Java code lines to remember**: `new Socket(host, port)`, `new ServerSocket(port)`, `socket.accept()`, `getInputStream()`, `getOutputStream()`, `socket.close()`. - ✅ **Real-world mapping**: HTTP (port 80), HTTPS (443), FTP (20/21), SSH (22), Telnet (23), SMTP (25), DNS (53). - ✅ **Concurrent server** requires **multi-threading** (or `Selector`/`NIO` for high scalability). - ✅ **Kurose-Ross textbook** (Chapter 2, Section 2.7) is the **official reference** for this module.

topics.title: I/O Multiplexing: The select and poll Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** I/O Multiplexing: The select and poll Functions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Academic Definition **I/O Multiplexing** is a synchronous I/O notification mechanism that allows a single application thread to simultaneously monitor multiple file descriptors (sockets, pipes, terminals) and proceed with I/O operations only on those descriptors that are reported as *ready* by the kernel. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCST501)** syllabus, it is positioned under *Network Programming / Socket APIs* as the canonical solution to the classical "one-thread-per-client" scalability bottleneck. The two standard POSIX interfaces that provide this facility are: - **`select()`** — a bitmask-based, POSIX-mandated API defined in ``. - **`poll()`** — an array-based, POSIX.1-2001 generalization of `select()` defined in ``. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Both `select` and `poll` are classified as **Level 2 / Level 3 (Understand–Apply)** topics under the **CO2: Socket Programming & Application Layer** outcome. Students are expected to (a) write the function prototypes, (b) explain the role of each argument, (c) describe kernel-level readiness semantics, and (d) write short C snippets for a TCP echo server using either API. ## Conceptual Analogy — "The Switchboard Operator" Imagine you are the **sole receptionist** at a busy hotel front desk with **10 landlines** ringing intermittently. - **Naïve Blocking I/O** — You pick up Phone 1 and *wait until the caller stops talking*. Meanwhile, Phones 2–10 ring unanswered and customers hang up. *(One descriptor blocks all others.)* - **Naïve Non-Blocking I/O (busy polling)** — Every second, you glance at every phone, ask "Are you done?" ten times, and only act if one says yes. *(Wastes CPU.)* - **`select()` / `poll()` (The Switchboard)** — You install a **lightboard**. Each phone has a lamp. You tell the operator "wake me only when at least one lamp lights up, or after 5 minutes, whichever is first." When a lamp lights, you pick up *only that phone*. The operator (the **kernel**) handles all the listening. The lightboard = the **fd_set / pollfd array** passed to the system call. The kernel = the switchboard operator. The waking event = the **readiness notification**. ## Why I/O Multiplexing Matters — The Three Models Compared | Model | Throughput | CPU Usage | Programming Complexity | Scalability | |---|---|---|---|---| | Blocking I/O (one process per client) | Low | Medium | Low | Poor — **fork()** per client | | Non-Blocking I/O (busy poll) | Medium | **High** | Medium | Poor — wastes CPU cycles | | **I/O Multiplexing (`select`/`poll`)** | **High** | **Low** | Medium | Good for **low–moderate** concurrency | > [!NOTE] > For truly massive concurrency (C10K problem), `epoll` (Linux), `kqueue` (BSD/macOS), and IOCP (Windows) supersede `select`/`poll`. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats `select`/`poll` as the **portable, foundational** model that students must master before learning OS-specific alternatives. ## Standard System Limits & Constants (Recall Exact Values) | Symbol | Header | Value / Meaning | |---|---|---| | `FD_SETSIZE` | `` | **1024** — maximum descriptors in an `fd_set` on most Linux systems | | `FD_ZERO(fdsetp)` | `` | Clear all bits in the descriptor set | | `FD_SET(fd, fdsetp)` | `` | Turn **on** the bit for descriptor `fd` | | `FD_CLR(fd, fdsetp)` | `` | Turn **off** the bit for descriptor `fd` | | `FD_ISSET(fd, fdsetp)` | `` | Test whether `fd` is set in the set | | `POLLIN` | `` | **0x0001** — non-priority data available to read | | `POLLOUT` | `` | **0x0004** — normal data can be written without blocking | | `POLLPRI` | `` | **0x0002** — priority / out-of-band data readable | | `POLLERR` | `` | **0x0008** — error condition (always reported in `revents`) | | `POLLHUP` | `` | **0x0010** — hang-up (peer closed connection) | | `POLLNVAL` | `` | **0x0020** — invalid descriptor (e.g., fd not open) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The `select()` Function — Formal Specification The kernel prototype for `select` (POSIX 1003.1g) is: $$ \texttt{int select(int nfds, fd\_set *readfds, fd\_set *writefds, fd\_set *exceptfds, struct timeval *timeout);} $$ ### Argument-by-Argument Breakdown | Argument | Type | Direction | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | `nfds` | `int` | IN | **Largest fd to be tested + 1.** The kernel scans only bits `[0, nfds)`. Passing the *maximum + 1* avoids the kernel iterating all 1024 bits. | | `readfds` | `fd_set*` | IN/OUT | On entry: descriptors to watch for **read-readiness**. On return: kernel **modifies** the set to contain only those fds that are currently ready. | | `writefds` | `fd_set*` | IN/OUT | Same semantics, for **write-readiness**. May be `NULL` if not interested. | | `exceptfds` | `fd_set*` | IN/OUT | Watch for **out-of-band data** or **TCP urgent** conditions. Usually `NULL`. | | `timeout` | `struct timeval*` | IN | Upper bound on blocking time. `NULL` ⇒ block forever; `&{0,0}` ⇒ return immediately (**polling**). | ### Return Value Semantics $$ \texttt{Return value} = \begin{cases} > 0 & \text{Number of ready descriptors across all sets (count, NOT bit index)} \\ 0 & \text{Timeout expired with no readiness} \\ -1 & \text{Error (errno set; e.g., EBADF, EINTR)} \end{cases} $$ ### Kernel-Level Read-Rediness Conditions (TCP Socket) A socket `fd` is marked **readable** by the kernel when any of the following is true (RFC 793 / Stevens, *UNIX Network Programming*, Vol. 1): 1. Data has arrived on the socket — `read()` will not block and will return ≥ 1 byte. 2. The **FIN** (peer half-close) has been received — `read()` returns 0 (EOF). 3. A listening socket has a completed connection — `accept()` will not block. 4. The receive buffer is full — `read()` will not block (subsequent reads will). 5. A TCP **out-of-band** byte is available (only when `SO_OOBINLINE` is disabled). > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Exam Point:** Readiness means `read()` *will not block*, **NOT** that `read()` *will return all the bytes you asked for*. A readable socket may return fewer bytes than requested (partial read). This is the cornerstone of every KTU "explain readiness semantics" question. ## 2.2 The `poll()` Function — Formal Specification $$ \texttt{int poll(struct pollfd *fds, nfds\_t nfds, int timeout);} $$ The array element structure is: $$ \texttt{struct pollfd} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \texttt{int fd;} \\ \texttt{short events;} \quad \text{(requested events — INPUT)} \\ \texttt{short revents;} \quad \text{(returned events — OUTPUT)} \end{array} \right. $$ ### Argument-by-Argument Breakdown | Argument | Purpose | |---|---| | `fds` | Pointer to caller-allocated array of `pollfd` structures. **Each entry has its own `fd`.** A negative `fd` field causes that entry to be ignored; the `revents` is set to 0. | | `nfds` | Number of elements in `fds[]` (NOT the highest fd + 1). | | `timeout` | **Milliseconds:** `-1` ⇒ block forever; `0` ⇒ return immediately; `>0` ⇒ wait at most that long. | > [!NOTE] > **Key Difference vs `select`:** `poll` does **not** modify the `events` field — only `revents` is overwritten by the kernel. This avoids the `select` footgun where the kernel rewrites all three `fd_set`s and the program must reinitialize them every call. ### Return Value Semantics | Return | Meaning | |---|---| | `> 0` | Total count of array entries with non-zero `revents` | | `0` | Timeout expired; no fds ready | | `-1` | Error (e.g., `EFAULT`, `EINTR`, `ENOMEM`) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | Formula / API Element | Statement | |---|---| | `select()` prototype | $\texttt{int select(int nfds, fd\_set *r, fd\_set *w, fd\_set *e, struct timeval *t)}$ | | `select()` max argument | $\texttt{nfds} = \max(\text{watched fds}) + 1$ | | `select()` default max | $\texttt{FD\_SETSIZE} = \mathbf{1024}$ | | `select()` timeout block | `NULL` ⇒ $\infty$ ; `&{0,0}` ⇒ $0$ ; `&{sec,usec}` ⇒ $\sec + \usec/10^{6}$ seconds | | `poll()` prototype | $\texttt{int poll(struct pollfd *fds, nfds\_t n, int timeout)}$ | | `poll()` timeout units | **Milliseconds** (`-1`, `0`, $>0$) | | Read readiness | Data available OR FIN received OR accept queue non-empty | | Write readiness | Send buffer has space; kernel will accept $\geq 1$ byte without blocking | | `select()` bit position | Bit $\texttt{fd}$ of `fd_set` (LSB = fd 0) | | `nfds` index conversion | $\text{index in array} = \text{fd} - \text{lowest watched fd}$ | | Events mask | $\text{revents} = (\text{events} \cap \text{actual ready events}) \cup \text{implicit flags}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Implicit Flags in `poll`:** `POLLERR`, `POLLHUP`, and `POLLNVAL` are always reported in `revents` *even if not requested* in `events`. This is a frequently tested KTU nuance. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **TCP Concurrent Servers** — Single-process echo, chat, proxy servers (e.g., classic *Stevens* examples). - **Embedded / IoT Gateways** — Low-resource devices where threading cost is prohibitive. - **Network Monitoring Tools** — `ping`, `traceroute` primitives, packet sniffers that multiplex the raw socket + control pipe. - **GUI Event Loops** — X11, GTK, Qt historically used `select` to integrate X events with network sockets. - **Real-Time Industrial Protocols** — Modbus/TCP pollers multiplexing multiple PLC connections on a single SCADA thread. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Readiness Logic Because `select`/`poll` are **system-level** rather than purely mathematical, the "derivation" here takes the form of a **formal operational analysis** — i.e., an exhaustive walk-through of how the kernel transitions the application from the *blocked* state to the *ready* state. This is what KTU board examiners expect under the **"explain the operation of `select`/`poll` with a diagram"** question pattern. ## 3.1 Exhaustive Operational Walkthrough of `select()` We trace the execution of a **TCP server** that uses `select` to multiplex its **listening socket** and an arbitrary number of **connected client sockets**. ### Step 1 — Initialize the Master Descriptor Set The server maintains a *master* set that is **never destroyed** by the kernel — only a *working* set is passed into `select`. ```c fd_set master_set, working_set; FD_ZERO(&master_set); FD_SET(listen_fd, &master_set); // always watch the listening socket max_fd = listen_fd; ``` > Rationale: `select` *mutates* the sets it receives. If we passed `master_set` directly, every call would shrink it. We therefore copy it into `working_set` each iteration. ### Step 2 — Construct the Working Copy Before Each Call $$ \texttt{working\_set} \;\leftarrow\; \texttt{master\_set} \quad \text{(bitwise copy of all 1024 bits)} $$ This is an $O(\text{FD\_SETSIZE})$ operation per call — a major inefficiency at high concurrency. ### Step 3 — Block Inside the Kernel The user process transitions from **RUNNING** to **SLEEPING (interruptible)** in the kernel. The kernel's `poll()` wait-queue mechanism (not to be confused with the user-space `poll()` API) attaches the process to every watched descriptor's wait-queue. ### Step 4 — Kernel Polls Devices on User's Behalf When network data arrives, the **NIC raises an interrupt**. The kernel's network stack: 1. Places the packet in the socket's receive buffer. 2. **Walks** the `socket->wq` (wait-queue) and awakens sleeping processes whose filter (`sk_data_ready` callback) returns true. 3. Marks the socket as **readable** in its internal `sk_state_change` / `sk_data_ready` callbacks. ### Step 5 — Kernel Re-scans `nfds` Bits For each bit $b \in [0, \texttt{nfds})$, the kernel checks: $$ \texttt{is\_ready}(b) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } b \in \texttt{readfds} \text{ and } \text{socket}_b.\text{recv\_buf}.\text{len} > 0 \\ 1 & \text{if } b \in \texttt{readfds} \text{ and } \text{peer sent FIN} \\ 1 & \text{if } b \in \texttt{writefds} \text{ and } \text{socket}_b.\text{send\_buf}.\text{free} > 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ The kernel **clears all bits that are not ready** in each of the three sets. The application receives a *filtered* set on return. ### Step 6 — User Process Resumes, Returns to RUNNING `select` returns the integer count $N = \sum_b \texttt{is\_ready}(b)$ across all three sets. ### Step 7 — Iterate and Service Each Ready fd ```c for (fd = 0; fd <= max_fd && nready > 0; fd++) { if (FD_ISSET(fd, &working_set)) { nready--; if (fd == listen_fd) { cli_fd = accept(listen_fd, ...); // does not block FD_SET(cli_fd, &master_set); max_fd = max(max_fd, cli_fd); } else { nread = read(fd, buf, BUFSIZE); if (nread == 0) { // FIN received close(fd); FD_CLR(fd, &master_set); } else { write(fd, buf, nread); // echo back } } } } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The `nready` counter must be decremented** inside the loop. KTU students frequently forget this, causing the loop to iterate over 1024 bits even when only 2 fds are ready. This is a documented board-exam pitfall. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Operational Walkthrough of `poll()` ### Step 1 — Build the `pollfd` Array ```c struct pollfd pfds[MAX_CLIENTS + 1]; pfds[0].fd = listen_fd; pfds[0].events = POLLIN; for (i = 1; i <= nclients; i++) { pfds[i].fd = client_fds[i]; pfds[i].events = POLLIN; // watch for read-readiness } ``` ### Step 2 — Call `poll()` $$ \texttt{ret} = \texttt{poll(pfds, nclients + 1, -1)} $$ ### Step 3 — Kernel Examines Each Element Independently For each array index $i \in [0, \texttt{nfds})$, the kernel evaluates: $$ \texttt{revents}_i = \big(\texttt{events}_i \cap \text{actual conditions on } \texttt{fd}_i\big) \;\cup\; \{\text{POLLERR}, \text{POLLHUP}, \text{POLLNVAL}\} $$ The `events` field is **never** modified. ### Step 4 — User Iterates and Dispatches ```c for (i = 0; i < nclients + 1; i++) { if (pfds[i].revents & POLLIN) { if (pfds[i].fd == listen_fd) { /* accept */ } else { /* read & echo */ } } if (pfds[i].revents & (POLLERR | POLLHUP)) { /* cleanup */ } } ``` ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why `poll` Scales Better Than `select` | Cost Component | `select` | `poll` | |---|---|---| | Set representation | $O(\text{FD\_SETSIZE}) = O(1024)$ bits always copied in/out | $O(\text{nfds})$ array of `struct pollfd` (12 bytes/entry) | | Memory copy per call | $\sim 128$ bytes × 3 sets = **384 bytes** | $12 \cdot \texttt{nfds}$ bytes — grows only with active fds | | Re-initialization per call | **Mandatory** — must reconstruct `readfds` from master | **None** — `events` is preserved | | Max descriptors | **Hard cap `FD_SETSIZE = 1024`** (Linux) | **No fixed limit** — limited only by `nfds_t` and memory | | Ready-set delivery | Bitmask (must scan to find set bits) | Per-entry `revents` (direct index lookup) | | Partial-copy on return | Kernel rewrites all 3 sets entirely | Kernel writes only `revents` of each entry | | Portability | Universal (BSD, SVR4, Windows Winsock) | POSIX 2001 (less universal; older Windows requires WSAPoll) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Linear-Time Scan Cost:** Both `select` and `poll` are **$O(\text{max fd})$** in the worst case because the kernel must scan all watched descriptors. This is the *fundamental* reason they do not scale to C10K and why `epoll` (Linux) / `kqueue` (BSD) replaced them in high-performance servers. KTU 2024 expects students to know *why* this limitation exists, even if `epoll` itself is outside the syllabus. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical-Style Example — Time Budget Calculation While `select` is not a numerical algorithm, KTU frequently asks to **compute the effective block time** given the `timeval` struct. We provide a complete derivation: > **Q:** A `select` call uses `timeout = { 3, 500000 }`. Express the effective maximum block time in (a) seconds, (b) milliseconds. $$ T_{\max} = \texttt{tv\_sec} + \frac{\texttt{tv\_usec}}{10^{6}} = 3 + \frac{500{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 3.5 \text{ seconds} $$ $$ T_{\max} = 3.5 \times 1000 = 3500 \text{ milliseconds} $$ > **Q (poll variant):** Same timeout in `poll`? $$ \texttt{poll.timeout} = T_{\max} \times 1000 = 3500 \text{ ms} $$ If `tv_usec` were `500001` (rounded by kernel to nearest $10^{4}\,\mu s$ on Linux), the kernel actually waits $\approx 3.500010$ s — a rounding subtlety often noted in KTU numerical answer keys. ## 3.5 Error Analysis (errno values tested in viva) | errno | Meaning | Typical Cause | |---|---|---| | `EBADF` | Invalid fd in one of the sets | fd closed but not `FD_CLR`ed | | `EINTR` | Caught a signal | Always wrap `select`/`poll` in a `while` loop | | `EINVAL` | Invalid `nfds` or `timeout` | `nfds < 0`, or `timeout` negative | | `ENOMEM` | Out of kernel memory | Internal allocation failure | | `EFAULT` | Set pointer points to invalid memory | Programming bug — pointer not in user space | **Robust template (the KTU-mandated pattern):** $$ \texttt{while}(\texttt{ready} = \texttt{select}(\ldots)) < 0 \; \&\& \; \texttt{errno} == \texttt{EINTR}) \; \{\; \text{retry}\;\;\} $$ This loop correctly handles signal interruption, the most common production failure of `select`/`poll` programs. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The I/O Multiplexing Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) The following Mermaid diagram renders the **functional architecture** of a `select()`/`poll()`-based concurrent server. It shows how the single user thread is multiplexed across the listening socket, multiple client sockets, and the kernel's wait-queue mechanism. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph USERSPACE["USER SPACE (Application Process)"] APP["Application Thread
main loop"] MASTER["master fdset / pollfd array
persistent state"] WORK["working copy
passed to kernel"] DISPATCH["Dispatch Table
fd to handler function"] end subgraph KERNELSPACE["KERNEL SPACE (OS)"] SOCKLIST["Socket Table
listensock + nclients"] WQ["Per-Socket Wait Queue
sk data ready callback"] READBUF["Receive Buffer
sk buff head"] READYCALC["Readiness Calculator
filters by events / eventset"] end subgraph HARDWARE["HARDWARE"] NIC["Network Interface Card
packet arrival interrupt"] NET["Network Medium
Ethernet / Wi-Fi"] end NIC -. "DMA packet" .-> READBUF NET -. "incoming frames" .-> NIC READBUF -- "sk_data_ready" --> WQ WQ -- "wakeup" --> APP SOCKLIST --> READYCALC READYCALC -- "modify readfds / revents" --> APP APP -- "1 build from master" --> WORK WORK -- "2 syscall select or poll" --> KERNELSPACE KERNELSPACE -- "3 returns ready count" --> APP APP -- "4 FD_ISSET or revents test" --> DISPATCH DISPATCH -- "5 accept / read / write" --> SOCKLIST MASTER -- "rebuild each loop" --> WORK ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Step 1–2 represent the user-space preparation. Step 3 is the kernel **block + wakeup** transition. Step 4–5 represent the ready-event processing. The bidirectional arrow between APP and SOCKLIST captures the fact that each successful `accept`/`read`/`write` mutates the kernel's socket table. ## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram of a Watched Descriptor This Mermaid diagram illustrates the **kernel-side state machine** that `select`/`poll` observe on each socket. Read-readiness transitions are shown explicitly. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 direction LR [*] --> IDLE: socket created / opened IDLE --> READABLE: data arrives in recv buffer IDLE --> READABLE: peer sends FIN IDLE --> READABLE: accept queue non empty
listening socket only READABLE --> IDLE: app reads all buffered data READABLE --> READABLE: app does partial read
still readable if more data remains IDLE --> WRITABLE: send buffer has free space WRITABLE --> IDLE: send buffer full
e.g. peer slow or window zero READABLE --> EOF: peer half close FIN received EOF --> CLOSED: app calls close fd WRITABLE --> CLOSED: app calls close fd IDLE --> CLOSED: app calls close fd CLOSED --> [*] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Exam Interpretation:** When the KTU question asks *"What is the condition for a socket to be readable?"*, students must mention **all three** arrows leaving `IDLE` to `READABLE` — data arrival, FIN, and accept-queue non-empty. Mentioning only "data arrival" loses marks. ## 4.3 Comparison Schematic — `select` vs `poll` Data Structures ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SELECT_DS["select fd set bitmask representation"] direction TB B0["Bit 0
fd 0"] B1["Bit 1
fd 1"] B2["Bit 2
fd 2"] B3["Bit 3
fd 3"] BDOTS["..."] B1023["Bit 1023
fd 1023"] B0 --- B1 --- B2 --- B3 --- BDOTS --- B1023 end subgraph POLL_DS["poll struct pollfd array"] direction TB P0["index 0
fd 5
events POLLIN
revents 0"] P1["index 1
fd 8
events POLLIN
revents POLLIN"] P2["index 2
fd 11
events POLLIN
revents POLLHUP"] P0 --- P1 --- P2 end SELECT_DS -. "must scan all 1024 bits
even if only 3 are set" .-> POLL_DS ``` This visualization makes the **fundamental scalability difference** between the two APIs immediately visible — `select` carries a fixed $O(1024)$ cost regardless of the number of active descriptors, whereas `poll`'s cost grows with the number of watched fds but remains **bounded by actual usage**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define I/O multiplexing. Why is it preferred over creating one process per client in a TCP server?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **I/O multiplexing** is a mechanism that allows a single process to simultaneously monitor multiple file descriptors and perform I/O only on those that are *ready* (i.e., will not block). The POSIX APIs `select()` and `poll()` are standard implementations. > > It is preferred over the **fork-per-client** model for the following reasons: > 1. **No process-creation overhead** — `fork()` consumes CPU and memory; multiplexing avoids it entirely. > 2. **No IPC overhead** — data does not need to be passed between parent and child via pipes or shared memory; the single process owns all sockets. > 3. **Better scalability under low-to-moderate load** — up to ~1024 concurrent connections on Linux without exhausting process table entries. > 4. **Simpler shutdown semantics** — closing the process closes all sockets; no need to track child PIDs and reap zombies. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Explain the meaning of "read-readiness" in the context of `select()`. List any three conditions under which a socket is considered readable.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > A socket is said to be **read-ready** when a call to `read()` (or `recv()`) on that socket will return **without blocking** — i.e., the kernel can satisfy the request immediately with available data or with an EOF indication. > > **Three conditions** under which a socket is readable: > 1. The kernel's receive buffer for the socket contains **at least one byte** of data, so `read()` will return immediately. > 2. The **FIN segment** has been received from the peer, indicating orderly half-close; `read()` will return `0` (EOF). > 3. The socket is a **listening socket** with at least one completed connection in the accept queue, so `accept()` will return immediately. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `select()` Deep Dive **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the working of the `select()` system call. List its arguments and describe the role of `fd_set`. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a C program that uses `select()` to implement a **TCP echo server** that handles multiple clients simultaneously. Explain the need to maintain a *master set* separate from the *working set*. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) **The `select()` function prototype** (1 mark): $$ \texttt{int select(int nfds, fd\_set *readfds, fd\_set *writefds, fd\_set *exceptfds, struct timeval *timeout);} $$ **Argument roles** (2 marks): | Arg | Role | |---|---| | `nfds` | Range of bits to test: `max_fd + 1`. Kernel scans only `[0, nfds)` bits of each set. | | `readfds` | Set of descriptors watched for read-readiness. | | `writefds` | Set watched for write-readiness. | | `exceptfds` | Set watched for out-of-band / exceptional conditions. | | `timeout` | `NULL` ⇒ block; `&{0,0}` ⇒ poll; `&{s,us}` ⇒ wait `s + us/10⁶` s. | **Working (4 marks) — refer to Section 4.1 block diagram and Section 3.1 walk-through:** 1. Application constructs a *working copy* of the master `fd_set` for each of the three interest sets. 2. Application calls `select`, which transitions the process from **RUNNING → SLEEPING** in the kernel. 3. Kernel attaches the process to each watched socket's wait-queue via the `poll_wait` callback. 4. On I/O arrival (NIC interrupt → `sk_data_ready`), the kernel wakes the process. 5. Kernel re-scans `[0, nfds)`, *clears* all bits in each set that are NOT ready, and returns the count of ready descriptors. 6. Application iterates with `FD_ISSET` to identify and service each ready descriptor. **Role of `fd_set`** (covered transitively in steps 1 and 5): a bitmask structure, typically **1024 bits**, used as the input-output container for the descriptors of interest. The macros `FD_ZERO`, `FD_SET`, `FD_CLR`, and `FD_ISSET` manipulate this structure. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) **Complete C source code** (5 marks): ```c #include #include #include #include #include #include #include #include #define MAXLINE 4096 #define SERV_PORT 9877 int main(int argc, char **argv) { int i, maxi, maxfd, listenfd, connfd, sockfd; int nready, client[FD_SETSIZE]; ssize_t n; char buf[MAXLINE]; socklen_t clilen; struct sockaddr_in cliaddr, servaddr; fd_set rset, allset; listenfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0); memset(&servaddr, 0, sizeof(servaddr)); servaddr.sin_family = AF_INET; servaddr.sin_addr.s_addr = htonl(INADDR_ANY); servaddr.sin_port = htons(SERV_PORT); bind(listenfd, (struct sockaddr *)&servaddr, sizeof(servaddr)); listen(listenfd, 1024); maxfd = listenfd; maxi = -1; for (i = 0; i < FD_SETSIZE; i++) client[i] = -1; FD_ZERO(&allset); FD_SET(listenfd, &allset); for (;;) { rset = allset; /* working copy */ nready = select(maxfd + 1, &rset, NULL, NULL, NULL); if (FD_ISSET(listenfd, &rset)) { clilen = sizeof(cliaddr); connfd = accept(listenfd, (struct sockaddr *)&cliaddr, &clilen); for (i = 0; i < FD_SETSIZE; i++) if (client[i] < 0) { client[i] = connfd; break; } if (i == FD_SETSIZE) { perror("too many clients"); exit(1); } FD_SET(connfd, &allset); if (connfd > maxfd) maxfd = connfd; if (i > maxi) maxi = i; if (--nready <= 0) continue; } for (i = 0; i <= maxi; i++) { if ((sockfd = client[i]) < 0) continue; if (FD_ISSET(sockfd, &rset)) { if ((n = read(sockfd, buf, MAXLINE)) == 0) { close(sockfd); FD_CLR(sockfd, &allset); client[i] = -1; } else { write(sockfd, buf, n); } if (--nready <= 0) break; } } } } ``` **Why master vs working set is required** (2 marks): `select()` *modifies* the sets passed to it — on return, only the ready fds remain set; all others are cleared. If we passed `allset` directly, the next iteration would see an empty set (or only the listening socket). Hence we **copy `allset` into `rset` before every call**, then rebuild `allset` as new connections arrive and old ones are closed. This ensures the application's *persistent view* of which fds to monitor is never corrupted by the kernel. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `poll()` Deep Dive (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the `poll()` system call. Compare `poll()` with `select()` in terms of scalability and flexibility. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Rewrite the TCP echo server from Question A using `poll()`. Highlight the differences in code structure. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) **`poll()` prototype** (1 mark): $$ \texttt{int poll(struct pollfd *fds, nfds\_t nfds, int timeout);} $$ where each element is $$ \texttt{struct pollfd} \{ \texttt{int fd; short events; short revents;}\} $$ **Operational description** (3 marks): - Application builds an array of `pollfd` entries, one per watched descriptor. - For each entry, `events` is set to the OR of requested event flags (e.g., `POLLIN`). - On return, the kernel sets `revents` to indicate **actual** conditions. `events` is **not** modified. - Implicit flags `POLLERR`, `POLLHUP`, `POLLNVAL` are reported in `revents` regardless of `events`. - `timeout` is in **milliseconds**: `-1` block forever, `0` return immediately, `>0` wait that long. - Application iterates over the array testing `pfds[i].revents` rather than using `FD_ISSET` on bitmasks. **Comparison table** (3 marks): | Aspect | `select()` | `poll()` | |---|---|---| | Descriptor limit | `FD_SETSIZE` = **1024** (hard-coded) | **No fixed limit**; only by `nfds_t` and memory | | Set representation | Bitmask, always `O(1024)` | Array of `struct pollfd`, size = watched fds | | Re-init per call | **Mandatory** | Not required | | Returned data | Three modified bitmasks | `revents` per array entry | | Scan cost | $O(1024)$ worst case | $O(\text{nfds})$ worst case | | Timeout units | `timeval` (sec + μsec) | `int` milliseconds | | Portability | Higher (BSD/SVR4/Winsock) | POSIX 2001, less so on legacy Windows | | Use case | Small portable servers, < 1024 fds | Larger servers, more flexible fds | **Verdict:** `poll` scales **better** and is more **flexible** (no fixed limit, no re-initialization), but `select` is more **portable** to very old or non-POSIX systems. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) **Complete C source using `poll`** (5 marks): ```c #include #include #include #include #include #include #include #include #include #define MAXLINE 4096 #define OPEN_MAX 1024 #define SERV_PORT 9877 int main(int argc, char **argv) { int i, maxi, listenfd, connfd, sockfd; int nready; ssize_t n; char buf[MAXLINE]; socklen_t clilen; struct sockaddr_in cliaddr, servaddr; struct pollfd client[OPEN_MAX + 1]; listenfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0); memset(&servaddr, 0, sizeof(servaddr));
