# 📚 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term *Routing Cache* in the context of the network layer. Explain briefly how it helps in accelerating the packet-forwarding process.**

**Model Answer:**

A **routing cache** is a small, high-speed in-memory data structure maintained in a router (or end-host) that stores the **routing decisions for recently seen destination IP addresses**. Each entry typically maps a destination prefix to a pre-computed **next-hop interface and MAC rewrite information**.

It accelerates forwarding by exploiting the principle of **temporal locality** — the empirical observation that a small fraction of destinations accounts for the majority of packets. For a cached destination, the router avoids the expensive **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** lookup against the full FIB and instead retrieves the next-hop directly from a **hash-table lookup** in a few nanoseconds. This raises effective lookup throughput from **~10 Mpps (cache-less)** to **~100+ Mpps (cache-assisted)** at line rate.

> **Keyword triggers for full marks:** "in-memory", "temporal locality", "longest prefix match bypass", "next-hop".

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between RIB, FIB, and Route Cache. State the primary purpose of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Primary Purpose | Updated By | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RIB** | Computes best paths from routing protocols | Routing protocols (OSPF, BGP) | Slowest |
| **FIB** | Forwarding table optimized for LPM lookups | Control plane (on RIB change) | Fast |
| **Route Cache** | Accelerates repeated lookups via hash table | Dataplane (on cache miss) | Fastest |

- The **RIB** is the *control plane's* database of all known routes.
- The **FIB** is a *compressed* copy of the RIB, restructured for fast lookups (often using TCAM).
- The **Route Cache** is a *dataplane* optimization that stores only the *last few* looked-up entries in a hash table, enabling O(1) retrieval.

> **Keyword triggers for full marks:** "control plane vs. data plane", "TCAM", "hash table", "O(1) vs. O(log N)".

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🔵 Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A router maintains a routing cache. The cache lookup time is 5 ns and the FIB-based LPM lookup time is 95 ns. The cache hit rate is observed to be 92%.**

**(a)** Derive the formula for the expected forwarding time per packet. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compute the expected forwarding time and the speedup compared to a cache-less router. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Define variables:**
Let $T_{cache}$ = cache lookup time, $T_{LPM}$ = FIB LPM lookup time, $H$ = cache hit rate.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Identify the two cases:**
- On a **cache hit**, the router spends time $T_A = T_{cache}$ and then forwards the packet.
- On a **cache miss**, the router spends time $T_{cache} + T_{LPM}$ (cache lookup first, then FIB) and then forwards the packet.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Write the expectation:**
The expected forwarding time is the probability-weighted sum:

$$ E[T_{forward}] = H \cdot T_A + (1 - H) \cdot T_B $$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Substitute and expand:**

$$ E[T_{forward}] = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot (T_{cache} + T_{LPM}) $$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Simplify to the canonical form:**

$$ \boxed{E[T_{forward}] = T_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{LPM}} $$

**Interpretation (bonus 1 mark):** As $H \rightarrow 1$, the forwarding time approaches the cache lookup time. As $H \rightarrow 0$, the forwarding time approaches $T_{cache} + T_{LPM}$, the worst case.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Extract given values:**
- $T_{cache} = 5$ ns
- $T_{LPM} = 95$ ns
- $H = 0.92$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Substitute into the formula:**

$$ E[T_{forward}] = 5 + (1 - 0.92) \cdot 95 $$

$$ E[T_{forward}] = 5 + 0.08 \cdot 95 $$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute the product:**

$$ 0.08 \cdot 95 = 7.6 $$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final addition:**

$$ E[T_{forward}] = 5 + 7.6 = 12.6 \text{ ns} $$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Compute the cache-less time:**

$$ T_{no\_cache} = T_{cache} + T_{LPM} = 5 + 95 = 100 \text{ ns} $$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Compute the speedup:**

$$ S = \frac{T_{no\_cache}}{E[T_{forward}]} = \frac{100}{12.6} \approx 7.94 $$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$ \boxed{E[T_{forward}] = 12.6 \text{ ns}, \quad S \approx 7.94 \times} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Many students write the speedup as $S = 95 / 12.6$, forgetting that a cache-less router **also pays the cache lookup cost** because the cache is always consulted first. The correct baseline is $T_{cache} + T_{LPM}$, not $T_{LPM}$ alone.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Failing to write the units (**ns**) in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Skipping the intermediate step $0.08 \times 95$. Always show the multiplication explicitly.

---

### 🔵 Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a routing cache in a high-speed router. Discuss the role of hash-based lookup, cache hit/miss handling, and cache replacement policy. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss three cache invalidation strategies. Why is cache invalidation considered one of the hardest problems in computer networking? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Architectural Position (2 marks):**
A routing cache sits between the **packet parser** and the **Forwarding Information Base (FIB)** in a router's dataplane. It is implemented as a **hash table** in high-speed SRAM, allowing lookups in **1–5 nanoseconds**.

**2. Hash-Based Lookup (2 marks):**
The destination IP address $D$ is fed into a **non-cryptographic hash function** (e.g., MurmurHash3, CRC32). The hash value modulo the number of buckets $C$ yields an index $h$:

$$ h = \text{HASH}(D) \mod C $$

The router inspects the bucket at index $h$ and verifies the full key match (to handle hash collisions). On a match, the cached next-hop is returned. Hashing reduces the lookup complexity from **O(N) (linear scan)** to **O(1) (constant time)**.

**3. Hit/Miss Handling (2 marks):**
- **Hit:** The cached entry is used to rewrite the L2 header and forward the packet. No FIB access is needed.
- **Miss:** The router performs a **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** against the FIB, which may use a **TCAM** for hardware-accelerated matching. The result is then **inserted into the cache** for future packets to the same destination.

**4. Replacement Policy (1 mark):**
When the cache is full, the **LRU (Least Recently Used)** policy evicts the oldest entry. Approximated LRU is used in real hardware due to the impracticality of tracking exact timestamps at line rate.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Strategy 1 — TTL-Based Expiry (2 marks):**
Each cache entry is stamped with a **Time-To-Live (TTL)** value (typically 30–300 seconds). When the TTL elapses, the entry is treated as **stale** and must be revalidated against the FIB. This is the **simplest** strategy but can cause **spikes in FIB load** if many entries expire simultaneously.

**2. Strategy 2 — Event-Driven Invalidation (2 marks):**
When a routing protocol detects a topology change (e.g., **OSPF LSA update**, **BGP withdrawal**), it signals the dataplane to **flush all affected cache entries**. This is the **most accurate** strategy but requires tight coupling between the control plane and the dataplane.

**3. Strategy 3 — Active Revalidation (1 mark):**
Before forwarding a packet, the router **periodically re-checks** the cached entry against the FIB. This is the **safest** strategy but adds **per-packet latency** and is rarely used in production.

**4. Why Cache Invalidation Is Hard (2 marks):**
Cache invalidation is famously called *"one of the two hard problems in computer science"* (along with naming things and off-by-one errors). In networking, it is hard because:
- **Network topology changes are unpredictable** — a link can fail at any moment.
- **Distributed consensus is expensive** — multiple routers must agree on the new topology.
- **Stale entries cause silent failures** — packets are forwarded to wrong next-hops, creating hard-to-diagnose **black holes** and **routing loops**.
- **Cache invalidation storms** — a single route flap can invalidate thousands of cache entries simultaneously, causing a **thundering herd** against the FIB.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Confusing **TTL** (Time-To-Live in the IP header) with **cache TTL**. They are unrelated. KTU examiners specifically watch for this confusion.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to mention the **Linux kernel's 2012 deprecation** of the per-destination route cache due to invalidation storms. Mentioning this shows **deeper understanding** and can earn a bonus mark.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Writing vague answers like *"cache invalidation is hard because of networks"* — be **specific** about *what* fails and *how* (e.g., black holes, loops, micro-loops).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Definition:** A routing cache is a **high-speed in-memory hash table** that stores recently used routing decisions to **bypass Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** lookups.
- ✅ **Three-Tier Hierarchy:** RIB (slowest, control plane) → FIB (medium, hardware-optimized) → Route Cache (fastest, dataplane).
- ✅ **Temporal Locality:** Routing caches exploit the fact that a **small number of destinations** receive **most of the traffic** (Zipfian distribution, $\alpha \approx 0.8$).
- ✅ **Canonical Formula (memorize verbatim):**
  
  $$ E[T_{forward}] = T_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{LPM} $$

- ✅ **Typical Real-World Values:** $T_{cache} = 1$–$5$ ns, $T_{LPM} = 10$–$100$ ns, $H = 0.85$–$0.95$.
- ✅ **Cache Replacement Policy:** **LRU (Least Recently Used)** is the standard; real hardware uses **approximated LRU**.
- ✅ **Invalidation Strategies:** TTL-based, event-driven, and active revalidation. **Event-driven** is most accurate.
- ✅ **Linux Historical Fact:** Linux kernel **3.6+** deprecated the per-destination route cache (in `net/ipv4/route.c`) in favor of the **FIB trie** due to **DDoS-induced invalidation storms**.
- ✅ **Cache is NOT a Replacement:** The route cache is a **performance layer** in front of the FIB/RIB, never a substitute.
- ✅ **Stale Cache = Catastrophe:** A stale cache entry causes **packet mis-forwarding, black-holing, and routing loops** — one of the top causes of production network outages.
- ✅ **Hit Rate vs. Cache Size:** Empirically, a **4K–16K-entry cache** achieves **85–95% hit rate** for typical Internet traffic.
- ✅ **Exam-Specific Tip:** Always **show intermediate calculations** in numerical problems; examiners allocate marks for **each algebraic step**, not just the final answer.
- ✅ **Unit Hygiene:** Normalize all time units to **nanoseconds (ns)** before plugging into formulas.
- ✅ **Speedup Baseline:** When computing speedup, the cache-less baseline is $T_{cache} + T_{LPM}$, **not** $T_{LPM}$ alone.
- ✅ **Adjacency Table:** Routers like Cisco also maintain a separate **Adjacency Table** that caches **Layer-2 rewrite information** — conceptually a sister cache to the routing cache.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adding New Entry in the Routing Table Using IP Command
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Adding New Entry in the Routing Table Using IP Command

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **TCP/IP protocol suite** and **Linux/Unix network stack**, a **Routing Table** is a data table stored in the kernel (specifically in the routing subsystem of the operating system) that lists the routes to particular network destinations. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the Linux networking stack, a **new entry** in the routing table is a manually-installed static rule that tells the kernel: *"If a packet's destination IP address matches this rule, forward it through this specific next-hop address and/or via this specific network interface."*

The modern, canonical utility used to add, view, modify, and delete entries in the routing table is the **`ip` command** (from the `iproute2` package). The specific sub-command used for routing table manipulation is **`ip route`**. The general syntax for *adding* a new entry is:

```
ip route add <destination_network>/<prefix_length> via <next_hop_ip> [dev <interface>]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Network Layer & Routing):**
> The `ip` command has officially replaced the older, deprecated `route` command (`net-tools` package) in all modern Linux distributions (RHEL 7+, Ubuntu 16.04+, Debian 9+). Examiners expect students to demonstrate proficiency in the **`ip route`** syntax — not the legacy `route add` syntax.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are inside a large **multi-floor shopping mall** (your computer), and you want to visit a specific shop on a specific floor (the destination IP address). You go to the **mall's central information directory** (the **Routing Table**) and look up how to get there.

- If your destination is *inside the same mall building* — you walk there directly. (This is a **directly connected route**.)
- If your destination is in *another building across the street* — the directory says: *"Go to the skybridge on Floor 3, then ask the security desk there"* (This is a route specifying a **next-hop IP**).
- If your destination is *somewhere unknown or generic like "any restaurant in the city"* — the directory says: *"Just leave through the main exit, the outside world will figure it out"* (This is the **default route / default gateway**, `0.0.0.0/0`).

**Adding a new entry in the routing table** is exactly equivalent to sticking a **new instruction card** onto this directory board — you are explicitly teaching your computer a new path it didn't know about before.

---

### 1.3 Key Technical Terms (Bolded Glossary)

- **Routing Table** — A kernel-level data structure maintaining the set of rules used by the IP layer to forward packets.
- **`ip route`** — The sub-command of the `iproute2` suite used to manipulate the routing table.
- **Next-Hop IP Address** — The IP address of the *adjacent* router to which the packet must be sent next.
- **Prefix Length (`/n`)** — The CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) notation representing the number of leading `1` bits in the subnet mask.
- **Default Route** — A catch-all route (`0.0.0.0/0`) used when no specific match is found in the routing table.
- **Static Route** — A manually configured route (as opposed to one learned dynamically via routing protocols like OSPF, RIP, or BGP).

> [!NOTE]
> The unitless metric called the **prefix length** is conventionally an integer between **0** and **32** for IPv4 (and **0** to **128** for IPv6), representing the number of contiguous network bits in the subnet mask.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Routing Table Entry

Every entry inserted by `ip route add` contains at minimum the following logical components. Understanding each component is essential for KTU 14-mark derivations.

1. **Destination Network / Prefix** — *Where do you want to go?*
   Written in CIDR format: `192.168.10.0/24` means the first 24 bits are the network portion.
2. **Next-Hop IP Address (via)** — *Who do I hand the packet to first?*
   Must be reachable on a directly-connected network of the local host.
3. **Output Interface (dev)** — *Which physical/virtual port do I use to leave?*
   Examples: `eth0`, `wlan0`, `ens33`, `tun0`.
4. **Metric (optional)** — *How "expensive" is this path?*
   Lower metric = preferred route when multiple paths exist.
5. **Scope (optional)** — *How far can this route propagate?* (`global`, `link`, `host`).
6. **Protocol (optional)** — *How was this route learned?* (`static`, `kernel`, `boot`, `dhcp`, `redirect`).

---

### 2.2 The `ip route` Command Family — Reference Sheet

| Purpose | Command Syntax | Real-World Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Display the entire routing table | `ip route show` (or `ip route`) | Diagnose why traffic isn't reaching a subnet |
| Add a network route | `ip route add <NET>/<PREFIX> via <NH> dev <IF>` | Tell the host to reach a corporate branch via a specific router |
| Add a host route (`/32`) | `ip route add <HOST_IP>/32 via <NH> dev <IF>` | Pinpoint a single server (e.g., printer, NAS, DB) |
| Add the default route | `ip route add default via <GW_IP> dev <IF>` | Provide internet/external connectivity |
| Delete a specific entry | `ip route del <NET>/<PREFIX>` | Roll back an incorrect static route |
| Replace an existing entry | `ip route replace <NET>/<PREFIX> via <NH>` | Modify a route atomically (no error if it already exists) |
| Flush the entire table | `ip route flush` | Clear all routes (used in container/netns cleanup) |
| Get a single route to a destination | `ip route get <DEST_IP>` | Simulate the kernel's forwarding decision for a packet |
| Add a route via a specific interface only | `ip route add <NET>/<PREFIX> dev <IF>` | Used for point-to-point links (no next-hop needed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KPI Metric for Routing Decisions:** When multiple matching routes exist, the Linux kernel uses the **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** rule. A `/32` host route will *always* be preferred over a `/24` network route for the same destination because it has a longer prefix (32 > 24).

---

### 2.3 Mathematical Foundation: Subnet Mask & Prefix Length

The **prefix length** `n` directly translates into the subnet mask through the following formula:

$$
\text{Subnet Mask} = \underbrace{1\,1\,1\,1 \ldots 1}_{n \text{ consecutive 1s}} \quad \underbrace{0\,0\,0\,0 \ldots 0}_{32 - n \text{ zeros}}
$$

The numerical value of the subnet mask can be computed as:

$$
\text{Mask Value} = 2^{32} - 2^{(32 - n)}
$$

The number of usable host addresses in the resulting subnet is given by:

$$
\text{Usable Hosts} = 2^{(32 - n)} - 2
$$

(The "-2" subtracts the **Network Address** and the **Broadcast Address**, which are reserved.)

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Quick Revision Table)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|
| CIDR → Subnet Mask | $\text{Mask} = 2^{32} - 2^{(32-n)}$ | $0 \le n \le 32$ |
| Number of Hosts | $\text{Hosts} = 2^{(32-n)} - 2$ | Subtract 2 for Net & Bcast |
| Subnets in a Class B | $2^{(n - 16)}$ | Only valid for $n \ge 16$ |
| Longest Prefix Match | $\text{Choose the entry with the largest } n$ | Kernel routing precedence rule |
| Total routes in FIB | $N_{\text{FIB}} = N_{\text{connected}} + N_{\text{static}} + N_{\text{dynamic}}$ | Sum of all sources |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Engineering:** Configuring static routes in multi-router enterprise networks to optimize path selection.
- **Cloud & DevOps:** Defining pod-to-pod and pod-to-service routes in Kubernetes CNI plugins (Calico, Cilium) using `ip route` underneath.
- **System Administration:** Setting up `default` gateways in headless servers and data-center machines.
- **Network Troubleshooting:** Using `ip route get <dest>` to verify exactly which path the kernel will choose for a given destination — a critical debugging step.
- **Cybersecurity (Blue Team):** Auditing routes on a compromised host to detect malicious redirection (e.g., a rogue `0.0.0.0/0` via an attacker's IP).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Adding a Network Route (The Standard Case)

**Problem Statement:**
A Linux host has the IP `10.0.0.5/24` on interface `eth0`. It needs to reach the remote network `172.16.50.0/24`, which is accessible through the router at `10.0.0.1`. Write the exact `ip route` command and verify it.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**

$$
\text{Destination} = 172.16.50.0/24
$$

$$
\text{Next Hop} = 10.0.0.1
$$

$$
\text{Interface} = \text{eth0}
$$

**Step 2 — Compose the command:**

```
ip route add 172.16.50.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 dev eth0
```

**Step 3 — Verify the route was added:**

```
ip route show
```

The output should now contain a line similar to:

```
172.16.50.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 dev eth0
```

**Step 4 — Test connectivity (optional but KTU-recommended in lab exams):**

```
ping 172.16.50.10
ip route get 172.16.50.10
```

The `ip route get` output will reveal the next-hop and the interface the kernel would use:

```
172.16.50.10 via 10.0.0.1 dev eth0 src 10.0.0.5 uid 0
```

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Adding the Default Route (Gateway of Last Resort)

**Problem Statement:**
A host connects to the internet through a residential/office router with gateway IP `192.168.1.1` on interface `wlan0`. Configure the default route.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$
\text{Destination} = \text{default} \;\; \equiv \;\; 0.0.0.0/0
$$

$$
\text{Next Hop} = 192.168.1.1
$$

$$
\text{Interface} = \text{wlan0}
$$

**Step 2 — Execute the command:**

```
ip route add default via 192.168.1.1 dev wlan0
```

**Step 3 — Mathematical validation of the default route:**

The default route has a prefix length of **0**, meaning it matches *every* IPv4 address. The Longest Prefix Match rule guarantees that any more specific route (e.g., `/24`, `/16`) will take precedence over this default. The default route is therefore the **fallback path** of the routing algorithm.

$$
n_{\text{default}} = 0, \quad n_{\text{specific}} \ge 8
$$

Since $0 < n_{\text{specific}}$, the default is used only when no specific match exists.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Adding a Host Route (`/32`)

**Problem Statement:**
You need to reach a specific server (a database at `203.0.113.42`) through a dedicated management router at `10.10.10.1`, even though there is a generic `/16` route pointing elsewhere.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$
\text{Destination} = 203.0.113.42/32 \quad (\text{single host})
$$

$$
\text{Next Hop} = 10.10.10.1
$$

**Step 2 — Execute the command:**

```
ip route add 203.0.113.42/32 via 10.10.10.1
```

**Step 3 — Proof of LPM precedence:**

The kernel routing decision for destination $D = 203.0.113.42$ now finds two matches:

| Route Entry | Prefix | Next Hop |
|---|---|---|
| Generic route | `/16` (prefix length 16) | Router A |
| Specific host route | `/32` (prefix length 32) | Router B |

Since $32 > 16$, the LPM algorithm selects the `/32` entry. Therefore, packets to `203.0.113.42` will *always* traverse Router B, regardless of the `/16` route. The mathematical condition is:

$$
\text{Selected} = \arg\max_{r \in \text{Routes}} \left( n_r \right) \quad \text{subject to} \quad D \in \text{Network}(r)
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Adding a Route via Interface Only (Point-to-Point Links)

**Problem Statement:**
You have a point-to-point GRE tunnel named `tun0` whose remote end is `198.51.100.0/24`. On a P2P link, no next-hop is needed because the interface itself uniquely identifies the path.

**Step 1 — Execute the command:**

```
ip route add 198.51.100.0/24 dev tun0
```

**Step 2 — Conceptual difference:**

| Type | When to use |
|---|---|
| `via <NH> dev <IF>` | Multi-access networks (Ethernet, Wi-Fi) where multiple routers share the segment |
| `dev <IF>` | Point-to-point links (P2P, GRE, PPP, serial) where there is only one possible neighbor |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: How the Kernel Processes a Packet Using the Routing Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input:} \quad & \text{Packet with destination } D \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Scan routing table; collect all entries } E_i \text{ such that } D \in \text{Network}(E_i) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{If } \vert \{E_i\} \vert = 0 \rightarrow \text{drop packet, send ICMP Destination Unreachable} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{If } \vert \{E_i\} \vert \ge 1 \rightarrow \text{select } E^* = \arg\max_i(n_i) \quad \text{(LPM rule)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{If multiple entries share } n^*, \text{break tie by lowest metric } m \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Forward packet to next-hop of } E^* \text{ via the specified interface}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the exact decision flow implemented in the Linux kernel's `fib_lookup()` function. The `ip route get <D>` command is essentially a user-space wrapper that calls this function for a single destination.

---

### 3.6 Common Pitfalls When Adding Routes (Step-by-Step Debugging)

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `RTNETLINK answers: Network is unreachable` | Next-hop is on a non-connected subnet | Add a connected route first, or use `dev` only |
| `RTNETLINK answers: File exists` | The route is already present | Use `ip route replace` instead of `add` |
| Route vanishes after reboot | Routes are not persistent | Place in `/etc/network/interfaces` or `NetworkManager` dispatcher |
| Traffic goes the wrong way | Metric conflict / wrong interface | Check with `ip route get <dest>` and adjust metric |
| Permission denied | Running as a non-root user | Prefix with `sudo` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How `ip route add` Interacts with the Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Shell] --> B[Sudo Privileges Check]
    B --> C[ip route add Command Parser]
    C --> D{iproute2 Netlink Library}
    D --> E[Kernel Netlink Socket]
    E --> F[Routing Subsystem in Kernel]
    F --> G{New Route Validated?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Insert into Routing Table FIB]
    G -- No --> I[Return RTNETLINK Error to User]
    H --> J[Route Now Active for Forwarding]
    J --> K[ip route show Displays the Entry]
    J --> L[ip route get Resolves Destinations]
```

---

### 4.2 Workflow Diagram — Decision Flow When Adding a New Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: ip route add invoked] --> P[Parse destination, next-hop, interface]
    P --> Q{Next-hop or interface reachable?}
    Q -- No --> R1[Error: Network unreachable]
    Q -- Yes --> T{Route already exists?}
    T -- Yes --> R2[Error: File exists / use replace]
    T -- No --> U[Allocate FIB entry in kernel memory]
    U --> V[Bind entry to protocol = static]
    V --> W[Update kernel routing cache]
    W --> X[Success: route is now active]
    X --> Y[Notify userspace via RTM_NEWROUTE]
```

---

### 4.3 Network Topology — Typical Scenario for Adding a Static Route

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HostA[Host A: 10.0.0.5/24 on eth0] -->|via 10.0.0.1| R1[Router 1: 10.0.0.1]
    R1 -->|via 192.168.1.1| R2[Router 2: 192.168.1.1]
    R2 --> NetA[Target Network 172.16.50.0/24]
    HostA -.->|ip route add 172.16.50.0/24 via 10.0.0.1| HostA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed arrow inside `HostA` represents the static route being added to its routing table. After the route is installed, the solid path from `HostA` → `R1` → `R2` → `NetA` becomes the forwarding path for any packet destined to `172.16.50.0/24`.

---

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — `add` vs `replace` vs `change`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Command] --> B{Choose action}
    B -- Strict insert --> A1[ip route add]
    B -- Insert or update --> A2[ip route replace]
    B -- Modify existing --> A3[ip route change]
    A1 --> R1[Kernel: insert new entry; FAIL if exists]
    A2 --> R2[Kernel: insert if absent; UPSERT if exists]
    A3 --> R3[Kernel: modify parameters; FAIL if absent]
```

---

### 4.5 Persistence Architecture — Making Routes Survive Reboots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Add route in CLI] --> T{Route type?}
    T -- Temporary session --> T1[Lost on reboot]
    T -- Persistent Debian --> T2[Edit /etc/network/interfaces]
    T -- Persistent RHEL --> T3[Edit /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/route-ethX]
    T -- Persistent NetworkManager --> T4[nmcli con mod or dispatcher script]
    T -- Persistent systemd --> T5[systemd-networkd .network file]
    T2 --> F[Persisted across reboots]
    T3 --> F
    T4 --> F
    T5 --> F
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** What is the purpose of the `ip route add default via 192.168.1.1 dev eth0` command in a Linux system? Explain the meaning of each argument.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

This command adds a **default route** to the system's routing table.

- **`default`** — Represents the destination `0.0.0.0/0`, a catch-all address that matches every possible IP destination. Used when no specific route matches the packet's destination.
- **`via 192.168.1.1`** — Specifies the **next-hop IP address** (the gateway router) to which packets should be forwarded.
- **`dev eth0`** — Specifies the **outgoing network interface** (`eth0`) through which the packets will leave the host.

After execution, any packet whose destination does not match a more specific entry in the routing table will be forwarded to `192.168.1.1` via `eth0`. **(1 mark per argument, with overall 1 mark for stating the purpose.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between a **host route** and a **network route** in the Linux routing table. Give one example of each using the `ip route add` command.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Host Route | Network Route |
|---|---|---|
| Prefix length | `/32` (IPv4) | `/1` to `/31` |
| Matches | A single IP address | An entire subnet |
| Use case | Pinpoint a specific server (DB, printer) | Direct traffic to a corporate subnetwork |

- **Host route example:** `ip route add 203.0.113.42/32 via 10.10.10.1` — *(1.5 marks)*
- **Network route example:** `ip route add 192.168.20.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 dev eth0` — *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Set A]**

**Q (a)** Explain the structure of a Linux routing table entry. With a suitable network diagram, demonstrate how a new entry is added using the `ip route add` command when a host at `192.168.1.10/24` needs to reach the network `10.20.30.0/24` through router `192.168.1.1` on interface `eth0`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Linux routing table entry has the following fields: **Destination, Gateway (next-hop), Genmask, Flags, Metric, Ref, Use, Iface**. The `ip route` command presents them in a more compact form. **(1 mark)**

- **Destination** — Target network in CIDR notation.
- **Next-Hop (via)** — IP of the adjacent router.
- **Interface (dev)** — Outgoing port.
- **Metric** — Cost (lower is better).
- **Scope** — Propagation boundary.

**Diagram (described):** A host `H` (`192.168.1.10/24`) connects to router `R` (`192.168.1.1`). Router `R` is further connected to network `10.20.30.0/24`.

**Command:** `ip route add 10.20.30.0/24 via 192.168.1.1 dev eth0` **(3 marks)**

**Verification:** `ip route get 10.20.30.5` should return `10.20.30.5 via 192.168.1.1 dev eth0 src 192.168.1.10`. **(1 mark)**

**Explanation:** The kernel installs this as a static route. When the host generates a packet to any IP in `10.20.30.0/24`, the LPM algorithm matches this entry and forwards the packet to `192.168.1.1` through `eth0`. **(2 marks)**

---

**Q (b)** Describe the **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** rule used by the Linux kernel for routing decisions. Suppose the routing table contains the following two entries. Predict which route the kernel will choose for a packet destined to `172.16.5.99`, and justify your answer mathematically. **(7 marks)**

```
172.16.0.0/16   via 10.0.0.1
172.16.5.0/24   via 10.0.0.2
```

**Model Solution:**

The **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** rule states that when multiple routing table entries match a given destination IP, the entry with the **largest prefix length** is selected. **(2 marks)**

**Step 1 — Check matching entries for destination $D = 172.16.5.99$:**

- Entry 1: `172.16.0.0/16` — network range: `172.16.0.0` – `172.16.255.255` ✅ Match
- Entry 2: `172.16.5.0/24` — network range: `172.16.5.0` – `172.16.5.255` ✅ Match

Both entries match. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Apply LPM rule:**

$$
n_1 = 16, \quad n_2 = 24
$$

$$
n_2 > n_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 24 > 16
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

The kernel selects **Entry 2 (`172.16.5.0/24 via 10.0.0.2`)** because it has the longer prefix length (24 > 16). The packet will be forwarded to next-hop `10.0.0.2`. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Set B]**

**Q (a)** List and explain any **four types of routes** that can be added to the Linux routing table using the `ip route` command. Provide one example command for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Connected Route** — Automatically created by the kernel when an interface is configured with an IP. Example: `ip addr add 192.168.1.10/24 dev eth0` automatically adds `192.168.1.0/24 dev eth0` to the routing table. *(1.5 marks)*

**2. Static Network Route** — Manually added route to a specific destination network.
   Example: `ip route add 10.0.0.0/8 via 192.168.1.1 dev eth0` *(1.5 marks)*

**3. Host Route** — A `/32` route to a single IP address.
   Example: `ip route add 8.8.8.8/32 via 192.168.1.254` *(1 mark)*

**4. Default Route** — The catch-all route (`0.0.0.0/0`).
   Example: `ip route add default via 192.168.1.1 dev eth0` *(1.5 marks)*

**5. (Bonus) Blackhole Route** — A route that silently drops traffic to a destination.
   Example: `ip route add blackhole 10.10.10.0/24` *(1 mark)*

---

**Q (b)** A network administrator observes that a Linux server cannot reach a remote database at `203.0.113.50`. The server's routing table shows only the default route. Diagnose the issue and propose a step-by-step remediation plan using `ip route` commands. Justify each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagnosis (2 marks):** The `ip route show` command will reveal only:
```
default via 192.168.1.1 dev eth0
```
This means the server sends *all* external traffic to `192.168.1.1`. If the database is in a private subnet that is *not* directly reachable via the default gateway, the packets will be dropped.

**Remediation Plan:**

**Step 1** — Identify the network on which the database resides. Suppose the database is on `10.50.0.0/16`, accessible via a VPN router at `192.168.1.100`.
**Step 2** — Add a specific static route:
```
ip route add 10.50.0.0/16 via 192.168.1.100 dev eth0
```
*(2 marks)*

**Step 3** — Verify the new entry:
```
ip route show | grep 10.50
```
Expected output: `10.50.0.0/16 via 192.168.1.100 dev eth0`
*(1 mark)*

**Step 4** — Test connectivity:
```
ip route get 203.0.113.50
ping -c 4 203.0.113.50
```
*(1 mark)*

**Step 5** — (Optional but KTU-recommended) Make the route persistent by adding it to `/etc/network/interfaces`:
```
up ip route add 10.50.0.0/16 via 192.168.1.100 dev eth0
```
*(1 mark)*

**Justification:** The new static route takes precedence over the default route for traffic to `10.50.0.0/16` due to the LPM rule (prefix length 16 > 0). All other traffic continues to use the default route, leaving the rest of the routing untouched.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting `sudo`** → Command silently fails. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if not mentioned.
> 2. **Using deprecated `route add` syntax** → Marks may be partially deducted under the 2024 scheme (which emphasizes `iproute2`).
> 3. **Not specifying the `dev` interface** → Still works in many cases, but examiners prefer explicit interface declaration.
> 4. **Confusing `replace` with `change`** → `replace` upserts, `change` only modifies existing entries. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting that routes are non-persistent** → Examiners always test awareness of persistence (e.g., `/etc/network/interfaces`).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ The modern command is **`ip route`** (from `iproute2`), not the legacy `route`.
- ✅ General syntax: `ip route add <DEST>/<PREFIX> via <NH> [dev <IF>]`.
- ✅ **`default`** is shorthand for `0.0.0.0/0` (matches every destination).
- ✅ **Host route** = `/32` prefix; **Network route** = `/1` to `/31` prefix.
- ✅ **LPM rule** = entry with the **largest prefix length** always wins.
- ✅ Use `ip route show` to view, `ip route get <D>` to simulate, `ip route del` to remove.
- ✅ `ip route add` fails if route exists; use `ip route replace` to upsert.
- ✅ Routes added via CLI are **lost on reboot** — persist via `/etc/network/interfaces` or `NetworkManager`.
- ✅ Common error messages: `RTNETLINK answers: File exists` (duplicate), `RTNETLINK answers: Network is unreachable` (next-hop not connected).
- ✅ The metric parameter breaks ties between equal-prefix routes; lower metric is preferred.
- ✅ Always verify after adding: `ip route get <destination>` is the most reliable check.
- ✅ The `blackhole` route type silently discards traffic (useful for security blacklists).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Link Control (DLC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Link Control (DLC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Link Control (DLC)** is the set of protocols and mechanisms implemented at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI reference model that govern the **reliable transfer of frames** between two adjacent nodes connected by a physical link. It combines **Framing**, **Flow Control**, and **Error Control** to convert an unreliable raw bit stream into a reliable, ordered, and error-free communication channel.

The three primary functions of DLC are:

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| **Framing** | Encapsulates the network-layer packet into a manageable block (frame) with delimiters and control information. |
| **Flow Control** | Regulates the rate of frame transmission so that a fast sender does not overwhelm a slow receiver. |
| **Error Control** | Detects and retransmits lost or damaged frames using acknowledgment (ACK) and timer-based Automatic Repeat reQuest (ARQ) schemes. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two people, **A** (Sender) and **B** (Receiver), talking through a long, noisy pipe.

- **Framing** is like **breaking a long essay into paragraphs** — each paragraph is one "frame," making it easier to handle, retransmit if lost, and detect mistakes.
- **Flow Control** is like **A speaking slowly because B writes slowly** — A must wait for B's nod before sending the next sentence.
- **Error Control** is like **B saying "Repeat please"** when the words are garbled by pipe noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DLC in the KTU 2024 PCCST501 syllabus strictly covers **Framing, Flow Control (Stop-and-Wait, Sliding Window), Error Control (Stop-and-Wait ARQ, Go-Back-N ARQ, Selective-Repeat ARQ), and HDLC**. Piggybacking and HDLC frame structure are also examinable.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in DLC

- **Propagation Time ($T_p$):** Time taken by a bit to travel from sender to receiver across the medium. $T_p = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}}$
- **Transmission Time ($T_t$):** Time taken to push all the bits of a frame onto the medium. $T_t = \dfrac{\text{Frame Length (bits)}}{\text{Data Rate (bps)}}$
- **Round-Trip Time (RTT):** Time from sending a frame until the acknowledgment is received. **RTT = $2 T_p$** (ignoring transmission time of ACK).
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP):** Number of bits that can fill the link. $\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times T_p$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Framing Techniques

Framing divides the bit stream into discrete frames. Three classical methods exist:

1. **Fixed-Size Framing:** No framing needed if every frame has the same length (e.g., ATM uses 53-byte cells). Simple but inefficient for variable-length packets.
2. **Variable-Size Framing — Byte Count:** A header field declares the number of bytes in the frame. Error in count desynchronizes the entire stream.
3. **Variable-Size Framing — Byte Stuffing:** Uses special flag bytes (`FLAG = 01111110`). Sender inserts an extra `0` after every five consecutive `1`s inside the data (bit stuffing in HDLC). Receiver removes it.
4. **Variable-Size Framing — Bit Stuffing:** Used by HDLC/PPP. Flag = `01111110`. After every five `1`s, sender inserts a `0`. Receiver removes any `0` that follows five `1`s.

## 2.2 Flow Control Protocols

### 2.2.1 Stop-and-Wait Flow Control
- Sender transmits **one frame**, then waits for an acknowledgment (`ACK`) before sending the next.
- Wastes bandwidth if the link has a high bandwidth-delay product.

### 2.2.2 Sliding Window Flow Control
- Both sender and receiver maintain a **window** of sequence numbers.
- Sender can transmit multiple frames (up to window size) without waiting for individual ACKs.
- Increases **link utilization** dramatically on long-fat pipes.

## 2.3 Error Control Protocols (ARQ)

### 2.3.1 Stop-and-Wait ARQ
- Uses a **1-bit sequence number** (`0` or `1`).
- On timeout (frame or ACK lost), the sender retransmits the last frame.
- Duplicate frames at receiver are detected via sequence number and discarded.

### 2.3.2 Go-Back-N ARQ
- Sender window size $N_s \geq 1$; receiver window size $N_r = 1$.
- Receiver **discards** all out-of-order frames and sends a **cumulative ACK** for the last correctly received in-order frame.
- On error/timeout, sender goes back and retransmits that frame **and all subsequent frames**.

### 2.3.3 Selective Repeat ARQ
- Sender window size $N_s > 1$; receiver window size $N_r > 1$.
- Receiver **buffers** all correctly received frames (even out-of-order) and sends individual ACKs.
- On error, **only** the lost/damaged frame is retransmitted.
- Constraint: $N_s + N_r \leq 2^m$ where $m$ is the number of sequence-number bits.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory for KTU university exam numericals.** Memorize them.

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Transmission Time | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = frame length (bits), $R$ = data rate (bps) |
| Propagation Time | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = distance, $v$ = propagation speed |
| Utilization Parameter | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | Ratio of propagation to transmission time |
| Stop-and-Wait Utilization | $U_{SW} = \dfrac{1}{1+2a}$ | Maximum link utilization |
| Stop-and-Wait Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = R \cdot U_{SW}$ | Effective data rate |
| Sliding Window Utilization | $U_{SWIN} = \dfrac{W}{1+2a}$ | $W$ = window size (must be $< 2a+1$ for full capacity) |
| Go-Back-N Sender Window | $N_s \leq 2^m - 1$ | $m$ = sequence number bits |
| Selective Repeat Windows | $N_s + N_r \leq 2^m$ | Symmetric: $N_s = N_r = 2^{m-1}$ |

### Real-World Utility of DLC

- **HDLC** is the foundation for **PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)** used in dial-up and broadband internet connections.
- **Selective Repeat ARQ** variants power modern **Wi-Fi (802.11)** reliability mechanisms.
- **Sliding Window techniques** underlay **TCP's** sliding window flow & congestion control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stop-and-Wait Link Utilization ($U_{SW}$)

**Given:**
- $T_t$ = time to transmit one frame
- $T_p$ = propagation time in one direction
- ACK is assumed instantaneous / negligible in transmission time.

**Step 1: Total cycle time for one successful frame.**

The sender must wait for the frame to reach the receiver AND for the ACK to return before sending the next frame.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_t + T_p + T_p + T_{ACK}
$$

**Step 2: Neglect $T_{ACK}$ (small).**

$$
T_{\text{total}} \approx T_t + 2T_p
$$

**Step 3: Define utilization $U$ as fraction of time the link is used for useful frame transmission.**

$$
U = \frac{\text{Useful transmission time}}{\text{Total cycle time}}
$$

$$
U = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute $a = T_p / T_t$.**

Divide numerator and denominator by $T_t$:

$$
U_{SW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2 \left( \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} \right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{U_{SW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}}
$$

**Step 5: Throughput.**

$$
\text{Throughput}_{SW} = R \times U_{SW} = \frac{R}{1+2a}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Sliding Window Utilization (Go-Back-N / Selective Repeat)

**Step 1: Sender can transmit up to $W$ frames back-to-back.**

Time to transmit $W$ frames:

$$
T_{W} = W \cdot T_t
$$

**Step 2: Time until the first ACK returns.**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 2T_p + T_t \quad \text{(approximately)}
$$

**Step 3: Utilization.**

$$
U = \frac{W \cdot T_t}{2T_p + T_t}
$$

$$
\boxed{U = \frac{W}{1 + 2a}}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum utilization.**

If $W \geq 1 + 2a$, the sender never has to wait and utilization $U \to 1$ (100%).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 (KTU-Style 3-Mark Numerical)

> **Problem:** A channel has a bit rate of **4 kbps** and a propagation delay of **20 ms**. Frame size is **200 bits**. Find:
> (i) Transmission time, (ii) Utilization for Stop-and-Wait.

**Solution:**

(i) Transmission time:

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{200 \text{ bits}}{4000 \text{ bps}} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}
$$

Propagation time: $T_p = 20 \text{ ms}$

(ii) Parameter $a$:

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{20}{50} = 0.4
$$

Stop-and-Wait utilization:

$$
U_{SW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.8} = \frac{1}{1.8} \approx 0.555
$$

$$
\boxed{U_{SW} \approx 55.5\%}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 (KTU-Style 14-Mark Numerical)

> **Problem:** A satellite link has a **1 Mbps** data rate. Frame length **L = 2000 bits**. One-way propagation delay **T_p = 270 ms**. Acknowledgment time is negligible. Find:
> (a) The link utilization using Stop-and-Wait ARQ.
> (b) The minimum window size $W$ such that Go-Back-N achieves **100%** utilization.
> (c) The number of sequence bits $m$ required if Selective Repeat ARQ is used with $W = 7$.

**Solution:**

(a) Transmission time:

$$
T_t = \frac{2000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bps}} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

Parameter $a$:

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{270}{2} = 135
$$

Stop-and-Wait utilization:

$$
U_{SW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 270} = \frac{1}{271} \approx 0.00369
$$

$$
\boxed{U_{SW} \approx 0.369\%}
$$

This shows how inefficient Stop-and-Wait is on long-delay links.

(b) For 100% utilization:

$$
W \geq 1 + 2a = 1 + 270 = 271
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{\min} = 271 \text{ frames}}
$$

(c) For Selective Repeat with $W = 7$:

Required: $N_s + N_r \leq 2^m$ and $N_s = N_r = 7$.

$$
7 + 7 \leq 2^m \implies 2^m \geq 14 \implies m = 4
$$

$$
\boxed{m = 4 \text{ sequence bits}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of ARQ Protocols

| Parameter | Stop-and-Wait ARQ | Go-Back-N ARQ | Selective Repeat ARQ |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Sender Window $N_s$ | **1** | $\leq 2^m - 1$ | $\leq 2^{m-1}$ |
| Receiver Window $N_r$ | **1** | **1** | $> 1$ |
| Sequence Number Bits $m$ | 1 | $m$ | $m$ |
| Retransmission on Error | Last frame | Last frame + all subsequent | Only the lost frame |
| Receiver Buffer | No | No | Yes |
| Complexity | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Efficiency (high error) | Low | Very Low | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stop-and-Wait ARQ — Time Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant R as Receiver
    S->>R: Frame 0 (T_t)
    Note over S,R: Propagation delay T_p
    R->>S: ACK 1 (T_p)
    S->>R: Frame 1 (T_t)
    R-->>S: ACK 0
    Note over S,R: Idle/wait time wasted
```

## 4.2 Go-Back-N ARQ — Loss and Recovery

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant R as Receiver
    S->>R: Frame 0
    S->>R: Frame 1
    S->>R: Frame 2
    R-->>S: ACK 1
    S->>R: Frame 3 (LOST)
    S->>R: Frame 4
    Note over R: Discard Frame 4 (out of order)
    R-->>S: ACK 1 (still expecting Frame 2)
    Note over S: Timeout for Frame 3
    S->>R: Frame 3 (RETRANSMIT)
    S->>R: Frame 4 (RETRANSMIT)
    R-->>S: ACK 5 (cumulative)
```

## 4.3 Selective Repeat ARQ — Loss and Selective Retransmission

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant R as Receiver
    S->>R: Frame 0
    S->>R: Frame 1
    S->>R: Frame 2
    S->>R: Frame 3
    R-->>S: ACK 0
    R-->>S: ACK 1
    Note over R: Frame 2 lost!
    R-->>S: NAK 2
    S->>R: Frame 2 (RETRANSMIT ONLY)
    R-->>S: ACK 2
    R-->>S: ACK 3 (delivered in order now)
```

## 4.4 HDLC Frame Structure

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 11
    block:frame["HDLC FRAME STRUCTURE"]
        columns 11
        f["FLAG 8b"]:::flag
        a["ADDRESS 8b"]:::addr
        c["CONTROL 8b or 16b"]:::ctrl
        i["INFORMATION variable"]:::info
        fcs["FCS 16b or 32b"]:::fcs
        e["FLAG 8b"]:::flag
    end
    classDef flag fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef addr fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef info fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fcs fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Description of HDLC Fields:**
- **Flag (`01111110`):** Opening and closing delimiter. Bit stuffing is applied between flags.
- **Address:** Identifies the secondary station in point-to-multipoint links.
- **Control:** Identifies frame type (Information `I`, Supervisory `S`, Unnumbered `U`) and contains sequence numbers.
- **Information:** Payload from upper layer (only present in `I`-frames and some `U`-frames).
- **FCS (Frame Check Sequence):** CRC-16 or CRC-32 for error detection.

## 4.5 Sliding Window — Sender & Receiver Window Movement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sender Window W=5"]:::send
    B["Pipelined Frames 0-4"]:::pipe
    C["Receiver Window W=1 to 5"]:::recv
    A --> B --> C
    classDef send fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef pipe fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef recv fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the Data Link Layer. Explain the various framing techniques.**

**Model Answer (12–15 lines):**

The **Data Link Layer** is Layer 2 of the OSI model responsible for **node-to-node data transfer**, transforming a stream of bits into discrete, manageable units called **frames**, and ensuring reliable transmission across the physical link.

**Framing techniques:**

1. **Fixed-Size Framing:** All frames are the same length. No delimiters needed; receiver counts bytes. Example: ATM cells (53 bytes).

2. **Variable-Size Framing — Byte Count:** A header field carries the number of bytes in the frame. Disadvantage: a single error in the count desynchronizes the entire stream.

3. **Variable-Size Framing — Byte Stuffing:** Special bytes such as `FLAG` mark frame boundaries. If `FLAG` appears in data, it is preceded by an escape byte (`ESC`). Disadvantage: 8-bit alignment dependency.

4. **Variable-Size Framing — Bit Stuffing (HDLC):** Flag = `01111110`. Sender inserts a `0` after every five consecutive `1`s in the data. Receiver removes any `0` that follows five `1`s. This is robust and bit-oriented, used in HDLC and PPP.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Differentiate between Flow Control and Error Control.**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

| Aspect | Flow Control | Error Control |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| **Goal** | Prevent the sender from overwhelming a slow receiver. | Detect and recover from lost, corrupted, or duplicate frames. |
| **Mechanism** | Uses ACK feedback to pace the sender. | Uses ACK/NAK, timers, and sequence numbers; retransmission via ARQ. |
| **Example Protocols** | Stop-and-Wait, Sliding Window. | Stop-and-Wait ARQ, Go-Back-N ARQ, Selective Repeat ARQ. |
| **Problem Addressed** | Speed mismatch between sender and receiver. | Bit errors and frame loss on the channel. |
| **Limitation Handled** | Receiver buffer overflow. | Unreliable transmission medium. |

Flow control handles **speed mismatch**, while error control handles **unreliability**. They are **complementary** and together provide reliable data-link communication.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part B carries **14 marks** and must be answered in detail. Internal choice: students may attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Explain **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** in detail with a suitable diagram. Discuss its **efficiency** and derive its utilization formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A channel has a **bandwidth of 1 Mbps** and a **propagation delay of 20 ms**. Frame size is **1000 bits**. Compute the **efficiency** and **throughput** of Stop-and-Wait ARQ. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Stop-and-Wait ARQ (7 marks):**

**Concept:**
- Sender transmits a single frame and waits for an acknowledgment (ACK).
- A **1-bit sequence number** (`0` or `1`) distinguishes new frames from retransmissions.
- A **timer** is started when a frame is sent. If it expires before ACK is received, the frame is **retransmitted**.
- The receiver discards duplicate frames (detected by sequence number) and resends the ACK.

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 above — mention in words):**
Sender → Frame 0 → (Propagation $T_p$) → Receiver → ACK 1 → (Propagation $T_p$) → Sender → Frame 1 → …

**Derivation of utilization:**

Let $T_t$ = transmission time, $T_p$ = one-way propagation time.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_t + 2T_p
$$

$$
U = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(T_p/T_t)}
$$

Substituting $a = T_p / T_t$:

$$
\boxed{U_{SW} = \frac{1}{1+2a}}
$$

**Limitations:**
- Highly inefficient for long-delay / high-bandwidth links (e.g., satellite).
- Bandwidth-Delay Product ($BDP = R \cdot T_p$) can be enormous; one frame cannot fill the pipe.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given:
- $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps
- $T_p = 20$ ms $= 0.02$ s
- $L = 1000$ bits

Transmission time:

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

Compute $a$:

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{20}{1} = 20
$$

Efficiency:

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2(20)} = \frac{1}{41} \approx 0.0244 = 2.44\%
$$

Throughput:

$$
\text{Throughput} = R \times U = 10^6 \times \frac{1}{41} \approx 24{,}390 \text{ bps} \approx 24.4 \text{ kbps}
$$

$$
\boxed{U \approx 2.44\%, \quad \text{Throughput} \approx 24.4 \text{ kbps}}
$$

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Explain **Go-Back-N ARQ** and **Selective Repeat ARQ** with diagrams. Compare them in terms of **window size**, **retransmission strategy**, and **receiver complexity**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a **bandwidth of 1 Gbps** and **propagation delay of 15 ms**, compute the **minimum window size $W$** for full utilization with a frame length of **1500 bytes**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

**Go-Back-N ARQ:**
- Sender window: $W_s \leq 2^m - 1$. Receiver window: $W_r = 1$.
- On timeout/NAK, sender retransmits the **errored frame and all subsequent** frames already sent.
- Receiver **discards** out-of-order frames and sends a **cumulative ACK** for the next expected in-order frame.
- **Advantage:** Simple receiver (no buffer). **Disadvantage:** Wastes bandwidth by retransmitting correctly received frames.

**Selective Repeat ARQ:**
- Sender window: $W_s \leq 2^{m-1}$. Receiver window: $W_r > 1$.
- Receiver **accepts and buffers** out-of-order frames; sends individual ACKs.
- On NAK/timeout, sender retransmits **only** the lost frame.
- **Advantage:** Efficient under high error rates. **Disadvantage:** Receiver needs buffer + more complex logic.
- **Constraint:** $W_s + W_r \leq 2^m$.

| Feature | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| Receiver window | 1 | $> 1$ |
| Retransmissions | All post-error | Only the lost one |
| Receiver buffer | No | Yes |
| Best for | Low error rates | High error rates |

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $R = 10^9$ bps, $T_p = 15$ ms, $L = 1500 \times 8 = 12{,}000$ bits.

Transmission time:

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12{,}000}{10^9} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 12 \, \mu s
$$

Parameter $a$:

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{12 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{15{,}000}{12} = 1250
$$

Minimum window for full utilization:

$$
W \geq 1 + 2a = 1 + 2500 = 2501
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{\min} = 2501 \text{ frames}}
$$

Bandwidth-Delay Product sanity check:

$$
BDP = R \cdot T_p = 10^9 \times 15 \times 10^{-3} = 15 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ bits}
$$

This matches $W \times L = 2501 \times 12{,}000 = 30{,}012{,}000$ bits, confirming the link can be kept full.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $T_p$ given in **ms** but $T_t$ in **$\mu$s** — convert both to seconds before computing $a$. Mixing units is the #1 reason students lose 3–5 marks.
> 2. **Wrong window-size formula:** Go-Back-N needs $W \leq 2^m - 1$, Selective Repeat needs $W_s + W_r \leq 2^m$. Confusing these costs full 7-mark marks in numericals.
> 3. **Missing "frame lost" details:** When asked about ARQ, students forget to mention **timer expiration** and **sequence numbers**. Examiners explicitly look for these in 7-mark theory questions.
> 4. **HDLC frame drawing:** Always show **both flags** at start and end of frame, and **mention bit stuffing** if asked.
> 5. **Not labelling diagrams:** A diagram without labels of `T_p`, `T_t`, ACK, and frame numbers fetches only partial marks (typically 50% credit).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Link Control (DLC):**
>
> - DLC = **Framing + Flow Control + Error Control** at Layer 2.
> - **Stop-and-Wait** is simple but inefficient on long-delay links; $U_{SW} = \dfrac{1}{1+2a}$.
> - **Sliding Window** pipelines multiple frames; $U = \dfrac{W}{1+2a}$.
> - **Go-Back-N ARQ:** Receiver window = 1, retransmits all post-error frames; needs $W_s \leq 2^m - 1$.
> - **Selective Repeat ARQ:** Receiver buffers out-of-order frames; needs $W_s + W_r \leq 2^m$.
> - **HDLC** is **bit-oriented**, uses **bit stuffing**, has Flag `01111110`, and a Control field that defines `I`, `S`, and `U` frames.
> - **Definition of $a$:** Always compute as $a = T_p / T_t$ — **propagation over transmission time**.
> - **Efficiency rule:** For 100% utilization, sliding window $W \geq 1 + 2a$.
> - **Satellite / long-fat links** always require very large windows; Stop-and-Wait is unusable.
> - **Memorize:** $T_t = L/R$, $T_p = d/v$, $\text{RTT} = 2T_p$, $\text{BDP} = R \cdot T_p$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Access Protocols (MAC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiple Access Protocols (MAC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **Data Link Layer (DLL)** of the OSI / TCP-IP reference model, the sub-layer responsible for deciding **"who gets to transmit on a shared medium, and when,"** is known as the **Medium Access Control (MAC) sub-layer**. The set of rules and mechanisms it employs to arbitrate this access among multiple competing stations is collectively called a **Multiple Access Protocol (MAC Protocol)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A Multiple Access Protocol is a set of rules that govern the access of multiple stations to a shared communication channel (broadcast link / multipoint link) such that the available bandwidth of the channel is utilized efficiently, with minimum collisions and maximum throughput.

### 1.2 Why is it Needed? — The Shared Medium Problem

When several devices (stations) are connected to a **single physical communication channel** (such as a coaxial Ethernet bus, a wireless frequency band, or a satellite uplink), **only one signal can be transmitted successfully at any given instant** in that frequency band. If two stations transmit simultaneously, the signals interfere — a phenomenon called a **collision** — and the data becomes unintelligible.

The **Multiple Access Problem** is therefore the problem of:
- **Allocating channel capacity** to many competing users.
- **Avoiding or resolving collisions** when they occur.
- **Maximizing throughput** while **minimizing delay**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Coffee Shop Conversation"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Multi-Talker Coffee Shop**
>
> Imagine you are sitting in a coffee shop with 10 friends. The "shared medium" is the air in the room. Everyone wants to talk, but if two people speak at the same time, no one understands anything. How do you manage this?
>
> 1. **Pure ALOHA:** Everyone just starts talking whenever they feel like it. When two people collide (talk over each other), both stop, wait a random time, and try again. → *Inefficient, lots of confusion.*
> 2. **Slotted ALOHA:** The shop owner installs a clock that ticks every second. You can ONLY start talking at the "tick." This halves the vulnerable window of collision.
> 3. **CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access):** Before you speak, you *listen*. If the air is silent, you speak. This is the polite version — "Don't speak when someone is already talking."
> 4. **CSMA/CD (with Collision Detection):** While you are speaking, you *also listen*. If you hear someone else talking simultaneously, you immediately stop, apologize, wait a random backoff time, and try again. (Used in classic Ethernet.)
> 5. **CSMA/CA (with Collision Avoidance):** Since you can't listen and talk at the same time on a wireless channel, you first *announce your intention* (RTS/CTS handshake) before transmitting, to *avoid* collisions proactively. (Used in Wi-Fi.)
> 6. **Token Passing:** A physical "talking stick" is passed around. Only the person holding the stick may talk. When done, they pass it to the next person. → *Zero collisions, but overhead of token passing.*
> 7. **TDMA/FDMA/CDMA:** The shop owner divides the room into time slots (TDMA), frequency bands (FDMA), or assigns unique "voice codes" (CDMA) so that multiple conversations can happen in parallel without interference.

### 1.4 Classification of Multiple Access Protocols (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

The three broad families into which every MAC protocol falls are tabulated below:

| **Family** | **Access Philosophy** | **Collision Behavior** | **Representative Protocols** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Random Access (Contention-Based)** | Stations compete freely for the channel | Collisions **allowed**, then resolved by retransmission | ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA |
| **Controlled Access (Deterministic)** | A controlling mechanism grants permission | **No collisions** by design | Reservation, Polling, Token Passing |
| **Channelization (Static Partitioning)** | Channel bandwidth is statically divided | **No collisions** (orthogonal partitions) | FDMA, TDMA, CDMA |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 RANDOM ACCESS PROTOCOLS

#### 2.1.1 Pure ALOHA

**Operational Logic:**
- A station has a frame ready → it **transmits immediately** without checking the channel.
- The receiver acknowledges (ACK) on successful receipt.
- If no ACK arrives within a timeout → the sender assumes a collision occurred.
- Sender waits for a **random backoff time** and **retransmits**.

**Why and How:**
- **Why immediate transmission?** Because ALOHA was designed for satellite / rural wireless networks where propagation delay dominates, and listening before transmitting does not help much.
- **How is collision detected?** Through **lack of ACK** (no positive feedback = assumption of collision).
- **Vulnerable Time:** A frame of duration $T_{fr}$ is vulnerable to collision for a window of $2 \cdot T_{fr}$ — a colliding frame arriving anywhere within $\pm T_{fr}$ of the start of our frame will destroy it.

#### 2.1.2 Slotted ALOHA

**Operational Logic:**
- Time is divided into **discrete slots**, each of duration $T_{fr}$ (one frame length).
- A station is allowed to transmit **only at the beginning of a slot**.
- If collision occurs, retransmit in a *future* randomly-chosen slot.

**Why and How:**
- **Why slots?** Discrete time synchronization **halves the vulnerable window** from $2 \cdot T_{fr}$ to $T_{fr}$ because now a colliding frame must arrive in the *same slot*, not an adjacent partial slot.
- **Effect on throughput:** Doubles the maximum throughput of pure ALOHA.

#### 2.1.3 CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access)

**Operational Logic:** "Listen before talk."
- Station senses the carrier (channel) before transmitting.
- If channel is **idle** → transmit.
- If channel is **busy** → defer transmission (various strategies below).

**Variants of CSMA (based on what a station does when channel is busy):**

| **Persistence Strategy** | **Behavior on Busy Channel** | **Throughput** | **Delay** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1-persistent CSMA** | Wait for channel to become idle, then **transmit immediately** with probability **1** | High (greedy) | High collision risk |
| **Non-persistent CSMA** | Wait a **random** time before sensing again | Lower | Lower delay variance |
| **p-persistent CSMA** (slotted) | If idle, transmit with probability **p**; otherwise defer to next slot with probability $(1-p)$ | Tunable | Tunable |

**Why and How:**
- **Why does CSMA fail in long-distance networks?** Because of **propagation delay** $T_p$. A station at distance $D$ senses the channel idle, but a frame from a far station may still be *in transit*. This means CSMA cannot eliminate collisions — only reduce them.

#### 2.1.4 CSMA/CD (Collision Detection)

**Operational Logic:** "Listen while talk."
- Combines CSMA with **collision detection** during transmission.
- If collision is detected → **abort transmission immediately**, send a **jam signal** (to ensure all other stations also detect the collision), wait a **random backoff**, and retry.
- Used in **classical Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**.

**Why and How:**
- **Why abort immediately?** A long frame that has already collided wastes channel time. Stopping early frees the channel sooner.
- **Why jam signal?** The collision produces a small voltage spike; the jam signal is a deliberately strong 32-bit pattern that guarantees all stations register the collision.
- **Critical Requirement:** The frame transmission time $T_{fr}$ must be at least $2 \cdot T_p$ (twice the maximum propagation delay) so that the sender is still transmitting when the collision signal returns. This is the basis of the Ethernet **minimum frame size of 64 bytes**.

#### 2.1.5 CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)

**Operational Logic:** "Announce before talk."
- Used in **wireless LANs (IEEE 802.11 / Wi-Fi)** where **collision detection is impossible** because a station cannot listen and transmit on the same frequency simultaneously (the transmitted signal dwarfs any incoming signal at the antenna).
- Uses **RTS/CTS handshake** to virtually reserve the channel.
- Uses **ACK** to confirm successful reception.
- Uses **interframe spaces (DIFS, SIFS)** to prioritize access.

**Why and How:**
- **Why not detect collisions in wireless?** Because of the **near-far problem** and the fact that the transmitted energy at the antenna is $\approx 10^9$ times stronger than any received energy — the receiver's circuitry is saturated.
- **How does RTS/CTS avoid hidden node problems?** Even if Station C cannot hear Station A, it WILL hear the CTS sent by Station B (destination), so it knows the medium is reserved.

---

### 2.2 CONTROLLED ACCESS PROTOCOLS

#### 2.2.1 Reservation
- Stations first **reserve** future time slots using small control frames.
- Once reserved, only the reserving station may transmit in that slot.
- Used in **satellite and cellular systems** (e.g., reservation ALOHA).

#### 2.2.2 Polling
- A **primary controller (master)** polls each secondary station in turn.
- **Roll-Call Polling:** Master sends a "Select" message to each station in a fixed order.
- **Hub Polling:** Stations form a logical ring; the "poll" token is forwarded.
- Drawback: **Single point of failure** (master) and **polling overhead**.

#### 2.2.3 Token Passing
- A small control frame called a **token** circulates among stations in a logical ring.
- A station may transmit **only when it holds the token**.
- After transmitting (or if it has nothing to send), it passes the token to the next station.
- Used in **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)** and **Token Bus (IEEE 802.4)**.
- **Drawback:** Token loss, token duplication, and insertion/removal overhead.

---

### 2.3 CHANNELIZATION PROTOCOLS

#### 2.3.1 FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)
- The total bandwidth $B$ of the channel is divided into **$N$ non-overlapping frequency bands**, each allocated to one station.
- Each band acts as an independent channel (with guard bands between them to prevent inter-channel interference).
- **Used in:** Analog cellular (1G AMPS), FM radio broadcasting, traditional TV.

#### 2.3.2 TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)
- All stations use the **same frequency band** but transmit in **non-overlapping time slots** in a repeating frame structure.
- Requires **precise time synchronization** between stations.
- **Used in:** 2G GSM (combined with FDMA), satellite systems, Bluetooth (combined with FHSS).

#### 2.3.3 CDMA (Code Division Multiple Access)
- All stations transmit **simultaneously on the same frequency and at the same time**, but each is assigned a **unique orthogonal pseudo-noise (PN) code**.
- The receiver correlates the incoming signal with the intended sender's code to extract the original data, treating other senders' signals as low-level noise.
- **Used in:** 3G cellular (UMTS, CDMA2000), GPS, WCDMA.
- **Key property:** Soft capacity, inherent security (codes are private), resistance to narrowband interference.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** In all throughput formulas, $G$ = **average number of transmission attempts per frame time** (including both new frames and retransmissions), and $S$ = **average number of successful transmissions per frame time** (throughput).

| **Protocol** | **Throughput $S$ (as function of $G$)** | **Max Throughput $S_{max}$** | **Occurs at $G =$** | **Vulnerable Time** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pure ALOHA** | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ (i.e., $\approx 18.4\%$) | $G = 0.5$ | $2 \cdot T_{fr}$ |
| **Slotted ALOHA** | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ (i.e., $\approx 36.8\%$) | $G = 1.0$ | $T_{fr}$ |
| **CSMA/CD (Ethernet, persistent)** | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}$ (approx.) | $\approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.6$ | — | Prop. delay dependent |
| **FDMA** | $S = \dfrac{1}{N}$ (ideal, no overhead) | $1/N$ | — | 0 (no collisions) |
| **TDMA** | $S = \dfrac{1}{N}$ (ideal, no overhead) | $1/N$ | — | 0 (no collisions) |
| **CDMA (ideal)** | $S \approx 1$ (orthogonal codes) | $\approx 1$ | — | 0 (no collisions) |

**Where:**
- $T_{fr} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ = frame transmission time, with $L$ = frame length (bits) and $R$ = channel bit rate (bps).
- $T_p = \dfrac{D}{V}$ = one-way propagation delay, with $D$ = cable length (m) and $V$ = signal propagation speed (m/s). For copper, $V \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_{fr}}$ = **normalized propagation delay** (a key dimensionless parameter).
- **Ethernet Rule:** $T_{fr} \geq 2 \cdot T_p$ (i.e., $a \leq 0.5$), which gives the minimum frame size of **64 bytes** at **10 Mbps** for a max segment of **2500 m** with 4 repeaters.

**Maximum Frame Size Relationship (CSMA/CD Ethernet):**
$$\text{Min Frame Size} = 2 \times T_p \times R$$

Or equivalently:
$$L_{min} = 2 \times \dfrac{D}{V} \times R$$

**Real-world utility of these formulas:** Network engineers use the ALOHA throughput curves to size satellite uplink channels, choose backoff algorithms, and predict the load at which a network will "saturate" and collapse.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Throughput of Pure ALOHA

**Assumption:** Infinite number of stations generating *new* frames according to a Poisson process with mean rate $\lambda$ frames per frame-time. Including retransmissions, the total attempted load is $G$ frames per frame-time. The combined (new + retransmitted) attempts also follow Poisson statistics with mean $G$.

**Step 1 — Probability of zero other transmissions in the vulnerable window.**

The vulnerable window for a frame of duration $T_{fr}$ in Pure ALOHA is $2 \cdot T_{fr}$ — a collision can be caused by any other transmission starting anywhere in the interval $[t_0 - T_{fr}, \, t_0 + T_{fr}]$, i.e., a window of length $2 T_{fr}$.

For a Poisson process with mean $G$ attempts per $T_{fr}$, the mean number of attempts in $2T_{fr}$ is $2G$. The probability of $k$ attempts is:

$$P[k] = \dfrac{(2G)^k \cdot e^{-2G}}{k!}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of successful transmission.**

A frame succeeds only if **no other station transmits** during the vulnerable window, i.e., $k = 0$:

$$P[\text{success}] = P[0] = e^{-2G}$$

**Step 3 — Throughput equation.**

Throughput $S$ = (transmission rate per frame-time) × (probability each is successful):

$$S = G \cdot P[\text{success}] = G \cdot e^{-2G}$$

This is the **Pure ALOHA throughput formula**.

**Step 4 — Maximum throughput (peak).**

Differentiate with respect to $G$ and set to zero:

$$\dfrac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) \cdot e^{-2G} = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0$$

Solving $1 - 2G = 0$ gives $G = 0.5$. Substituting back:

$$S_{max} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-2(0.5)} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.1839$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Pure ALOHA can sustain a useful throughput of at most **~18.4%** of the raw channel capacity. The remaining **~81.6%** is wasted on collisions and idle time.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Throughput of Slotted ALOHA

**Modification:** Now a frame may only be transmitted at the *start* of a slot of duration $T_{fr}$. The vulnerable window shrinks to exactly $T_{fr}$ (only attempts in the *same* slot cause a collision).

**Step 1 — Mean attempts in one slot** = $G$ (since slot = $T_{fr}$).

**Step 2 — Probability of zero other attempts in the slot:**

$$P[\text{success}] = e^{-G}$$

**Step 3 — Throughput equation:**

$$S = G \cdot e^{-G}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum throughput:**

$$\dfrac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G} - G e^{-G} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \implies G = 1$$

Substituting back:

$$S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.3678$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison:** Slotted ALOHA doubles the maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA ($1/e$ vs $1/2e$) — but at the cost of requiring **global time synchronization** between all stations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Frame Size in CSMA/CD Ethernet

**Given:**
- Maximum cable length: $D = 2500$ m (with 4 repeaters)
- Propagation speed in copper: $V = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Data rate: $R = 10$ Mbps $= 10^7$ bps

**Required:** Minimum frame size $L_{min}$ such that the sender is still transmitting when the collision signal returns.

**Step 1 — One-way propagation delay:**

$$T_p = \dfrac{D}{V} = \dfrac{2500 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$$

$$T_p = 12.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 12.5 \ \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip propagation delay (collision must travel back to sender):**

$$T_{rt} = 2 \cdot T_p = 2 \times 12.5 \ \mu s = 25 \ \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Minimum frame transmission time:**

For the sender to still be on the air when the collision returns:

$$T_{fr} \geq T_{rt} = 25 \ \mu s$$

**Step 4 — Minimum frame size in bits:**

$$L_{min} = R \cdot T_{fr} = 10 \times 10^6 \ \text{bits/s} \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{s}$$

$$L_{min} = 250 \ \text{bits} = \dfrac{250}{8} = 31.25 \ \text{bytes}$$

**Step 5 — Actual IEEE 802.3 specification:**

The IEEE 802.3 standard mandates a minimum frame size of **512 bits = 64 bytes** to provide a safety margin (factor of 2) and to account for repeater delay. This explains why **all Ethernet frames must be at least 64 bytes**, and why *short* frames (called *runts*) are discarded by switches as collision fragments.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Favorite Table)

| **Property** | **Pure ALOHA** | **Slotted ALOHA** | **CSMA** | **CSMA/CD** | **CSMA/CA** | **Token Passing** | **TDMA/FDMA/CDMA** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carrier Sensing | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | N/A (controlled) | N/A |
| Collision Detection | No | No | No | Yes (wired) | No (wireless — uses avoidance) | Impossible by design | Impossible by design |
| Maximum Throughput | 18.4% | 36.8% | Depends on $a$ | 50–60% | Lower than CD due to overhead | ~100% in light load | ~100% in full load |
| Synchronization | None | Slot-level | None | None | Slot-level (DIFS/SIFS) | Token order | Time / Frequency / Code |
| Best For | Satellite, low traffic | Slotted satellite | Low-load LANs | Wired Ethernet | Wi-Fi (802.11) | Industrial LANs | Cellular, satellite, GPS |
| Vulnerability to Single Pt. Failure | No | No | No | No | No | Yes (token loss) | No |
