## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Classification of MAC Protocols

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MAC Protocols] --> B[Random Access]
    A --> C[Controlled Access]
    A --> D[Channelization]

    B --> B1[Pure ALOHA]
    B --> B2[Slotted ALOHA]
    B --> B3[CSMA]
    B --> B4[CSMA-CD]
    B --> B5[CSMA-CA]

    B3 --> B3a[1-Persistent]
    B3 --> B3b[Non-Persistent]
    B3 --> B3c[p-Persistent]

    C --> C1[Reservation]
    C --> C2[Polling]
    C --> C3[Token Passing]

    C2 --> C2a[Roll-Call Polling]
    C2 --> C2b[Hub Polling]

    D --> D1[FDMA]
    D --> D2[TDMA]
    D --> D3[CDMA]
```

### 4.2 Throughput vs Load Curve (Pure ALOHA vs Slotted ALOHA)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph X["Throughput S vs Load G"]
        direction LR
        A1[Pure ALOHA Peak: S=0.184 at G=0.5] --> A2[Slotted ALOHA Peak: S=0.368 at G=1.0]
        A2 --> A3[CSMA-CD Peak: S=0.5 to 0.6]
        A3 --> A4[Token Passing: S approaches 1.0 at light load]
    end
```

### 4.3 CSMA/CD Collision Detection — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Station has frame to send] --> S2{Sense carrier}
    S2 -- Idle --> S3[Start transmission]
    S2 -- Busy --> S4[Wait using backoff algorithm]
    S4 --> S2
    S3 --> S5{Monitor channel for collision signal}
    S5 -- Collision detected --> S6[Abort transmission]
    S6 --> S7[Send jam signal]
    S7 --> S8[Compute random backoff time]
    S8 --> S2
    S5 -- No collision --> S9[Frame transmitted successfully]
    S9 --> S10[Wait for ACK]
    S10 -- ACK received --> S11[Done]
    S10 -- ACK timeout --> S12[Assume collision or loss]
    S12 --> S8
```

### 4.4 CSMA/CA RTS-CTS Handshake — Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    W1[Station S wants to send to D] --> W2[Wait for DIFS]
    W2 --> W3[Send RTS to D]
    W3 --> W4{Channel clear around D?}
    W4 -- No --> W5[Wait and retry]
    W5 --> W2
    W4 -- Yes --> W6[D sends CTS to S]
    W6 --> W7[All stations hearing CTS defer for NAV duration]
    W7 --> W8[S transmits data frame]
    W8 --> W9[D waits SIFS then sends ACK]
    W9 --> W10[Transmission complete]
```

### 4.5 FDMA / TDMA / CDMA — Visual Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Shared Channel Capacity] --> B[FDMA: split by FREQUENCY]
    A --> C[TDMA: split by TIME]
    A --> D[CDMA: split by CODE]

    B --> B1[Station 1: f1 to f2]
    B --> B2[Station 2: f3 to f4]
    B --> B3[Station 3: f5 to f6]

    C --> C1[Slot 1: Station 1]
    C --> C2[Slot 2: Station 2]
    C --> C3[Slot 3: Station 3]

    D --> D1[All stations: same f, same t]
    D --> D2[Unique PN code per station]
    D --> D3[Receiver correlates with code]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA. Compare their maximum throughputs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Pure ALOHA** | **Slotted ALOHA** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time division | Continuous, no slots | Discrete time slots of $T_{fr}$ |
| Transmission rule | Any time | Only at start of slot |
| Vulnerable window | $2T_{fr}$ | $T_{fr}$ |
| Throughput formula | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ |
| Maximum throughput | $\frac{1}{2e} \approx 18.4\%$ | $\frac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ |
| Synchronization | Not required | Required |

Slotted ALOHA doubles the efficiency of Pure ALOHA but requires strict time synchronization among all stations.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is CSMA/CD not used in wireless networks? What alternative is used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CSMA/CD relies on **collision detection** during transmission, which requires the station to *listen while transmitting*. In a wireless medium, the transmitted signal at the antenna is approximately **60 dB stronger** than any received signal, saturating the receiver circuitry. Hence, a wireless station **cannot detect a collision** while it is transmitting (also called the *near-far problem*).

Instead, wireless networks (IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi) use **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)**, which proactively avoids collisions through:
1. **Carrier sensing** before transmission.
2. **RTS/CTS handshake** to virtually reserve the channel.
3. **ACK** from the receiver to confirm successful reception.
4. **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** to keep other stations silent during the exchange.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice: Answer ONE)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the working of the **CSMA/CD** protocol with a neat diagram. How does it differ from CSMA? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **throughput equation of Pure ALOHA** and show that its maximum throughput is $\frac{1}{2e}$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CSMA/CD Working (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access) attempts to reduce collisions by sensing the carrier before transmitting. However, due to propagation delay, it cannot eliminate collisions entirely. CSMA/CD *improves* on this by **detecting collisions as soon as they occur** and aborting the colliding transmission.

**Step 2 — Algorithm:**
1. The station senses the carrier.
2. If idle → start transmission; if busy → wait using a backoff algorithm.
3. While transmitting, the station **continuously monitors the channel** for a collision signal (abnormal voltage / energy level).
4. If collision detected:
   - **Abort transmission immediately** (do not waste bandwidth).
   - Send a **jam signal (32 bits)** to ensure all other stations also detect the collision.
   - Compute a **random backoff time** using the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm.
   - Retry after backoff expires.
5. If no collision → frame is transmitted successfully; wait for ACK.

**Step 3 — Key Requirement:** The frame transmission time $T_{fr}$ must be **at least $2T_p$** (twice the one-way propagation delay), so that the sender is still on the air when the collision signal returns. This is why IEEE 802.3 Ethernet mandates a **minimum frame size of 64 bytes**.

**Step 4 — Difference from CSMA:**

| CSMA | CSMA/CD |
| :--- | :--- |
| Listens *before* transmission only | Listens *during* transmission also |
| Cannot detect collisions | Detects collisions within $2T_p$ |
| Continues transmitting even on collision | Aborts transmission on collision |
| Used in early Ethernet / wireless | Used in classic wired Ethernet |

**(b) Pure ALOHA Throughput Derivation (7 marks):**

*(See full derivation in Section 3.1 above.)*

Assumption: Poisson process with mean $G$ total attempts per frame-time.

**Step 1:** Vulnerable window $= 2T_{fr}$. Mean attempts in $2T_{fr}$ is $2G$.

**Step 2:** Probability of zero other attempts in $2T_{fr}$:
$$P[\text{success}] = e^{-2G}$$

**Step 3:** Throughput:
$$S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate and set to zero:
$$\dfrac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0 \implies G = 0.5$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $G = 0.5$:
$$S_{max} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$$

**Conclusion:** Maximum channel utilization in Pure ALOHA is only **18.4%** of the raw capacity. The remaining 81.6% is wasted on collisions and idle time.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of **CSMA/CA**. Why is RTS/CTS used in wireless networks? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the three **Channelization protocols — FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA** — with their relative advantages and disadvantages. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CSMA/CA and RTS/CTS (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Why not CSMA/CD in wireless?** Wireless stations cannot listen and transmit simultaneously. The transmitted signal is ~**60 dB** stronger than any received signal, saturating the receiver. Hence, collision detection is infeasible.

**Step 2 — CSMA/CA Algorithm:**
1. Sense the channel. If idle, wait for a **DIFS (Distributed Inter-Frame Space)**.
2. If still idle, start transmission.
3. Receiver waits a **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space)** and sends an **ACK**.
4. On collision (no ACK) → exponential backoff.

**Step 3 — RTS/CTS Mechanism (solves the *Hidden Node Problem*):**
1. Sender transmits **RTS (Request To Send)** containing duration of intended exchange.
2. Receiver replies with **CTS (Clear To Send)** after a SIFS.
3. All stations that hear either RTS or CTS set their **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** for the announced duration, deferring all transmissions.
4. Hidden nodes (which cannot hear the sender) can still hear the CTS and therefore remain silent.

**Step 4 — Frame Exchange Sequence:**
$$ \text{SIFS} < \text{PIFS} < \text{DIFS} < \text{EIFS} $$
This hierarchy prioritizes ACKs > CTS > data > new transmissions.

**(b) Channelization Protocols (7 marks):**

**FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access):**
- Bandwidth $B$ is split into $N$ non-overlapping frequency sub-bands, each assigned to one user.
- **Guard bands** between sub-bands prevent inter-channel interference.
- **Advantages:** Simple, no coordination between users, simultaneous transmission.
- **Disadvantages:** Wasted bandwidth in guard bands, inflexible allocation, requires precise filters.
- **Example:** 1G AMPS analog cellular, FM radio.

**TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access):**
- All users share the **same frequency band** but transmit in **non-overlapping time slots** in a repeating frame.
- Requires **precise time synchronization** (acquired via beacon / base station).
- **Advantages:** Single frequency per user, flexible slot allocation, supports digital modulation.
- **Disadvantages:** Synchronization overhead, propagation delay limits slot size, guard times required.
- **Example:** 2G GSM (combined with FDMA), Bluetooth.

**CDMA (Code Division Multiple Access):**
- All users transmit **simultaneously on the same frequency and at the same time**, distinguished by unique **orthogonal pseudo-random (PN) codes**.
- Receiver correlates the incoming signal with the intended user's code to recover data; other users appear as low-level noise.
- **Advantages:** Soft capacity (graceful degradation with load), inherent security (codes are private), resistance to narrowband interference, no frequency/time planning.
- **Disadvantages:** Complex signal processing, power control critical (near-far problem), lower spectral efficiency under heavy load.
- **Example:** 3G UMTS, GPS, IS-95.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| **Property** | **FDMA** | **TDMA** | **CDMA** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Resource Shared | Frequency | Time | Code |
| Synchronization | Frequency only | Time (strict) | Code (orthogonal) |
| Guard Overhead | Frequency guard bands | Time guard slots | Power control overhead |
| Capacity Behavior | Hard (fixed) | Hard (fixed) | Soft (graceful) |
| Best Use | Analog broadcast | Digital cellular | Modern 3G/4G cellular, GPS |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in the KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $S$ and $G$:** Many students write throughput = $e^{-2G}$ instead of $G \cdot e^{-2G}$. **The product $G \cdot$ (success probability) is the throughput — never omit the leading $G$ term.**
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Pure ALOHA:** ALOHA's vulnerable time is $2T_{fr}$, not $T_{fr}$. The $-2G$ in the exponent is not a typo; it is the entire reason Slotted ALOHA is better.
> 3. **Wrong normalization for $a$:** $a = T_p / T_{fr}$ (NOT $T_{fr} / T_p$). Many students invert this and get the wrong answer.
> 4. **CSMA/CD requires min 64-byte frame:** Students often say "min frame = $2T_p \cdot R$ bits" without converting to bytes. The IEEE 802.3 standard is in **bytes** (64 bytes), not bits.
> 5. **Saying "CSMA/CD is used in Wi-Fi":** This is FALSE. **CSMA/CD is for wired Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**. **CSMA/CA is for Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11).** This is one of the most common errors in KTU answer sheets.
> 6. **Skipping the jam signal:** When describing CSMA/CD, students forget to mention the 32-bit jam signal that propagates the collision notification to *all* stations.
> 7. **Confusing ALOHA with ARQ:** ALOHA is a **medium access** protocol, not a flow-control or error-control protocol. ARQ (Stop-and-Wait, Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat) belongs to the *Logical Link Control* sub-layer.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Three families of MAC protocols:** Random Access (contention), Controlled Access (deterministic), Channelization (orthogonal partitioning).
- **Pure ALOHA:** $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$, max $S = 1/(2e) \approx 18.4\%$, vulnerable time $= 2T_{fr}$.
- **Slotted ALOHA:** $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$, max $S = 1/e \approx 36.8\%$, vulnerable time $= T_{fr}$, requires sync.
- **CSMA:** "Listen before talk" — reduces but does not eliminate collisions due to $T_p$.
- **CSMA/CD:** Adds "listen while talk" — used in wired Ethernet, requires $T_{fr} \geq 2T_p$, min frame 64 bytes.
- **CSMA/CA:** "Announce before talk" — used in Wi-Fi, uses RTS/CTS/ACK/NAV/DIFS/SIFS. No collision detection.
- **Token Passing:** No collisions, but token management overhead and single point of failure.
- **Polling:** Master–slave; roll-call vs hub polling; polling overhead grows with stations.
- **FDMA:** Frequency partitioning with guard bands; inflexible; used in 1G and broadcasting.
- **TDMA:** Time-slot partitioning; requires strict synchronization; used in 2G GSM.
- **CDMA:** Code partitioning with orthogonal PN codes; soft capacity; used in 3G and GPS.
- **Key dimensionless parameter:** $a = T_p / T_{fr}$ — controls CSMA/CD performance.
- **Numerical identity:** $\frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ and $\frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ — memorize these for $S_{max}$.
- **Hidden node problem** is solved by **RTS/CTS** in 802.11, NOT by carrier sensing alone.
- **Near-far problem** is the fundamental reason CSMA/CD fails in wireless.
- **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB):** After $i$ collisions, choose random backoff uniformly in $\{0, 1, ..., 2^i - 1\}$ slots, capped at $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$ (IEEE 802.3).
- **JAM signal:** 32-bit pattern in Ethernet to ensure all stations detect the collision.
- **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping:** This module primarily addresses **CO3: Design and analyze data link layer protocols for reliable communication** in the KTU PCCST501 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Link-Layer Addressing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Link-Layer Addressing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Link-Layer Addressing?

In the TCP/IP protocol suite, the **Data Link Layer** (Layer 2 of the OSI model) is responsible for node-to-node communication over a single physical link (a Local Area Network). To deliver a frame from one device to another across a shared medium, every device on that link must have a **unique hardware identifier** at the link layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Link-Layer Addressing** is the mechanism by which a frame is delivered from one node to another within the same Local Area Network (LAN) using a globally unique, **48-bit physical address** hard-wired into the Network Interface Card (NIC) — commonly known as the **MAC Address (Media Access Control Address)** or **LAN address / Physical address / Hardware address**.

The address is administered by the **IEEE** (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers) under the **EUI-48** (Extended Unique Identifier - 48 bits) standard, also referred to as **MAC-48** in legacy systems.

---

## 1.2 Address Size & Format

$$\text{MAC Address Size} = 48 \text{ bits} = 6 \text{ octets (bytes)}$$

A MAC address is typically written as **6 pairs of hexadecimal digits** separated by colons or hyphens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example MAC Address (Colon-Hexadecimal Notation):**
> `01:23:45:67:89:AB`  — 6 bytes = 12 hex digits.
>
> Other accepted notations:
> - **Hyphen notation:** `01-23-45-67-89-AB`
> - **Cisco-style 3-group notation:** `0123.4567.89AB`
> - **Bare hex (no separators, 12 chars):** `0123456789AB`

---

## 1.3 Structure of a MAC Address (Two Halves)

A 48-bit MAC address is divided into two distinct 24-bit fields:

| Field | Length | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier)** | First 24 bits (`OUI/24`) | Identifies the **vendor/manufacturer** (assigned by IEEE). |
| **NIC-specific / Serial Number** | Last 24 bits (`VENDOR/24`) | Assigned by the manufacturer; uniquely identifies the NIC. |

> [!EXAMPLE]
> In `AC:DE:48:00:11:22`:
> - `AC:DE:48` → OUI (vendor — e.g., a specific Realtek or D-Link block).
> - `00:11:22` → The specific card produced by that vendor.

---

## 1.4 The Two Functional Identities of a MAC Address

The first transmitted byte of a MAC address carries two special bits (in IEEE 802 terminology):

| Bit | Name | Logic `0` | Logic `1` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LSB of 1st byte (b0)** | **I/G — Individual/Group** | **Unicast** (one NIC) | **Multicast** (group) |
| **2nd LSB of 1st byte (b1)** | **U/L — Universal/Local** | **Globally unique (IEEE-assigned)** | **Locally administered (manually set)** |

> [!NOTE]
> If both bits are `1` (e.g., `FF:...`), the address is a **broadcast** (group + locally irrelevant). The all-ones destination `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` is the canonical **LAN broadcast address**.

---

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal System for One Building"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:**
> Think of an **office building** housing many employees.
> - **IP address** = the building's **street address** (used by the postal service to reach the building from anywhere in the world).
> - **MAC address** = the **employee's desk number** inside the building (used by the internal mailroom to drop a letter into the right physical cubicle).
>
> When a letter arrives at the building, the postal system does not know *which employee* it is for. The mailroom opens the outer envelope, reads the **internal desk number (MAC)**, and walks it to the correct cubicle.
>
> Similarly, when a packet arrives at a router, the router reads the **IP address** to forward it to the next LAN. Once the packet is on the destination LAN, **ARP converts the IP address to a MAC address** so the frame can be physically delivered to the correct NIC.

---

## 1.6 Link-Layer Addressing vs. Network-Layer Addressing

| Property | IP Address (Network Layer) | MAC Address (Link Layer) |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 3 | Layer 2 |
| Size | **32 bits (IPv4)** or **128 bits (IPv6)** | **48 bits** |
| Scope | Global (across internetworks) | Local (single LAN segment) |
| Assigned by | Network administrator / DHCP | Burned-in at factory (IEEE OUI) |
| Changes when? | Host moves to a different network | Stays with the NIC (unless spoofed) |
| Used by | Routers to forward packets between networks | Switches/NICs to deliver frames on a LAN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> A host on a LAN can communicate with another host on the **same LAN** using **only MAC addresses** — no router or IP routing is required (Layer-2 switching). The IP address becomes mandatory only when traffic must cross **router boundaries**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) — The Heart of Link-Layer Addressing

When a host on a LAN wants to send an IP packet to another host **on the same LAN**, it knows the destination's **IP address** but not its **MAC address**. It needs a mechanism to "ask": *"Who owns IP 192.168.1.5? Tell me your MAC."* That mechanism is **ARP**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)** — Defined in **RFC 826** (1982, Plummer, MIT) — is a request-and-answer protocol used by a sending host to **map a known 32-bit IPv4 address to an unknown 48-bit MAC address** on the local broadcast domain. The query is broadcast; the reply is unicast.

---

## 2.2 ARP Packet Format (28 bytes — RFC 826)

The ARP message is encapsulated directly inside an **Ethernet frame** (EtherType = `0x0806`).

| Offset (bytes) | Field | Size (bytes) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | **Hardware Type** | 2 | `1` = Ethernet |
| 2 | **Protocol Type** | 2 | `0x0800` = IPv4 |
| 4 | **Hardware Address Length** | 1 | `6` (MAC = 48 bits) |
| 5 | **Protocol Address Length** | 1 | `4` (IPv4 = 32 bits) |
| 6 | **Opcode** | 2 | `1` = request, `2` = reply, `3` = RARP request, `4` = RARP reply |
| 8 | **Sender Hardware Address (SHA)** | 6 | MAC of sender |
| 14 | **Sender Protocol Address (SPA)** | 4 | IP of sender |
| 18 | **Target Hardware Address (THA)** | 6 | MAC of target (unknown → `00:00:00:00:00:00` in request) |
| 24 | **Target Protocol Address (TPA)** | 4 | IP being queried |
| **Total** | — | **28** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The ARP message is **not** a fixed header of 28 bytes — these are 8 variable-sized fields. With Ethernet (6-byte MACs) and IPv4 (4-byte IPs), it totals 28 bytes.

---

## 2.3 The Four Operational Cases of ARP

There are **exactly four scenarios** (a hallmark KTU exam question):

| # | Sender & Target LANs | Sender knows Target's IP? | Sender knows Target's MAC? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Same LAN | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | **ARP request/reply on local LAN** (broadcast query, unicast reply). |
| **2** | Same LAN | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (cached) | **No ARP needed**; send frame directly. |
| **3** | Different LANs | ✅ Yes (dest IP) | Need MAC of **default gateway** | **ARP for the router's MAC**, then send frame to router. |
| **4** | Different LANs | ❌ No | — | Use **DNS** first to resolve the IP. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case 1 vs. Case 3 — the most-tested KTU distinction:**
> - **Same LAN** → ARP resolves the **target's MAC directly**.
> - **Different LAN** → ARP resolves the **default gateway's (router's) MAC**, because the sender cannot address a frame across a router — link-layer frames are terminated at every Layer-3 hop.

---

## 2.4 ARP Cache (ARP Table)

To avoid broadcasting an ARP request for every single frame, each host keeps an **ARP cache** (a small in-memory table) of recent IP-to-MAC bindings.

| IP Address | MAC Address | Type | Age (TTL) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 192.168.1.1 | `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:01` | Dynamic | 15:32 |
| 192.168.1.5 | `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:05` | Dynamic | 02:11 |
| 192.168.1.10 | `11:22:33:44:55:66` | Static (permanent) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cache Timeout:** Entries typically expire after **~15–20 minutes** (Windows default: 15 min; Linux default: 60 sec for unused entries) to handle topology changes (e.g., NIC replacement, DHCP lease renewal).

### 2.4.1 Command-line inspection

- **Windows:** `arp -a` (view), `arp -d` (flush).
- **Linux:** `ip neigh show` (or legacy `arp -an`).

---

## 2.5 ARP Proxy (Proxy ARP)

A **router** can be configured to answer ARP requests on behalf of a host that lives on a **different subnet** behind it. The router responds with **its own MAC address**, causing the sender to send the frame to the router, which then forwards the IP packet onward. This technique hides subnet boundaries from end hosts but is rarely used today (security risk: enables MITM attacks).

---

## 2.6 RARP, BOOTP, and DHCP (Historical & Modern Context)

> [!TIP]
> **RARP (Reverse ARP — RFC 903):**
> The opposite of ARP — a diskless workstation broadcasts its **MAC address** to ask: *"What is my IP address?"* The RARP server replies with the assigned IP. Replaced by **BOOTP** (static config) and finally **DHCP** (dynamic config).

| Protocol | Era | Function | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RARP** | 1984 | MAC → IP (limited, no subnet info) | **Obsolete** |
| **BOOTP** | 1985 | MAC → IP + subnet mask + boot server | Largely obsolete |
| **DHCP** | 1993 → today | IP + mask + gateway + DNS + lease | **Current standard** |

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Quick Reference

| Concept | Equation / Constant | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| MAC address length | $L_{MAC}$ | $\mathbf{48 \text{ bits} = 6 \text{ bytes}}$ |
| Unique MAC addresses in 48-bit space | $N_{MAC} = 2^{48}$ | $\approx 2.81 \times 10^{14}$ addresses |
| Broadcast address | `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` | All 48 bits = 1 |
| OUI length | $L_{OUI}$ | $\mathbf{24 \text{ bits}}$ (first half) |
| NIC-specific length | $L_{NIC}$ | $\mathbf{24 \text{ bits}}$ (second half) |
| ARP packet total (Ethernet + IPv4) | $8 \text{ fields} = 2+2+1+1+2+6+4+6+4$ | $\mathbf{28 \text{ bytes}}$ |
| Ethernet frame data field for ARP | Holds 1 ARP packet | 28 bytes payload |
| Ethernet frame overhead (header + CRC) | $14 + 4 = 18$ bytes | Excludes preamble/SFD |
| ARP request EtherType | `0x0806` | Constant |
| I/G bit (Unicast vs Multicast) | LSB of first byte | `0` = unicast, `1` = multicast |
| U/L bit (Universal vs Local) | 2nd LSB of first byte | `0` = IEEE-assigned, `1` = local |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Network forensics & security:** MAC address filtering, DHCP logs, switch port security.
2. **ARP Spoofing / Poisoning:** A malicious host can send gratuitous ARP replies to associate the **gateway's IP** with its **own MAC**, enabling **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attacks. Mitigated by **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** on managed switches.
3. **Wake-on-LAN (WoL):** A special "magic packet" containing the target's MAC is broadcast to power on a sleeping PC.
4. **IoT & Embedded Systems:** Unique MAC addresses are baked into every Ethernet, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth chip for device identity.

---

## 2.9 Address Mapping Logic — When Is ARP Used?

The following decision flowchart is the KTU gold standard for Case 1 vs. Case 3:

```
IF destination IP is on the SAME subnet (ANDed with local mask == local subnet)
       → ARP for the DESTINATION'S MAC
       → Send frame directly to that MAC
ELSE
       → ARP for the DEFAULT GATEWAY'S MAC
       → Send frame to gateway's MAC (router will then IP-forward)
```

Mathematically, the "same subnet" test is:

$$( \text{My IP} \;\; \& \;\; \text{My Subnet Mask} ) \;\; = \;\; ( \text{Dest IP} \;\; \& \;\; \text{My Subnet Mask} )$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, line-by-line analytical derivations of every quantitative concept in link-layer addressing. **No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Number of Unique MAC Addresses

The IEEE-802 standard specifies a **48-bit** address space. The number of distinct addresses is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{MAC} &= 2^{\text{address width in bits}} \\[6pt]
        &= 2^{48} \\[6pt]
        &= 2^{40} \times 2^{8} \\[6pt]
        &= 1\,099\,511\,627\,776 \times 256 \\[6pt]
        &= 281\,474\,976\,710\,656 \\[6pt]
        &\approx 2.815 \times 10^{14}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is **~281 trillion** unique MAC addresses — enough to assign 1 address to every ant on Earth (estimated 20 quadrillion) and still have leftover. The 48-bit space was chosen in 1980 to be "permanently sufficient," though IoT pressure has driven IEEE to also define **64-bit EUI-64** for some standards (e.g., IPv6 interface IDs, Bluetooth).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Address Capacity Per OUI Vendor

The IEEE assigns each manufacturer a **24-bit OUI block**, leaving the manufacturer 24 bits to serialize.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{per OUI}} &= 2^{24} \\[6pt]
                   &= 16\,777\,216
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each vendor (Apple, Intel, Cisco, Realtek, etc.) can produce **~16.77 million** NICs with globally unique addresses from a single OUI assignment. Large vendors hold **multiple OUIs** (e.g., Apple has been assigned over 70 OUIs).

**Total vendor count possible:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{OUI vendors}} &= 2^{24} = 16\,777\,216
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Locally Administered Address (LAA) Space

Setting the **U/L bit** to `1` allows a network administrator to override the burned-in address. The 47 remaining bits (excluding the U/L bit) yield:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{LAA} &= 2^{47} \\[6pt]
       &= 140\,737\,488\,355\,328
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the address space available for virtual machines, software-defined MACs, and manual overrides.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Multicast Group Address Space

When the **I/G bit = 1**, the address is a multicast address. The 47 remaining bits (excluding I/G) define group IDs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{multicast} &= 2^{47} \\[6pt]
              &= 140\,737\,488\,355\,328
\end{aligned}
$$

The **IEEE 802.1D** standard reserves certain ranges for protocol-specific multicast (e.g., IPv4 multicast mapping uses the lower 23 bits of an IP multicast group mapped to `01:00:5E:0` + lower 23 bits of IP).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Total Length of an ARP Packet

From the 8 fields of RFC 826:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{ARP} &= L_{HTYPE} + L_{PTYPE} + L_{HLEN} + L_{PLEN} + L_{OPCODE} + L_{SHA} + L_{SPA} + L_{THA} + L_{TPA} \\[6pt]
        &= 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 6 + 4 + 6 + 4 \quad \text{(bytes)} \\[6pt]
        &= 28 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:**
> - `HTYPE` & `PTYPE` = 16-bit protocol identifiers → **2 bytes each**.
> - `HLEN` & `PLEN` = 8-bit length indicators → **1 byte each**.
> - `OPCODE` = 16-bit message type → **2 bytes**.
> - `SHA` (Ethernet MAC) = **6 bytes**.
> - `SPA` (IPv4) = **4 bytes**.
> - `THA` = **6 bytes** (zero-filled in a request).
> - `TPA` = **4 bytes**.

**Sum:** $2+2+1+1+2+6+4+6+4 = 28$ bytes ✔

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Total Length of the Encapsulating Ethernet Frame

An Ethernet II frame carrying one ARP packet has the following structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{frame} &= L_{preamble} + L_{SFD} + L_{destMAC} + L_{srcMAC} + L_{ethertype} + L_{payload} + L_{FCS} \\[6pt]
          &= 7 + 1 + 6 + 6 + 2 + 28 + 4 \\[6pt]
          &= 54 \text{ bytes (physical layer + data link layer combined)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The **preamble** (7 bytes) and **SFD** (1 byte) are physical-layer synchronization, not counted in the L2 PDU. The L2 frame (header + payload + CRC) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{L2} &= 6 + 6 + 2 + 28 + 4 = 46 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Minimum Ethernet frame size (IEEE 802.3):** 64 bytes. ARP frames are well above this (60 bytes of header+payload before FCS), so no padding is required.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Subnet Test for "Same LAN vs Different LAN" Decision

Given: Host IP = `192.168.1.20`, Subnet Mask = `255.255.255.0` (`/24`), Destination IP = `192.168.1.50`.

**Step 1 — Convert IP to 32-bit binary** (first octet shown for clarity):
- Host IP first octet: $192 = 11000000_2$
- Dest IP first octet: $192 = 11000000_2$

**Step 2 — Apply mask** (last octet zeroed by mask):
- Host Network: `192.168.1.0`
- Dest Network: `192.168.1.0`

**Step 3 — Compare networks:**
$$
(192.168.1.20 \;\; \& \;\; 255.255.255.0) \;=\; 192.168.1.0 \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; (192.168.1.50 \;\; \& \;\; 255.255.255.0) \;=\; 192.168.1.0
$$

**Conclusion:** Networks are **equal** → **same LAN** → ARP for `192.168.1.50`'s MAC.

---

**Counter-example:** Host IP = `192.168.1.20` / Mask = `255.255.255.0`, Destination IP = `192.168.2.50`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Host net} &= 192.168.1.20 \;\; \& \;\; 255.255.255.0 = 192.168.1.0 \\[4pt]
\text{Dest net} &= 192.168.2.50 \;\; \& \;\; 255.255.255.0 = 192.168.2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Networks differ → **different LANs** → ARP for the **default gateway's MAC**, not the destination's.

---

## 3.8 Derivation 8 — ARP Cache Aging Arithmetic (Linux-style)

Linux ARP entries are aged using a 3-state model defined in `net/ipv4/arp.c`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{state\;1} &= 1 \text{ minute}  &&\text{(base reachable)} \\
T_{state\;2} &= (1 \to 3) \text{ minutes randomized} &&\text{(probe delay)} \\
T_{state\;3} &= (1 \to 3) \text{ minutes randomized} &&\text{(unreachable probe)} \\
T_{GC}       &= 30 \text{ seconds GC interval}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective time an entry can live without confirmation:**
$$
T_{max} = 1 + 3 + 3 + 0.5 \approx 7.5 \text{ minutes}
$$

A confirmed-reachable entry is reset to the base state upon each **valid layer-2 receipt**.

---

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix — KTU "Same LAN vs Different LAN" Decision (Reference Table)

| Host IP | Mask | Dest IP | Host Net | Dest Net | Same LAN? | ARP for whom? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `10.0.0.5` | `255.0.0.0` | `10.0.0.99` | `10.0.0.0` | `10.0.0.0` | ✅ Yes | Dest MAC |
| `10.0.0.5` | `255.0.0.0` | `10.1.0.99` | `10.0.0.0` | `10.0.0.0` | ✅ Yes | Dest MAC |
| `10.0.0.5` | `255.0.0.0` | `11.0.0.99` | `10.0.0.0` | `11.0.0.0` | ❌ No | Gateway MAC |
| `192.168.5.10` | `255.255.255.0` | `192.168.5.200` | `192.168.5.0` | `192.168.5.0` | ✅ Yes | Dest MAC |
| `192.168.5.10` | `255.255.255.0` | `192.168.6.200` | `192.168.5.0` | `192.168.6.0` | ❌ No | Gateway MAC |
| `172.16.4.30` | `255.255.0.0` | `172.16.99.1` | `172.16.0.0` | `172.16.0.0` | ✅ Yes | Dest MAC |

> **Quick test:** If `(Dest IP AND Mask) == (My IP AND Mask)` → same LAN.

---

## 3.10 Derivation 9 — Address Format Conversion (Decimal-Binary-Hex)

Given MAC `AC:DE:48:00:11:22`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AC}_{16} &= 10 \times 16 + 12 = 172_{10} = 10101100_2 \\
\text{DE}_{16} &= 13 \times 16 + 14 = 222_{10} = 11011110_2 \\
\text{48}_{16} &= 4 \times 16 + 8   = 72_{10}  = 01001000_2 \\
\text{00}_{16} &= 0                & = 00000000_2 \\
\text{11}_{16} &= 1 \times 16 + 1  = 17_{10}  = 00010001_2 \\
\text{22}_{16} &= 2 \times 16 + 2  = 34_{10}  = 00100010_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenated 48-bit representation:
$$
10101100 \;\; 11011110 \;\; 01001000 \;\; 00000000 \;\; 00010001 \;\; 00100010
$$

**I/G bit** = LSB of byte 1 = `0` → **Unicast**.
**U/L bit** = 2nd LSB of byte 1 = `0` → **Universal (IEEE-assigned)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — MAC Address Structural Anatomy (Bit-Level)

This Mermaid diagram shows the bit-level breakdown of a 48-bit MAC address, including the two flag bits.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["BYTE 1 - 8 bits"] --> A1["bit7 - bit2 - vendor and card identity bits"]
    A --> A2["bit1 - U or L bit - Universal or Local flag"]
    A --> A3["bit0 - I or G bit - Individual or Group flag"]
    B["BYTE 2 - 8 bits - OUI continued"] --> C["BYTE 3 - 8 bits - OUI ends"]
    C --> D["BYTE 4 - 8 bits - NIC serial starts"]
    D --> E["BYTE 5 - 8 bits - NIC serial continues"]
    E --> F["BYTE 6 - 8 bits - NIC serial ends"]
    A1 --> G["OUI - 24 bits - first three bytes"]
    C --> G
    G --> H["Vendor assigned block by IEEE"]
    D --> I["NIC Serial - 24 bits - last three bytes"]
    I --> J["Assigned by manufacturer"]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — ARP Operational Flow (Same-LAN Scenario)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant HostA as Host A - IP 192.168.1.10 - MAC AA
    participant LAN as LAN Broadcast Domain
    participant HostB as Host B - IP 192.168.1.20 - MAC BB
    participant Router as Router - 192.168.1.1 - MAC CC

    Note over HostA: Step 1 - Check ARP cache for 192.168.1.20
    alt Cache miss
        HostA->>LAN: Step 2 - ARP Request - broadcast FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF - who has 192.168.1.20
        LAN->>HostB: Step 3 - All NICs receive the broadcast
        HostB->>HostA: Step 4 - ARP Reply - unicast - I am 192.168.1.20 and my MAC is BB
        HostA->>HostA: Step 5 - Cache the binding - IP 192.168.1.20 equals MAC BB
        HostA->>HostB: Step 6 - Send Ethernet frame with dest MAC BB
    else Cache hit
        HostA->>HostB: Direct send - no ARP needed
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — ARP Operational Flow (Different-LAN Scenario)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant HostA as Host A - 192.168.1.10
    participant Router as Default Gateway - 192.168.1.1 - MAC CC
    participant HostC as Host C - 192.168.2.20 - MAC DD - on another LAN

    Note over HostA: Step 1 - Subnet test fails - dest is on a different LAN
    Note over HostA: Step 2 - Need MAC of the default gateway
    HostA->>Router: ARP Request - who has 192.168.1.1
    Router->>HostA: ARP Reply - MAC is CC
    HostA->>Router: Ethernet frame - dest MAC CC - payload IP packet for 192.168.2.20
    Note over Router: Step 3 - Router strips L2 header - reads IP dest
    Router->>HostC: ARP for 192.168.2.20 on its LAN - then forward
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture (ARP Engine Inside a Host)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ApplicationLayer["Application Layer"]
        App["User Application - HTTP - FTP - SSH"]
    end
    subgraph TransportLayer["Transport Layer"]
        TCP["TCP or UDP Segment"]
    end
    subgraph NetworkLayer["Network Layer - Layer 3"]
        IPPacket["IP Packet - dest IP 192.168.1.20"]
        SubnetTest["Subnet Test Module - is dest on my LAN"]
        ARPLookup["ARP Cache Lookup - key dest IP"]
        ARPCache["ARP Cache - RAM table"]
        ARPEngine["ARP Engine - request reply logic"]
    end
    subgraph DataLinkLayer["Data Link Layer - Layer 2"]
        FrameBuilder["Frame Builder - src MAC mine - dest MAC from cache"]
        NIC["NIC - Physical Transmission"]
    end

    App --> TCP --> IPPacket
    IPPacket --> SubnetTest
    SubnetTest -->|Same LAN| ARPLookup
    SubnetTest -->|Different LAN| ARPLookupGateway["Lookup Gateway MAC"]
    ARPLookup --> ARPCache
    ARPCache -->|Hit| FrameBuilder
    ARPCache -->|Miss| ARPEngine
    ARPEngine -->|Broadcast request| NIC
    ARPEngine -->|Update cache| ARPCache
    ARPLookupGateway --> ARPCache
    FrameBuilder --> NIC
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — ARP Packet Field Map (28-Byte View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    H["Hardware Type - 2 bytes - value 1 for Ethernet"] --> P["Protocol Type - 2 bytes - 0x0800 for IPv4"]
    P --> HL["Hardware Len - 1 byte - value 6"]
    HL --> PL["Protocol Len - 1 byte - value 4"]
    PL --> OP["Opcode - 2 bytes - 1 request or 2 reply"]
    OP --> SHA["Sender HW Addr - 6 bytes - MAC of sender"]
    SHA --> SPA["Sender Proto Addr - 4 bytes - IP of sender"]
    SPA --> THA["Target HW Addr - 6 bytes - 0 in request"]
    THA --> TPA["Target Proto Addr - 4 bytes - IP being resolved"]
    TPA --> TOT["Total 28 bytes"]
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Ethernet Frame Encapsulating an ARP Packet

```mermaid
graph LR
    PRE["Preamble - 7 bytes"] --> SFD["SFD - 1 byte"]
    SFD --> DMAC["Dest MAC - 6 bytes - FF for request"]
    DMAC --> SMAC["Src MAC - 6 bytes - sender"]
    SMAC --> ET["EtherType - 2 bytes - 0x0806 ARP"]
    ET --> ARPP["ARP Message - 28 bytes"]
    ARPP --> FCS["FCS CRC32 - 4 bytes"]
    FCS --> TOT["Total 54 bytes - 46 L2 bytes plus preamble SFD"]
```

---

## 4.7 Diagram 7 — ARP Spoofing Attack Flow (For Security Awareness)

```mermaid
graph TB
    V["Victim Host - IP 10.0.0.5"] -->|1 Sends ARP request for gateway| AS["Attacker Host - same LAN"]
    AS -->|2 Sends gratuitous ARP reply| V
    V -->|3 Caches false binding - gateway IP now maps to attacker MAC| V
    V -->|4 Sends all traffic to attacker MAC| AS
    AS -->|5 Forwards to real gateway - captures traffic| RG["Real Gateway - 10.0.0.1"]
    RG -->|6 Replies go back to attacker| AS
    AS -->|7 Forwards to victim| V
    Note["Mitigation - DAI Dynamic ARP Inspection on managed switches"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is included for **security context** (frequently asked in KTU as a "case study" question). The fix is **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** with **DHCP Snooping** on managed switches — valid ARP replies must match the snooped DHCP bindings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *Explain the structure of a MAC address. What is the role of the I/G and U/L bits?* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A MAC address is a **48-bit** (6-byte) physical identifier assigned to every Ethernet/Wi-Fi NIC. It is divided into two 24-bit halves:
- **OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier)** — first 3 bytes — identifies the **vendor/manufacturer** (assigned by IEEE).
- **NIC-specific** — last 3 bytes — uniquely identifies the card, assigned by the vendor.

**I/G bit (Individual/Group)** — the **LSB of the 1st byte**:
- `0` → **Unicast** (single NIC).
- `1` → **Multicast** (group of NICs).

**U/L bit (Universal/Local)** — the **2nd LSB of the 1st byte**:
- `0` → **Universally administered** (factory-burned by IEEE).
- `1` → **Locally administered** (manually set by OS/admin).

> *[Examiner's note: 1 mark for structure, 1 for OUI/NIC split, 1 for I/G and U/L.]*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *List the four cases in which ARP may be used. When is ARP used for the gateway only?* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Four cases of ARP (Forouzan):**
1. The sender is a **host** and wants to send a packet to **another host on the same LAN** → ARP for the **destination host's MAC**.
2. The sender is a **host** and wants to send a packet to **another host on a different LAN** → ARP for the **default gateway's MAC**.
3. The sender is a **router** and the datagram must be delivered to the **next-hop router on the same LAN** → ARP for the **next-hop router's MAC**.
4. The sender is a **router** and the datagram must be delivered to a **host on the directly connected LAN** → ARP for the **destination host's MAC**.

> **ARP is used for the gateway only in Case 2** — when the destination IP belongs to a different subnet, the sender cannot ARP across routers; it must give the frame to its default gateway.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** (two alternatives). Each alternative has **two sub-parts**, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q.A.(a)** Describe the **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** with a neat diagram of the ARP packet format. Explain how a host on LAN-A can resolve the MAC address of a host on LAN-B when the destination is on a different LAN. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** ARP is a Layer 2/3 protocol defined in **RFC 826** used to **map a 32-bit IPv4 address to a 48-bit MAC address** on the local broadcast domain.

**ARP Packet Format (3 marks):**

| Field | Bytes | Value/Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware Type | 2 | `1` = Ethernet |
| Protocol Type | 2 | `0x0800` = IPv4 |
| HW Addr Length | 1 | `6` |
| Proto Addr Length | 1 | `4` |
| Opcode | 2 | `1` = request, `2` = reply |
| Sender HW Addr | 6 | Sender's MAC |
| Sender Proto Addr | 4 | Sender's IP |
| Target HW Addr | 6 | `00:00:00:00:00:00` in request |
| Target Proto Addr | 4 | IP being resolved |
| **Total** | **28** | bytes |

**Cross-LAN Scenario (3 marks):** When the destination IP is on a **different subnet**, the sender:
1. Performs the **subnet test**: `(DestIP AND Mask) ≠ (MyIP AND Mask)` → different LAN.
2. Looks up the **default gateway's IP** from its routing table.
3. Checks the ARP cache for the **gateway's IP**; if missing, broadcasts an ARP request for the gateway's IP.
4. Receives the gateway's MAC in the ARP reply.
5. Builds the Ethernet frame with **destination MAC = gateway's MAC** but **destination IP = original host's IP** in the IP header.
6. The router strips the L2 frame, consults its routing table, and forwards the IP packet to the next hop (repeating ARP for the next hop if needed).

**Diagram (illustrative):**
```
Host A (192.168.1.5)  --ARP for 192.168.1.1-->  Router (MAC: CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC)
Host A  --Eth frame (dest MAC CC) (IP 192.168.2.20)-->  Router
Router  --ARP for 192.168.2.20-->  Host C
```

---

**Q.A.(b)** With a suitable example, explain **how ARP works on the same LAN**. Discuss the role of the **ARP cache** and **cache timeout**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Same-LAN ARP Operation (4 marks):**

Suppose Host A (`192.168.1.5`, MAC `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`) wants to ping Host B (`192.168.1.10`, MAC `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`).

1. **Subnet test passes** → destination is on the same LAN.
2. Host A inspects the **ARP cache** for `192.168.1.10`. Suppose no entry.
3. Host A constructs an **ARP request** with:
   - `SHA = AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`, `SPA = 192.168.1.5`
   - `THA = 00:00:00:00:00:00`, `TPA = 192.168.1.10`
   - `Opcode = 1` (request).
4. Host A encapsulates it in an **Ethernet broadcast frame** with `dest MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` and `EtherType = 0x0806`.
5. **Every NIC on the LAN** receives the frame. Each compares `TPA` with its own IP. Only Host B matches.
6. Host B sends an **ARP reply** (unicast) to `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA` with `Opcode = 2` and `SHA = BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`.
7. Host A receives the reply, **stores it in the ARP cache**, and proceeds to send the IP packet.

**ARP Cache (2 marks):**
- An in-memory table on every host mapping **IP → MAC** bindings.
- Avoids the need to broadcast for every frame.
- Inspected with `arp -a` (Windows) or `ip neigh show` (Linux).

**Cache Timeout (1 mark):**
- Entries expire automatically to handle topology changes (NIC replacement, DHCP lease renewal).
- **Windows default:** 15 minutes.
- **Linux default:** A 3-state machine with timers of 1 min, 1–3 min, and 1–3 min (total ~5–7 min).
- **Static (manual) entries** have no timeout.

---

### **QUESTION B — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q.B.(a)** Compare and contrast the **MAC address** and the **IP address**. Why are both needed for end-to-end communication? Illustrate with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | IP Address | MAC Address |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 2 (Data Link) |
| Size | 32 bits (IPv4) | 48 bits |
| Scope | Global (across internetworks) | Local (single LAN) |
| Assigned by | Network admin / DHCP | Vendor (IEEE OUI) + manufacturer |
| Changes when? | Host moves networks | Stays with the NIC |
| Used by | Routers (IP forwarding) | Switches/NICs (frame delivery) |
| Notation | Dotted decimal `192.168.1.5` | Hex `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF` |

**Why both are needed (2 marks):**
- **IP address** is the **end-to-end logical address** used by routers across many hops to deliver a packet from source to destination.
- **MAC address** is the **hop-by-hop physical address** used by switches/NICs to deliver each individual frame across one local link.
- An IP packet may traverse **dozens of links**, each requiring a **different pair of MAC addresses**, while the **IP source and destination remain unchanged** end-to-end (in IPv4 — though NAT can rewrite them).

**Example (1 mark):**
- Host A (`10.1.1.5`, MAC `AA`) sends a packet to Host B (`10.2.2.7`, MAC `DD`).
- Frame 1: Src MAC `AA`, Dst MAC `BB` (Router1's MAC) — IP Src `10.1.1.5`, IP Dst `10.2.2.7`.
- Router1 strips the frame, reads IP, decides next hop is Router2. Frame 2: Src MAC `BB`, Dst MAC `CC` (Router2's MAC) — IP addresses unchanged.
- Router2 forwards to LAN-B. Frame 3: Src MAC `CC`, Dst MAC `DD` (Host B's MAC) — IP addresses unchanged.

> **Result:** The IP addresses (logical) stayed constant; the MAC addresses (physical) changed at every hop. This is the **"IP end-to-end, MAC hop-by-hop"** principle.

---

**Q.B.(b)** Explain the **format of the ARP packet** in detail. Why is the **Sender Hardware Address** included in an ARP **request**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ARP Packet Format (5 marks):** (Refer to the table in Q.A.(a) for full layout.)

The ARP message consists of 8 fields totalling 28 bytes for an Ethernet/IPv4 combination. The first two fields (Hardware Type, Protocol Type) tell the receiver what kind of network and protocol addresses are in use. The next two fields (lengths) tell it how to parse the variable-sized address fields. The **Opcode** distinguishes a request from a reply (and from RARP). The remaining four fields carry the two pairs of addresses (sender and target, each with HW + protocol). In a **request**, the THA is unknown and set to all-zeros.

**Why the Sender Hardware Address is included in the Request (2 marks):**

The SHA field serves **two critical functions** in an ARP request:
1. **It tells the receiver who to send the unicast reply to.** Without the SHA, the owner of the target IP would not know where to send the ARP reply (it cannot unicast to a MAC it is asking about).
2. **It enables the receiver to also learn a binding.** When Host B receives the ARP request, it discovers Host A's IP-to-MAC mapping for free and caches it — a useful side-effect that reduces future ARP traffic.

> *This is also why the same 8-field structure works for both request and reply: the reply simply reuses the sender/target fields with swapped roles, and the opcode changes from `1` to `2`.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "broadcast destination MAC" with broadcast at the IP layer.** ARP uses an Ethernet broadcast (Layer 2) — routers do not forward it.
> 2. **Forgetting the subnet test** when answering "same vs. different LAN" — many students incorrectly say ARP is used for the **destination** even when on a different LAN.
> 3. **Mixing up the U/L and I/G bit positions.** The **I/G bit is the LSB (bit 0) of byte 1**; the U/L bit is **bit 1 of byte 1** — order matters.
> 4. **Stating the broadcast address incorrectly.** The canonical broadcast is `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` (48 ones), not `255.255.255.255` (which is the IP-layer broadcast).
> 5. **Skipping the "Reply is unicast" detail** in ARP operation — this is a guaranteed 1-mark trap.
> 6. **Forgetting that ARP is only for IPv4** — for IPv6, the equivalent is **NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol)** using **ICMPv6**, not ARP.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Bulletins — Memorize These for KTU:**

- **MAC address = 48 bits = 6 bytes**, written as 12 hex digits, often colon-separated.
- **Two halves:** OUI (24 bits, vendor) + NIC serial (24 bits, manufacturer-assigned).
- **I/G bit (LSB of byte 1):** `0` unicast, `1` multicast; broadcast = `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`.
- **U/L bit (2nd LSB of byte 1):** `0` universally administered, `1` locally administered.
- **ARP = IPv4 → MAC** mapping; defined in **RFC 826**; encapsulated in **Ethernet EtherType 0x0806**.
- **ARP packet = 28 bytes** for Ethernet + IPv4 (8 fields: HTYPE, PTYPE, HLEN, PLEN, OPCODE, SHA, SPA, THA, TPA).
- **ARP request → broadcast** (dest MAC = all-ones). **ARP reply → unicast**.
- **Same-LAN communication:** ARP for the **destination's MAC**; send frame directly.
- **Different-LAN communication:** ARP for the **default gateway's MAC**; the IP destination is unchanged.
- **ARP cache** stores IP→MAC mappings with a timeout (~15 min on Windows, state-based on Linux).
- **4 ARP cases** (Forouzan): host→host (same LAN), host→host (different LAN), router→router, router→host.
- **RARP (obsolete), BOOTP (legacy), DHCP (current)** = MAC → IP resolution; **ARP** = IP → MAC.
- **Security alert:** ARP has no authentication — **ARP spoofing/poisoning** is a real MITM threat; mitigated by **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)**.
- **IPv6 equivalent of ARP = NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) using ICMPv6** — *not* ARP.
- **Frame transmission rule:** the L2 destination MAC changes at every router hop, but the L3 source/destination IP remain unchanged end-to-end (in IPv4).
- **EUI-48 / EUI-64:** IEEE identifier standards; 48-bit for legacy MACs, 64-bit for newer protocols (Bluetooth, IPv6).
- **Wake-on-LAN** uses a magic broadcast frame containing the target's MAC — useful IoT corollary.
- **Remember the formula:** Number of unique MACs = $2^{48} \approx 2.81 \times 10^{14}$.
- **Per OUI block:** $2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$ unique NICs per vendor assignment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ethernet Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ethernet** is a family of **wired Local Area Network (LAN)** technologies formally defined by the **IEEE 802.3** standard, operating primarily at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** and **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** of the OSI reference model. It uses the **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** medium access control protocol over a shared bus or switched topology, supporting data rates ranging from **10 Mbps** to **400 Gbps** in modern deployments.

Ethernet is a **connectionless, unreliable, best-effort delivery** service at the MAC sublayer. The reliability is optionally provided by upper-layer protocols (e.g., TCP), not by Ethernet itself. The IEEE 802.3 standard governs the Physical and MAC layers, while **IEEE 802.2** defines the **Logical Link Control (LLC)** sublayer that sits above the MAC sublayer.

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Full Form | Layer | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MAC** | Media Access Control | Data Link (Lower) | Framing, Addressing, Access Control |
| **LLC** | Logical Link Control | Data Link (Upper) | Multiplexing, Flow Control, Error Notification |
| **CSMA/CD** | Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection | MAC | Manages shared medium access |
| **FCS** | Frame Check Sequence | MAC | CRC-32 error detection |
| **SFD** | Start Frame Delimiter | MAC | Marks frame start |
| **MTU** | Maximum Transmission Unit | Data Link | **1500 bytes** for standard Ethernet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** The combination **IEEE 802.3 + IEEE 802.2** is the complete Ethernet specification. The MAC address is **48 bits (6 bytes)** long, globally unique, and assigned to every Network Interface Card (NIC).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **single-lane narrow bridge** in a village where vehicles from both sides must cross:

1. **Multiple vehicles** (computers) want to cross the same narrow bridge (shared Ethernet cable).
2. Before entering, drivers **listen** (Carrier Sense) — can they hear any other vehicle already on the bridge?
3. If the bridge is clear, they **start crossing** (transmit).
4. If two vehicles enter from opposite ends simultaneously, they **collide** — both must back up.
5. After a collision, each driver **waits a random time** (Binary Exponential Backoff) before trying again.
6. Larger collisions cause drivers to wait **progressively longer** random intervals, preventing repeated collisions.

### The Real-World Modern Upgrade

Modern Ethernet is no longer that single-lane bridge. It has evolved into a **multi-lane expressway with controlled entry/exit ramps** (Switches), where each vehicle has its own dedicated lane to the intersection (Switch port), eliminating collisions entirely. This is **Switched Full-Duplex Ethernet**.

### Standardized Speed Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **10BASE-T** → **10 Mbps** over Twisted Pair
> - **100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet)** → **100 Mbps**
> - **1000BASE-T (Gigabit Ethernet)** → **1 Gbps**
> - **10GBASE-T** → **10 Gbps**
> - **40GBASE-T / 100GBASE-T** → **40 / 100 Gbps** (Data Center standards)
> - **200GBASE / 400GBASE** → **200 / 400 Gbps** (Modern Hyperscale Standards)

---

## 1.3 Historical Evolution (KTU Short-Answer Favorite)

| Year | Standard | Speed | Medium | Topology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1973** | Xerox PARC (Original) | 2.94 Mbps | Coaxial | Bus |
| **1980** | DIX (DEC-Intel-Xerox) | 10 Mbps | Thick Coax | Bus |
| **1983** | IEEE 802.3 (10BASE5) | 10 Mbps | Thick Coaxial | Bus |
| **1985** | IEEE 802.3a (10BASE2) | 10 Mbps | Thin Coax | Bus |
| **1990** | IEEE 802.3i (10BASE-T) | 10 Mbps | Twisted Pair | Star |
| **1995** | IEEE 802.3u (Fast Ethernet) | 100 Mbps | Twisted Pair | Star |
| **1998** | IEEE 802.3z (Gigabit - Fiber) | 1 Gbps | Fiber/ Copper | Star |
| **1999** | IEEE 802.3ab (1000BASE-T) | 1 Gbps | Cat 5e Copper | Star |
| **2006** | IEEE 802.3an (10GBASE-T) | 10 Gbps | Cat 6a/7 | Star |
| **2010+** | IEEE 802.3ba/bj | 40G / 100G | Fiber | Switched |

> [!NOTE]
> **Thick Ethernet (10BASE5)** = "Yellow Cable", up to **500 meters**.
> **Thin Ethernet (10BASE2)** = "CheaperNet", up to **185 meters**.
> **10BASE-T** uses **RJ-45 connectors** and **Cat 3 / Cat 5** UTP cable, max segment **100 meters**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IEEE 802.3 Ethernet Frame Format (Exhaustive)

The standard IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame has a **minimum size of 64 bytes (512 bits)** and a **maximum size of 1518 bytes** (excluding the preamble). The structure is as follows:

| Field | Size (Bytes) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Preamble** | 7 | Alternating `10101010` pattern for clock synchronization |
| **SFD** | 1 | `10101011` — marks the start of the actual frame |
| **Destination MAC (DA)** | 6 | Hardware address of the recipient |
| **Source MAC (SA)** | 6 | Hardware address of the sender |
| **Length / Type** | 2 | If ≤ 1500 → Length (IEEE 802.3); If ≥ 1536 → EtherType (Ethernet II) |
| **Data (Payload)** | 46 – 1500 | Upper-layer data (LLC PDU) |
| **Padding** | 0 – 46 | Ensures minimum 64-byte frame |
| **FCS** | 4 | CRC-32 checksum for error detection |

### Why 64 Bytes Minimum?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A station must be able to detect a collision **before it finishes transmitting the entire frame**. Since the maximum propagation delay in a 10BASE5 network allows a round-trip of **51.2 μs**, and at **10 Mbps** this equals **512 bit-times = 64 bytes**, the minimum frame size is set to **64 bytes**.

---

## 2.2 MAC Addressing (48-Bit Hardware Address)

- **Format**: 6-byte hexadecimal address, usually written as `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF` or `AA-BB-CC-DD-EE-FF`.
- **OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier)**: First **3 bytes (24 bits)** assigned by **IEEE** to manufacturers.
- **NIC-Specific**: Last **3 bytes (24 bits)** assigned by the manufacturer — guarantees global uniqueness.
- **Unicast vs Multicast vs Broadcast**:
  - **Unicast**: Least Significant Bit (LSB) of first byte = **0** (sent to one device).
  - **Multicast**: LSB of first byte = **1** (sent to a group).
  - **Broadcast**: All 48 bits = **1** (`FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`, sent to all devices on the LAN).

---

## 2.3 CSMA/CD — Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection

### Operational Steps:

1. **Carrier Sense**: A station that wants to transmit first listens to the medium.
2. **If Idle** → Begin transmission immediately (**1-persistent** mode).
3. **If Busy** → Wait until the medium becomes idle, then transmit.
4. **Collision Detection**: While transmitting, monitor for voltage anomalies (signals > expected).
5. **If Collision Detected**:
   - Stop transmission immediately.
   - Transmit a **32-bit Jam Signal** to ensure all other stations also detect the collision.
   - Invoke the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm.
6. **After Backoff** → Retry transmission.

### Why 1-Persistent?

Because the maximum propagation delay is much smaller than the transmission time of a typical frame, the chance of finding the medium idle is high; thus, transmitting immediately is efficient.

---

## 2.4 Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) — Detailed

Let **n** = number of collision attempts (re-transmissions) for the current frame.

- After the **n-th collision**, the station chooses a random integer **k** uniformly from the set $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^n - 1\}$.
- The station then waits for **k × 51.2 μs (512 bit-times)** before attempting re-transmission.
- **Limit Table**:

| Collision # (n) | Range of k | Max Wait |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 – 1 | 1 slot |
| 2 | 0 – 3 | 3 slots |
| 3 | 0 – 7 | 7 slots |
| ... | ... | ... |
| 10 | 0 – 1023 | 1023 slots |
| 11 – 15 | 0 – 1023 | 1023 slots (capped) |
| 16 | — | **Frame discarded** (transmission failure) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 51.2 μs value is the **slot time** for **10 Mbps Ethernet**, equal to the time to transmit **512 bits = 64 bytes** — exactly the minimum frame size. For **100 Mbps**, slot time = **5.12 μs**; for **1 Gbps**, slot time = **0.512 μs**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for the KTU University Exam. Memorize the units, boundary conditions, and the **use of `\vert` in tables** (per KTU formatting rule).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Propagation Time | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | seconds | $d$ = distance, $v$ = signal velocity ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper) |
| 2 | Transmission Time | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds | $L$ = frame size (bits), $R$ = data rate (bps) |
| 3 | Slot Time (10 Mbps) | $T_{slot} = \dfrac{512 \text{ bits}}{10^7 \text{ bps}} = 51.2 \text{ μs}$ | μs | Defined by IEEE 802.3 |
| 4 | Min Frame Size (10BASE5) | $L_{min} = R \times T_{slot}$ | bytes | $L_{min} = 64$ bytes |
| 5 | Max Network Length (10BASE5) | $L_{max} = v \times \dfrac{T_{slot}}{2}$ | meters | $L_{max} \approx 5000$ m (practical: 2500 m due to repeaters) |
| 6 | Backoff Delay (after n collisions) | $T_b = k \times T_{slot}$ | seconds | $k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 2^n - 1\}$ |
| 7 | Max Backoff Wait | $T_{b,max} = 1023 \times T_{slot}$ | seconds | $1023 \times 51.2 \text{ μs} \approx 52.4$ ms |
| 8 | Parameter "a" | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | dimensionless | Ratio; smaller "a" → higher efficiency |
| 9 | CSMA/CD Efficiency | $E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44a}$ | fraction | For 1-persistent CSMA/CD |
| 10 | Pure ALOHA Efficiency | $E_{PA} = \dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 18.4\%$ | fraction | Maximum theoretical |
| 11 | Slotted ALOHA Efficiency | $E_{SA} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ | fraction | Maximum theoretical |
| 12 | Throughput | $S = E \times R$ | bps | $E$ from CSMA/CD formula, $R$ = link rate |
| 13 | Jam Signal Duration | $T_{jam} = 32 \text{ bit-times}$ | seconds | Ensures collision is detected by all |
| 14 | Address Capacity | $2^{48}$ unique MACs | addresses | $\approx 281$ trillion devices |
| 15 | Interframe Gap (IFG) | $T_{IFG} = 96 \text{ bit-times} = 9.6 \text{ μs}$ | μs | Idle time between frames |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Capacity Planning**: Use $T_p$ and $T_t$ to dimension LAN segments (especially in industrial Ethernet like PROFINET).
- **Collapsed Backbone Design**: Switched Ethernet in data centers uses **full-duplex** to bypass CSMA/CD and achieve $E = 1$ (100% efficiency).
- **Quality of Service (QoS)**: $T_{slot}$ directly determines collision domain limits in legacy shared Ethernet.
- **IoT & Embedded Systems**: The **minimum 64-byte frame** forces padding, which is a known inefficiency in low-bandwidth sensor networks (e.g., 6LoWPAN over IEEE 802.15.4).

---

## 2.6 Switched Ethernet vs Shared Ethernet

| Feature | Shared Ethernet (Legacy) | Switched Ethernet (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Bus / Hub-based Star | Star with full-mesh switching |
| **CSMA/CD** | Required | Not used (Full-Duplex) |
| **Collision Domain** | One large domain (entire LAN) | One per port (micro-segmentation) |
| **Bandwidth** | Shared (e.g., 10 Mbps total) | Dedicated per port (e.g., 1 Gbps × 24 ports) |
| **Maximum Efficiency** | Limited by CSMA/CD ($\approx 1/(1+6.44a)$) | Theoretically 100% |
| **Device** | Hub (Layer 1 repeater) | Switch (Layer 2 bridge) |
| **Learning** | None | MAC address table learning (forwarding) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Frame Size for Collision Detection

### Problem Setup

For collision detection to be **guaranteed**, a station must still be transmitting when the collision signal returns from the farthest point of the network. Thus:

$$T_t \geq 2 \times T_p$$

**Where:**
- $T_t$ = Time taken to transmit the minimum frame
- $T_p$ = One-way propagation time
- $2 T_p$ = Round-trip propagation time (for the worst-case collision path)

### Derivation Steps

Step 1: The IEEE 802.3 standard defines the maximum one-way propagation time for a 10BASE5 segment to be $T_p = 25.6$ μs (half the slot time).

$$T_{slot} = 2 \times T_{p,max} = 51.2 \text{ μs}$$

Step 2: At 10 Mbps, the number of bits transmitted in $T_{slot}$:

$$L_{min} = R \times T_{slot}$$

Step 3: Substitute $R = 10^7$ bps and $T_{slot} = 51.2 \times 10^{-6}$ s:

$$L_{min} = 10^7 \text{ bps} \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

$$L_{min} = 512 \text{ bits}$$

Step 4: Convert to bytes:

$$L_{min} = \dfrac{512 \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} = 64 \text{ bytes}$$

### Conclusion

The minimum Ethernet frame size is **64 bytes**, which forces a **minimum payload of 46 bytes** (64 − 18 bytes of header/FCS). If the user data is smaller, **padding** is added.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CSMA/CD Efficiency Formula

Let $T_t$ be the frame transmission time, and $T_p$ the maximum one-way propagation time. Define:

$$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Step 1: Total time for a successful transmission in CSMA/CD. On average, a station that wants to transmit waits $T_p$ for the medium to be sensed idle, then a collision window $T_p$ elapses. The expected wasted time per collision cycle (in a 1-persistent CSMA/CD model) is approximately:

$$W \approx 2 T_p$$

Step 2: For a heavily loaded network, the total time per successful frame transmission becomes:

$$T_{total} = T_t + 2 T_p \cdot E[\text{number of collisions}]$$

Step 3: Using the Poisson collision model, the average number of contention slots is given by:

$$E[C] = e \approx 2.718$$

The wasted time per slot is $2 T_p$. Substituting:

$$T_{total} = T_t + e \cdot (2 T_p) \cdot \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} \cdot \text{adjustment}$$

Step 4: A more rigorous analysis (Lam, 1980) gives the efficiency as:

$$E = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2 T_p \cdot (e - 1) \cdot \text{normalized factor}}$$

Step 5: Combining all proportionality constants into a single empirically determined value **6.44**, the final closed-form is:

$$E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 a}$$

Where $a = T_p / T_t$.

### Numerical Example

For 10BASE5: $T_p = 25.6$ μs, $T_t = (512 \text{ bits}) / (10^7 \text{ bps}) = 51.2$ μs for a minimum frame.

$$a = \dfrac{25.6}{51.2} = 0.5$$

$$E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 3.22} = \dfrac{1}{4.22} \approx 0.237$$

So, with minimum frames, efficiency is **~23.7%**. For longer frames (e.g., 1500 bytes → $T_t = 1200$ μs), $a = 0.0213$ and:

$$E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.0213} = \dfrac{1}{1.137} \approx 0.879 \;\; (\text{or } 87.9\%)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Binary Exponential Backoff — Expected Delay

### Step 1: Backoff After $n$ Collisions

The waiting time after the $n$-th collision is:

$$T_w(n) = k \times T_{slot}, \quad k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^n - 1\}$$

### Step 2: Expected (Average) Backoff Delay

Since $k$ is uniformly distributed over $2^n$ values:

$$E[k] = \dfrac{0 + 1 + 2 + \cdots + (2^n - 1)}{2^n} = \dfrac{2^n - 1}{2}$$

Therefore, the **expected backoff time** after the $n$-th collision is:

$$E[T_w(n)] = \dfrac{2^n - 1}{2} \times T_{slot}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Computations

| Collision # (n) | Window size $2^n$ | Max k | Expected E[k] | Expected $E[T_w(n)]$ at 10 Mbps |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 1 | 0.5 | 25.6 μs |
| 2 | 4 | 3 | 1.5 | 76.8 μs |
| 3 | 8 | 7 | 3.5 | 179.2 μs |
| 4 | 16 | 15 | 7.5 | 384.0 μs |
| 10 | 1024 | 1023 | 511.5 | 26.19 ms |
| ≥10 | Capped 1024 | 1023 | 511.5 | 26.19 ms |

### Step 4: Total Expected Delay (Approximation)

The total expected time spent in backoff for a frame that requires $N$ collisions before success:

$$E[T_{backoff}] = T_{slot} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \dfrac{2^n - 1}{2} = \dfrac{T_{slot}}{2} \sum_{n=1}^{N} (2^n - 1)$$

Simplifying the geometric series:

$$E[T_{backoff}] = \dfrac{T_{slot}}{2} \left( 2^{N+1} - N - 2 \right)$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Throughput in a Shared Ethernet Segment

### Given

- Link rate $R = 100$ Mbps
- Frame size $L = 1500$ bytes = 12,000 bits
- Propagation time $T_p = 5.12$ μs (typical 1 km fiber at $2 \times 10^8$ m/s)

### Step 1: Compute Transmission Time

$$T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{100 \times 10^6} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 120 \text{ μs}$$

### Step 2: Compute Parameter $a$

$$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{5.12 \text{ μs}}{120 \text{ μs}} = 0.0427$$

### Step 3: Compute CSMA/CD Efficiency

$$E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.0427}$$

$$E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.275} = \dfrac{1}{1.275} \approx 0.784$$

### Step 4: Compute Throughput

$$S = E \times R = 0.784 \times 100 \text{ Mbps} \approx 78.4 \text{ Mbps}$$

This is the **maximum useful data rate** deliverable on this shared 100 Mbps segment — much less than the nominal 100 Mbps, due to CSMA/CD overhead.

---

## 3.5 Analytic Comparison Table — Ethernet Standards

| Parameter | 10BASE5 | 10BASE2 | 10BASE-T | 100BASE-TX | 1000BASE-T | 10GBASE-T |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Speed | 10 Mbps | 10 Mbps | 10 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 1 Gbps | 10 Gbps |
| Medium | Thick Coax | Thin Coax | UTP Cat 3+ | UTP Cat 5 | UTP Cat 5e/6 | UTP Cat 6a/7 |
| Max Segment | 500 m | 185 m | 100 m | 100 m | 100 m | 100 m |
| Topology | Bus | Bus | Star | Star | Star | Star |
| Encoding | Manchester | Manchester | Manchester | MLT-3 / 4B5B | 5-level PAM | 16-level PAM (PAM-16) |
| Connectors | AUI / Vampire | BNC / T | RJ-45 | RJ-45 | RJ-45 | RJ-45 |
| Slot Time | 51.2 μs | 51.2 μs | 51.2 μs | 5.12 μs | 0.512 μs | 0.0512 μs |
| Min Frame | 64 B | 64 B | 64 B | 64 B | 64 B (carrier extension to 512 B in half-duplex) | 64 B |
| CSMA/CD | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (with carrier extension) | Optional (mostly full-duplex) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEEE 802.3 Ethernet Frame — Field-by-Field Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Preamble 7B] --> B[SFD 1B]
    B --> C[Destination MAC 6B]
    C --> D[Source MAC 6B]
    D --> E[Length or Type 2B]
    E --> F[Data Payload 46 to 1500B]
    F --> G[Padding 0 to 46B]
    G --> H[FCS CRC32 4B]
    H --> I[Interframe Gap 12B]
    I --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Interframe Gap (IFG)** of 12 bytes (96 bit-times) is **not part of the frame** but is the required idle period between consecutive frames on the medium.

---

## 4.2 CSMA/CD Operational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Station wants to transmit frame] --> checkA{Carrier Sense\nIs medium idle?}
    checkA -- No --> waitA[Wait and keep sensing]
    waitA --> checkA
    checkA -- Yes --> transA[Begin transmission]
    transA --> checkB{Collision\nDetected?}
    checkB -- No --> doneA[Frame transmitted successfully]
    checkB -- Yes --> jamA[Send 32-bit jam signal]
    jamA --> incA[Increment collision count n]
    incA --> checkC{n greater than 16?}
    checkC -- Yes --> dropA[Discard frame and report error]
    checkC -- No --> kselectA[Pick random k in range 0 to 2 to the power n minus 1]
    kselectA --> waitB[Wait k times 51.2 microseconds]
    waitB --> checkA
```

---

## 4.3 Binary Exponential Backoff State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Attempt1
    Attempt1: Attempt 1\nk in 0 to 1
    Attempt1 --> Attempt2: Collision
    Attempt2: Attempt 2\nk in 0 to 3
    Attempt2 --> Attempt3: Collision
    Attempt3: Attempt 3\nk in 0 to 7
    Attempt3 --> AttemptN: Collision
    AttemptN: Attempt 4 to 10\nk in 0 to 2 to the n minus 1
    AttemptN --> AttemptCap: Collision
    AttemptCap: Attempts 11 to 15\nk in 0 to 1023 capped
    AttemptCap --> Success: No Collision
    AttemptCap --> Failure: Collision
    Success: Success\nFrame accepted
    Failure: Failure\n16 attempts exceeded\nFrame dropped
    Success --> [*]
    Failure --> [*]
```
