> 5. **PPA vs SAP confusion:** PPA identifies the **physical interface**; SAP identifies the **logical protocol** on that interface. They are not interchangeable.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention UDP port 434** for Mobile IP registration — a frequently asked sub-question.
> 7. **Mobile IPv6 vs IPv4 differences:** Don't write "Mobile IPv6 also uses FA" — FA is **eliminated** in Mobile IPv6.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Mobile IP** = RFC 3344 (v4) / RFC 6275 (v6). Solves *roaming without breaking TCP*.
- ✅ **Three entities:** MN, HA, FA. **Three functions:** Discovery, Registration, Tunneling.
- ✅ **CoA types:** Foreign Agent CoA (shared) vs Co-located CoA (unique to MN).
- ✅ **Registration uses UDP port 434**, authenticated via **MD5 + SPI** with replay protection (64-bit Identification).
- ✅ **Tunneling protocols:** IP-in-IP (**proto 4**), Minimal Encapsulation (**proto 55**), GRE (**proto 47**).
- ✅ **Triangle Routing** is the v4 problem; **Route Optimization + Binding Update** solves it in v6.
- ✅ **Mobile IPv6 advantages:** No FA, IPsec mandatory, integrated route optimization, 128-bit CoA from stateless autoconfig.
- ✅ **DLPI** is STREAMS-based, used on **Solaris**; modern wrapper is **`libdlpi`**.
- ✅ **DLPI modes:** Connection-mode (like X.25/LAP-B) vs Connectionless-mode (Ethernet/Wi-Fi/FDDI).
- ✅ **Key SAPs in Ethernet II:** `0x0800` (IPv4), `0x86DD` (IPv6), `0x0806` (ARP).
- ✅ **PPA** = Physical Point of Attachment (interface index). **SAP** = Service Access Point (logical address).
- ✅ **MAC length = 48 bits**, **Ethernet MTU = 1500 bytes**, **Ethernet II header = 14 bytes**.
- ✅ **Promiscuous mode** in DLPI receives all frames; **DL_PROMISC_PHYS** is the physical-level variant.
- ✅ **Error acknowledgments** use codes like `DL_BADPPA`, `DL_BADSAP`, `DL_UNDELIVERABLE`.
- ✅ **libdlpi core calls:** `dlpi_open`, `dlpi_attach`, `dlpi_bind`, `dlpi_send`, `dlpi_recv`, `dlpi_detach`, `dlpi_close`.
- ✅ **Lifetime default** for Mobile IP registration = **1800 s** (30 min) in many implementations; some use 3600 s.
- ✅ **ICMP Router Discovery** is the foundation of Mobile IP Agent Discovery (RFC 1256 extensions).
- ✅ **DN bit** (Decapsulation) and **T-bit** (Reverse Tunneling) are critical flags in Agent Advertisement.
- ✅ **Path MTU in tunneled packets = 1500 − 20 = 1480 bytes** for IP-in-IP over Ethernet.
- ✅ **DLPI primitives are of two categories:** **Request** (consumer → provider) and **Indication/Ack** (provider → consumer).
- ✅ **Handoff trigger:** MN moves to a new subnet → **Agent Advertisement from a new FA** → re-register.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SOCK_PACKET and PF_PACKET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** SOCK_PACKET and PF_PACKET # SOCK_PACKET and PF_PACKET — Module 3 Premium Notes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the Linux networking stack, **`PF_PACKET`** is a **Protocol Family** identifier (defined in ``) that allows a user-space process to **send and receive raw packets at the Data Link Layer (OSI Layer 2)** without the kernel stripping the Layer-2 header. **`SOCK_PACKET`** is a legacy, **deprecated socket type** (obsolete since Linux 2.0) that was originally used to achieve this functionality before `PF_PACKET` was standardized. In modern Linux, the equivalent and recommended approach is `socket(PF_PACKET, SOCK_RAW, protocol)` or `socket(PF_PACKET, SOCK_DGRAM, protocol)`, which gives the application **direct access to the Ethernet frame** including MAC addresses, EtherType, and payload. | Term | Layer | Standardization | Status | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `PF_PACKET` | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Linux 2.0+ | **Current standard** | | `SOCK_PACKET` | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Linux 1.x | **Obsolete / Deprecated** | | `AF_PACKET` | Alias of `PF_PACKET` | Linux 2.0+ | Equivalent name | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** `PF_PACKET` is the **official Linux API** for raw packet capture and transmission. `SOCK_PACKET` is studied primarily for **historical context** and to understand the evolution of raw socket programming. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Network Post Office" Imagine the Linux kernel as a **highly automated national post office**: - **Normal Sockets (`SOCK_STREAM`, `SOCK_DGRAM`)** are like receiving a **sealed letter** — you only see the *content* (TCP/UDP payload), but the **envelope** (IP header, MAC header) has been opened and discarded by the post office. - **`PF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW`** is like being a **postal inspector with full access to the sorting room**. You can see the **entire envelope** — destination address (MAC), return address (MAC), postal service logo (EtherType), and even the letter inside. - **`SOCK_PACKET`** is the **old, rusty version** of the inspector's badge from the 1990s. It still works in some old post offices, but modern systems have replaced it with a **digital security pass** (`PF_PACKET`). > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** `PF_PACKET` operates at the **lowest user-accessible layer** of the network stack — directly on the **Ethernet frame** (Layer 2). This is the foundation for tools like **Wireshark, tcpdump, nmap**, and custom **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)**. ### 1.3 The Socket Type Spectrum Linux provides a layered hierarchy of socket types, each operating at a different OSI layer: $$\text{Layer 2} \;\;<\;\; \text{Layer 3} \;\;<\;\; \text{Layer 4} \;\;<\;\; \text{Application}$$ | Socket Type | OSI Layer | Header Visibility | Typical Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `PF_PACKET / SOCK_RAW` | **Layer 2** | Ethernet + IP + TCP/UDP | Packet sniffers, IDS | | `PF_PACKET / SOCK_DGRAM` | **Layer 2** | Ethernet (cooked) | Link-layer monitoring | | `PF_INET / SOCK_RAW` | Layer 3 | IP + TCP/UDP | Custom ping, traceroute | | `PF_INET / SOCK_DGRAM` | Layer 4 | UDP header stripped | DNS, DHCP clients | | `PF_INET / SOCK_STREAM` | Layer 4 | TCP header stripped | HTTP, SSH, email | > [!IMPORTANT] > **`SOCK_DGRAM` vs `SOCK_RAW` with `PF_PACKET`:** > - `SOCK_RAW` → You receive the **full Ethernet frame** including the 14-byte Ethernet header. > - `SOCK_DGRAM` → The **Ethernet header is removed**; you receive a "cooked" (link-layer decoded) packet. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The `socket()` System Call for Packet Sockets The `socket()` system call is the entry point for creating any socket in Linux. For packet sockets, the prototype is: $$\text{int } \text{sockfd} = \text{socket}\!\left(\text{int } \text{domain},\; \text{int } \text{type},\; \text{int } \text{protocol}\right)$$ The three parameters for `PF_PACKET` are: - **`domain = PF_PACKET`** (or `AF_PACKET` — they are synonymous in Linux). - **`type = SOCK_RAW`** for full frame access, or **`SOCK_DGRAM`** for cooked packets. - **`protocol`** is an **unsigned short in network byte order** specifying the **EtherType** to capture. Common values are listed below. ### 2.2 Standard Protocol (EtherType) Values | Protocol Constant | Numeric Value (Hex) | Numeric Value (Dec) | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `ETH_P_ALL` | `0x0003` | 3 | **Capture all protocols** | | `ETH_P_IP` | `0x0800` | 2048 | IPv4 frames only | | `ETH_P_IPV6` | `0x86DD` | 34525 | IPv6 frames only | | `ETH_P_ARP` | `0x0806` | 2054 | ARP frames only | | `ETH_P_RARP` | `0x8035` | 32821 | Reverse ARP frames | > [!NOTE] > When using `SOCK_RAW`, the `protocol` value is placed in the **EtherType field** of the outgoing frame. The value `ETH_P_ALL` (`0x0003`) is a special Linux extension that is **not a valid IEEE 802.3 EtherType** — it is a kernel convention meaning "match every frame." ## 2.3 The `sockaddr_ll` Structure To `bind()` a packet socket to a **specific network interface** (e.g., `wlan0`, `eth0`), Linux uses the **`sockaddr_ll`** structure (defined in ``): $$\begin{aligned} \text{struct sockaddr\_ll} \{ & \\ \quad \text{unsigned short sll\_family;} & \quad \text{// Always AF\_PACKET} \\ \quad \text{unsigned short sll\_protocol;} & \quad \text{// EtherType in network byte order} \\ \quad \text{int sll\_ifindex;} & \quad \text{// Interface index (e.g., from if\_nametoindex)} \\ \quad \text{unsigned short sll\_halen;} & \quad \text{// Hardware address length (6 for Ethernet)} \\ \quad \text{unsigned char sll\_addr[8];} & \quad \text{// Hardware (MAC) address} \\ \}; \end{aligned}$$ The **interface index** is obtained dynamically: $$\text{sll\_ifindex} = \text{if\_nametoindex}(\text{"eth0"})$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation / API Call | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Socket creation | `socket(PF_PACKET, SOCK_RAW, htons(ETH_P_IP))` | Captures IPv4 frames | | Bind to interface | `bind(fd, (struct sockaddr*)&sll, sizeof(sll))` | Requires `sockaddr_ll` | | Receive frame | `recvfrom(fd, buffer, BUF_SIZE, 0, NULL, NULL)` | Returns frame length in bytes | | Send frame | `sendto(fd, buffer, len, 0, (struct sockaddr*)&sll, sizeof(sll))` | Inject arbitrary frames | | Get interface index | `if_nametoindex("eth0")` | Returns non-zero integer | | Frame size (Ethernet) | $\text{MTU} + 14 \text{ bytes}$ (header) | Standard Ethernet MTU = **1500** | | Min Ethernet frame | $\text{64 bytes}$ (including FCS) | Enforced by CSMA/CD | | Max Ethernet frame | $\text{1518 bytes}$ (without Jumbo) | 1500 payload + 14 header + 4 FCS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** `PF_PACKET` sockets power: > - **Network monitoring** (Nagios, Zabbix agents) > - **Intrusion Detection Systems** (Snort, Suricata) > - **Network simulators** (ns-3 uses raw sockets internally) > - **Latency-sensitive trading systems** that bypass kernel TCP/IP stack overhead > - **Custom protocol implementations** (e.g., proprietary industrial protocols over Ethernet) ## 2.5 Operational Workflow — Sending a Raw Frame The five steps to transmit a custom Ethernet frame using `PF_PACKET`: 1. **Create the socket** with `socket(PF_PACKET, SOCK_RAW, ETH_P_ALL)`. 2. **Construct the destination address** using `sockaddr_ll` with the target MAC and interface index. 3. **Build the frame buffer** by concatenating the Ethernet header (14 bytes) + payload. 4. **Call `sendto()`** to inject the frame onto the wire. 5. **Close the socket** with `close(fd)` to release kernel resources. The corresponding receive workflow mirrors this: `socket()` → `bind()` to specific interface → `recvfrom()` in a loop. # 3. Step-by-Step Code Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive C Code — Raw Packet Sniffer (Receive-Only) > [!NOTE] > The following is a **complete, compilable C program** that demonstrates the full lifecycle of a `PF_PACKET` socket. Every line is annotated for KTU 2024 board examination clarity. ```c /* packet_sniffer.c * Demonstrates PF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW for capturing Ethernet frames. * Compile: gcc packet_sniffer.c -o sniffer * Run: sudo ./sniffer (root required for raw sockets) */ #include #include #include #include #include #include #include #include /* For ETH_P_ALL, ether_header */ #include /* For sockaddr_ll */ #include /* For if_nametoindex */ #define BUF_SIZE 65536 int main(void) { int sockfd; ssize_t num_bytes; char buffer[BUF_SIZE]; struct sockaddr_ll sll; struct ether_header *eth_hdr; /* ---------- STEP 1: Create the raw packet socket ---------- */ sockfd = socket(PF_PACKET, SOCK_RAW, htons(ETH_P_ALL)); if (sockfd < 0) { perror("socket() failed"); return EXIT_FAILURE; } /* ---------- STEP 2: Bind to a specific interface (eth0) ---------- */ memset(&sll, 0, sizeof(sll)); sll.sll_family = AF_PACKET; sll.sll_protocol = htons(ETH_P_ALL); sll.sll_ifindex = if_nametoindex("eth0"); if (bind(sockfd, (struct sockaddr *)&sll, sizeof(sll)) < 0) { perror("bind() failed"); close(sockfd); return EXIT_FAILURE; } printf("Sniffer started on eth0. Waiting for frames...\n\n"); /* ---------- STEP 3: Receive loop ---------- */ while (1) { num_bytes = recvfrom(sockfd, buffer, BUF_SIZE, 0, NULL, NULL); if (num_bytes < 0) { perror("recvfrom() failed"); break; } /* Cast the buffer to an Ethernet header structure */ eth_hdr = (struct ether_header *)buffer; /* Print source MAC, destination MAC, and EtherType */ printf("Frame: %zd bytes | ", num_bytes); printf("Dst MAC: %02x:%02x:%02x:%02x:%02x:%02x | ", eth_hdr->ether_dhost[0], eth_hdr->ether_dhost[1], eth_hdr->ether_dhost[2], eth_hdr->ether_dhost[3], eth_hdr->ether_dhost[4], eth_hdr->ether_dhost[5]); printf("Src MAC: %02x:%02x:%02x:%02x:%02x:%02x | ", eth_hdr->ether_shost[0], eth_hdr->ether_shost[1], eth_hdr->ether_shost[2], eth_hdr->ether_shost[3], eth_hdr->ether_shost[4], eth_hdr->ether_shost[5]); printf("EtherType: 0x%04x\n", ntohs(eth_hdr->ether_type)); } /* ---------- STEP 4: Cleanup ---------- */ close(sockfd); return EXIT_SUCCESS; } ``` ## 3.2 Line-by-Line Logical Decomposition | Code Segment | What It Does | Why It Matters (KTU Context) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `socket(PF_PACKET, SOCK_RAW, htons(ETH_P_ALL))` | Creates Layer-2 raw socket capturing **all** EtherTypes. | `htons()` converts host byte order to **network byte order** (big-endian) — mandatory for protocol fields. | | `struct ether_header *eth_hdr` | Casts raw buffer to Ethernet frame structure. | The Ethernet header is exactly **14 bytes**: 6 (dst MAC) + 6 (src MAC) + 2 (EtherType). | | `if_nametoindex("eth0")` | Maps interface name → kernel index number. | Linux interfaces are tracked by **index**, not name, in the kernel. | | `bind(...)` | Restricts capture to a single NIC. | Without `bind()`, the socket may capture frames from **all** interfaces (and even non-existent virtual ones). | | `recvfrom(..., NULL, NULL)` | Blocks until a frame arrives. | For a **promiscuous** sniffer, add `setsockopt(fd, SOL_PACKET, PACKET_ADD_MEMBERSHIP, ...)`. | | `ntohs(eth_hdr->ether_type)` | Converts EtherType from network → host order. | **Byte order bug** is the #1 reason KTU students lose marks on raw socket questions. | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SOCK_PACKET vs PF_PACKET | Feature | `SOCK_PACKET` (Legacy) | `PF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW` (Modern) | `PF_PACKET + SOCK_DGRAM` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Introduced | Linux 1.x (pre-2.0) | Linux 2.0 (1996) | Linux 2.0 (1996) | | `domain` argument | `AF_INET` (misuse) | `PF_PACKET` | `PF_PACKET` | | Frame received | Full frame | **Full frame (14-byte header visible)** | Frame with header **stripped** | | `bind()` support | Limited (by interface name string) | Full (via `sockaddr_ll`) | Full (via `sockaddr_ll`) | | Protocol filtering | No (captures all) | Yes (EtherType filter) | Yes (EtherType filter) | | Promiscuous mode | Not cleanly supported | Yes (via `PACKET_ADD_MEMBERSHIP`) | Limited | | Send capability | Yes (unreliable) | Yes (reliable, full control) | Yes (kernel adds header) | | Current status | **Deprecated, removed in newer kernels** | **Recommended for Linux** | Used for cooked packet capture | | Used by `tcpdump` | No (historical) | **Yes** (with `libpcap`) | No | ## 3.4 Why `SOCK_PACKET` Was Deprecated — Derivation of the Problem The historical reasoning, expressed as a logical chain: 1. In **Linux 1.x**, no `PF_PACKET` existed. Engineers wanting raw frames passed `SOCK_PACKET` with `AF_INET` as the `domain`. This was a **kludge** — it abused the IPv4 protocol family to access Layer 2. 2. As networking diversified (IPv6, multiple link-layer technologies), this approach **did not scale**. 3. The Linux 2.0 kernel (1996) introduced **`AF_PACKET`/`PF_PACKET`** as a clean, dedicated protocol family, modeled after the **BSD socket API philosophy**. 4. **`SOCK_PACKET`** was retained for **backward compatibility** but flagged for removal. 5. Modern Linux kernels (≥ 2.6) still expose `SOCK_PACKET` but **emit kernel warnings** and the man page (`man 7 packet`) explicitly states it is **obsolete**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Aid for Exams:** "`SOCK_PACKET` is the **grandfather** — respected for historical contributions, but **retired** in favor of the disciplined `PF_PACKET` family." # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 OSI Layer Mapping of Socket Types ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP_LAYER["APPLICATION LAYER (L7)"] A1["HTTP, SSH, DNS, FTP"] end subgraph TRANS_LAYER["TRANSPORT LAYER (L4)"] A2["SOCK_STREAM (TCP)"] A3["SOCK_DGRAM (UDP)"] end subgraph NET_LAYER["NETWORK LAYER (L3)"] A4["PF_INET + SOCK_RAW (IP + TCP/UDP)"] end subgraph LINK_LAYER["DATA LINK LAYER (L2)"] A5["PF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW"] A6["PF_PACKET + SOCK_DGRAM"] A7["SOCK_PACKET [DEPRECATED]"] end subgraph PHY_LAYER["PHYSICAL LAYER (L1)"] A8["Copper, Fiber, Wireless"] end A8 --> A7 A8 --> A6 A8 --> A5 A5 --> A4 A4 --> A2 A4 --> A3 A2 --> A1 A3 --> A1 style A5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px style A7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style A6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 PF_PACKET Architecture — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph USER["USER SPACE APPLICATION"] U1["Application Code
(Sniffer / IDS)"] U2["recvfrom() / sendto()"] end subgraph KERNEL["LINUX KERNEL SPACE"] K1["PF_PACKET Socket Layer"] K2["Protocol Filter
(ETH_P_IP, ETH_P_ARP)"] K3["Network Device Driver
(eth0, wlan0)"] end subgraph HARDWARE["HARDWARE"] H1["NIC — Network Interface Card"] end H1 -->|Raw bits| K3 K3 -->|Sk_buff| K2 K2 -->|Filtered frame| K1 K1 -->|fd reference| U2 U2 -->|Data buffer| U1 U1 -->|Inject frame| U2 U2 -->|sendto| K1 K1 -->|Bypass TCP/IP| K3 K3 -->|Electrical signal| H1 style K1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px style U1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px style H1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Raw Frame Capture — Sequence Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant NIC as Network Interface Card participant DRV as Linux Device Driver participant PKT as PF_PACKET Socket participant APP as User Application NIC->>DRV: Incoming electrical signal DRV->>DRV: Convert to sk_buff (socket buffer) DRV->>PKT: Deliver sk_buff to packet socket layer PKT->>PKT: Apply protocol filter (ETH_P_ALL) PKT->>APP: recvfrom() returns frame bytes APP->>APP: Parse Ethernet header APP->>APP: Parse IP header APP->>APP: Parse TCP/UDP header APP-->>PKT: Process complete ``` ## 4.4 sockaddr_ll Binding — Interface Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB START(["Application calls
socket(PF_PACKET, SOCK_RAW, ETH_P_ALL)"]) B1["Create sockaddr_ll struct"] B2["sll_family = AF_PACKET"] B3["sll_protocol = htons(ETH_P_ALL)"] B4["sll_ifindex = if_nametoindex('eth0')"] B5["Call bind(sockfd, &sll, sizeof(sll))"] RESULT["Socket now captures
frames ONLY on eth0"] START --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 B5 --> RESULT style START fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style RESULT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define `PF_PACKET` socket. List any four standard `ETH_P_*` protocol constants used with it.** **Model Answer:** `PF_PACKET` is a Linux **Protocol Family** that enables a user-space process to send and receive **raw frames at the OSI Data Link Layer (Layer 2)**. It allows applications to read or write complete Ethernet frames (including MAC headers) without the kernel's TCP/IP stack processing them. Four standard `ETH_P_*` constants: 1. **`ETH_P_ALL`** (0x0003) — Captures or sends frames of **all** EtherTypes. 2. **`ETH_P_IP`** (0x0800) — Restricts capture to **IPv4** frames only. 3. **`ETH_P_ARP`** (0x0806) — Captures **Address Resolution Protocol** frames. 4. **`ETH_P_IPV6`** (0x86DD) — Captures **IPv6** frames. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 for constants.)* --- ### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between `SOCK_PACKET` and `PF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW` in Linux.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | `SOCK_PACKET` (Legacy) | `PF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW` (Modern) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `domain` argument | Used with `AF_INET` (a misuse) | Uses dedicated `PF_PACKET` | | Introduced in | Linux 1.x | Linux 2.0 (1996) | | Filtering by EtherType | Not supported | Fully supported | | Interface binding | Crude / unreliable | Clean via `sockaddr_ll` | | Current status | **Deprecated** | **Industry standard** | *(3 marks: 1.5 marks per parameter × 2 parameters.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- ### Question B1. **[QUESTION A — 14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the `sockaddr_ll` structure used for binding a `PF_PACKET` socket to a specific network interface. List its fields with their data types and purpose. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write a C program snippet that creates a `PF_PACKET` raw socket bound to interface `wlan0` and captures only IPv4 (`ETH_P_IP`) frames in an infinite loop, printing the destination MAC address of each frame. *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution for B1(a) The `sockaddr_ll` structure is defined in `` and is used to bind a `PF_PACKET` socket to a specific network interface card (NIC) and optionally to a specific hardware (MAC) address. ```c struct sockaddr_ll { unsigned short sll_family; /* Always AF_PACKET */ unsigned short sll_protocol; /* IEEE 802.3 protocol in network byte order */ int sll_ifindex; /* Interface index (from if_nametoindex) */ unsigned short sll_halen; /* Length of address (6 for Ethernet) */ unsigned char sll_addr[8]; /* Physical (MAC) address */ }; ``` **Field-by-field purpose:** - **`sll_family`**: Must be set to `AF_PACKET`. It tells the kernel that the address belongs to the packet socket family. - **`sll_protocol`**: The **EtherType** in **network byte order** (big-endian). Use `htons(ETH_P_IP)` to specify IPv4. This acts as a receive filter. - **`sll_ifindex`**: The **kernel-assigned integer index** of the network interface. Obtained via `if_nametoindex("wlan0")`. Index 0 means "any interface." - **`sll_halen`**: Length of the MAC address in bytes. For Ethernet, this is **6**. - **`sll_addr[8]`**: The actual MAC address bytes. 8 bytes is allocated to accommodate future link-layer types; only the first `sll_halen` bytes are meaningful. *(7 marks: 2 marks structure + 1 mark per field × 5 fields.)* --- ### Model Solution for B1(b) ```c #include #include #include #include #include #include #include #include #define BUF_SIZE 65536 int main(void) { int sockfd; ssize_t n; char buf[BUF_SIZE]; struct sockaddr_ll sll; struct ether_header *eth; /* Step 1: Create raw packet socket for IPv4 only */ sockfd = socket(PF_PACKET, SOCK_RAW, htons(ETH_P_IP)); if (sockfd < 0) { perror("socket"); return 1; } /* Step 2: Populate sockaddr_ll for wlan0 */ memset(&sll, 0, sizeof(sll)); sll.sll_family = AF_PACKET; sll.sll_protocol = htons(ETH_P_IP); sll.sll_ifindex = if_nametoindex("wlan0"); /* Step 3: Bind to wlan0 */ if (bind(sockfd, (struct sockaddr *)&sll, sizeof(sll)) < 0) { perror("bind"); return 1; } /* Step 4: Receive loop */ while (1) { n = recvfrom(sockfd, buf, BUF_SIZE, 0, NULL, NULL); if (n < 0) { perror("recvfrom"); break; } eth = (struct ether_header *)buf; printf("Dst MAC: %02x:%02x:%02x:%02x:%02x:%02x\n", eth->ether_dhost[0], eth->ether_dhost[1], eth->ether_dhost[2], eth->ether_dhost[3], eth->ether_dhost[4], eth->ether_dhost[5]); } close(sockfd); return 0; } ``` **Key points the examiner expects:** - Correct header inclusion (``, ``). - Use of `htons(ETH_P_IP)` for network byte order. - Use of `if_nametoindex("wlan0")` (not hardcoded index). - Correct cast of buffer to `struct ether_header *`. *(7 marks: 1.5 marks socket creation + 1.5 marks bind setup + 2 marks recvfrom loop + 2 marks MAC printing.)* --- ### Question B2. **[QUESTION B — 14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Compare and contrast `SOCK_PACKET`, `PF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW`, and `PF_PACKET + SOCK_DRAW` (note: `SOCK_DGRAM` intended) along the dimensions of (i) OSI layer, (ii) Ethernet header visibility, (iii) protocol filtering, and (iv) current Linux support status. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A network security firm wants to build a custom Intrusion Detection System (IDS) that inspects **every** packet on a gigabit Ethernet link. Explain why `PF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW` with `ETH_P_ALL` is preferred over (i) `PF_INET + SOCK_RAW` and (ii) reading from `/proc/net/dev`. *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution for B2(a) | Dimension | `SOCK_PACKET` | `PF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW` | `PF_PACKET + SOCK_DGRAM` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | (i) OSI Layer | Layer 2 (abused via `AF_INET`) | **Layer 2** | **Layer 2** | | (ii) Ethernet header | Visible | **Visible (14 bytes)** | **Stripped** by kernel | | (iii) Protocol filtering | None | **Yes (via `protocol` arg)** | **Yes (via `protocol` arg)** | | (iv) Support status | **Deprecated** | **Fully supported** | **Supported** | *(7 marks: 1.75 marks per dimension × 4 dimensions.)* --- ### Model Solution for B2(b) **(i) Why `PF_PACKET` over `PF_INET + SOCK_RAW`:** `PF_INET + SOCK_RAW` operates at **Layer 3**. The kernel **strips the Ethernet header** before delivery, so the IDS **cannot see MAC addresses**. An attacker can spoof MAC addresses to evade Layer-3-only detection. `PF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW` exposes the **full Layer-2 frame**, including source/destination MAC, EtherType, and even VLAN tags (802.1Q). For a security tool, this **MAC-layer visibility is critical** for detecting ARP spoofing, MAC flooding, and Layer-2 reconnaissance. **(ii) Why `PF_PACKET` over `/proc/net/dev`:** `/proc/net/dev` is a **statistical snapshot** — it provides **byte and packet counters** per interface, not the **actual packet contents**. An IDS needs to inspect **payloads** (e.g., HTTP headers, SQL injection strings) to detect attacks. Counters alone cannot reveal **which** packets are malicious. Furthermore, `/proc/net/dev` is **read-only** and **polling-based**, introducing latency. `PF_PACKET` provides **real-time, zero-copy** access to live frame data. *(7 marks: 3.5 marks per sub-comparison.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `htons()`** on `sll_protocol` or `eth_type` — silent failure, socket binds but receives **nothing**. Examiners deduct 1 full mark. > 2. **Confusing `PF_PACKET` with `AF_INET`** in the `socket()` call — students often write `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, ...)` and lose 2 marks. > 3. **Writing `if_nametoindex` as `if_nametoindex("eth0")` returns a pointer** — it actually returns an **integer**. Common mistake. > 4. **Not including ``** — without it, `struct sockaddr_ll` is undefined, causing compilation errors that students cannot debug in exams. > 5. **Confusing `SOCK_PACKET` with `SOCK_RAW`** in essay questions — always clarify that `SOCK_PACKET` is the **deprecated socket type**, while `SOCK_RAW` is the **modern type used with `PF_PACKET`**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exams** - **`PF_PACKET`** is a **protocol family (domain)** for Layer-2 raw socket access in Linux. - **`SOCK_PACKET`** is a **deprecated socket type** from pre-2.0 kernels; **do not use** in new code. - The modern socket call is: **`socket(PF_PACKET, SOCK_RAW, htons(ETH_P_*))`**. - `SOCK_RAW` → **full Ethernet frame visible**; `SOCK_DGRAM` → **header stripped** (cooked). - Key EtherType values: `ETH_P_ALL = 0x0003`, `ETH_P_IP = 0x0800`, `ETH_P_ARP = 0x0806`. - `sockaddr_ll` binds the socket to a **specific interface** via `sll_ifindex = if_nametoindex("eth0")`. - **Always use `htons()`** on protocol fields (network byte order is **big-endian**). - Raw sockets require **root privileges** (CAP_NET_RAW) — running without `sudo` returns `EPERM`. - Real-world users: **tcpdump, Wireshark, Snort, nmap** — all use `PF_PACKET` under the hood. - `PF_PACKET` is to **Layer 2** what `PF_INET` is to **Layer 3** — they are sibling protocol families. - The `recvfrom()` return value gives the **total frame length** including the 14-byte Ethernet header. - For a **promiscuous mode** sniffer, use `setsockopt()` with `PACKET_ADD_MEMBERSHIP` and a `packet_mreq` struct. - **Common pitfall:** binding to interface index 0 captures from **all** interfaces — be explicit with `if_nametoindex`.

topics.title: Data and Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data and Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Data and What is a Signal?

In the formal vocabulary of **KTU 2024 Scheme — PCCST501 (Computer Networks)**, *Data* and *Signals* are two distinct but inseparable abstractions of the same physical phenomenon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Data** refers to a *fact*, *entity*, or *information* (such as text, numbers, images, audio, or video) that is to be transmitted from one device to another. Data exists in two fundamental forms:
> - **Analog Data** — Continuous and smooth in time (e.g., human voice, temperature).
> - **Digital Data** — Discrete values represented as a finite set of symbols, typically binary `0`s and `1`s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal** is the *electromagnetic or optical encoding* of data. It is the actual physical quantity (voltage, current, light pulse, or radio wave) that travels through the transmission medium. Signals, like data, are of two kinds:
> - **Analog Signal** — Continuously varying electromagnetic wave that can take **infinitely many values** over time.
> - **Digital Signal** — A sequence of discrete voltage pulses (high/low) representing binary bits.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Talking Drum 🪘

Imagine two villages separated by a deep forest. The chief in Village A wants to send an important *fact* (the harvest number — let's say "**120 bags of rice**") to Village B.

- The **data** is the *idea* "120 bags of rice." It is the conceptual payload — it has no physical existence until it is expressed.
- The chief encodes this idea by beating the **talking drum** in a specific pattern: *BUM-bum-BUM-BUM*. That audible pattern is the **signal** — the physical representation that actually travels through the air to Village B.
- In the forest, sound waves (analog) could carry the drumbeats; in modern networks, *electromagnetic waves* or *voltage pulses* (analog or digital) carry the bits.

**Key Takeaway:** *Data is the "what," Signal is the "how it travels."*

---

## 1.2 The Three Foundational Distinctions

### A. Analog vs Digital Data
| Property | Analog Data | Digital Data |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Continuous | Discrete |
| Examples | Voice, temperature, light intensity | Text files, binary computer memory |
| Precision | Infinite resolution | Finite resolution (bit-depth) |

### B. Analog vs Digital Signals
| Property | Analog Signal | Digital Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Waveform | Smooth, continuous | Step-like, square pulses |
| Values | Infinitely many amplitudes | Finite number of levels (commonly 2) |
| Susceptibility to Noise | High | Low (regenerable) |

### C. Periodic vs Non-Periodic (Aperiodic) Signals
- **Periodic Signal** — Repeats its pattern after a fixed time interval **T** (called the *period*).
- **Non-Periodic Signal** — Never repeats, or repeats only after an *infinite* interval.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU examinations, most theory questions test whether the student can correctly **identify a signal as periodic or aperiodic** and **compute its period/frequency** from a waveform.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Speed of light in vacuum (c) = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Speed of light in fiber optics ≈ 2 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Speed of electromagnetic waves in copper cable ≈ 2 × 10⁸ m/s** (≈ 2/3 the speed of light)
- Standard frequency unit: **Hertz (Hz)** = 1 cycle per second
- Standard bandwidth unit: **bits per second (bps)**
- Standard power-ratio unit for noise: **decibels (dB)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Sine Wave — The Building Block of Signals

A simple **analog signal** is mathematically described by the **sine wave**:

$$s(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$$

Every sine wave is fully characterized by **four parameters**. KTU examiners *love* testing these — memorize them cold.

### Parameter 1 — Amplitude (A)
- **Definition:** The absolute value of the signal's highest intensity at any point in time.
- **Unit:** Volts (V) for electrical signals.
- **Physical meaning:** Represents the *energy* or *strength* of the signal.

### Parameter 2 — Period (T)
- **Definition:** The time (in seconds) required to complete **one full cycle** of the waveform.
- **Unit:** Seconds (s).

### Parameter 3 — Frequency (f)
- **Definition:** The number of cycles completed per second.
- **Unit:** Hertz (Hz) = cycles/second.

### Parameter 4 — Phase (φ)
- **Definition:** The position of the waveform relative to time zero (t = 0).
- **Unit:** Radians (or degrees).
- **Physical meaning:** Describes *where* the wave "starts" — used to align two signals.

---

## 2.2 The Critical Relationship — Frequency, Period, and Wavelength

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Frequency and Period are reciprocals.**
   *Why?* Because frequency counts cycles *per second*, while period is *seconds per cycle*.
2. **Wavelength (λ)** is the *spatial distance* occupied by one full cycle as the wave propagates through a medium.
3. **Wavelength = wave speed × period**, because in `T` seconds the wave travels exactly one cycle's worth of distance.
4. Combining 1 and 3 yields the elegant triangular relationship.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Frequency ↔ Period | Hz, s |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Period ↔ Frequency | s, Hz |
| 3 | $\lambda = v \cdot T$ | Wavelength from period and propagation speed | m |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Wavelength from frequency and speed | m |
| 5 | $s(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$ | General sine wave equation | V (or A) |
| 6 | $\text{Bit Rate } N = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | Bits transmitted per second | bps |
| 7 | $\text{Bit Interval } T_b = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Time duration of one bit | s |
| 8 | $\text{Bit Length} = v \cdot T_b$ | Distance a bit occupies on the medium | m |
| 9 | $\text{Nyquist Bit Rate: } C = 2B \log_{2} M$ | Max data rate, **no-noise**, M-level signal | bps |
| 10 | $\text{Shannon Capacity: } C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Max data rate, **noisy channel** | bps |
| 11 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in decibels | dB |
| 12 | $\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Power loss over distance | dB |
| 13 | $\text{Bandwidth (analog)} = f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}$ | Range of frequencies passed | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** Always remember the **triangle of frequency, period, and wavelength**:
> 
> Frequency ↕ Period — reciprocal pair
> 
> Wavelength × Frequency = Speed (v)

---

## 2.3 Time-Domain vs Frequency-Domain Representation

A signal can be viewed in **two equivalent ways**:

1. **Time-Domain Diagram:** Amplitude (y-axis) vs Time (x-axis) — shows *what the signal looks like*.
2. **Frequency-Domain Diagram:** Amplitude (y-axis) vs Frequency (x-axis) — shows *what frequencies are present*.

> [!TIP]
> **Why frequency-domain matters:** Engineers analyze signals in frequency domain because it instantly reveals **bandwidth** (the spectrum the signal occupies). A KTU favourite question is to convert a simple sine wave time-domain plot to its frequency-domain single-line spike at frequency `f`.

---

## 2.4 Composite Signals and Fourier Analysis

A **composite signal** is composed of multiple sine waves of different frequencies, amplitudes, and phases. According to **Jean-Baptiste Joseph Fourier's theorem (1822)**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Fourier Theorem:** *Any composite periodic signal can be represented as a sum of harmonically related sine waves — a fundamental frequency plus integer multiples (harmonics).*

### The Three Categories of Composite Signals
- **Decomposable to simple sine waves** — if the signal obeys Fourier's conditions.
- **Not decomposable to sine waves** — but can be approximated via Fourier series.
- **Inherently non-sine** — e.g., a pure square pulse train — requires *infinite* harmonics to reconstruct perfectly.

---

## 2.5 Digital Signal Characteristics

A digital signal is fundamentally characterized by:

- **Bit Rate (N)** — Number of bits sent per second (bps).
- **Bit Interval (T_b)** — Time taken to transmit one bit. **T_b = 1/N**.
- **Bit Length** — Distance that one bit occupies on the transmission medium = `propagation speed × T_b`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher bit rate ⇒ Shorter bit interval ⇒ Shorter bit length.** This has a direct consequence: *higher bit rates require higher bandwidth channels.*

---

## 2.6 Bandwidth — The "Highway Width"

**Bandwidth (B)** in two contexts:
- **Analog bandwidth:** The range of frequencies a medium can pass without significant attenuation = `f_high − f_low`. (Unit: Hz)
- **Digital bandwidth:** The maximum data rate a channel can support. (Unit: bps)

In KTU problems, unless stated otherwise, **bandwidth = analog bandwidth in Hz**.

---

## 2.7 Transmission Impairments

Three primary impairments distort signals during travel:

| Impairment | Description | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Attenuation** | Loss of signal energy with distance | Resistance of medium |
| **Distortion** | Signal shape changes | Different frequencies travel at different speeds |
| **Noise** | Unwanted added energy | Thermal, induced, crosstalk |

**Attenuation Compensation:** Engineers use **amplifiers** (analog) or **repeaters** (digital) to restore signal strength. Measured in **decibels (dB)**.

---

## 2.8 Data Rate Limits — The Two Pillars

### (a) Noiseless Channel — Nyquist Bit Rate
For a noise-free channel of bandwidth **B** Hz and **M** discrete signal levels:

$$C = 2B \log_{2} M \quad \text{(bits/second)}$$

### (b) Noisy Channel — Shannon Capacity
For a noisy channel with bandwidth **B** Hz and signal-to-noise ratio **S/N**:

$$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{(bits/second)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Shannon limit is unbreakable.** No encoding scheme, however clever, can exceed `C = B log₂(1 + S/N)`. Nyquist is for *noiseless* channels, Shannon is for *real* channels.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Cell towers are placed using Nyquist/Shannon calculations to maximize throughput.
- **Fiber Optics:** Wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) exploits the frequency-domain view to pack many signals into one fiber.
- **Wi-Fi Design:** Channel bandwidth (20/40/80 MHz) is chosen based on Shannon capacity for given noise floors.
- **Audio Engineering:** Nyquist's theorem explains why CDs sample audio at **44.1 kHz** (just above 2 × 20 kHz human hearing range).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Frequency and Period Are Reciprocals

**Statement to prove:** $f \cdot T = 1$

**Step 1:** Define frequency operationally.

$$f = \frac{\text{Number of cycles}}{\text{Time elapsed (seconds)}}$$

**Step 2:** Define period operationally.

$$T = \frac{\text{Time elapsed (seconds)}}{\text{Number of cycles}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply the two definitions.

$$f \cdot T = \frac{\text{Number of cycles}}{\text{Time}} \cdot \frac{\text{Time}}{\text{Number of cycles}} = 1$$

**Step 4:** Conclude.

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad T = \frac{1}{f}}$$

**Explanation:** A cycle is a *count*; a period is the *time per count*. Multiplying *cycles per second* by *seconds per cycle* is dimensionless and equals exactly 1.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Wavelength from Frequency

**Starting point:** In one period `T`, the wave travels exactly one wavelength `λ`.

$$\lambda = v \cdot T$$

Substituting `T = 1/f`:

$$\lambda = v \cdot \frac{1}{f}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{v}{f}}$$

**Explanation:** Higher frequency ⇒ shorter wavelength (at constant propagation speed). For radio at 1 MHz, λ ≈ 300 m; for visible light at 10¹⁴ Hz, λ ≈ 500 nm.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Bit Length from Bit Rate

**Starting point:** A bit travels for time `T_b` at speed `v` through the medium.

$$\text{Bit Length} = v \cdot T_b$$

Substituting `T_b = 1/N`:

$$\boxed{\text{Bit Length} = \frac{v}{N}}$$

**Explanation:** Doubling the bit rate halves the bit length — meaning each bit "occupies" less physical space on the wire.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Nyquist Bit Rate

**Setup:** Consider a noiseless channel of bandwidth `B` Hz. The highest signal frequency that can pass is `B` Hz.

**Step 1:** A signal at frequency `B` has period `T = 1/B`.

**Step 2:** To preserve the signal shape, by the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem we must sample at least **twice** per cycle (2 samples per cycle).

**Step 3:** Therefore, the maximum sampling rate is:

$$f_s = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{T} = 2B \quad \text{samples/second}$$

**Step 4:** If each sample can represent one of `M` discrete levels, then each sample carries `log₂ M` bits.

**Step 5:** Multiplying bits-per-sample by samples-per-second:

$$\boxed{C = 2B \log_{2} M \quad \text{bits/second}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Shannon Channel Capacity

**Setup:** In a noisy channel, signal power `S` and noise power `N` both compete.

**Step 1:** The *information-carrying capacity* of a level is `log₂(1 + S/N)` bits. This comes from the logarithm of distinguishable signal levels above the noise floor.

**Step 2:** The channel can carry `2B` independent levels per second (same reasoning as Nyquist).

**Step 3:** Multiply:

$$\boxed{C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/second}}$$

**Important:** This formula gives the **maximum theoretical** rate regardless of the number of signal levels `M`. It is a *hard ceiling* on reliable communication.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### 🧮 Example 1 — Period and Frequency of a Power-Line Signal

> **Problem:** A sinusoidal signal has a period of 8.33 ms. Find its frequency in Hz and kHz.

**Solution:**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{8.33 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}}$$

$$f \approx 120.05\ \text{Hz}$$

$$f \approx 0.120\ \text{kHz}$$

This matches the **50 Hz / 60 Hz AC power** standards (after rectification cycles).

---

### 🧮 Example 2 — Bit Length on a Cable

> **Problem:** A 10 Mbps Ethernet signal travels through a copper cable at `v = 2 × 10⁸` m/s. Find the bit length.

**Solution:**

$$T_b = \frac{1}{N} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 10^{-7}\ \text{s} = 100\ \text{ns}$$

$$\text{Bit Length} = v \cdot T_b = (2 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (10^{-7})$$

$$\boxed{\text{Bit Length} = 20\ \text{meters}}$$

---

### 🧮 Example 3 — Nyquist vs Shannon Comparison

> **Problem:** A channel has bandwidth `B = 4000 Hz`. The signal-to-noise ratio is `S/N = 1000`. Find:
> (a) The Nyquist maximum bit rate for M = 4 signal levels.
> (b) The Shannon theoretical maximum bit rate.

**Solution (a) — Nyquist:**

$$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_{2} M = 2 \cdot 4000 \cdot \log_{2} 4$$

$$\log_{2} 4 = 2$$

$$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 \cdot 4000 \cdot 2 = 16{,}000\ \text{bps}$$

**Solution (b) — Shannon:**

$$C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N) = 4000 \cdot \log_{2}(1001)$$

$$\log_{2}(1001) \approx 9.967$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{Shannon}} \approx 39{,}868\ \text{bps}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Shannon's limit (~39.9 kbps) is *higher* than Nyquist's noiseless limit (16 kbps) here because Shannon accounts for the high SNR — the noiseless formula's `M=4` cap is artificially restrictive. The *true* ceiling is Shannon's value.

---

### 🧮 Example 4 — Attenuation in Decibels

> **Problem:** A signal of power `P₁ = 100 mW` enters a 10 km fiber segment and exits at `P₂ = 50 μW`. Find attenuation in dB.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-3}}\right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}(5 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$= 10 \cdot (-3.301)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Attenuation} = -33.01\ \text{dB}}$$

The negative sign indicates power loss.

---

### 🧮 Example 5 — SNR Conversion

> **Problem:** The SNR of a channel is 40 dB. Find the linear `S/N` ratio.

**Solution:**

$$40 = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{40/10} = 10^{4}$$

$$\boxed{S/N = 10{,}000 \colon 1}$$

---

## 3.7 Summary Analytical Reference Table

| Concept | Input | Formula | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency from Period | T | f = 1/T | Hz |
| Wavelength | f, v | λ = v/f | m |
| Bit Interval | N | T_b = 1/N | s |
| Bit Length | v, N | v × (1/N) | m |
| Nyquist Limit | B, M | 2B log₂ M | bps |
| Shannon Limit | B, S/N | B log₂(1+S/N) | bps |
| SNR (dB ↔ Linear) | SNR_dB | 10^(SNR_dB/10) | ratio |
| Attenuation (dB) | P₁, P₂ | 10 log₁₀(P₂/P₁) | dB |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Data and Signals

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DATA AND SIGNALS] --> B[DATA]
    A --> C[SIGNAL]
    A --> D[TRANSMISSION IMPAIRMENTS]
    A --> E[DATA RATE LIMITS]

    B --> B1[Analog Data]
    B --> B2[Digital Data]
    B1 --> B1A[Voice]
    B1 --> B1B[Temperature]
    B1 --> B1C[Light Intensity]
    B2 --> B2A[Text]
    B2 --> B2B[Binary Files]

    C --> C1[Analog Signal]
    C --> C2[Digital Signal]
    C1 --> C1A[Sine Wave]
    C1 --> C1B[Periodic]
    C1 --> C1C[Non-Periodic]
    C2 --> C2A[Bit Stream]
    C2 --> C2B[Bit Rate N]
    C2 --> C2C[Bit Interval Tb]

    D --> D1[Attenuation]
    D --> D2[Distortion]
    D --> D3[Noise]

    E --> E1[Nyquist Noiseless]
    E --> E2[Shannon Noisy]
    E1 --> E1A[C = 2B log2 M]
    E2 --> E2A[C = B log2 1 plus SN]
```

---

## 4.2 Sine Wave Anatomy — Functional Block View

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SineWaveAnatomy[Sine Wave s of t equals A sin 2 pi f t plus phi]
        Param1[Amplitude A]
        Param2[Frequency f]
        Param3[Period T]
        Param4[Phase phi]
    end

    Param2 -.Reciprocal.-> Param3
    Param2 -.Wavelength lambda.-> Output[lambda equals v divided by f]
    Param1 -.Energy.-> Output
    Param4 -.Time Shift.-> Output

    Note1[Time Domain View]
    Note2[Frequency Domain View]
    Output --> Note1
    Output --> Note2
```

---

## 4.3 Signal Transmission Pipeline — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    Source[Data Source] --> Encoder[Encoder or Modulator]
    Encoder --> Channel[Transmission Channel]
    Channel --> Imp1{Attenuation}
    Channel --> Imp2{Distortion}
    Channel --> Imp3{Noise}
    Imp1 --> Receiver[Receiver or Decoder]
    Imp2 --> Receiver
    Imp3 --> Receiver
    Receiver --> Destination[Data Destination]

    CapacityCheck[Capacity Check] --> Channel
    CapacityCheck -.Uses.-> NyqForm[Nyquist C equals 2B log2 M]
    CapacityCheck -.Uses.-> ShanForm[Shannon C equals B log2 1 plus SN]
```

---

## 4.4 Time-Domain to Frequency-Domain Conversion Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    TD[Time Domain Signal] --> FFT[Fourier Transform]
    FFT --> FD[Frequency Domain Spectrum]
    FD --> BW[Bandwidth Identification]
    BW --> Dec1[Fundamental Frequency f0]
    BW --> Dec2[Harmonics 2f0 3f0 ...]
    BW --> Dec3[Amplitude per Frequency]

    TD2[Analog Signal] --> SQ{Square or Sine?}
    SQ --> SinPath[Pure Sine]
    SQ --> SqPath[Square Pulse Requires Infinite Harmonics]
    SinPath --> SingleFreq[Single Frequency Spike]
    SqPath --> MultiFreq[Multi Frequency Spikes]
```

---

## 4.5 Digital Signal Bit Stream — Functional Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    BitStream[Bit Stream 1 0 1 1 0 0 1] --> BitRate[Bit Rate N bps]
    BitRate --> BitInt[Bit Interval Tb equals 1 over N]
    BitInt --> BitLen[Bit Length equals v times Tb]
    BitLen --> MediumProp[Propagation on Medium]

    NoteA[Higher N equals Shorter Tb equals Shorter Bit Length equals Needs Higher Bandwidth]
    BitRate -.-> NoteA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the following with respect to signals:**
**(a) Amplitude (b) Period (c) Frequency (d) Phase.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Amplitude (A):** The absolute value of the signal's highest intensity at any point in time. It represents the energy of the signal and is measured in **volts** (for electrical signals) or **watts** (for power).

**(b) Period (T):** The amount of time (in seconds) required to complete one full cycle of a periodic signal. **Unit: seconds (s).**

**(c) Frequency (f):** The number of cycles (repetitions) of a periodic signal per unit time. **Unit: Hertz (Hz) = cycles/second.** Mathematically, `f = 1/T`.

**(d) Phase (φ):** The position of the waveform relative to the origin (time = 0). It is measured in **radians** (or degrees) and indicates whether the wave is shifted forward or backward in time.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between analog signals and digital signals. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Analog Signal | Digital Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of amplitude | Continuously varying | Discrete levels (steps) |
| Number of possible values | Infinite | Finite (commonly 2 — binary) |
| Noise susceptibility | High | Low (regenerable) |
| Bandwidth requirement | Lower | Higher |
| Example | Human voice transmitted over telephone line | Binary voltage pulses on Ethernet cable |

> **Examples:**
> - *Analog:* The audio signal from a microphone on a landline phone.
> - *Digital:* The voltage pulses on a USB data line representing a binary file.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### **(a)** Explain the four characteristics of a sine wave with suitable diagrams. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A sine wave is the most fundamental analog signal and is described by:

$$s(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$$

The four characteristics are:

**1. Amplitude (A):**
- Represents the *peak value* or strength of the signal.
- For the equation above, the signal oscillates between `+A` and `−A`.
- **Unit:** Volts (V) for voltage signals.
- In frequency-domain, amplitude appears as the *height* of the spectral line.

**2. Period (T):**
- Time taken for the wave to complete **one full cycle**.
- Measured in **seconds**.
- Visual: the horizontal distance between two consecutive peaks (or troughs) on the time-domain plot.

**3. Frequency (f):**
- Number of cycles completed per second.
- **Unit:** Hertz (Hz). `f = 1/T`.
- Higher frequency ⇒ more compressed waveform on the time-domain plot.

**4. Phase (φ):**
- Describes the *horizontal shift* of the waveform relative to t = 0.
- Two sine waves of the same frequency can be *in phase* (φ₁ = φ₂) or *out of phase* (φ₁ ≠ φ₂).
- Critical for signal synchronization in modulation schemes.

> *(Diagrams: Two separate sine waves with labeled A, T, φ — and the frequency-domain representation showing a single spike at f = 1/T.)*

---

#### **(b)** A signal travels through a copper cable at a propagation speed of `2 × 10⁸` m/s. The signal has a frequency of 1 MHz. Calculate the period and the wavelength of the signal. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Propagation speed `v = 2 × 10⁸ m/s`
- Frequency `f = 1\ \text{MHz} = 10⁶\ \text{Hz}`

**Step 1 — Calculate the Period (T):**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{10^{6}\ \text{Hz}} = 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T = 1\ \mu\text{s}\ (= 1\ \text{microsecond})}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Wavelength (λ):**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{2 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}}{10^{6}\ \text{Hz}}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 200\ \text{meters}}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The signal occupies 200 meters of physical cable per cycle. At this propagation speed and frequency, every cycle of the wave stretches 200 m down the wire.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### **(a)** Explain the Nyquist bit rate formula and Shannon capacity formula. Discuss how they differ. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless Channel):**

For a *noiseless* channel of bandwidth `B` Hz using a signal with `M` discrete levels:

$$C = 2B \log_{2} M \quad \text{bits/second}$$

- **Derived from** the sampling theorem: at least 2 samples per cycle are needed.
- **Assumes:** no noise, only bandwidth-limited.
- **Doubling M** (e.g., from 2 to 4 levels) increases capacity by `log₂ M` factor, not by 4 directly.

**2. Shannon Capacity (Noisy Channel):**

For a *noisy* channel with bandwidth `B` Hz and signal-to-noise ratio `S/N`:

$$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/second}$$

- **Takes noise into account** — the (1 + S/N) term shows that even with infinite levels, capacity is bounded.
- **Defines the absolute upper limit** of reliable data transmission for a given channel.

**3. Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Nyquist | Shannon |
|---|---|---|
| Channel type | Noiseless | Noisy |
| Requires | M (signal levels) | S/N ratio |
| Nature | Practical design bound | Theoretical ceiling |
| Can be exceeded by clever encoding? | Yes (by reducing M dependence) | **No — ever** |

---

#### **(b)** A channel has a bandwidth of 3 kHz and a signal-to-noise ratio of 30 dB. Calculate:
(i) The Nyquist bit rate for a binary signal (M = 2).
(ii) The Shannon capacity.
(iii) Comment on which is the actual limit. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Bandwidth `B = 3000\ \text{Hz} = 3\ \text{kHz}`
- `SNR = 30 dB`

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale:**

$$30 = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Nyquist Bit Rate (M = 2):**

$$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_{2} M = 2 \cdot 3000 \cdot \log_{2} 2$$

$$\log_{2} 2 = 1$$

$$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 \cdot 3000 \cdot 1 = 6000\ \text{bps}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 6\ \text{kbps}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Shannon Capacity:**

$$C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 3000 \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 1000)$$

$$\log_{2}(1001) \approx 9.967\ \text{bits/cycle}$$

$$C_{\text{Shannon}} \approx 3000 \cdot 9.967 \approx 29{,}902\ \text{bps}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{Shannon}} \approx 29.9\ \text{kbps}}$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**

> **The actual data rate limit is given by Shannon's formula (~29.9 kbps).** The Nyquist formula's 6 kbps is a *design* bound assuming M = 2; by increasing signal levels (M > 2) and using sophisticated encoding (e.g., trellis coding, OFDM), engineers can push the actual rate closer to the Shannon limit but *never exceed it*.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up Hz and bps:** Bandwidth is in **Hz** (analog frequency range); data rate is in **bps**. Never use them interchangeably in Nyquist/Shannon unless the problem explicitly states so.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear** before plugging into Shannon. The `(1 + S/N)` term expects a *ratio*, not a dB value.
> 3. **Confusing `log₂ M` with `log₁₀ M`.** Always use log base 2 for digital signal levels and bit-rate formulas.
> 4. **Reporting negative attenuation:** When `P₂ < P₁`, attenuation is *negative in dB* — this is correct and represents power loss. Don't panic and change the sign.
> 5. **Ignoring propagation speed in wavelength questions.** Forgetting the `v` factor in `λ = v/f` is the #1 lost-mark reason.
> 6. **In Nyquist vs Shannon comparison:** Students often say Shannon assumes "no noise" — the *opposite* is true. Shannon is for *noisy* channels.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Data** = information concept; **Signal** = physical representation of data on the medium.
- ✅ **Analog signal** = continuous; **Digital signal** = discrete steps (binary).
- ✅ **Periodic signal** repeats after fixed time **T**; **aperiodic** does not.
- ✅ **Four sine-wave parameters:** Amplitude (A), Period (T), Frequency (f), Phase (φ).
- ✅ **Reciprocal pair:** $f = 1/T$ and $T = 1/f$.
- ✅ **Wavelength formula:** $\lambda = v/f$ (or $\lambda = vT$).
- ✅ **Bit Rate N** = bits per second (bps); **Bit Interval T_b** = 1/N (seconds).
- ✅ **Bit Length** = $v \times T_b = v / N$.
- ✅ **Time-domain** = amplitude vs time; **Frequency-domain** = amplitude vs frequency.
- ✅ **Bandwidth (Hz)** = range of frequencies passed = $f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}$.
- ✅ **Fourier's Theorem:** any periodic signal = sum of sine waves (fundamental + harmonics).
- ✅ **Three impairments:** Attenuation, Distortion, Noise.
- ✅ **Attenuation (dB)** = $10 \log_{10}(P_2/P_1)$; **SNR (dB)** = $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$.
- ✅ **Nyquist (noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_{2} M$ bps.
- ✅ **Shannon (noisy):** $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ bps — the *true* upper limit.
- ✅ **Higher bit rate ⇒ shorter bit interval ⇒ shorter bit length ⇒ needs higher bandwidth.**
- ✅ **Standard speeds:** light in vacuum ≈ $3 \times 10^8$ m/s; in cable/fiber ≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **Always remember:** Nyquist = *practical*, Shannon = *theoretical ceiling*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Digital Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Digital Transmission?

**Digital Transmission** is the process of transmitting data in the form of **discrete (binary) signals**—typically represented as voltage pulses—between two or more points across a communication medium. Unlike analog transmission, which conveys continuously varying signals proportional to the source information, digital transmission uses a finite set of well-defined amplitude levels (most commonly two: 0 and 1) to encode information.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Forouzan):** "Digital transmission requires a conversion of data from a digital-to-digital, analog-to-digital, or digital-to-analog form, depending on the original nature of the data and the destination medium."

In the context of the **KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4)**, Digital Transmission encompasses four fundamental conversion scenarios:

| Conversion Type | Source → Destination | Example Application |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Digital-to-Digital** | Digital data → Digital signals | Computer to computer over a LAN |
| **Analog-to-Digital** | Analog data → Digital signals | Voice signal converted via PCM |
| **Digital-to-Analog** | Digital data → Analog signals | Wi-Fi transmission using PSK/QAM |
| **Analog-to-Analog** | Analog data → Analog signals | Radio broadcasting (AM/FM) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 4 primarily emphasizes **Digital-to-Digital Conversion (Line Coding)**, **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (PCM & Delta Modulation)**, and **Digital-to-Analog Conversion (ASK/FSK/PSK)**. Analog-to-Analog is sometimes treated briefly as a comparative concept.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lighthouse and Morse Code"

Imagine a **lighthouse keeper** who must communicate with another keeper **50 km away** during thick fog. The keeper cannot use voice; he only has a **bright lamp** (ON or OFF). To send a meaningful message, he encodes the alphabet using **dots (short flashes)** and **dashes (long flashes)** — this is essentially **Morse code**.

Now translate this to networking:
- The **lamp** is the **transmission medium** (copper, fiber, wireless).
- The **flash/dark state** is the **binary bit (1 or 0)**.
- The **Morse code rules** (when to flash, how long, how to separate letters) are the **line coding scheme** (NRZ, Manchester, AMI, etc.).
- The **fog and weather** represent **noise and attenuation**, which the line coding must be robust against.

Just as Morse code chooses its encoding carefully so that operators can still decode the message despite noise, **line coding schemes** in computer networks are carefully chosen to ensure **clock recovery, DC balance, and error detection**.

---

## 1.3 Why Digital Transmission? — The Three Engineering Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why do we prefer digital transmission over analog?"* — memorize the following three points.

1. **Noise Immunity:** Digital signals can be perfectly regenerated (using *repeaters*), whereas analog signals accumulate noise.
2. **Multiplexing Efficiency:** Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) is far more efficient with discrete pulses than with continuous waves.
3. **Error Detection & Correction:** Parity bits, CRC, and Hamming codes are only possible with digital bitstreams.

---

## 1.4 Components of a Digital Transmission System

A complete digital transmission chain consists of:

1. **Source:** Produces digital data (PC, server, sensor).
2. **Source Encoder:** Compresses the data (optional).
3. **Channel Encoder (Line Coder):** Converts bits into signal pulses (NRZ, Manchester, etc.).
4. **Channel (Medium):** Copper wire, optical fiber, or air.
5. **Repeater/Regenerator:** Cleans up the signal at intermediate points.
6. **Channel Decoder (Line Decoder):** Recovers the original bits.
7. **Destination:** Receives the digital data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The **Data Element** is the smallest entity carrying information (a bit), while the **Signal Element** is the physical representation of that bit on the medium.
> - **Data Rate (N):** Bits per second (bps).
> - **Signal Rate (S):** Signal elements per second (baud).
> - **Relationship:** $S = N \times \frac{1}{r}$, where $r = \log_2 L$ and $L$ = number of signal levels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Digital-to-Digital Conversion (Line Coding)

Line coding is the process of converting **binary data (0s and 1s)** into a **digital signal** (voltage/current pulses) suitable for transmission. The goal is to ensure the resulting signal has properties favorable for synchronization, DC balance, and noise immunity.

### 2.1.1 Classification of Line Coding Schemes

```
Line Coding
├── Unipolar      (Single polarity; e.g., NRZ-Unipolar)
├── Polar         (Two polarities: +V and -V)
│   ├── NRZ-L (Non-Return-to-Zero Level)
│   ├── NRZ-I (Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted)
│   ├── RZ     (Return-to-Zero)
│   ├── Biphase
│   │   ├── Manchester
│   │   └── Differential Manchester
├── Bipolar       (Three levels: +V, 0, -V)
│   ├── AMI (Alternate Mark Inversion)
│   └── Pseudoternary
└── Multilevel / Multitransition
    ├── 2B1Q
    ├── 8B6T
    ├── 4D-PAM5
    └── MLT-3
```

---

### 2.1.2 Unipolar Scheme (NRZ)

- Uses **only one voltage level** (e.g., +5V for bit 1, 0V for bit 0).
- **DC component** is present (poor for channels that cannot pass DC, like transformers).
- **No self-synchronization** for long runs of 0s or 1s.
- Signal Rate $S = N$ (one signal element per bit).

> [!NOTE]
> Forouzan labels this simply as the **Unipolar NRZ** scheme. It is **rarely used** in practice but is conceptually important.

---

### 2.1.3 Polar Schemes (NRZ, RZ, Biphase)

#### A) NRZ-L (Non-Return-to-Zero Level)
- Bit 1 → positive voltage (+V)
- Bit 0 → negative voltage (-V)
- **No transition in the middle of the bit.**
- Pros: Simple, low signal rate ($S = N$).
- Cons: DC component, baseline wandering, poor synchronization.

#### B) NRZ-I (Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted)
- Bit 1 → **transition** at the beginning of the bit period.
- Bit 0 → **no transition**.
- Pros: Better synchronization than NRZ-L.
- Cons: Long runs of 0s still cause synchronization loss.
- Used in **USB** and **100BASE-TX Ethernet (with MLT-3)**.

#### C) RZ (Return-to-Zero)
- Bit 1 → goes to +V in first half, returns to 0 in second half.
- Bit 0 → stays at 0 throughout.
- **Transition in every bit** → excellent synchronization.
- Cons: Requires **2× bandwidth** of NRZ ($S = 2N$).

#### D) Manchester (Biphase-L)
- Bit 1 → transition from **High to Low** at the middle.
- Bit 0 → transition from **Low to High** at the middle.
- **Always has a transition in the middle** → excellent clock recovery.
- $S = 2N$ (double bandwidth).
- Used in **10BASE-T Ethernet** and **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"1 = H-to-L (High to Low = down), 0 = L-to-H (up)"* — visualize the waveform mentally.

#### E) Differential Manchester
- **Always has a transition in the middle** (like Manchester).
- Bit 1 → **no transition** at the start of the bit.
- Bit 0 → **transition** at the start of the bit.
- More immune to polarity inversion (used in **Token Ring**).

---

### 2.1.4 Bipolar Schemes (AMI & Pseudoternary)

#### A) AMI (Alternate Mark Inversion)
- Bit 0 → **0V (no voltage)**.
- Bit 1 → alternating between **+V** and **-V**.
- **No DC component** (average is 0).
- Long runs of 0s are still a problem → solved by **B8ZS** or **HDB3** in T1/E1 lines.

#### B) Pseudoternary
- Bit 1 → **0V**.
- Bit 0 → alternating between +V and -V.
- Opposite of AMI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AMI and Pseudoternary are **ternary (3-level)** schemes. They have a **DC-balanced** property (long-term average is 0), making them suitable for transformer-coupled channels.

---

### 2.1.5 Multilevel and Multitransition Schemes (Advanced)

#### A) 2B1Q (2 Binary, 1 Quaternary)
- Maps **2 bits** into **one of 4 voltage levels** ($-3V, -V, +V, +3V$).
- $S = N/2$ → **halves the baud rate**! Excellent for high-speed DSL.
- Used in **HDSL, SDSL, ISDN BRI**.

#### B) MLT-3 (Multi-Level Transmit - 3)
- Uses **3 voltage levels** ($-V, 0, +V$).
- Bit 1 → cycles through levels in sequence (0 → +V → 0 → -V → 0 ...).
- Bit 0 → no transition.
- Used in **100BASE-TX Fast Ethernet**.

#### C) 8B6T, 4D-PAM5
- Multi-bit/multi-level mappings used in **100BASE-T4 (8B6T)** and **Gigabit Ethernet (4D-PAM5)**.

---

## 2.2 Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) — Analog to Digital

PCM is the **foundational method** for converting **analog signals into digital form**. It was developed by **Alec Reeves in 1937**.

> [!NOTE]
> PCM has three steps: **Sampling → Quantization → Encoding (Line Coding)**.

### 2.2.1 Sampling Theorem (Nyquist-Shannon)

A bandlimited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be **completely reconstructed** from its samples if the sampling rate is at least **twice the highest frequency**.

**Formula (Nyquist Rate):**

$$f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $f_s = 2 \times f_{max}$, it is called the **Nyquist Rate**. If $f_s > 2 \times f_{max}$, it is **oversampling**. If $f_s < 2 \times f_{max}$, we get **aliasing (distortion)**.

### 2.2.2 Quantization

Each sampled amplitude is rounded to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels, where $n$ = number of bits per sample.

**Number of levels:** $L = 2^n$

**Quantization step size:**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L}$$

**Quantization error** (uniform midtread quantizer): $-\Delta/2 \leq e_q \leq +\Delta/2$

**Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR):**

$$SQNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is one of the most-tested formulas in KTU exams! Each additional bit adds **~6 dB** of SQNR.

### 2.2.3 Encoding and Bit Rate

Each sample (after quantization) is encoded into an $n$-bit binary code.

**Bit rate of PCM system:**

$$R_b = f_s \times n \quad \text{(bps)}$$

For telephone systems, $f_{max} = 4000 \text{ Hz}$, so $f_s = 8000$ samples/sec, with $n = 8$ bits → $R_b = 64 \text{ kbps}$ (the famous **DS0** standard).

---

## 2.3 Delta Modulation (DM) — Simplified PCM Variant

DM is a **differential** version of PCM that transmits only **1 bit per sample**, indicating whether the signal has **increased or decreased** relative to the previous sample.

- **Staircase approximation** of the analog signal.
- **Step size ($\Delta$):** fixed or adaptive.
- **Two problems:** *Slope overload distortion* (step too small for fast changes) and *Granular noise* (step too large for slow changes).
- Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM) solves both.

---

## 2.4 Digital-to-Analog Conversion (Modulation Techniques)

When the medium is **bandpass** (wireless, telephone line), we must convert digital bits into analog carrier waves.

### 2.4.1 ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying)

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{bit 1} \\ 0 & \text{bit 0} \end{cases}$$

- **Susceptible to noise** (amplitude is what noise affects most).
- Inefficient bandwidth usage.

### 2.4.2 FSK (Frequency Shift Keying)

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A \cos(2\pi f_1 t) & \text{bit 1} \\ A \cos(2\pi f_2 t) & \text{bit 0} \end{cases}$$

- **Less affected by noise** than ASK.
- Used in **low-speed modems** (up to 1200 bps).

### 2.4.3 PSK (Phase Shift Keying)

$$s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi) \quad \text{where } \phi = 0 \text{ or } \pi$$

- **BPSK** = 2 phases; **QPSK** = 4 phases (2 bits/symbol).
- Robust against amplitude noise.

### 2.4.4 QAM (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation)

Combines **ASK and PSK**. Both **amplitude and phase** vary.

- **4-QAM** = QPSK (2 bits/symbol).
- **16-QAM** = 4 bits/symbol, **64-QAM** = 6 bits/symbol.
- Used in **Wi-Fi (802.11), cable modems, LTE**.

**General QAM bits per symbol:**

$$n = \log_2 M$$

where $M$ = number of symbols (constellation points).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table completely — these are the **most-frequently tested** formulas in KTU ESE Module 4.

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz |
| Quantization Levels | $L = 2^n$ | $n$ = bits per sample |
| Step Size | $\Delta = (V_{max} - V_{min}) / L$ | Volts |
| SQNR | $SQNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Decibels (dB) |
| PCM Bit Rate | $R_b = f_s \times n$ | bps |
| Signal Rate (NRZ) | $S = N$ | baud |
| Signal Rate (Manchester / RZ) | $S = 2N$ | baud |
| Signal Rate (2B1Q, MLT-3) | $S = N$ (ideal) or $N/2$ (2B1Q) | baud |
| Bits per symbol | $n = \log_2 M$ | $M$ = number of signal points |
| Bandwidth (Nyquist) | $B_{min} = N / \log_2 L$ (bipolar) or $N \times 2$ (NRZ) | Hz |
| Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps |
| Avg. Signal Rate | $S = c \times N \times (1/r)$ | baud, $c$ = case factor |
| QAM bits/symbol | $n = \log_2 M$ | — |

> **CRITICAL NOTE on average signal rate:** For **NRZ** schemes, $S_{avg} = N/2$ (only one transition per 2 bits on average). For **Manchester**, $S_{avg} = N$ (transition every bit).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCM (64 kbps):** Backbone of the global PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network) — every landline call uses PCM.
- **Manchester Encoding:** Foundational to **10 Mbps Ethernet (10BASE-T)**.
- **MLT-3 + 4B5B:** Used in **100BASE-TX Fast Ethernet** for noise-robust gigabit-class LANs.
- **2B1Q + AMI:** Inside **DSL modems** delivering broadband to homes.
- **QAM-256:** Carries **digital cable TV** and **DOCSIS 3.1** internet.
- **PSK/QAM:** Cellular networks from 2G (BPSK) to 5G (256-QAM, 1024-QAM).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section contains **fully-worked numerical problems** of the type frequently asked in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1: PCM Bit Rate Calculation (Telephone Channel)

> **Question:** A telephone channel has a maximum frequency of 4 kHz. It is sampled at the Nyquist rate and quantized with 256 levels. Calculate:
> (a) Sampling frequency
> (b) Number of bits per sample
> (c) Resulting bit rate
> (d) Minimum transmission bandwidth (assuming NRZ polar encoding)

### Solution

**Given:**
- $f_{max} = 4 \text{ kHz}$
- $L = 256$ levels
- Line coding: NRZ polar

**Step (a): Sampling frequency**

By Nyquist theorem:

$$f_s = 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ samples/sec}$$

**Step (b): Number of bits per sample**

We need $L = 2^n$:

$$256 = 2^n \implies n = \log_2 256 = 8 \text{ bits/sample}$$

**Step (c): Bit rate**

$$R_b = f_s \times n = 8000 \times 8 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step (d): Minimum bandwidth (NRZ polar)**

For NRZ polar, $S = N$ (one signal element per bit), and minimum bandwidth is:

$$B_{min} = S / 2 = R_b / 2 = 32{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

> **Result:** This is precisely the **DS0** digital channel — the building block of all TDM telephone hierarchies.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2: SQNR Calculation and Improvement

> **Question:** A PCM system uses 8 bits per sample. Calculate:
> (a) The theoretical SQNR in dB.
> (b) If the system is upgraded to 14 bits per sample, by how many dB does the SQNR improve?

### Solution

**Step (a): SQNR for n = 8**

$$SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76 = 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

**Step (b): SQNR for n = 14**

$$SQNR' = 6.02(14) + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04 \text{ dB}$$

**Step (c): Improvement**

$$\Delta SQNR = 86.04 - 49.92 = 36.12 \text{ dB}$$

**Verification using the rule of thumb:** Each additional bit adds 6 dB. So $6 \times (14 - 8) = 36$ dB. ✓ (Matches within rounding.)

---

## 3.3 Problem 3: Signal Rate and Bandwidth for 2B1Q

> **Question:** A system transmits data at 4 Mbps using the 2B1Q line coding scheme. Find:
> (a) The signal rate (baud).
> (b) The minimum bandwidth required.

### Solution

**Given:** $N = 4 \text{ Mbps}$, 2B1Q has $L = 4$ levels ($\log_2 L = 2$ bits per signal element).

**Step (a): Signal rate**

$$S = \frac{N}{\log_2 L} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{2} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ baud} = 2 \text{ Mbaud}$$

**Step (b): Minimum bandwidth**

For a baseband transmission with $L$ levels, the theoretical minimum is the **Nyquist bandwidth**:

$$B_{min} = \frac{S}{2} = \frac{2 \text{ Mbaud}}{2} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why 2B1Q is so popular in **DSL** — it cuts the baud rate in half compared to NRZ.

---

## 3.4 Problem 4: QAM Constellation Analysis

> **Question:** A QAM modem uses a 64-point constellation. If the channel bandwidth is 4 kHz, what is the maximum bit rate achievable using Nyquist criterion?

### Solution

**Given:** $M = 64$ symbols, $B = 4000$ Hz.

**Step 1: Bits per symbol**

$$n = \log_2 M = \log_2 64 = 6 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 2: Maximum symbol rate (Nyquist) for noiseless channel**

$$S = 2B = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ symbols/sec} = 8000 \text{ baud}$$

**Step 3: Maximum bit rate**

$$R_b = S \times n = 8000 \times 6 = 48{,}000 \text{ bps} = 48 \text{ kbps}$$

---

## 3.5 Problem 5: Delta Modulation Slope Overload

> **Question:** An analog signal $m(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$ is delta modulated with step size $\Delta$. Derive the condition to avoid slope overload distortion.

### Solution

The maximum rate of change of the analog signal is:

$$\left| \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \right|_{max} = 2\pi f A$$

For **no slope overload**, the staircase approximation must keep up with the signal. The DM output can change by at most $\pm \Delta$ per sample period $T_s$:

$$\frac{\Delta}{T_s} \geq 2\pi f A$$

Solving for $\Delta$:

$$\Delta \geq 2\pi f A T_s = \frac{2\pi f A}{f_s}$$

> **Interpretation:** Higher signal frequency $f$ or higher amplitude $A$ requires a larger step size $\Delta$ (or higher sampling rate $f_s$).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Line Coding Schemes

| **Scheme** | **Signal Levels (L)** | **DC Component** | **Self-Clocking** | **Bandwidth (relative)** | **Used In** |
|------------|:--------------------:|:----------------:|:-----------------:|:------------------------:|-------------|
| NRZ-L | 2 | Yes (high) | Poor | $B = N$ | RS-232 |
| NRZ-I | 2 | Yes | Better (for 1s) | $B = N$ | USB |
| RZ | 2 | Yes | Excellent | $B = 2N$ | — |
| Manchester | 2 | No | Excellent | $B = 2N$ | 10BASE-T, Token Ring |
| Differential Manchester | 2 | No | Excellent | $B = 2N$ | Token Ring |
| AMI | 3 (ternary) | No (balanced) | Poor (for 0s) | $B = N$ | T1, ISDN |
| 2B1Q | 4 | No | Moderate | $B = N/2$ | DSL, HDSL |
| MLT-3 | 3 | No | Moderate | $B = N/2$ (with 4B5B) | 100BASE-TX |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking **comparison questions** ("Differentiate between NRZ-I and Differential Manchester" or "Compare Manchester and AMI"). Memorize this matrix!

---

## 3.7 Comprehensive Derivation: Minimum Bandwidth vs. Data Rate (Nyquist's Two Formulas)

> **Question:** Prove that for a noiseless channel, the maximum data rate is $N_{max} = 2B \log_2 L$ bps.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Nyquist's first theorem states that a signal of bandwidth $B$ can support at most $2B$ independent signal elements per second. So the **maximum signal rate** is:

$$S_{max} = 2B \quad \text{(baud)}$$

**Step 2:** Each signal element can represent $\log_2 L$ bits, where $L$ is the number of distinct levels.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the maximum data rate is:

$$N_{max} = S_{max} \times \log_2 L = 2B \log_2 L \quad \text{(bps)}$$

**Step 4 (extension with noise — Shannon's theorem):** If noise is present with Signal-to-Noise Ratio $S/N$:

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) \quad \text{(bps)}$$

The **practical** achievable rate is the minimum of the Nyquist and Shannon bounds:

$$N = \min(2B \log_2 L, \; B \log_2(1 + S/N))$$

---

## 3.8 Worked Problem: QPSK Bandwidth Efficiency

> **Question:** A QPSK system uses a carrier of 1 MHz and transmits at 2 Mbps. Find:
> (a) Bits per symbol
> (b) Symbol rate
> (c) Minimum Nyquist bandwidth

### Solution

**Step (a):** QPSK has $M = 4$ phase points:

$$n = \log_2 M = \log_2 4 = 2 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step (b):**

$$S = \frac{N}{n} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2} = 1 \times 10^6 = 1 \text{ Mbaud}$$

**Step (c):**

$$B_{min} = \frac{S}{2} = \frac{1 \text{ Mbaud}}{2} = 500 \text{ kHz}$$

> **Insight:** QPSK is **bandwidth-efficient** — it transmits 2 bits per Hz of bandwidth (at Nyquist limit), making it ideal for cellular 3G/4G/5G systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Line Coding Waveform Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **conceptual pipeline** from binary data to transmitted waveform for various line coding schemes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Binary Data Stream] --> B{Choose Line Coding}
    B -->|Simplicity| C1[Unipolar NRZ]
    B -->|Synchronization| C2[RZ]
    B -->|Clock Recovery| C3[Manchester]
    B -->|DC Balance| C4[AMI]
    B -->|Bandwidth Saving| C5[2B1Q]
    B -->|High Speed| C6[MLT-3]
    C1 --> D1[+V or 0V]
    C2 --> D2[+V/0V with half-bit return]
    C3 --> D3[Mid-bit transition]
    C4 --> D4[Alternating +V, -V, 0]
    C5 --> D5[4 levels: -3V, -V, +V, +3V]
    C6 --> D6[3 levels cycled: 0 to +V to 0 to -V]
    D1 --> E[Physical Channel]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[Repeater or Regenerator]
    F --> G[Line Decoder]
    G --> H[Recovered Binary Stream]
```

---

## 4.2 PCM System Block Diagram

The end-to-end process of converting an **analog voice signal** to a **digital bitstream** is captured below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Signal Source] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    B --> C[Sampler at fs samples per sec]
    C --> D[Quantizer with L levels]
    D --> E[Binary Encoder with n bits per sample]
    E --> F[Line Coder for Transmission]
    F --> G[Channel]
    G --> H[Line Decoder]
    H --> I[Binary Decoder]
    I --> J[Reconstruction Filter]
    J --> K[Reconstructed Analog Signal]
```

> [!NOTE]
