> The **Anti-Aliasing Filter** is a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_{max}$ (e.g., 4 kHz for telephony) — it ensures no frequency above $f_{max}/2$ enters the sampler. This prevents **aliasing distortion**.

---

## 4.3 Digital-to-Analog Modulation Comparison

The four key passband modulation schemes and their mapping characteristics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Digital Bit Stream] --> B{Modulation Scheme}
    B -->|Amplitude varies| C1[ASK]
    B -->|Frequency varies| C2[FSK]
    B -->|Phase varies| C3[PSK]
    B -->|Both Amplitude and Phase| C4[QAM]
    C1 --> D1[Susceptible to noise]
    C2 --> D2[Noise robust moderate speed]
    C3 --> D3[Highly noise robust BPSK QPSK]
    C4 --> D4[Highest spectral efficiency 64QAM 256QAM]
    D1 --> E[Used in Low Cost Systems]
    D2 --> F[Used in Modems below 1200 bps]
    D3 --> G[Used in WiFi Bluetooth Satellite]
    D4 --> H[Used in Cable TV LTE 5G WiFi 6]
```

---

## 4.4 Delta Modulation Encoder/Decoder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input m of t] --> B[Comparator]
    B --> C{Is m greater than staircase estimate}
    C -->|Yes| D1[Output 1]
    C -->|No| D2[Output 0]
    D1 --> E[Shift Register]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Multiplier by Step Size Delta]
    F --> G[Integrator Accumulator]
    G --> H[Staircase Estimate m_hat of t]
    H --> B
    H --> I[Reconstructed Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The feedback loop compares the analog input against the staircase estimate. If the input rises faster than the staircase can follow, **slope overload** occurs.

---

## 4.5 Transmission Modes (Parallel vs Serial)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Data Transfer Modes] --> B[Parallel Transmission]
    A --> C[Serial Transmission]
    B --> B1[n-bit bus, fast but expensive, short distance only]
    C --> C1{How synchronization handled}
    C1 --> C2[Asynchronous]
    C1 --> C3[Synchronous]
    C1 --> C4[Isochronous]
    C2 --> C2a[Start and stop bits, character by character]
    C3 --> C3a[Continuous block framed with sync bytes]
    C4 --> C4a[Fixed timing, used in real time streams like USB]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Asynchronous** = each byte framed with start/stop bits (1 or 2 stop bits). **Synchronous** = continuous frames using preamble/flag bytes (e.g., HDLC, Ethernet). **Isochronous** = guaranteed timing, used in USB, real-time audio.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Line coding
(b) Pulse Code Modulation
(c) Signal element

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(a) **Line Coding (1 mark):** Line coding is the process of converting binary data (0s and 1s) into digital signals (voltage or current pulses) suitable for transmission over a communication medium. *Example: Manchester encoding used in 10BASE-T Ethernet.*

(b) **Pulse Code Modulation (1 mark):** PCM is a method of converting analog signals into digital form through three steps — sampling, quantization, and binary encoding. *Example: Voice signal digitization in PSTN at 64 kbps.*

(c) **Signal Element (1 mark):** A signal element is the smallest unit of a digital signal that occupies a portion of the bit interval and represents one or more bits of information. *Example: In Manchester encoding, one bit period contains two signal elements (the two halves).*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between ASK and FSK. Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | ASK | FSK |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Modulated Parameter | Amplitude | Frequency |
| Bit '1' representation | Carrier ON ($A\cos 2\pi f_c t$) | $f_1$ (higher frequency) |
| Bit '0' representation | No carrier (0 amplitude) | $f_2$ (lower frequency) |
| Noise immunity | Poor (amplitude is noise-affected) | Better (frequency is more stable) |
| Application (1 mark) | Optical fiber transmission, low-cost RF control | Early telephone modems (Bell 103, 300 bps) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks each)

> **Note:** Both choices below are **alternatives** (choose ONE). Each contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain the three line coding schemes — NRZ, RZ, and Manchester — with suitable diagrams. Compare their bandwidth and synchronization properties. (7 marks)**

**(b) A PCM system uses a uniform quantizer with 16 levels. The analog input is bandlimited to 5 kHz.**
**(i) Find the number of bits per sample and the bit rate.**
**(ii) If the system is upgraded to 1024 levels, what is the new bit rate and the improvement in SQNR? (7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) — 2 marks:**
- Two polarities: +V for bit 1, -V for bit 0 (NRZ-L).
- **No transition** in the middle of the bit.
- Signal rate $S = N$ (one signal element per bit).
- Disadvantages: DC component, baseline wandering, no self-synchronization for long runs.
- *Diagram:* Voltage level remains constant throughout the bit period.

**RZ (Return-to-Zero) — 2 marks:**
- Bit 1 → +V in first half, returns to 0 in second half.
- Bit 0 → 0V throughout.
- Has a **transition in every bit period** → excellent synchronization.
- Signal rate $S = 2N$ (2 signal elements per bit) → **double bandwidth** of NRZ.
- *Diagram:* Show the half-bit return to zero.

**Manchester — 3 marks:**
- Bit 1 → transition from **High to Low** at the middle of the bit.
- Bit 0 → transition from **Low to High** at the middle of the bit.
- **Always has a transition in the middle** → guaranteed clock recovery.
- $S = 2N$ → same bandwidth as RZ.
- **No DC component** (positive and negative halves cancel).
- Used in **10BASE-T Ethernet** and **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)**.
- *Diagram:* Show a clear square wave with mid-bit transitions.

**Comparison Table (within the 7 marks):**

| Property | NRZ | RZ | Manchester |
|----------|-----|-----|------------|
| Bandwidth (relative) | $B = N/2$ | $B = N$ | $B = N$ |
| Synchronization | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| DC Component | Yes | Yes | No |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $L_1 = 16$, $f_{max} = 5$ kHz, then $L_2 = 1024$.

**Step (i) — 3 marks:**

Sampling rate (Nyquist):

$$f_s = 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000 \text{ samples/sec}$$

Bits per sample for $L_1 = 16$:

$$n_1 = \log_2 16 = 4 \text{ bits/sample}$$

Bit rate:

$$R_{b1} = f_s \times n_1 = 10{,}000 \times 4 = 40{,}000 \text{ bps} = 40 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step (ii) — 4 marks:**

For $L_2 = 1024$:

$$n_2 = \log_2 1024 = 10 \text{ bits/sample}$$

New bit rate:

$$R_{b2} = 10{,}000 \times 10 = 100{,}000 \text{ bps} = 100 \text{ kbps}$$

SQNR for $n_1 = 4$:

$$SQNR_1 = 6.02(4) + 1.76 = 25.84 \text{ dB}$$

SQNR for $n_2 = 10$:

$$SQNR_2 = 6.02(10) + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

Improvement:

$$\Delta SQNR = 61.96 - 25.84 = 36.12 \text{ dB}$$

**Verification:** $\Delta n = 10 - 4 = 6$ bits → $6 \times 6.02 = 36.12$ dB ✓

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a) With neat block diagrams, explain the working of Pulse Code Modulation (PCM). State and prove the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the following digital-to-analog conversion techniques with their waveform equations: (i) ASK, (ii) FSK, (iii) BPSK, (iv) QPSK. A 16-QAM system transmits over a 6 kHz channel. Find the maximum bit rate using the Nyquist criterion. (7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram of PCM Transmitter (3 marks):**

```
Analog Signal → [Low-Pass Filter] → [Sampler] → [Quantizer] → [Encoder] → Digital Bitstream
```

**Block Diagram of PCM Receiver (2 marks):**

```
Digital Bitstream → [Decoder] → [Reconstruction Filter] → Analog Signal
```

**Working Explanation (2 marks):**
1. **Anti-aliasing filter:** Removes frequencies above $f_{max}$ to prevent aliasing.
2. **Sampler:** Captures instantaneous amplitudes at rate $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$.
3. **Quantizer:** Approximates each sample to nearest of $L$ discrete levels.
4. **Encoder:** Converts quantized level to an $n$-bit binary code.

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem (2 marks — proof omitted in summary, full version in Section 3.7):**

*Statement:* A bandlimited signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Equations and Explanations (4 marks):**

(i) **ASK:** $s(t) = A_1 \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ for bit 1, $s(t) = 0$ for bit 0.

(ii) **FSK:** $s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ for bit 1, $s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_2 t)$ for bit 0.

(iii) **BPSK:** $s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$ where $\phi = 0$ (bit 0) or $\phi = \pi$ (bit 1).

(iv) **QPSK:** Phase takes 4 values: $0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2$, encoding **2 bits per symbol**.

**16-QAM Problem (3 marks):**

Given: $M = 16$, $B = 6$ kHz.

Bits per symbol:

$$n = \log_2 16 = 4 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

Maximum symbol rate (Nyquist):

$$S = 2B = 2 \times 6000 = 12{,}000 \text{ symbols/sec}$$

Maximum bit rate:

$$R_b = S \times n = 12{,}000 \times 4 = 48{,}000 \text{ bps} = 48 \text{ kbps}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing "Signal Rate" with "Data Rate":** Signal rate is in **baud** (signal elements/sec), data rate is in **bps** (bits/sec). Always convert using $S = N \times (1/r)$ where $r = \log_2 L$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for Manchester/RZ:** $S = 2N$ — examiners deduct 2 marks for omitting this.
> 3. **Wrong Nyquist formula:** Some students write $f_s = 2f$ only when $f_s$ is the **minimum**. The full inequality is $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$.
> 4. **Manchester convention error:** "Bit 1 = H-to-L" is the **IEEE 802.3** convention; G.E. Thomas convention reverses it. State your convention explicitly in the exam.
> 5. **QAM bit-rate calculation:** Students often forget to multiply $S$ by $n$ bits/symbol. The final answer should be in **bps**, not baud.
> 6. **PCM bit rate formula:** $R_b = f_s \times n$ — mixing this up with $R_b = 2B \log_2 L$ (which is the maximum capacity) loses 1 mark.
> 7. **AMI "self-clocking" trap:** AMI has **no DC component** but does **not** synchronize on long runs of 0s. It synchronizes only on 1s. Mention both.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist for last-night revision!**

### ✅ Line Coding
- **NRZ-L:** Bit 1 = +V, Bit 0 = -V; $S = N$, has DC.
- **NRZ-I:** Bit 1 = transition, Bit 0 = no transition; $S = N$.
- **RZ:** Bit 1 = +V/0, Bit 0 = 0; $S = 2N$, excellent sync.
- **Manchester:** Bit 1 = H→L, Bit 0 = L→H (IEEE 802.3); $S = 2N$.
- **Differential Manchester:** Mid-bit always transitions; bit 1 = no start transition, bit 0 = start transition; polarity-immune.
- **AMI:** Bit 0 = 0V, Bit 1 = alternating +V/-V; DC-balanced, no sync on 0s.
- **2B1Q:** 2 bits → 1 of 4 levels; $S = N/2$, used in DSL.
- **MLT-3:** 3 levels cycled; used in 100BASE-TX.

### ✅ PCM (Analog → Digital)
- Three steps: **Sampling → Quantization → Encoding**.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- $L = 2^n$ levels with $n$ bits/sample.
- **Bit rate:** $R_b = f_s \times n$.
- **SQNR:** $6.02n + 1.76$ dB (each bit ≈ 6 dB improvement).
- Telephony: $f_s = 8$ kHz, $n = 8$ → $R_b = 64$ kbps (DS0).

### ✅ Delta Modulation
- 1 bit per sample: '1' if signal rising, '0' if falling.
- **Slope overload** when $\Delta / T_s < 2\pi f A$.
- **Granular noise** when $\Delta$ is too large for slow signals.
- **ADM** varies $\Delta$ adaptively to fix both problems.

### ✅ Digital-to-Analog (Passband Modulation)
- **ASK:** $s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ or 0.
- **FSK:** Two frequencies $f_1, f_2$.
- **BPSK:** $s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$, $\phi \in \{0, \pi\}$, $n = 1$ bit/symbol.
- **QPSK:** 4 phases, $n = 2$ bits/symbol.
- **QAM:** Both amplitude and phase vary; $n = \log_2 M$.
- **Bit rate of QAM:** $R_b = 2B \log_2 M$ (Nyquist limit for noiseless channel).

### ✅ Bandwidth & Capacity
- **Nyquist (noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2 L$ bps.
- **Shannon (noisy):** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ bps.
- **Practical:** $R = \min(\text{Nyquist}, \text{Shannon})$.

### ✅ Transmission Modes
- **Parallel:** $n$ wires for $n$ bits; fast, short distance only (e.g., old printer ports).
- **Serial Asynchronous:** Start bit + data + parity + stop bits (e.g., RS-232).
- **Serial Synchronous:** Continuous framed blocks (e.g., HDLC, Ethernet).
- **Isochronous:** Guaranteed timing, used in USB and real-time streams.

### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize
- Telephony: **4 kHz** bandwidth → **8 kHz** sampling → **8 bits** → **64 kbps** DS0.
- Each extra bit in PCM: **+6 dB** SQNR.
- Manchester bandwidth: **2× NRZ**.
- 2B1Q: **halves** the baud rate.

### ✅ Most-Tested KTU Question Types
1. "Compare NRZ, RZ, Manchester with diagrams." (14 marks)
2. "A PCM system samples at ... kHz with ... bits/sample. Find bit rate and SQNR." (7 marks)
3. "Derive the Nyquist sampling theorem." (7 marks)
4. "A 16-QAM system transmits over ... kHz channel. Find bit rate." (7 marks)
5. "Explain PCM transmitter/receiver with block diagram." (7 marks)
6. "Differentiate between ASK, FSK, PSK." (3 marks)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Analog Transmission # Analog Transmission – Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 4):** Analog Transmission covers the mechanisms used to transmit **digital bit-streams** and **analog information** over a bandpass channel by modulating a high-frequency **carrier signal**. This is the fundamental bridge between baseband data and the physical radio/copper/wireless medium. ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Analog Transmission** is the process of converting a baseband signal (digital or analog) into a bandpass signal by varying one or more properties (amplitude, frequency, or phase) of a high-frequency sinusoidal **carrier wave**. The carrier has the general form: $$ s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_c) $$ where: - $A_c$ = Carrier **amplitude** - $f_c$ = Carrier **frequency** - $\phi_c$ = Carrier **phase** Two principal conversions exist in the KTU syllabus: | Conversion Type | Input | Output | Modulation Schemes | |---|---|---|---| | **Digital-to-Analog (D/A)** | Digital bit-stream | Analog bandpass signal | ASK, FSK, PSK, QPSK, QAM | | **Analog-to-Analog (A/A)** | Analog baseband signal | Analog bandpass signal | AM, FM, PM | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuition: The "Shouting Across a Noisy Playground" Analogy** > > Imagine trying to pass a secret message across a noisy school playground. Your voice alone (the *baseband signal*) is too weak and gets lost. So you use a **whistle** (the *carrier signal*). You don't change the whistle's identity — you **modulate** it: > - **Blow harder (Amplitude change)** → ASK / AM > - **Change whistle pitch (Frequency change)** → FSK / FM > - **Start/stop the whistle at exact moments (Phase change)** → PSK / PM > > A friend on the other side **demodulates** (interprets) the whistle to recover the original secret. The "whistle" is the carrier, and your modulation choices are the "language" of transmission. ## 1.3 Why Analog Transmission Exists in a "Digital" World > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Engineering Reason:** Most practical communication channels (telephone lines, optical fiber drivers, RF antennas, satellite links) are **bandpass** in nature. They physically **cannot** transmit low-frequency baseband signals over long distances without severe attenuation and interference. Modulation shifts the signal's spectrum into the channel's passband, enables **multiplexing (FDM)**, and improves **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** through bandwidth expansion. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Carrier frequency ($f_c$)**: typically in kHz (AM radio), MHz (FM radio), or GHz (satellite/Wi-Fi). - **Bit Rate ($N_b$)**: bits per second (bps). - **Baud Rate ($S$)**: signal elements (symbols) per second. - **Bandwidth ($B$)**: in Hz, the spectral width occupied by the modulated signal. - **Bit Rate vs Baud Rate relationship**: $N_b = S \times \log_2 L$, where $L$ = number of signal levels (distinct symbols). # Analog Transmission – Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Digital-to-Analog Conversion — The Four Pillars The KTU syllabus treats **four** D/A schemes in depth. All of them take a digital bitstream $b(t)$ and use it to modulate one attribute of the carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_c)$. ### Pillar 1 — ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying) - **Operational Logic:** The amplitude $A_c$ is switched between two (binary) or more (M-ary) discrete levels. Frequency and phase are held constant. - **Binary ASK (OOK – On-Off Keying):** $$ s(t) = \begin{cases} A_1 \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{bit} = 1 \\ A_2 \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{bit} = 0 \end{cases} $$ - **Susceptibility:** Highly vulnerable to **noise** (noise directly corrupts amplitude) — hence rarely used alone in modern systems except in low-cost optical links (e.g., 433 MHz RF remotes). ### Pillar 2 — FSK (Frequency Shift Keying) - **Operational Logic:** The frequency $f_c$ is shifted between $f_1$ and $f_2$ while amplitude stays constant. $$ s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_1 t), & \text{bit} = 1 \\ A_c \cos(2\pi f_2 t), & \text{bit} = 0 \end{cases} $$ - **Special Case — MSK (Minimum Shift Keying):** $f_2 - f_1 = 0.5 \times \text{baud rate}$, which gives the **most spectrally efficient** FSK variant. - **Susceptibility:** Immune to amplitude noise; more bandwidth than PSK. ### Pillar 3 — PSK (Phase Shift Keying) - **Operational Logic:** The phase $\phi_c$ is rotated to encode bits. Amplitude and frequency are constant. - **Binary PSK (BPSK):** $\phi_c \in \{0, \pi\}$. $$ s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_c), \quad \phi_c = 0 \text{ or } \pi $$ - **QPSK (Quadrature PSK):** $\phi_c \in \{0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2\}$ — encodes **2 bits per symbol**, halving the bandwidth requirement of BPSK at the same bit rate. - **Susceptibility:** Very robust; used in deep-space communication (NASA standard), Wi-Fi legacy modes, and cellular. ### Pillar 4 — QAM (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation) - **Operational Logic:** **Simultaneous** variation of **both** amplitude **and** phase. Two carriers — one in-phase ($I$) and one quadrature ($Q$, 90° shifted) — are summed. $$ s(t) = d_1(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) + d_2(t) \sin(2\pi f_c t) $$ - **M-ary QAM:** $M = 2^n$ signal points in the I-Q constellation (e.g., 16-QAM = 4 bits/symbol, 64-QAM = 6 bits/symbol, 256-QAM = 8 bits/symbol). - **Use Case:** Wi-Fi (802.11ac/ax), 4G/5G LTE, DVB-C cable TV, ADSL. ## 2.2 Analog-to-Analog Conversion — The Three Pillars ### Pillar 1 — AM (Amplitude Modulation) $$ s_{AM}(t) = [A_c + m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ - $m(t)$ = baseband message signal (band-limited to $W$ Hz). - **Bandwidth:** $B_{AM} = 2W$ (double sideband with carrier). - **Disadvantage:** Wasteful — the carrier carries **no information**; up to **2/3 of the transmitted power** is in the carrier. ### Pillar 2 — FM (Frequency Modulation) $$ s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{0}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau\right) $$ - $k_f$ = frequency sensitivity (Hz/volt). - **Bandwidth (Carson's Rule):** $$ B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W) $$ where $\Delta f = k_f \cdot |m(t)|_{\max}$ and $W$ is the message bandwidth. ### Pillar 3 — PM (Phase Modulation) $$ s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + k_p \, m(t)) $$ - $k_p$ = phase sensitivity (radians/volt). - Closely related to FM — if $m(t)$ is integrated before PM, you get FM, and vice-versa. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULA CHEAT-SHEET — Memorize for KTU University Exam (ESE).** > **Note on syntax:** Absolute value / vertical bar has been rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bit Rate to Baud Rate | $N_b = S \times \log_2 L$ | $N_b$ = bit rate, $S$ = baud rate, $L$ = signal levels | bps, baud | | 2 | Baud Rate to Bit Rate | $S = N_b / n$, where $n = \log_2 L$ | $n$ = bits per signal element | baud | | 3 | ASK Bandwidth | $B_{ASK} = (1+d) \times S$ | $d$ = duty cycle ($0 \le d \le 1$) | Hz | | 4 | BFSK Bandwidth | $B_{BFSK} = (1+d) \times S + 2\Delta f$ | $\Delta f = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert / 2$ | Hz | | 5 | BPSK Bandwidth | $B_{BPSK} = (1+d) \times S$ | — | Hz | | 6 | QPSK Bandwidth | $B_{QPSK} = (1+d) \times S / 2$ (per bit) | — | Hz | | 7 | M-ary PSK/QAM Bandwidth | $B = (1+d) \times N_b / n$ | $n$ = bits/symbol | Hz | | 8 | QAM Minimum Bandwidth | $B_{QAM,min} = N_b / \log_2 M$ | $M$ = constellation size | Hz | | 9 | AM Bandwidth | $B_{AM} = 2W$ | $W$ = message bandwidth | Hz | | 10 | FM Bandwidth (Carson) | $B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W)$ | $\Delta f$ = peak freq. deviation | Hz | | 11 | Carrier Power Efficiency (AM) | $\eta = \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2}$ | $m$ = modulation index ($0 \le m \le 1$) | dimensionless | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ASK** → Optical fiber (LED on/off), low-cost RFID, IR remote controls. - **BFSK** → Early modems (Bell 202, 1200 bps), Bluetooth (GFSK variant), pager networks. - **BPSK/QPSK** → GPS, satellite TV (DVB-S), deep-space probes (Voyager), Wi-Fi CCK modes. - **QAM** → Wi-Fi (802.11n → 256-QAM, 802.11ax → 1024-QAM), DOCSIS 3.1 cable modem (up to 4096-QAM), LTE/5G NR downlink. - **AM** → AM broadcast radio (535–1705 kHz), aviation NDB beacons. - **FM** → FM broadcast radio (88–108 MHz), two-way radios, analog TV audio. - **PM** → Used implicitly in PSK and QAM digital systems. # Analog Transmission – Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Anti-Truncation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition below is explicitly written. No shorthand, no skipped steps. Suitable for full KTU 14-mark derivations. ## 3.1 Derivation: General Bandwidth of a Modulated Digital Signal A digital signal of bit rate $N_b$ has a **baseband** (rectangular pulse) bandwidth approximately equal to $N_b/2$ Hz by Nyquist. However, after modulation onto a carrier, the signal is **doubled** in spectrum (positive and negative frequencies around $f_c$). Hence: $$ B_{\text{baseband, ideal}} = \frac{N_b}{2} $$ Multiplying by the carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ shifts this baseband to $\pm f_c$ but does **not** change its width: $$ B_{\text{modulated, ideal}} = \frac{N_b}{2} \quad \text{(for BPSK)} $$ In real systems, the pulse is not perfectly rectangular. The **first-null bandwidth** is given by: $$ B = \frac{N_b}{2} \times (1 + d) $$ where $d$ is a "roll-off" factor accounting for filter shape. Derivation of $d$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{A rectangular pulse of duration } T = 1/N_b \text{ has spectral nulls at } \pm 1/T. \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{First-null bandwidth} = 2 \times (1/T) = 2N_b. \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{For raised-cosine filtering, the roll-off is } d. \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & B = (1+d) \times N_b \quad \text{... but with one symbol per bit (BPSK), } N_b = S. \\ \text{Step 5:} \quad & B_{BPSK} = (1+d) \times S. \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: QAM Bit Rate vs Bandwidth (Most Important for KTU Problems) For an M-ary QAM constellation with $M = 2^n$ points: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Each symbol carries } n = \log_2 M \text{ bits.} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Therefore, baud rate } S = N_b / n. \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{For ideal Nyquist channel: } B_{\min} = S / 2. \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{With raised-cosine roll-off } d: \\ & B = \frac{S}{2} \times (1+d) = \frac{N_b}{2n} \times (1+d). \end{aligned} $$ **Final QAM bandwidth formula:** $$ \boxed{B_{QAM} = \frac{N_b \times (1+d)}{2 \log_2 M}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Carson's Rule for FM Bandwidth Starting from the FM signal: $$ s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{0}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau\right) $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Instantaneous frequency: } f_i(t) = f_c + k_f \, m(t). \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Peak deviation: } \Delta f = k_f \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{\max}. \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Spectrum has components up to } f_c + \Delta f \text{ (above) and } f_c - \Delta f \text{ (below).} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Total spectral width on one side} = \Delta f + W \text{ (where } W \text{ = message BW).} \\ \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Double it for both sidebands: } B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W). \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU University Exam Style) **Problem:** A 64-QAM system transmits at $N_b = 72 \text{ kbps}$ over a channel with raised-cosine roll-off $d = 0.2$. Calculate the **bandwidth** required. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & M = 64 \implies n = \log_2 64 = 6 \text{ bits/symbol.} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & S = N_b / n = 72000 / 6 = 12000 \text{ baud.} \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & B = \frac{S}{2} \times (1+d) = \frac{12000}{2} \times 1.2. \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & B = 6000 \times 1.2 = 7200 \text{ Hz} = 7.2 \text{ kHz}. \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** $B = 7.2 \text{ kHz}$. ## 3.5 Worked Example 2: FSK Bandwidth with Frequency Separation **Problem:** Binary FSK transmits at 4000 bps. The two carrier frequencies are 50 kHz and 55 kHz. Find the bandwidth assuming $d = 0$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & S = N_b = 4000 \text{ baud (binary, } n=1). \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \Delta f = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert / 2 = \vert 50000 - 55000 \vert / 2 = 2500 \text{ Hz}. \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & B_{BFSK} = (1+d) \times S + 2\Delta f = 1 \times 4000 + 2 \times 2500. \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & B = 4000 + 5000 = 9000 \text{ Hz} = 9 \text{ kHz}. \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** $B = 9 \text{ kHz}$. ## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Killer Comparison Table for ESE) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this comparison — it is the single most asked topic in KTU Module 4 (ESE).** | Parameter | ASK | FSK | PSK | QAM | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Varying attribute** | Amplitude | Frequency | Phase | Amplitude + Phase | | **Noise immunity** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good (constellation-dependent) | | **Bandwidth efficiency** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | **Highest** | | **Power efficiency** | Poor | Moderate | Best (BPSK) | Moderate | | **Detection complexity** | Trivial (envelope) | Moderate (filters) | Hard (coherent) | Hardest | | **Example applications** | Optical link, RFID | Bluetooth (GFSK) | GPS, satellite | Wi-Fi, LTE, DOCSIS | # Analog Transmission – Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: End-to-End Digital-to-Analog Transmission System ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Digital Source
e.g., Computer] --> enc[Channel Encoder
FEC Scrambler] enc --> pcm[PCM / Line Coding
NRZ Manchester] pcm --> mod[Digital to Analog
Modulator] subgraph CARRIER_GEN[Carrier Generation Subsystem] osc[Crystal Oscillator
fc Reference] --> car[Carrier Signal
Ac cos 2 pi fc t] end CARRIER_GEN --> mod mod --> chan[Bandpass Channel
Cable / RF / Fiber] chan --> noise[Channel Noise
+ Attenuation] noise --> demod[Analog to Digital
Demodulator] car2[Local Oscillator
Coherent Reference] --> demod demod --> dec[Channel Decoder
FEC Correction] dec --> sink[Digital Sink
e.g., Server] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** The block diagram above is the **canonical pipeline** the KTU board expects. Note the *Local Oscillator* feeding the demodulator — this is the **coherent reference** that makes PSK and QAM possible. A non-coherent (envelope) detector works for ASK and BFSK only. ## 4.2 Constellation Diagram: Visualizing PSK and QAM ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BPSK[BPSK Constellation - 2 points] P0((Bit 0
phase 0)) P1((Bit 1
phase pi)) end subgraph QPSK[QPSK Constellation - 4 points] Q00((00)) Q01((01)) Q11((11)) Q10((10)) end subgraph Q16[16-QAM Constellation - 16 points] direction TB row1((o o o o)) row2((o o o o)) row3((o o o o)) row4((o o o o)) end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why constellations matter for KTU:** The **distance between adjacent points** in a constellation diagram directly determines the **BER (Bit Error Rate)**. Larger separation ⇒ more noise tolerance. BPSK and QPSK are on a circle (constant amplitude); QAM points lie on a **grid** — explaining why QAM needs linear amplifiers and is more sensitive to amplitude distortion. ## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Comparison: AM vs FM Spectrum ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AM_SPEC[AM Spectrum] UC[fc Carrier
TALL spike] USB[fc + W
Upper Sideband] LSB[fc - W
Lower Sideband] end subgraph FM_SPEC[FM Spectrum] F0[fc - Delta f - W] F1[fc - Delta f] F2[fc] F3[fc + Delta f] F4[fc + Delta f + W] end style UC fill:#fbb,stroke:#c00 style F2 fill:#bfb,stroke:#080 ``` > [!NOTE] > **AM Spectrum Reading:** The tall red spike is the **carrier** — pure wasted power. Two small side lobes contain the actual information. > **FM Spectrum Reading:** The green central spike is the carrier; multiple sidebands extend out to $f_c \pm (\Delta f + W)$. Notice FM bandwidth is much wider than AM but trades bandwidth for superior noise immunity (Carson's rule governs the spread). ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Choosing a Modulation Scheme ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Need to transmit bits] --> q1{Power
constrained?} q1 -- Yes --> psk[Use BPSK or QPSK
constant envelope] q1 -- No --> q2{Bandwidth
constrained?} q2 -- Yes --> qam[Use high-order QAM
64 256 1024] q2 -- No --> q3{Channel has
amplitude noise?} q3 -- Severe --> fsk[Use FSK or GFSK
amplitude immune] q3 -- Mild --> psk2[Use PSK family
moderate complexity] ``` # Analog Transmission – Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model QP]` **Differentiate between Bit Rate and Baud Rate. A system uses 16-QAM with a bit rate of 48 kbps. Find the baud rate.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Bit Rate ($N_b$):** Number of bits transmitted **per second** (bps). - **Baud Rate ($S$):** Number of **signal elements (symbols)** transmitted per second. For 16-QAM: number of bits per signal element $n = \log_2 16 = 4$ bits/symbol. $$ S = N_b / n = 48000 / 4 = 12000 \text{ baud} $$ **Answer:** **Baud rate = 12 kbaud.** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP]` **State Carson's rule for FM bandwidth and define each term with units.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Carson's rule gives the approximate transmission bandwidth of an FM signal: $$ B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W) $$ - **$\Delta f$** = peak frequency deviation in **Hz** (maximum shift of instantaneous frequency from $f_c$). - **$W$** = bandwidth of the baseband modulating (message) signal in **Hz**. - **$B_{FM}$** = total FM transmission bandwidth in **Hz**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:** > - **Do NOT confuse bit rate and baud rate** — examiners deduct 2 marks instantly for this in any D/A problem. > - In **Carson rule** derivations, students forget to **double** the result; the entire bandwidth includes BOTH sidebands. > - For **QAM bandwidth problems**, forgetting to divide by $\log_2 M$ leads to massively wrong answers (off by a factor of 6 for 64-QAM). > - In diagrams, **never** use markdown bold (`**`) or italics inside Mermaid node labels — KTU online valuation portal will **not render** them. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme ESE]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Digital-to-Analog converter** system. Compare **ASK, FSK, and PSK** in terms of bandwidth, noise immunity, and detection complexity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 64-QAM modulator transmits at a bit rate of 54 Mbps over a channel with raised-cosine roll-off factor $d = 0.25$. Calculate: (i) the baud rate, (ii) the minimum Nyquist bandwidth, (iii) the actual bandwidth. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q.A(a) (7 marks): **1. Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 above — 2 marks for diagram and labeling). **2. Working Explanation** (2 marks): The digital source bit stream is encoded for line transmission (e.g., NRZ), then fed to a modulator that varies one attribute of the carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_c)$ in accordance with the bit pattern. The modulated bandpass signal propagates through the channel. At the receiver, a coherent (or non-coherent) detector recovers the baseband pattern, which is then decoded back to bits. **3. Comparison Table** (3 marks — see Section 3.6). #### Model Solution to Q.A(b) (7 marks): **(i) Baud rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} & M = 64 \implies n = \log_2 64 = 6 \text{ bits/symbol.} \\ & S = N_b / n = 54 \times 10^6 / 6 = 9 \times 10^6 = 9 \text{ Mbaud.} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Minimum Nyquist bandwidth** (ideal, $d = 0$): $$ B_{\min} = S / 2 = 9 \times 10^6 / 2 = 4.5 \text{ MHz.} $$ **(iii) Actual bandwidth** (with roll-off $d = 0.25$): $$ B = B_{\min} \times (1 + d) = 4.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.25 = 5.625 \text{ MHz.} $$ **Final answers:** $S = 9$ Mbaud, $B_{\min} = 4.5$ MHz, $B_{\text{actual}} = 5.625$ MHz. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme ESE]` **(a)** With necessary equations and neat spectra, explain **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** and **Frequency Modulation (FM)**. Why is FM preferred over AM in high-fidelity broadcasting? **(7 marks)** **(b)** An FM broadcast station transmits a music signal with maximum modulating frequency $W = 15 \text{ kHz}$ and peak frequency deviation $\Delta f = 75 \text{ kHz}$. Using Carson's rule, determine: (i) the transmission bandwidth, (ii) the modulation index $\beta$, (iii) comment on the bandwidth improvement if the same signal were transmitted using AM. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q.B(a) (7 marks): **AM Equation** (2 marks): $$ s_{AM}(t) = [A_c + m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ **AM Spectrum** (1 mark): Carrier spike at $f_c$ + two sidebands spanning $[f_c - W, f_c + W]$; **$B_{AM} = 2W$**. **FM Equation** (2 marks): $$ s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\,d\tau\right) $$ **FM Spectrum** (1 mark): Multiple sidebands; total width given by **$B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W)$**. **Why FM is preferred** (1 mark): FM has **superior noise immunity** because most noise is amplitude-based, and FM receivers clip amplitude variations before detection (the limiter). This is **traded for higher bandwidth** — a deliberate SNR-for-bandwidth swap. #### Model Solution to Q.B(b) (7 marks): **(i) Transmission bandwidth (Carson):** $$ B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(75000 + 15000) = 2 \times 90000 = 180 \text{ kHz.} $$ **(ii) Modulation index:** $$ \beta = \Delta f / W = 75000 / 15000 = 5. $$ **(iii) AM comparison:** $$ B_{AM} = 2W = 2 \times 15 = 30 \text{ kHz.} $$ **Comment:** FM uses $180 / 30 = 6 \times$ **more bandwidth** than AM, but achieves vastly superior noise rejection and audio fidelity — the canonical engineering trade-off. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Killer Revision Bullets — read this 5 minutes before walking into the exam hall.** - [x] **Bit Rate $\neq$ Baud Rate.** Baud = symbols/sec; Bit rate = baud × $\log_2 L$. - [x] **ASK bandwidth = BPSK bandwidth = $(1+d) \times S$** (when $n=1$). - [x] **BFSK bandwidth has the extra $2\Delta f$** term — this is the most-asked formula in KTU Module 4. - [x] **QAM formula:** $B = N_b(1+d) / (2 \log_2 M)$. **Forget the 2** and you lose 7 marks. - [x] **Carson Rule:** $B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W)$. Always **double** for both sidebands. - [x] **AM bandwidth = $2W$** (smallest analog bandwidth; carrier is wasted power). - [x] **Constellation diagrams:** PSK points lie on a **circle**; QAM points on a **grid**. - [x] **Coherent detection** is required for PSK and QAM; **non-coherent (envelope)** is enough for ASK and FSK. - [x] **$\Delta f$ in Carson rule is HALF the difference** between the two BFSK frequencies: $\Delta f = |f_1 - f_2|/2$. - [x] **Modulation index** $\beta = \Delta f / W$ for FM, and $m = A_m/A_c$ for AM. - [x] **Real-world link:** QAM = Wi-Fi/LTE; PSK = GPS/satellite; FSK = Bluetooth; AM/FM = broadcast radio. - [x] **Trade-off triangle:** Bandwidth ↔ Power ↔ Noise immunity. You can optimize any two, but not all three.

topics.title: Bandwidth Utilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bandwidth Utilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bandwidth Utilization** is the systematic use of available bandwidth (channel capacity) to achieve specific goals, primarily to **multiplex** multiple signals over a single transmission medium and to **spread** a signal to enhance security, resistance to interference, and immunity to jamming.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4), Bandwidth Utilization is divided into two main pillars:

1. **Multiplexing** — Sharing a single channel among multiple signals (FDM, TDM, WDM, CDM).
2. **Spreading** — Spreading the bandwidth of a signal to gain redundancy and resistance (FHSS, DSSS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4)**
> The official module descriptor explicitly lists:
> *Multiplexing (FDM, TDM, Synchronous vs Statistical TDM, WDM, CDMA), Spread Spectrum (FHSS, DSSS).*
> Mastery of this topic is **mandatory** for KTU End Semester Examinations and is a frequent carrier of 14-mark Part B questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **single-lane national highway** that you want to upgrade without acquiring more land. Three possible engineering solutions exist:

- **Frequency Division (FDM):** Divide the highway into parallel sub-lanes by **frequency** — different "speeds" of vehicles use different sub-lanes simultaneously. Each sub-lane is separated by a guard strip (guard band).
- **Time Division (TDM):** Install **traffic lights** at intervals. Each car takes a fixed time slot, and the highway is fully utilized in a round-robin fashion.
- **Code Division (CDM):** Every car is assigned a **unique language** (chipping code). All cars can speak at the same time, and the receiver knows each language, so it picks out its own car.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bandwidth Utilization ≠ Bandwidth itself.** Utilization is the *engineering discipline* of maximizing what a given bandwidth can do — analogous to how a logistics manager maximizes what a given truck can carry.

### 1.3 The Two Pillars at a Glance

| Pillar | Goal | Techniques |
|---|---|---|
| **Multiplexing** | Combine many signals → 1 channel | FDM, TDM, WDM, CDM |
| **Spreading** | Expand 1 signal → wider bandwidth | FHSS, DSSS |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember: **Multiplexing shares** the channel; **Spreading expands** the signal. They solve different problems and are sometimes used together (e.g., CDMA is a form of multiplexing that uses spread-spectrum codes).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Multiplexing — Detailed Theory

#### 2.1.1 Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)

**Concept:** Each source modulates a different **carrier frequency**. The modulated signals are then summed into a single composite signal and transmitted. At the receiver, **band-pass filters** demultiplex the channels.

- **Carrier frequencies** must be spaced apart by at least the bandwidth of each source signal.
- The unused gap between adjacent channels is called the **guard band** — necessary to prevent overlap due to imperfect filters.
- Analog technique; widely used in **AM/FM radio broadcasting, cable TV, and the analog telephone hierarchy**.

**Mathematical Model:**

If each source signal occupies a bandwidth of $B_s$ Hz, and there are $N$ sources, the total composite bandwidth is:

$$B_{FDM} = N \cdot B_s + (N-1) \cdot B_{gb}$$

where $B_{gb}$ is the guard band between channels.

#### 2.1.2 Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)

**Concept:** The full channel bandwidth is allocated to **one source at a time**, on a rotating basis. Each source gets a fixed time slot within a repeating **frame**.

- **Synchronous TDM (STDM):** Every source gets a slot in *every* frame, regardless of whether it has data. Simple but wasteful.
- **Asynchronous / Statistical TDM (ATDM):** Slots are assigned *only* to active sources. Requires addressing bits and a more complex frame, but achieves much higher utilization.
- Digital technique; foundation of **T1/E1 digital telephony** and **SONET/SDH**.

**Mathematical Model:**

If each source transmits at a bit rate of $R_s$ bits/s, the output link rate is:

$$R_{TDM} = N \cdot R_s + \text{(framing + addressing overhead)}$$

For synchronous TDM with no overhead, the frame duration is:

$$T_{frame} = N \cdot T_{slot} = N \cdot \frac{1}{R_s}$$

#### 2.1.3 Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)

**Concept:** A special case of FDM applied to **fiber-optic** channels. Different optical signals travel at different wavelengths (colors) through the same fiber.

- **DWDM (Dense WDM):** Up to 160+ channels in a single fiber, channel spacing of 25, 50, or 100 GHz.
- **CWDM (Coarse WDM):** Wider spacing (~20 nm), cheaper, fewer channels (up to 18).

#### 2.1.4 Code Division Multiplexing (CDM / CDMA)

**Concept:** All sources transmit **simultaneously over the entire bandwidth** using unique, orthogonal (or quasi-orthogonal) **chip codes**. The receiver uses correlation with the intended code to extract the desired signal.

- Each source's data bit (0 or 1) is multiplied by a chip sequence of length $m$ (e.g., $m = 8$ in the classic Walsh code example).
- The chip rate is therefore $m$ times the data rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CDMA = Multiplexing + Spread Spectrum.** It uses spread-spectrum codes as the multiplexing key — a hybrid technique that is heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 2.2 Spreading — Detailed Theory

The goal of spread spectrum is **not** bandwidth efficiency but **resilience**. By spreading the signal energy over a much wider bandwidth, we make it:

- Resistant to narrow-band interference and jamming.
- Hard to detect (low spectral density — looks like noise).
- Resistant to multipath fading.
- Capable of **code-division** multiple access (CDMA).

#### 2.2.1 Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)

**Concept:** The signal "hops" among many different carrier frequencies according to a **pseudo-random sequence** known to both transmitter and receiver. A "hop" occupies a short time called the **dwell time**.

- **Slow FHSS:** Multiple data bits per hop.
- **Fast FHSS:** Multiple hops per data bit.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$T_{hop} = \frac{1}{\text{Hop Rate}}$$

The total bandwidth occupied is the union of all $N$ hop channels:

$$B_{total} = N \cdot B_{channel}$$

#### 2.2.2 Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)

**Concept:** Each data bit is XORed with a high-rate **pseudo-random noise (PN) code** called a *chipping sequence*. The result has a much higher bit rate (chip rate) and occupies a wider bandwidth.

**Mathematical Model:**

Let $R_b$ be the data bit rate and $m$ be the number of chips per bit. Then:

- **Chip rate:** $R_c = m \cdot R_b$
- **Processing Gain:** $G_p = 10 \log_{10}(m)$ dB
- **Spread bandwidth (approx., Nyquist):** $B_{DSSS} \approx 2 \cdot R_c = 2 m R_b$

> [!NOTE]
> **Processing Gain** quantifies the SNR improvement from spreading. A larger $m$ → better interference rejection but wider bandwidth.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FDM total bandwidth | $B_{FDM} = N B_s + (N-1) B_{gb}$ | $N$ = sources, $B_s$ Hz, $B_{gb}$ Hz | Guard bands reduce net efficiency |
| 2 | TDM link rate (sync) | $R_{TDM} = N R_s$ | $R_s$ bits/s per source | No overhead case |
| 3 | TDM frame duration | $T_{frame} = N \cdot \frac{1}{R_s}$ | Seconds | Each source gets one slot |
| 4 | Statistical TDM efficiency | $\eta = \frac{k \cdot R_s}{R_{link}}$ | $k$ active sources out of $N$ | With addressing overhead |
| 5 | DSSS chip rate | $R_c = m R_b$ | $m$ = chips per bit, integer | Bit → chips expansion |
| 6 | DSSS Processing Gain | $G_p = 10 \log_{10}(m)$ | Decibels (dB) | Measures interference rejection |
| 7 | DSSS spread bandwidth | $B_{DSSS} \approx 2 R_c$ | Hz (Nyquist) | Signal is baseband |
| 8 | FHSS hop dwell time | $T_{hop} = \frac{1}{f_{hop}}$ | $f_{hop}$ = hops/sec | Slow vs Fast FHSS |
| 9 | CDMA channels (orthogonal) | $N_{max} = m$ | $m$ = code length (Walsh) | Hard limit on simultaneous users |
| 10 | Shannon capacity (for context) | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Bits/s | Shows why spreading trades efficiency for robustness |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pattern:** KTU loves numericals on FDM guard-band calculation, TDM frame/cycle time, and DSSS processing gain. Work through every step.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — FDM Bandwidth Calculation

**Problem:** Five voice channels, each with bandwidth of **4 kHz**, are multiplexed using FDM. The guard band between any two adjacent channels is **500 Hz**. Calculate the total composite bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**
- Number of sources: $N = 5$
- Per-source bandwidth: $B_s = 4 \text{ kHz} = 4000 \text{ Hz}$
- Guard band: $B_{gb} = 500 \text{ Hz}$

**Step 2 — Apply the FDM total bandwidth formula.**

$$B_{FDM} = N \cdot B_s + (N-1) \cdot B_{gb}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$B_{FDM} = 5 \times 4000 + (5 - 1) \times 500$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$B_{FDM} = 20{,}000 + 4 \times 500 = 20{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 22{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 22 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency check.**

$$\eta_{FDM} = \frac{N \cdot B_s}{B_{FDM}} = \frac{20{,}000}{22{,}000} = 0.909 = 90.9\%$$

The 9.1% "loss" represents the guard-band overhead.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Synchronous TDM Frame Timing

**Problem:** Four digital sources, each generating data at **8 kbps**, are multiplexed using Synchronous TDM. Each source sends 1 bit per time slot. Calculate:
- (a) The duration of one source time slot
- (b) The duration of one TDM frame
- (c) The output link bit rate

**Step 1 — Duration of one time slot.**

Each source sends 1 bit at 8 kbps, so the time to send 1 bit is:

$$T_{slot} = \frac{1}{R_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Duration of one frame.**

One frame contains one slot from each of the 4 sources:

$$T_{frame} = N \cdot T_{slot} = 4 \times 125 \, \mu s = 500 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Output link rate.**

$$R_{TDM} = N \cdot R_s = 4 \times 8000 = 32 \text{ kbps}$$

> [!TIP]
> General rule: Output link rate in synchronous TDM (no framing overhead) is the **sum of the source rates**. This is a recurring exam question.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — DSSS Processing Gain

**Problem:** A DSSS system uses a chipping sequence of length **m = 16** to spread a data signal at $R_b = 10 \text{ kbps}$. Calculate:
- (a) The chip rate
- (b) The processing gain in dB
- (c) The approximate spread bandwidth (Nyquist)

**Step 1 — Chip rate.**

$$R_c = m \cdot R_b = 16 \times 10{,}000 = 160{,}000 \text{ chips/s} = 160 \text{ kcps}$$

**Step 2 — Processing gain.**

$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}(m) = 10 \log_{10}(16)$$

$$\log_{10}(16) = \log_{10}(2^4) = 4 \log_{10}(2) = 4 \times 0.30103 = 1.20412$$

$$G_p = 10 \times 1.20412 = 12.04 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Spread bandwidth (Nyquist).**

$$B_{DSSS} \approx 2 \cdot R_c = 2 \times 160{,}000 = 320 \text{ kHz}$$

**Verification by substitution:**

The original signal (no spreading) would need only $2 R_b = 20 \text{ kHz}$. The spread signal uses $320 \text{ kHz}$ — a 16× expansion, exactly matching the chipping length $m = 16$. ✓

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — FHSS Hopping Calculations

**Problem:** An FHSS system uses **20** hop channels, each of bandwidth **400 kHz**, and hops every **10 ms**. Calculate:
- (a) The hop rate
- (b) The total bandwidth occupied
- (c) Whether it is slow or fast FHSS for a 1 kbps data source

**Step 1 — Hop rate.**

$$f_{hop} = \frac{1}{T_{hop}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = 100 \text{ hops/s}$$

**Step 2 — Total bandwidth.**

$$B_{total} = N \cdot B_{channel} = 20 \times 400 \text{ kHz} = 8 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Slow or fast?**

Bits per hop: $1 \text{ kbps} \times 10 \text{ ms} = 10$ bits per hop.

Since there are **multiple data bits per hop**, this is **Slow FHSS**. ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | FDM | TDM | WDM | CDM/CDMA | FHSS | DSSS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Domain | Frequency | Time | Wavelength | Code | Frequency (time-varying) | Time (code) |
| Signal type | Analog/Digital | Digital | Optical | Digital | Digital | Digital |
| Guard entity | Guard band | Sync time | Wavelength gap | Orthogonal codes | Hop spacing | Chip redundancy |
| Hardware complexity | Filters | Clock + sync | Prisms/gratings | Correlators | Synthesizer | Multiplier + PN generator |
| Best for | Radio, TV, cable | Telephony, T1/E1 | Backbone fiber | Cellular 3G | Bluetooth, Wi-Fi (older) | GPS, 3G cellular, Wi-Fi (b) |
| Main weakness | Wasted guard bands | Sync overhead | Optical nonlinearity | Near-far problem | Hop timing | Wide bandwidth |
| KTU exam weight | High | High | Medium | High | Medium | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FDM Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Source 1] -->|m1| C1[Carrier F1]
    S2[Source 2] -->|m2| C2[Carrier F2]
    S3[Source 3] -->|m3| C3[Carrier F3]
    S4[Source 4] -->|m4| C4[Carrier F4]
    C1 --> MUX[FDM Mux Combiner]
    C2 --> MUX
    C3 --> MUX
    C4 --> MUX
    MUX --> CH[Composite Channel]
    CH --> DEMUX[FDM DeMux Filter Bank]
    DEMUX --> R1[Filter F1 to Recv 1]
    DEMUX --> R2[Filter F2 to Recv 2]
    DEMUX --> R3[Filter F3 to Recv 3]
    DEMUX --> R4[Filter F4 to Recv 4]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each source modulates its own carrier. The Mux "Combiner" sums them in the frequency domain. The Demux uses **band-pass filters** tuned to the carrier frequencies to recover each source.

### 4.2 Synchronous TDM Frame Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[Frame N] --> A[Slot 1 Source A]
    A --> B[Slot 2 Source B]
    B --> C[Slot 3 Source C]
    C --> D[Slot 4 Source D]
    D --> E[Frame N plus 1 Begins]
    E --> A2[Slot 1 Source A]
    A2 --> B2[Slot 2 Source B]
    B2 --> C2[Slot 3 Source C]
    C2 --> D2[Slot 4 Source D]
```

### 4.3 DSSS Spreading Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DB[Data Bit 0 or 1] --> XOR[XOR Multiplier]
    PN[PN Chipping Sequence] --> XOR
    XOR --> TXS[Transmitted Spread Signal Wide BW]
    TXS --> CH[Channel plus Noise plus Jamming]
    CH --> RXS[Receiver Wideband Front End]
    RXS --> XOR2[XOR Despreading]
    PN2[Same PN Code Synchronized] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> RD[Recovered Data Bit]
    NOISE[Interference Narrowband] -.-> RXS
    NOISE -.-> XPD[Spreads into Noise Like Residue]
    XPD -.-> DISC[Discarded]
```

### 4.4 FHSS Hop Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time Slot 1 Freq F3] --> T1[Time Slot 2 Freq F7]
    T1 --> T2[Time Slot 3 Freq F1]
    T2 --> T3[Time Slot 4 Freq F5]
    T3 --> T4[Time Slot 5 Freq F2]
    T4 --> T5[Time Slot 6 Freq F8]
    T5 --> T6[Time Slot 7 Freq F4]
    T6 --> T7[Time Slot 8 Freq F6]
    T7 --> T8[Loops Back to Pattern]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the FHSS diagram:** The hop sequence $(3, 7, 1, 5, 2, 8, 4, 6)$ is the pseudo-random pattern known to both transmitter and receiver. An eavesdropper without the pattern sees only brief frequency bursts.

### 4.5 Statistical TDM vs Synchronous TDM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STDM[Synchronous TDM]
        S1A[Slot 1 A] --> S1B[Slot 2 B]
        S1B --> S1C[Slot 3 C IDLE]
        S1C --> S1D[Slot 4 D]
    end
    subgraph ATDM[Statistical TDM]
        A1[Slot 1 A plus Address] --> A2[Slot 2 C plus Address]
        A2 --> A3[Slot 3 D plus Address]
        A3 --> A4[Slot 4 B plus Address]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that in Synchronous TDM, **slot 3** is reserved for source C even if C has no data — wasted capacity. In Statistical TDM, **no slot is wasted**; instead, every slot carries an **address** to identify its owner.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) and Time Division Multiplexing (TDM). Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | FDM | TDM |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Frequency | Time |
| Simultaneity | All sources transmit **simultaneously** | Sources transmit **one at a time** in slots |
| Separation mechanism | Guard bands between carrier frequencies | Time-slot synchronization |
| Signal type | Mainly **analog** | Mainly **digital** |
| Hardware | Band-pass filters at receiver | Clock and frame synchronizer |
| Application | AM/FM radio broadcasting, cable TV | T1/E1 digital telephony, PCM |

*(3 marks — definition + 1 application each)*

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Processing Gain in DSSS? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

**Processing Gain** in Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum is the ratio of the spread (chip) rate to the original data rate, expressed in decibels:

$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{R_c}{R_b}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(m)$$

where $m$ is the number of chips per data bit.

**Importance:**
- It quantifies the **SNR improvement** achievable after dispreading at the receiver.
- A higher processing gain means **better immunity to narrowband interference and jamming**.
- It is a key metric in designing secure, robust wireless systems such as GPS and CDMA cellular.

*(3 marks — formula + importance)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> * Common mistake 1: In FDM problems, students forget to **multiply the guard band by (N − 1)**, not N. The guard band exists **between** channels, not on the outside.
> * Common mistake 2: In TDM, students confuse the **frame duration** with the **bit duration** of a single source. The frame contains slots from *all* sources.
> * Common mistake 3: In DSSS, students give the chip rate but forget to compute the **bandwidth using the Nyquist criterion** ($2 R_c$).
> * Common mistake 4: Mixing up **Slow FHSS** (multiple bits per hop) with **Fast FHSS** (multiple hops per bit). Remember: "**Slow** = bits **stay** longer in one hop" / "**Fast** = hops happen **fast** during one bit."

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of guard bands. A system multiplexes **6 voice channels** of bandwidth **4 kHz** each, with a **600 Hz** guard band between any two adjacent channels. Calculate the total composite bandwidth and the multiplexing efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Synchronous TDM and Statistical TDM with diagrams. Why is Statistical TDM more efficient? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — FDM with calculation:**

In FDM, multiple baseband signals are translated to disjoint **carrier frequencies** so they can share one physical channel. A **guard band** of unused spectrum is kept between adjacent carriers to prevent **inter-channel interference** caused by imperfect filters and spectral leakage.

For our numerical:

- $N = 6$, $B_s = 4 \text{ kHz}$, $B_{gb} = 600 \text{ Hz} = 0.6 \text{ kHz}$

$$B_{FDM} = N B_s + (N-1) B_{gb} = 6 \times 4 + 5 \times 0.6 = 24 + 3 = 27 \text{ kHz}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{N B_s}{B_{FDM}} = \frac{24}{27} = 0.8889 = 88.89\%$$

*(7 marks — 3 for explanation + 4 for calculation)*

**Part (b) — Synchronous vs Statistical TDM:**

- **Synchronous TDM:** Time is divided into fixed frames, and each source is **guaranteed** one slot per frame, even if it has no data. Simple, deterministic, no addressing needed.
- **Statistical (Asynchronous) TDM:** Slots are **dynamically** allocated only to active sources. Each slot carries a small **address field** to identify the source. Bandwidth-efficient but slightly more complex.
- **Why Statistical TDM is more efficient:** Eliminates idle slots; the addressing overhead is far smaller than the wasted slots of synchronous TDM, especially when traffic is **bursty** (as is typical in data networks).

*(7 marks — 3+3 for both explanations + 1 for the comparison justification)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** What is Spread Spectrum? Compare **FHSS** and **DSSS** in terms of their principle, advantages, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DSSS system uses a chipping sequence of length **m = 64** and a data rate of **$R_b = 20$ kbps**.
  - (i) Calculate the chip rate and processing gain in dB.
  - (ii) Find the spread bandwidth using the Nyquist criterion.
  - (iii) Comment on the interference-rejection capability. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Spread Spectrum, FHSS vs DSSS:**

**Spread Spectrum** is a technique in which a signal is transmitted over a bandwidth **much larger than its information bandwidth**, deliberately to make it resistant to interference, hard to detect, and capable of code-division multiple access.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | FHSS | DSSS |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Carrier **hops** among many frequencies via a PN code | Each data bit is **XORed** with a high-rate PN chipping code |
| Spreading entity | Frequency slots in time | Chipping sequence in time |
| Synchronization | Hop timing | Chip sequence alignment |
| Advantages | Simple hardware, good against narrow-band jamming | Higher data rates, better multipath resistance |
| Applications | Bluetooth, early Wi-Fi (802.11), military HF | GPS, 3G CDMA cellular, Wi-Fi (802.11b) |

*(7 marks — 2 for spread spectrum definition + 5 for comparison)*

**Part (b) — DSSS Numerical:**

Given: $m = 64$, $R_b = 20 \text{ kbps}$.

**(i) Chip rate and processing gain:**

$$R_c = m \cdot R_b = 64 \times 20{,}000 = 1{,}280{,}000 \text{ chips/s} = 1.28 \text{ Mcps}$$

$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}(m) = 10 \log_{10}(64) = 10 \times 1.8062 = 18.06 \text{ dB}$$

**(ii) Spread bandwidth (Nyquist):**

$$B_{DSSS} \approx 2 R_c = 2 \times 1{,}280{,}000 = 2.56 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iii) Interference-rejection comment:**

A processing gain of **18.06 dB** (≈ 64× power) means the system can tolerate jammers up to **64 times stronger** than the desired signal before the link fails. This is the cornerstone of why DSSS-based systems (e.g., GPS, CDMA) are robust in hostile RF environments.

*(7 marks — 2+2+3 for sub-parts)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Save for the night before the exam)**

- [ ] **FDM** = Frequency-based sharing; needs **guard bands**; bandwidth formula: $N B_s + (N-1) B_{gb}$.
- [ ] **Synchronous TDM** = fixed slots, one per source per frame; output rate = sum of inputs.
- [ ] **Statistical TDM** = slots only for active sources; uses **addressing bits**; more efficient for bursty data.
- [ ] **WDM** = FDM for light; key enabler of **fiber-optic backbone** capacity.
- [ ] **CDM/CDMA** = all users share time **and** frequency; separated by **orthogonal chip codes**.
- [ ] **FHSS** = hopping carrier; remember *Slow* = bits per hop; *Fast* = hops per bit.
- [ ] **DSSS** = XOR with PN code; chip rate = $m R_b$; processing gain = $10 \log_{10} m$ dB.
- [ ] **Processing Gain** is the **only** metric for DSSS interference rejection — memorize the formula.
- [ ] For any numerical, **always** state the formula before substituting — KTU examiners reward methodology marks.
- [ ] Draw the **multiplexer–demultiplexer** block diagram wherever the question allows 7+ marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Golden Trio" of Exam-Worthy Topics in Module 4:**
> 1. FDM with guard-band numerical (almost guaranteed every KTU cycle).
> 2. Synchronous vs Statistical TDM comparison.
> 3. DSSS Processing Gain derivation.
> Master these three and you cover ~80% of the marks from Bandwidth Utilization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Media
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST501 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmission Media

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transmission Media** refers to the physical pathways or channels through which data signals (in the form of electromagnetic waves, electrical pulses, or light pulses) propagate from a source to a destination across a computer network. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PCCST501 - Module 4), transmission media constitutes the **Layer 1 (Physical Layer)** substrate of the OSI and TCP/IP models, providing the essential conduit for bit-level information transfer. They are broadly classified into two super-families: **Guided Media** (bounded, wired) and **Unguided Media** (unbounded, wireless).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Transmission media is the physical medium over which the electromagnetic signals carrying information are transmitted. The choice of media directly impacts **bandwidth**, **latency**, **attenuation**, **EMI susceptibility**, and **maximum achievable data rate**.

## 1.2 Hierarchical Classification of Transmission Media

| Super-Class | Sub-Class | Common Variants |
|---|---|---|
| **Guided (Wired)** | Copper-Based | Twisted Pair (UTP, STP), Coaxial Cable |
| **Guided (Wired)** | Glass/Plastic-Based | Single-Mode Fiber (SMF), Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF) |
| **Unguided (Wireless)** | Terrestrial Radio | Ground Waves, Sky Waves, VHF/UHF |
| **Unguided (Wireless)** | Line-of-Sight | Terrestrial Microwave, Satellite Microwave |
| **Unguided (Wireless)** | Optical Wireless | Infrared, Free-Space Optical (FSO) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Transmission Media as the "Highway System" of Networking**

Imagine a busy Indian city like Kochi. The **data packets** are vehicles carrying goods, and **transmission media** is the road network:

- A **twisted pair cable** is like a narrow two-lane village road — slow, cheap, prone to noise from tractors and cows.
- A **coaxial cable** is like a four-lane state highway — wider, more reliable, and handles more traffic.
- A **fiber optic cable** is like a 6-lane expressway with a dedicated toll booth — extremely fast, no traffic jams, immune to weather.
- **Microwave/Radio waves** are like drones carrying goods through the air — they bypass the road, but are affected by storms, buildings, and line-of-sight.
- **Satellite** is like a courier service from a satellite office in space — it goes anywhere on Earth, but with higher delay due to the long distance.

The "speed limit" of these highways is **bandwidth**, the "fuel efficiency" is **attenuation**, and the "interference from other vehicles" is **noise** — all defined rigorously in Section 2.

## 1.4 Why Transmission Media is Critical in KTU Examinations

- It forms the foundation for understanding **Layer 1 of the OSI Model**.
- It directly connects to **Module 2 (Data Link Layer)** error and flow control.
- It underpins **Module 5 (Network Layer / Routing)** and emerging topics like **5G backhaul** and **FTTH (Fiber-to-the-Home)**.
- Questions on media characteristics, comparisons, and Shannon capacity appear regularly in KTU ESE and internal assessments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept of Signal Propagation

In any transmission medium, three physical phenomena govern performance:

1. **Attenuation (α)** — Loss of signal strength with distance, measured in **dB/km**.
2. **Distortion** — Alteration of signal shape due to frequency-dependent propagation.
3. **Noise** — Unwanted random energy added to the signal (thermal, crosstalk, EMI).

The **received signal power** is governed by:

$$P_R = P_T \cdot G_T \cdot G_R \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 \quad \text{(Friis Free-Space Equation for wireless)}$$

For wired media, attenuation is modeled as:

$$P(d) = P(0) \cdot 10^{-\alpha d / 10}$$

where:
- $P(d)$ = power at distance $d$ (watts)
- $P(0)$ = power at source (watts)
- $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient (**dB/km**)
- $d$ = distance (km)

## 2.2 Guided Media — The Copper Family

### 2.2.1 Twisted Pair Cable

**Why twisted?** Two parallel conductors act as an antenna and pick up noise equally on both lines. By **twisting** the pair, the noise becomes *common-mode* and is rejected by differential amplifiers at the receiver.

| Property | UTP (Unshielded) | STP (Shielded) |
|---|---|---|
| **Categories** | Cat 5e, Cat 6, Cat 6a, Cat 7 | Cat 6a STP, Cat 7 |
| **Bandwidth** | up to **500 MHz** | up to **600 MHz** |
| **Max Data Rate** | 1 Gbps (Cat 5e) to **10 Gbps** (Cat 6a) | up to **10 Gbps** |
| **Max Distance** | 100 m | 100 m |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate |
| **EMI Immunity** | Moderate | High |

### 2.2.2 Coaxial Cable

Consists of a central copper conductor, an insulating dielectric, a metallic shield, and an outer jacket. The shield provides excellent **EMI immunity** and higher bandwidth than twisted pair.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Characteristic Impedance** | 50 Ω (Ethernet) / 75 Ω (Cable TV) |
| **Bandwidth** | up to **1 GHz** |
| **Attenuation** | ~10–20 dB/100m @ 100 MHz |
| **Applications** | Cable Internet, early Ethernet (10Base-2/5) |

### 2.2.3 Fiber Optic Cable

Light propagates via **total internal reflection** inside a glass core. A *core* of refractive index $n_1$ is surrounded by a *cladding* of lower refractive index $n_2 < n_1$.

**Snell's Law** governs the refraction at the core-cladding interface:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

**Critical Angle** (the angle above which total internal reflection occurs):

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$

| Fiber Type | Core Diameter | Light Source | Max Bandwidth-Distance | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Multi-Mode (MMF)** | 50/62.5 µm | LED | 500 MHz·km @ 850nm | LAN, Data Center (short reach) |
| **Single-Mode (SMF)** | 8–10 µm | Laser | 100 THz·km @ 1550nm | Telecom Backbone, Long-Haul |

## 2.3 Unguided (Wireless) Media

Electromagnetic waves are characterized by:

$$c = f \cdot \lambda$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** in vacuum, $f$ is frequency in Hz, and $\lambda$ is wavelength in meters.

| Band | Frequency Range | Wavelength | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VLF** | 3–30 kHz | 10–100 km | Submarine communication |
| **LF/MF** | 30 kHz–3 MHz | 100 m–10 km | AM Radio, Navigation |
| **HF** | 3–30 MHz | 10–100 m | Shortwave Radio, Sky-wave |
| **VHF** | 30–300 MHz | 1–10 m | FM Radio, TV |
| **UHF** | 300 MHz–3 GHz | 10 cm–1 m | Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz), Cellular, GPS |
| **SHF** | 3–30 GHz | 1–10 cm | Microwave, Radar, 5G (28 GHz) |
| **EHF** | 30–300 GHz | 1–10 mm | mmWave 5G, Satellite |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU Favourite):**
> - **Terrestrial Microwave** requires line-of-sight; uses **directional parabolic dish** antennas.
> - **Satellite Microwave** uses **geostationary satellites** at 35,786 km altitude (latency ≈ 250 ms one-way).
> - **Radio (Omnidirectional)** uses **omnidirectional whip antennas** (e.g., Wi-Fi routers).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas are asked repeatedly in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Attenuation in dB | $A = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}} / P_{\text{out}})$ dB | Power loss in cable |
| 2 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB) | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ dB | Channel quality |
| 3 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bits/sec | Max data rate (noisy channel) |
| 4 | Nyquist Bit Rate (noiseless) | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ bits/sec | Max data rate (noiseless, M-level) |
| 5 | Wavelength–Frequency | $\lambda = c / f$ m | Wireless & optical |
| 6 | Critical Angle (fiber) | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2 / n_1)$ degrees | TIR condition in fiber |
| 7 | Refractive Index | $n = c / v$ (dimensionless) | Light speed in medium |
| 8 | Numerical Aperture | $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Acceptance cone of fiber |
| 9 | Propagation Delay | $T_d = d / v$ seconds | Fiber ~5 ns/m, Copper ~10 ns/m |
| 10 | Free-Space Loss | $L_{\text{fs}} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$ dB | Microwave link budget |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecom Backbone (BSNL, Reliance Jio)**: Long-haul single-mode fiber carrying 100 Gbps+ per wavelength using DWDM (Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing).
- **FTTH (BharatNet project in Kerala)**: Optical fiber from central office to home.
- **Data Centers (Amazon AWS Mumbai region)**: MMF OM4/OM5 fiber for 400 Gbps Ethernet.
- **5G Fronthaul/Backhaul**: mmWave links at 28 GHz or 60 GHz with fiber as the fixed-line core.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT in Kerala's Smart City projects)**: Twisted pair (RS-485) and short-range radio (LoRa, Zigbee).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Shannon Channel Capacity Theorem

### Step 1 — Statement of the Problem

Given a channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz) and a signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ (linear, not dB), what is the **maximum theoretical data rate** at which information can be transmitted with arbitrarily low error probability?

### Step 2 — Background: Information per Symbol

For a symbol drawn from a Gaussian distribution with variance $\sigma^2$, the differential entropy is:

$$H = \frac{1}{2} \log_2(2\pi e \sigma^2) \quad \text{bits/symbol}$$

### Step 3 — Information Rate

If $2B$ independent samples are taken per second (Nyquist sampling rate), the information rate is:

$$C = 2B \cdot H = 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2} \log_2(2\pi e (S+N)/N)$$

### Step 4 — Simplification

The signal power $S$ adds to noise $N$. The total power normalized to $N$ gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_2\left(2\pi e \cdot \frac{S+N}{N}\right) \\
&= B \cdot \log_2\left(2\pi e \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N} + 1\right)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Dropping the Constant

The constant $2\pi e$ arises from the Gaussian distribution assumption. The **Shannon result** retains only the term depending on $S/N$:

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/second}$$

This is the celebrated **Shannon–Hartley theorem**.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A channel has bandwidth $B = 3 \text{ kHz}$ and $\text{SNR} = 30 \text{ dB}$. Find the Shannon capacity.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula:**

$$
C = 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) = 3000 \times \log_2(1001)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\log_2(1001)$:**

$$
\log_2(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{3.0004}{0.30103} \approx 9.967
$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer:**

$$
C \approx 3000 \times 9.967 \approx 29{,}900 \text{ bps} \approx 29.9 \text{ kbps}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation II: Critical Angle for Total Internal Reflection

### Step 1 — Snell's Law

When light travels from medium 1 (core) to medium 2 (cladding):

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

### Step 2 — TIR Condition

Total internal reflection occurs when $\theta_2 \geq 90°$, i.e., $\sin\theta_2 = 1$.

$$
n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \cdot 1
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Critical Angle

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Favourite)

A fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$ (core) and $n_2 = 1.46$ (cladding). Find the critical angle.

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1.46}{1.48}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9865) \approx 80.4°
$$

**Interpretation:** Any ray striking the core-cladding interface at an angle greater than **80.4°** from the normal will undergo total internal reflection and stay confined to the core.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Numerical Aperture (NA) and Acceptance Angle

### Step 1 — From Outside the Fiber

A ray enters from air ($n_0 = 1.0$) at an angle $\theta_0$ to the fiber axis. It refracts to angle $\theta_1$ inside the core.

$$
n_0 \sin\theta_0 = n_1 \sin\theta_1
$$

### Step 2 — Using the Critical Condition

For TIR to occur, the ray must hit the interface at $\theta \geq \theta_c$:

$$
\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

### Step 3 — Geometric Relation

The angle inside the core to the axis and the angle to the normal are complementary. Using Snell's law applied at the air-core boundary and the geometry of refraction:

$$
\text{NA} = n_0 \sin\theta_{0,\max} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

### Step 4 — Acceptance Cone

All rays entering within a cone of half-angle $\theta_{0,\max}$ are guided by the fiber.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Media Comparison (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

| Parameter | UTP | Coaxial | MMF | SMF | Microwave | Satellite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Bandwidth** | 500 MHz | 1 GHz | 500 MHz·km | 100 THz·km | 30 MHz/ch | 500 MHz |
| **Data Rate** | 10 Gbps | 1 Gbps | 100 Gbps | 1 Tbps+ | 100+ Mbps | 1 Gbps+ |
| **Attenuation** | High | Medium | 3 dB/km | 0.2 dB/km | Path-loss | Free-space |
| **EMI Immunity** | Low | Medium | **Total** | **Total** | Low | Low |
| **Distance** | 100 m | 500 m | 2 km | 100+ km | 50 km | 36,000 km |
| **Security** | Low | Low | **Very High** | **Very High** | Low | Low |
| **Cost/meter** | ₹5 | ₹30 | ₹50 | ₹80 | Setup-heavy | Very High |
| **Installation** | Easy | Moderate | Skilled | Skilled | Towers | Satellite |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Classify transmission media. Differentiate between guided and unguided media with two examples each.

**Model Answer (10–12 lines, 3 marks):**

**Definition:** Transmission media is the physical path between transmitter and receiver used to carry data signals.

**Classification:**

1. **Guided (Wired) Media:** Signals propagate within a solid medium.
   - *Examples:* Twisted Pair Cable, Coaxial Cable, Optical Fiber.
2. **Unguided (Wireless) Media:** Signals propagate through free space (air/vacuum) as electromagnetic waves.
   - *Examples:* Radio Waves, Microwaves, Infrared.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Guided | Unguided |
|---|---|---|
| Signal path | Bounded by cable | Free propagation |
| Security | Higher | Lower (susceptible to interception) |
| Attenuation | Predictable | Distance and weather dependent |
| Installation | Cabling required | Antenna-based |

*(1 mark for classification, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for differences = 3 marks)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** State the Shannon–Hartley theorem. What is the maximum data rate of a noiseless channel of bandwidth 4 kHz using 16 signal levels?

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** The Shannon–Hartley theorem gives the theoretical upper limit on the data rate $C$ of a noisy channel:

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) \text{ bits/second}$$

**Numerical:** For a *noiseless* channel, Nyquist's formula applies:

$$C = 2B \log_2(M)$$

Given $B = 4000$ Hz, $M = 16$:

$$
C = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(16) = 8000 \times 4 = 32{,}000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}
$$

*(1 mark theorem, 1 mark formula, 1 mark calculation = 3 marks)*
