## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule:** In ESE Part B, students must answer ONE full question (14 marks) from a module, with sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 & Model Paper 2024]*

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of optical fiber cable. Derive the expression for the critical angle and numerical aperture.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (2 marks):**

An optical fiber consists of three concentric cylindrical sections:

- **Core:** Central glass/plastic region of refractive index $n_1$ (typical $n_1 = 1.48$). Diameter: 8–10 µm (SMF) or 50/62.5 µm (MMF).
- **Cladding:** Surrounding glass of lower refractive index $n_2$ (typical $n_2 = 1.46$).
- **Buffer/Coating:** Protective polymer jacket (yellow for SMF, orange/aqua for MMF).

**2. Working Principle — Total Internal Reflection (2 marks):**

Light rays entering the core at angles within the **acceptance cone** strike the core-cladding boundary at angles greater than the critical angle $\theta_c$. This causes **total internal reflection**, confining the light within the core and allowing it to propagate via successive reflections.

**3. Derivation of Critical Angle (2 marks):**

Applying Snell's law at the core-cladding interface:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

For TIR, $\theta_2 = 90°$, so $\sin\theta_2 = 1$. At the threshold:

$$
n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \Rightarrow \theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)
$$

**4. Numerical Aperture (1 mark):**

The acceptance angle $\theta_a$ outside the fiber relates to the NA as:

$$\text{NA} = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

This is the **light-gathering ability** of the fiber. Higher NA → more light coupled in → larger core acceptance.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A multimode graded-index fiber has a core diameter of 50 µm and operates at 850 nm. Compare its bandwidth-distance product with a single-mode fiber at 1550 nm. Why is SMF preferred for long-haul communication?

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | MMF (850 nm) | SMF (1550 nm) |
|---|---|---|
| Core Diameter | 50 µm | 8–10 µm |
| Bandwidth-Distance | ~500 MHz·km | ~100 THz·km |
| Modal Dispersion | **Severe** (inter-modal) | **Negligible** (single mode) |
| Attenuation | 3 dB/km | 0.2 dB/km |
| Light Source | LED | Laser |
| Cost per meter | Low | Moderate |

**Why SMF is preferred for long haul (4 marks):**

1. **Lowest Attenuation Window:** At 1550 nm, silica fiber has its **minimum attenuation** (~0.2 dB/km), allowing signals to travel 100+ km without regeneration.
2. **Zero Modal Dispersion:** Only one propagation mode exists in SMF, eliminating inter-modal dispersion that limits MMF.
3. **Highest Bandwidth:** Combined with DWDM (Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing), one SMF pair can carry **multi-Tbps** aggregate.
4. **EDFA Compatibility:** The 1550 nm window coincides with the Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier gain band, enabling in-line optical amplification without electrical conversion.
5. **Future-Proofing:** SMF supports higher-order modulation (QAM-64, QAM-256) and coherent detection for 100G/400G/1T links.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 & 2024 Model Paper]*

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain three categories of twisted pair cables. List any two IEEE standards that use twisted pair cabling.

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (Block-Level, 2 marks):**

```
Twisted Pair Cable Construction
+--------------------------+
|  Outer PVC Jacket        |
|  +--------------------+  |
|  | Twist Pitch ~5cm  |  |
|  |  o  o   o  o  o  o |  |
|  | (4 twisted pairs) |  |
|  +--------------------+  |
+--------------------------+
```

**Three Categories (4 marks):**

1. **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair):** No metallic shield; only the twist provides noise rejection. Used in Ethernet LANs, telephone lines. Cheapest, most flexible.
2. **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair):** Each pair wrapped in foil; overall braid shield. Better EMI immunity. Used in token ring, IBM cabling.
3. **ScTP / FTP (Screened/Foiled):** Pairs are unshielded but the entire cable is wrapped in foil. Common in Cat 6a installations.

**IEEE Standards Using Twisted Pair (1 mark):**

- **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet):** 10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T, 10GBASE-T.
- **IEEE 802.3an / 802.3bq:** 10 Gbps and 40 Gbps over twisted pair.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Calculate the maximum data rate achievable for a channel with SNR of 40 dB and bandwidth of 4 kHz using (i) Nyquist (assuming 4 signal levels) and (ii) Shannon's formula. Comment on the results.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $B = 4000$ Hz
- SNR = 40 dB → Linear SNR $= 10^{40/10} = 10{,}000$
- $M = 4$ signal levels (Nyquist)

**(i) Nyquist Bit Rate (3 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2(M) = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(4)
$$

Since $\log_2(4) = 2$:

$$
C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 8000 \times 2 = 16{,}000 \text{ bps} = 16 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(ii) Shannon Capacity (3 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \log_2(1 + S/N)
$$

$$
C_{\text{Shannon}} = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 10000) = 4000 \times \log_2(10001)
$$

$$
\log_2(10001) = \frac{\log_{10}(10001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{4.00004}{0.30103} \approx 13.288
$$

$$
C_{\text{Shannon}} \approx 4000 \times 13.288 = 53{,}152 \text{ bps} \approx 53.15 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**

The Shannon capacity (~53 kbps) is the **theoretical maximum** considering noise. The Nyquist rate (16 kbps) is achievable with **4 signal levels** in a *noiseless* case. In practice, the achievable rate lies between these bounds and depends on the modulation/coding scheme (M-ary QAM, OFDM, etc.).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert SNR from dB to linear scale** before substituting into Shannon's formula. This leads to a wrong answer worth 3–4 marks. Always do: $S/N_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)}$. Also, mark $\log_2(1+S/N)$ clearly — do not use $\log_{10}$ by mistake.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 4: Transmission Media)**

- **Two super-classes:** Guided (wired) and Unguided (wireless) — be ready to differentiate with parameters.
- **Twisted Pair:** Twisting rejects common-mode noise. Categories Cat 5e (1 Gbps) to Cat 7 (10 Gbps), 100 m limit.
- **Coaxial Cable:** 50 Ω for Ethernet, 75 Ω for CATV. Higher bandwidth than UTP.
- **Optical Fiber:** TIR-based; $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$; $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$.
- **SMF vs MMF:** SMF (8 µm core, 1550 nm laser) for long-haul; MMF (50/62.5 µm, LED 850 nm) for LAN.
- **Attenuation formula:** $A = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$ in dB; copper ~10 dB/100m, fiber ~0.2 dB/km.
- **Wavelength-Frequency:** $c = f\lambda = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **Shannon Theorem:** $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ — convert SNR to linear first.
- **Nyquist Bit Rate:** $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ for noiseless channel with $M$ levels.
- **Microwave:** Line-of-sight; 4–6 GHz terrestrial, 28 GHz/60 GHz for 5G.
- **Satellite:** Geostationary at **35,786 km**; latency ~250 ms; LEO constellations (Starlink) at ~550 km reduce this to ~25 ms.
- **Infrared:** Short-range (<10 m), line-of-sight, cannot penetrate walls (used in TV remotes, IrDA).
- **EMI Immunity Ranking:** Optical Fiber (Total) > Coaxial > STP > UTP > Twisted Pair Unshielded > Wireless.
- **Free-Space Loss:** $L_{\text{fs}} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$ dB.
- **Numerical values to memorize:** $n_1 \approx 1.48$, $n_2 \approx 1.46$ for silica fiber; $\theta_c \approx 80°$.
- **Mnemonic for wireless bands:** *"Viva La Vida, Mi Hermano" — VLF, LF, MF, HF, VHF, UHF, SHF, EHF.*

> **End of Module 4 — Transmission Media Notes. Best of luck for KTU ESE 2024!**
